For a path of length d, the total rain attenuation (with effective path length correction):

$$A_R = \gamma_R \cdot L_{eff} = k R^{\alpha} \cdot \frac{d}{1 + d/d_0}$$

where **d₀ ≈ 35 × exp(−0.015 R)** for R in mm/hr.

**Why it matters in Kerala:** Tropical rainfall rates often exceed 100 mm/hr, producing attenuation of **>10 dB** at Ku-band over a 5 km path — a major design consideration for VSAT and DTH links.

**(4) Fog and Cloud Attenuation**

For droplets much smaller than wavelength (Rayleigh regime), the attenuation is:

$$\gamma_f = K_l(T) \cdot M \;\; \text{(dB/km)}$$

where **M** is liquid-water density in g/m³. For dense fog (M ≈ 0.5 g/m³) at 30 GHz, γ_f ≈ 0.5 dB/km. Important at mmWave (60+ GHz) and Ka-band.

**(5) Scintillation and Depolarization**

Rapid signal-level variations due to small-scale refractive-index turbulence (especially near the ground on hot days). At frequencies above 10 GHz, **scintillation** of 1–5 dB peak-to-peak is common.

---

**(b) A microwave link operates at 6 GHz over a 40 km path. Calculate the free-space path loss. If the actual measured loss is 144 dB, what is the additional atmospheric and ground-loss contribution? Comment on the most likely cause.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):**

Using the standard KTU formula:

$$\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

With **d = 40 km** and **f = 6 GHz = 6000 MHz**:

$$20 \log_{10}(40) = 20 \times 1.6021 = 32.04 \text{ dB}$$

$$20 \log_{10}(6000) = 20 \times 3.7782 = 75.56 \text{ dB}$$

$$\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 32.04 + 75.56 = 140.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Excess Loss:**

$$\text{Excess Loss} = \text{Measured} - \text{FSPL} = 144 - 140.04 \approx 3.96 \text{ dB} \approx 4 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Likely causes of the ~4 dB excess:**

At **6 GHz over 40 km**, the dominant additional losses are:

1. **Atmospheric gaseous absorption (O₂ + H₂O):** at 6 GHz, γ_a ≈ 0.005 dB/km, giving **A_a ≈ 0.2 dB** over 40 km. **Negligible contribution.**
2. **Rain attenuation:** for moderate rain (R = 10 mm/hr) at 6 GHz with k ≈ 0.000715, α ≈ 1.155: **γ_R ≈ 0.011 dB/km**, total ≈ 0.44 dB. **Small.**
3. **Fading margin / multipath:** typically 2–6 dB peak, average ~2 dB. **Likely contributor.**
4. **Antenna misalignment, feeder loss, and obstruction loss:** typically 1–2 dB.
5. **Ground reflection loss:** if path profile is not well designed, partial obstruction of Fresnel zones can cost 2–5 dB.

**Conclusion:** The **most likely cause** of the 4 dB excess at 6 GHz is **multipath fading combined with partial Fresnel-zone obstruction** (or minor rain). For a well-engineered link, the designer should include a **fade margin of 4–5 dB** at 6 GHz for a 40 km path. The link is operating close to design specifications, with a small but acceptable margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mark Loss Area:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write **d = 40 m** or **f = 6 MHz** in the FSPL formula. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Forgetting the 32.44 constant:** the formula is incomplete without it.
> 3. **No unit conversion step:** KTU expects to see explicit conversion of f = 6 GHz → 6000 MHz *before* substituting.
> 4. **Misinterpreting "additional loss":** it is the **measured − FSPL**, not FSPL − measured. Sign matters.
> 5. **Wrong f-formula:** using `20 log(f_MHz)` when f was given in GHz — a 60 dB error!

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔖 High-Yield Points to Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- ⭐ **Standard k-factor = 4/3** and standard gradient **dn/dh = −39 N/km** — use these unless stated otherwise.
- ⭐ **R = 6370 km** (NOT 6400 km) — Earth radius constant in KTU problems.
- ⭐ **Critical gradient = −157 N/km** — boundary between super-refraction and ducting.
- ⭐ **FSPL formula:** 32.44 + 20log₁₀(dₖₘ) + 20log₁₀(f_MHz) — must use **km** and **MHz**.
- ⭐ **Earth bulge (mid):** H = d²/(8kR) — increases quadratically with distance.
- ⭐ **First Fresnel zone (mid):** F₁ = 0.5√(λd) — design rule: 60% clearance of F₁.
- ⭐ **Rain attenuation:** γ_R = kR^α (dB/km) — k, α are frequency-dependent ITU-R coefficients.
- ⭐ **Oxygen absorption peak:** 60 GHz; **water-vapor peaks:** 22 GHz and 183 GHz.
- ⭐ **Ducting →** dn/dh < −157 N/km; **Standard →** k = 4/3; **Sub-refraction →** k > 4/3.
- ⭐ **Tropospheric scatter** = beyond-horizon propagation (100–1000 km) via refractive-index turbulence.
- ⭐ **Ionospheric effects on satellite links:** Faraday rotation (must correct for polarization twist) and group delay.
- ⭐ **Effective Earth radius:** R_eff = kR = 8493 km for k = 4/3.
- ⭐ **Five-path clearance rule:** for line-of-sight links, 60% of the first Fresnel zone must be clear of obstructions including Earth bulge.
- ⭐ **Rain fade margin for tropical design (Kerala):** 6–10 dB at Ku-band; 10–15 dB at Ka-band.

> 🎯 **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always draw a **path profile diagram** showing antenna heights, line of sight, Earth bulge, and Fresnel zones for line-of-sight design problems — these visual elements carry 2–3 marks even when the numerical answer is correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cavity Resonator: TE and TM Modes in Waveguides (Review only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cavity Resonator: TE and TM Modes in Waveguides (Review only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Cavity Resonator** is a closed, hollow metallic enclosure bounded by perfectly conducting walls, in which electromagnetic energy can be stored in the form of standing waves at one or more discrete **resonant frequencies**. It is essentially a short-circuited section of a waveguide (rectangular, circular, or re-entrant) in which the propagating waveguide modes are converted into standing wave patterns by the two end shorting plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Since this is a *Review only* topic, the focus is on recollecting the governing resonant-frequency equations, the mode nomenclature (TE_{mnp} / TM_{mnp}), the field patterns, and the Quality factor (Q) — all of which are heavily used in subsequent modules dealing with klystrons, magnetrons, and microwave filters.

Mathematically, a cavity resonator is a boundary-value problem in which Maxwell's curl equations are solved subject to the boundary condition that the **tangential electric field must vanish on every conducting wall**:

$$\hat{n} \times \vec{E} = 0 \quad \text{on the cavity walls}$$

This boundary condition forces a discrete (quantised) set of allowed field configurations called **modes**, and a corresponding discrete set of allowed angular frequencies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a cavity resonator as the **electromagnetic equivalent of an organ pipe**, but completely closed at both ends:

- An **open organ pipe** ↔ An open-ended transmission-line stub (broadband, weakly resonant).
- A **closed organ pipe** ↔ A waveguide section shorted at both ends (cavity resonator — highly resonant, very narrow bandwidth).
- Just as an organ pipe supports standing acoustic waves at certain frequencies (fundamental, 2nd harmonic, 3rd harmonic, …), a cavity supports standing EM waves labelled **TE_{101}, TE_{102}, TE_{011}, TM_{110}, …**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A microwave oven uses a **rectangular cavity resonator** operating in the **TE_{10p}** family of modes. The microwaves bounce back and forth between the metal walls, creating standing-wave patterns. Food placed inside absorbs energy at the anti-node (peak E-field) locations.

## 1.3 Why Cavity Resonators?

| Property | LC Tank Circuit | Cavity Resonator |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Useful frequency range | Up to ~**1 GHz** | **1 GHz – 300 GHz** |
| Q-factor | 100 – 500 | 5 000 – 50 000+ |
| Size at 10 GHz | Impractical | Few cm³ |
| Power handling | Low (mW) | High (kW – MW) |

At microwave frequencies, ordinary lumped LC circuits become unusable because the inductor and capacitor lead lengths become comparable to a wavelength. The cavity resonator is the natural replacement.

**Engineering applications:** klystron input/output couplers, magnetron anode blocks, RF accelerating cavities in particle accelerators, microwave filters, wavemeters, frequency meters, and radar duplexers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of Cavity Resonators

A cavity resonator is constructed from a uniform waveguide that is **closed at both ends** by perfectly conducting (short-circuit) walls. The waveguide used determines the cavity type:

1. **Rectangular Cavity Resonator** — derived from a rectangular waveguide closed at z = 0 and z = d. The dominant mode is **TE_{10p}** (typically **TE_{101}**).
2. **Cylindrical (Circular) Cavity Resonator** — derived from a circular waveguide closed at z = 0 and z = d. The dominant mode is **TM_{010}**.

## 2.2 Mode Nomenclature — Why three indices?

A waveguide has a 2-D cross-section (m, n) and one propagation direction. A cavity, being closed in all three directions, has **three indices (m, n, p)**:

- **m, n** → variation across the broad × narrow wall (rectangular) or azimuthal × radial (cylindrical) cross-section.
- **p** → number of half-wave variations along the **length** of the cavity (the new dimension introduced by shorting).

For a rectangular cavity:
- **TE_{mnp} mode:** $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$. Indices m and n cannot both be zero (otherwise k_c = 0 → no field).
- **TM_{mnp} mode:** $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$. Both m and n must be ≥ 1.

For a cylindrical cavity:
- **TE_{nml} mode:** roots $X'_{nl}$ of $J_n(x) = 0$.
- **TM_{nml} mode:** roots $X_{nl}$ of $J_n'(x) = 0$.

## 2.3 The Resonant Condition — A Geometric Picture

When a forward wave ($e^{-j\beta z}$) reflects from a short circuit, it inverts sign and travels back ($e^{+j\beta z}$). Superposition of these two counter-propagating waves produces a **standing wave** whose spatial period is governed by:

$$\beta d = p \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = \frac{p \pi}{d}, \quad p = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

But the propagation constant in a hollow guide obeys:

$$\beta^2 = k^2 - k_c^2 = \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - k_c^2$$

Substituting the standing-wave condition:

$$\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon = k_c^2 + \left(\frac{p \pi}{d}\right)^2$$

This is the **fundamental transcendental equation of every cavity resonator**. The right side is purely geometric (size + mode indices), so the resonant angular frequency is a discrete function of cavity dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The cavity behaves like a 3-D "box" whose dimensions must each equal an integer number of half-wavelengths (for the appropriate mode pattern). The cavity can therefore resonate at a whole *family* of frequencies — one per (m, n, p) combination.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table — Commit these to memory.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Rectangular cavity TE_{mnp} / TM_{mnp} resonant frequency | $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p\pi}{d}\right)^2}$ | Hz; a, b, d = broad, narrow, length |
| 2 | Rectangular cavity: free-space form | $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$ | Hz; air-filled |
| 3 | Cut-off wavenumber of rectangular guide | $k_c^2 = \left(\dfrac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$ | rad/m |
| 4 | Cylindrical cavity TM_{nml} resonant frequency | $f_{nml} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$ | Hz; $X'_{nl}$ = lth root of $J_n$ |
| 5 | Cylindrical cavity TE_{nml} resonant frequency | $f_{nml} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{X_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$ | Hz; $X_{nl}$ = lth root of $J'_n$ |
| 6 | Dominant mode of cylindrical cavity | **TM_{010}** with $X'_{01} = 2.405$ | Gives $f_{010} = \dfrac{c \cdot 2.405}{2\pi a}$ |
| 7 | Quality factor (general) | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}} = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Q-factor of rectangular TE_{10l} cavity | $Q_c = \dfrac{k \eta \, a b d \,(a^2 + d^2)}{4 R_s\,[\,2 b(a^3 + d^3) + a d (a^2 + d^2)\,]}$ | $R_s$ = surface resistivity |
| 9 | Surface resistivity | $R_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu}{2 \sigma}}$ | Ω; σ = conductivity |
| 10 | Skin depth | $\delta_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | m |

> [!NOTE]
> Always express **$\vert x \vert$** as **`\vert x \vert`** in LaTeX, never as `|x|`, to avoid breaking markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Klystron input & output cavities** → operate as **TE_{10p}** re-entrant cavities. The resonant frequency determines the klystron's operating frequency.
- **Magnetron anode block** → an array of cylindrical **TM_{010}** cavities coupled through slots. Each cavity oscillates at the same frequency but at a specific RF phase.
- **Particle accelerators** (e.g., LHC, SLAC) → cylindrical **TM_{010}** accelerating structures where the on-axis E-field accelerates charged particles.
- **Microwave wavemeters** → tunable cylindrical cavities used for precise frequency measurement.
- **Radar duplexers** → high-Q cavities used to protect the receiver during transmit pulses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resonant Frequency of Rectangular Cavity (TE_{mnp} mode)

### Setup
A rectangular cavity of dimensions $a \times b \times d$ (along x, y, z) is formed by short-circuiting a rectangular waveguide at $z = 0$ and $z = d$. We start with a forward-propagating TE_{mn} mode in the parent waveguide.

### Step 1 — Forward-propagating wave inside the waveguide

The longitudinal magnetic field of a TE_{mn} mode propagating in the +z direction is:

$$H_z^+(x, y, z) = H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) e^{-j \beta z}$$

where $\beta$ is the propagation constant and $H_0$ is the amplitude.

### Step 2 — Reflected wave from the short circuit at $z = d$

A perfect conductor enforces $E_{\text{tangential}} = 0$ on its surface. For a short at $z = d$, the reflected wave must cancel the tangential E-field of the forward wave. The reflected wave therefore flips sign for the longitudinal H-component (which is even with respect to E in the y-direction convention):

$$H_z^-(x, y, z) = -H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) e^{+j \beta (z - 2d)}$$

### Step 3 — Total field in the cavity (superposition)

The standing wave is the sum of forward and reflected waves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_z &= H_z^+ + H_z^- \\
    &= H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)\Big[e^{-j \beta z} - e^{+j \beta (z - 2d)}\Big] \\
    &= H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)\Big[e^{-j \beta z} + e^{+j \beta z}\Big] \quad \text{(after applying short-circuit phase shift)} \\
    &= 2 H_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)\cos(\beta z)
\end{aligned}
$$

(We absorb the sign and the $2d$ phase shift into a redefined amplitude. The condition that the second short at $z=0$ also produces $E_{\text{tan}}=0$ forces $\beta d = p\pi$.)

### Step 4 — Standing-wave condition (boundary at z = 0 and z = d)

For the tangential E-field to vanish at both end walls:

$$\sin(\beta d) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta d = p\pi, \quad p = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

Thus:

$$\beta = \frac{p \pi}{d}$$

### Step 5 — Relate $\beta$ to the resonant frequency

The hollow-waveguide dispersion relation is:

$$\beta^2 = \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - k_c^2$$

with the cut-off wavenumber for the parent TE_{mn} waveguide mode:

$$k_c^2 = \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\beta = p\pi/d$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{p\pi}{d}\right)^2 &= \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2 \\
\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon &= \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p\pi}{d}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final resonant frequency

$$
\boxed{\,f_{mnp} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p\pi}{d}\right)^2}\,}
$$

For an air-filled cavity ($\mu = \mu_0$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$, $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$):

$$f_{mnp} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is identical in form for **TM_{mnp}** modes — only the boundary conditions on m, n differ (m, n ≥ 1 for TM).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resonant Frequency of Cylindrical Cavity (TM_{nml} mode)

### Setup
A cylindrical cavity of radius $a$ and length $d$ is obtained by short-circuiting a circular waveguide at $z = 0$ and $z = d$. We analyse the TM_{nm} parent waveguide mode.

### Step 1 — Longitudinal E-field of the parent TM_{nm} waveguide mode

$$E_z^+(\rho, \phi, z) = E_0 J_n(k_c \rho)\, \cos(n\phi)\, e^{-j \beta z}$$

where $J_n$ is the Bessel function of the first kind of order $n$, and $k_c$ is the radial wavenumber.

### Step 2 — Boundary condition at $\rho = a$

For a TM mode, $E_z$ must vanish at the conducting wall:

$$E_z(\rho = a) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_n(k_c a) = 0$$

Hence $k_c a$ must equal the $l^{\text{th}}$ root of $J_n$, which we denote $X'_{nl}$ (prime denotes the root of the function, not its derivative). The first few values:

| Mode | $X'_{nl}$ |
|------|-----------|
| $X'_{01}$ | 2.405 |
| $X'_{11}$ | 3.832 |
| $X'_{02}$ | 5.520 |
| $X'_{12}$ | 7.016 |

Therefore:

$$k_c = \frac{X'_{nl}}{a}$$

### Step 3 — Standing-wave condition along z

As in the rectangular case, the short-circuit end plates enforce:

$$\beta = \frac{l \pi}{d}, \quad l = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

(Note: $l$ replaces $p$ in the standard cylindrical-cavity notation.)

### Step 4 — Apply the dispersion relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta^2 &= \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - k_c^2 \\
\left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2 &= \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - \left(\frac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 \\
\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon &= \left(\frac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final resonant frequency

$$
\boxed{\,f_{nml}^{\text{TM}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}\,}
$$

For an air-filled cavity:

$$f_{nml}^{\text{TM}} = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\left(\frac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$$

The TE_{nml} mode formula is identical but uses the roots $X_{nl}$ of $J'_n(x) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The dominant mode of a cylindrical cavity is TM_{010}** because:
> 1. $X'_{01} = 2.405$ is the **smallest** Bessel root.
> 2. $l = 0$ gives the **lowest** axial half-wave count.
> 3. Therefore $f_{010} = \dfrac{c \cdot 2.405}{2\pi a}$ is the **lowest possible** resonant frequency.
> 4. Remarkably, the resonant frequency of TM_{010} is **independent of length d**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quality Factor of Rectangular TE_{10l} Cavity

The Q-factor of any resonator is:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 \, W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}}$$

At resonance, the time-averaged electric and magnetic stored energies are equal: $W_E = W_H = W/2$, so we may compute $W = 2 W_E$ and integrate the surface current losses.

### Step 1 — Fields of the TE_{10l} mode

For the dominant rectangular cavity mode (m=1, n=0, p=l):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_y &= E_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{l\pi z}{d}\right) \\
H_x &= \frac{l\pi}{d}\frac{E_0}{j\omega\mu}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{l\pi z}{d}\right) \\
H_z &= \frac{\pi}{a}\frac{E_0}{j\omega\mu}\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{l\pi z}{d}\right) \\
E_x &= E_z = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Stored electric energy

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_E &= \frac{\varepsilon}{4}\int_0^a\!\!\int_0^b\!\!\int_0^d |E_y|^2\, dx\, dy\, dz \\
    &= \frac{\varepsilon E_0^2}{4}\int_0^a\!\!\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right) dx \int_0^b\!\!\!dy \int_0^d\!\!\sin^2\!\left(\frac{l\pi z}{d}\right) dz \\
    &= \frac{\varepsilon E_0^2}{4}\cdot\frac{a}{2}\cdot b \cdot \frac{d}{2} \\
    &= \frac{\varepsilon\, E_0^2\, a\, b\, d}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $W_{\text{stored}} = 2 W_E = \dfrac{\varepsilon E_0^2 abd}{8}$.

### Step 3 — Wall loss — power dissipated in each wall

Power dissipated per unit area is $\frac{R_s}{2} |H_{\text{tan}}|^2$, where $H_{\text{tan}}$ is the magnetic field tangential to the wall. The total loss is the integral over all six walls.

**Walls at $y = 0$ and $y = b$ (top and bottom):** tangential H = $H_x$ + $H_z$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{y=0,b} &= \frac{R_s}{2}\cdot 2 \int_0^a\!\!\int_0^d \left(|H_x|^2 + |H_z|^2\right) dx\, dz \\
          &= R_s \int_0^a\!\!\int_0^d\!\left[\left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\frac{E_0}{\omega\mu}\right)^2\!\!\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos^2\!\left(\frac{l\pi z}{d}\right) + \left(\frac{\pi}{a}\frac{E_0}{\omega\mu}\right)^2\!\!\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin^2\!\left(\frac{l\pi z}{d}\right)\right]dx\, dz \\
          &= R_s\,\frac{E_0^2}{\omega^2\mu^2}\left[\left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2\frac{ad}{4} + \left(\frac{\pi}{a}\right)^2\frac{ad}{4}\right] \\
          &= \frac{R_s\, a\, d\, E_0^2\, \pi^2}{4\,\omega^2\mu^2}\left[\frac{l^2}{d^2} + \frac{1}{a^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Walls at $x = 0$ and $x = a$ (left and right):** tangential H = $H_y = 0$ and $H_z = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = a$ (because $\cos(\pi) = -1$ and $\cos(0) = 1$ but $\cos(\pi \cdot 1/a \cdot 0) = 1$; checking… at $x=0$, $\sin(\pi x/a)=0$ so $H_x = 0$; $\cos(\pi x/a) = 1$ so $H_z = E_0\pi/(\omega\mu a) \cdot 1 \cdot \sin(l\pi z/d)$ — non-zero!).

At $x = 0$ and $x = a$, the tangential magnetic field is $H_z$ (since $H_y = 0$ throughout). After integrating:

$$P_{x=0,a} = \frac{R_s\, b\, d\, E_0^2\, \pi^2}{4\,\omega^2\mu^2\, a^2}$$

**Walls at $z = 0$ and $z = d$ (front and back):** tangential H = $H_x$ (since $H_y = 0$, and the normal is $\pm\hat{z}$). After integration:

$$P_{z=0,d} = \frac{R_s\, a\, b\, E_0^2\, l^2\pi^2}{4\,\omega^2\mu^2\, d^2}$$

### Step 4 — Total loss and final Q

Adding all wall contributions and using $\omega^2\mu^2 = k^2\eta^2$ (with $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ and $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$):

$$
\boxed{\,Q_c \;=\; \frac{k\,\eta\, a\, b\, d\,(a^2 + d^2)}{4\,R_s\,[\,2 b(a^3 + d^3) + a d (a^2 + d^2)\,]}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of Q:**
> - High Q → narrow bandwidth → sharp frequency selectivity (used in filters and wavemeters).
> - Q is **inversely proportional** to wall conductivity loss (better silver-plating → higher Q).
> - Q is **proportional to cavity volume / surface area** ratio — bigger cavities store more energy per unit loss.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a cavity resonator. Why are cavity resonators preferred over conventional LC tank circuits at microwave frequencies?**

**Model Answer:**

A cavity resonator is a closed metallic enclosure in which electromagnetic energy is stored in the form of standing waves at discrete resonant frequencies. It is constructed by short-circuiting both ends of a waveguide (rectangular, circular, or coaxial).

At microwave frequencies (typically > 1 GHz), conventional LC tank circuits become impractical because:
1. The physical dimensions of L and C become comparable to a wavelength, leading to unwanted radiation and parasitic effects.
2. Skin-effect losses in the small inductors become severe.
3. The Q-factor degrades drastically (typically < 500).

Cavity resonators overcome these issues because:
- They are enclosed, hence no radiation loss.
- The Q-factor is very high (5 000 – 50 000).
- Power handling is high (kW to MW).
- They are compact (a few cm³ at 10 GHz).

> [!NOTE]
> Full-marks answer expected: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for LC limitation, 1 mark for cavity advantages.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State and explain the quality factor of a cavity resonator. What does a high Q imply?**

**Model Answer:**

The quality factor Q of a cavity resonator is defined as:

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Energy stored per cycle}}{\text{Energy dissipated per cycle}} = \frac{\omega_0 W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}} = \frac{\omega_0}{BW}$$

where $\omega_0$ is the resonant angular frequency and $BW$ is the 3-dB bandwidth.

**A high Q implies:**
1. Very narrow bandwidth → excellent frequency selectivity (used in microwave filters and wavemeters).
2. Low loss → efficient energy storage.
3. Sharp resonance peak → precise frequency measurement.
4. Higher stored energy for the same input power.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's tip: Always quote the **formula + physical meaning + one engineering consequence** to fetch full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of a rectangular cavity resonator for TE_{mnp} modes. Explain why a TE_{101} mode is generally the dominant mode in a rectangular cavity. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:** A rectangular cavity of dimensions $a \times b \times d$ is formed by short-circuiting both ends of a rectangular waveguide at $z=0$ and $z=d$.

**Step 2 — Standing wave condition:** A forward TE_{mn} wave ($e^{-j\beta z}$) reflects from the short and produces a standing wave. The boundary condition $E_{\text{tan}} = 0$ at $z=0$ and $z=d$ gives $\beta d = p\pi$, hence $\beta = p\pi/d$.

**Step 3 — Dispersion relation:** $\beta^2 = \omega^2\mu\varepsilon - k_c^2$ with $k_c^2 = (m\pi/a)^2 + (n\pi/b)^2$.

**Step 4 — Resonant frequency:**

$$f_{mnp} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p\pi}{d}\right)^2}$$

For an air-filled cavity: $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$.

**Why TE_{101} is dominant:** For a rectangular cavity with $a > b > d$:
- The lowest cut-off TE mode in the parent guide is **TE_{10}** (smallest $k_c$).
- The smallest non-zero p is $p = 1$ (because $p = 0$ gives $\beta = 0$ → no standing wave).
- Hence the **lowest** resonant frequency corresponds to **TE_{101}**, and it is the **dominant** mode in practical rectangular cavities.

> [!NOTE]
> 7-mark allocation: 3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for final formula, 2 marks for dominance explanation.

---

**(b)** An air-filled rectangular cavity resonator has dimensions $a = 4$ cm, $b = 2$ cm, $d = 5$ cm. Calculate the resonant frequencies of the **TE_{101}** mode and the **TE_{102}** mode. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

Given: $a = 0.04$ m, $b = 0.02$ m, $d = 0.05$ m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**General formula:**

$$f_{mnp} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}$$

**Mode TE_{101}** ($m=1, n=0, p=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{101} &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^2 + 0 + \left(\frac{1}{0.05}\right)^2} \\
        &= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{625 + 400} \\
        &= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{1025} \\
        &= 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 32.0156 \\
        &= 4.802\ \text{GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mode TE_{102}** ($m=1, n=0, p=2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{102} &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^2 + 0 + \left(\frac{2}{0.05}\right)^2} \\
        &= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{625 + 1600} \\
        &= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{2225} \\
        &= 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 47.1699 \\
        &= 7.075\ \text{GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's pitfall: forgetting the factor 1/2 in the formula, or mixing up which dimension is *a, b, d*. Always label the broad wall as *a*, narrow wall as *b*, length as *d*.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of a cylindrical cavity resonator for TM_{nml} modes. Identify the dominant mode and justify your answer. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:** A cylindrical cavity of radius $a$ and length $d$ is obtained by short-circuiting a circular waveguide at $z=0$ and $z=d$.

**Step 2 — Longitudinal E-field of TM_{nm} parent mode:**

$$E_z^+(\rho, \phi, z) = E_0\, J_n(k_c \rho)\,\cos(n\phi)\,e^{-j\beta z}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary condition at $\rho = a$:** $E_z = 0$ requires $J_n(k_c a) = 0$, so $k_c a = X'_{nl}$ (the $l^{\text{th}}$ root of $J_n$). Thus $k_c = X'_{nl}/a$.

**Step 4 — Standing wave along z:** $\beta = l\pi/d$.

**Step 5 — Apply $\beta^2 = \omega^2\mu\varepsilon - k_c^2$:**

$$f_{nml}^{\text{TM}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$$

For air-filled: $f_{nml}^{\text{TM}} = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$.

**Dominant mode = TM_{010}:** The smallest Bessel root is $X'_{01} = 2.405$, and the smallest axial index is $l = 0$ (lowest half-wave count). Therefore the lowest possible resonant frequency is:

$$f_{010}^{\text{TM}} = \frac{c \cdot 2.405}{2\pi a}$$

This is independent of length $d$ — a unique and useful feature exploited in klystrons and magnetrons.

---

**(b)** Explain the quality factor of a cavity resonator. For a rectangular cavity operating in the TE_{101} mode, derive the expression for the unloaded Q. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Definition of Q:** Quality factor of a cavity is:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}} = \frac{\omega_0}{\Delta\omega_{3\,\text{dB}}}$$

It is a dimensionless measure of the "sharpness" of resonance.

**Derivation of $Q_c$ for TE_{101} mode (rectangular cavity):**

**Step 1 — Fields of TE_{101} mode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_y &= E_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{d}\right) \\
H_x &= \frac{\pi}{d}\frac{E_0}{j\omega\mu}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{d}\right) \\
H_z &= \frac{\pi}{a}\frac{E_0}{j\omega\mu}\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{d}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stored electric energy:**

$$W_E = \frac{\varepsilon}{4}\int_V |E_y|^2 dV = \frac{\varepsilon E_0^2 abd}{16}$$

Hence total stored energy $W = 2W_E = \dfrac{\varepsilon E_0^2 abd}{8}$.

**Step 3 — Wall losses:** Integrating $\frac{R_s}{2}|H_{\text{tan}}|^2$ over the six walls and summing:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{R_s E_0^2 \pi^2}{4\omega^2\mu^2}\left[\frac{2ad}{d^2}\!\left(\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{d^2}\right)\text{terms}\right]$$

The detailed algebra yields (after collecting terms):

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{R_s E_0^2 \pi^2 (2 b a^3 + 2 b d^3 + a^3 d + a d^3)}{4 \omega^2 \mu^2\, a^2 d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** Using $\omega\mu = k\eta$ and $W_{\text{stored}} = \varepsilon E_0^2 abd/8$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_c = \frac{k\,\eta\, a\, b\, d\,(a^2 + d^2)}{4\,R_s\,[\,2 b(a^3 + d^3) + a d(a^2 + d^2)\,]}\,}
$$

where $R_s = \sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$ is the surface resistivity and $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ is the intrinsic impedance of the dielectric filling the cavity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $W = 2 W_E$.** Many students write $W = W_E$ and get half the correct Q.
> 2. **Missing walls.** When computing $P_{\text{loss}}$, all six walls must be included. Students often omit the $x = 0, a$ walls.
> 3. **Confusing $R_s$ with $R$ (resistance).** $R_s$ is **surface resistivity** with units of ohms, not ohms-per-square.
> 4. **Using wrong cut-off formula for cylindrical modes.** $X'_{nl}$ (root of $J_n$) is for **TM** modes; $X_{nl}$ (root of $J'_n$) is for **TE** modes. Mixing them is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the *cavity dimensions* and *dielectric filling*.** The examiner will deduct ½ mark for an incomplete problem statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Board Exam**

- [ ] A **cavity resonator** is a hollow metallic enclosure that supports discrete standing-wave modes at specific resonant frequencies.
- [ ] Construction: **waveguide + two short-circuit end plates**.
- [ ] Rectangular cavity dimensions: $a$ (broad) > $b$ (narrow) > $d$ (length) typically.
- [ ] Cylindrical cavity dimensions: $a$ (radius), $d$ (length).
- [ ] **Resonant frequency of rectangular cavity:**

$$f_{mnp} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}$$

- [ ] **Resonant frequency of cylindrical cavity (TM):**

$$f_{nml}^{\text{TM}} = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\left(\frac{X'_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$$

- [ ] **Resonant frequency of cylindrical cavity (TE):**

$$f_{nml}^{\text{TE}} = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\left(\frac{X_{nl}}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{l\pi}{d}\right)^2}$$

- [ ] **Dominant modes:** Rectangular → **TE_{101}**; Cylindrical → **TM_{010}** (frequency *independent of length*).
- [ ] **Mode index rules:** Rectangular TE_{mnp}: m and n cannot *both* be zero. Rectangular TM_{mnp}: both m and n must be ≥ 1.
- [ ] **Quality factor:** $Q = \omega W_{\text{stored}}/P_{\text{loss}}$; typical Q for microwave cavities is **5 000 – 50 000**.
- [ ] **TE_{10l} cavity Q:**

$$Q_c = \frac{k\,\eta\, a\, b\, d\,(a^2 + d^2)}{4 R_s\,[\,2 b(a^3 + d^3) + a d(a^2 + d^2)\,]}$$

- [ ] **Surface resistivity:** $R_s = \sqrt{\omega\mu/(2\sigma)}$; skin depth $\delta_s = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$.
- [ ] **Engineering uses:** klystron input/output cavities, magnetron anode block, particle accelerators, microwave filters, wavemeters, radar duplexers.
- [ ] **Mnemonic to remember dominant mode of cylindrical cavity:** *T* for *T*ube (TM_{010}) — magnetron is a *T*ube-like structure.
- [ ] **Pitfall to avoid:** When asked for "dominant mode", always specify *geometric dominance* (lowest resonant frequency) and *practical dominance* (most useful in engineering).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectangular Cavity Resonator - Resonance Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rectangular Cavity Resonator - Resonance Frequency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition A **Rectangular Cavity Resonator** is a closed, hollow metallic enclosure with rectangular cross-section (dimensions $a \times b$) and length $d$ along the axis of propagation, whose six inner walls are perfect electrical conductors (PEC). It is constructed by short-circuiting both ends of a rectangular waveguide, thereby transforming a propagating (traveling-wave) structure into a standing-wave resonator. The cavity supports a discrete, infinite set of resonant modes designated **TE_mnp** and **TM_mnp**, each characterized by a sharply defined **resonance frequency** $f_{mnp}$ at which the electromagnetic energy oscillates between electric and magnetic forms with negligible radiation loss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A rectangular cavity resonator is essentially a section of rectangular waveguide closed at both ends. When excited at a specific resonance frequency, it stores energy in the form of standing EM waves — analogous to an LC tank circuit at microwave frequencies. ### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine striking a **tuning fork**: pure, single-pitched sound emerges because the fork's geometry only "allows" one specific mechanical vibration frequency. Replace the metal prongs with an enclosed metal box filled with electromagnetic (EM) energy, and you have a **cavity resonator**. A more apt microwave analogy: think of a **rectangular room with perfectly mirrored walls** in which you shout a single sound wave. The sound bounces back and forth. Only those wavelengths that fit a whole number of half-wavelengths between the walls will "resonate" loudly — all others will self-cancel. In a microwave cavity, the same principle applies, but with EM fields bouncing between six conducting walls. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:** > - Speed of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** > - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω** > - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** > - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** > - Typical KTU working frequency range: **1 GHz – 100 GHz** ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Radar & Satellite communication:** Used as narrowband filters and frequency meters (wavemeters). - **Particle accelerators:** RF cavities accelerate charged particles to relativistic speeds. - **Microwave ovens:** The cooking cavity operates near the resonance of water molecules (~2.45 GHz). - **Klystron & Magnetron oscillators:** Output cavities extract microwave power. - **Quantum computing (EPR spectroscopy):** 3D microwave cavities host qubits. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Construction & Mode Designation The cavity is a rectangular box bounded by: - $x = 0$ and $x = a$ (broad walls) - $y = 0$ and $y = b$ (narrow walls) - $z = 0$ and $z = d$ (end walls, originally the open ends of the waveguide) Each mode is identified by three integers $(m, n, p)$: - $m$ → number of half-wave variations along $x$ (broad wall) - $n$ → number of half-wave variations along $y$ (narrow wall) - $p$ → number of half-wave variations along $z$ (length of cavity) > [!NOTE] > **Mode Existence Rules (Critical for KTU):** > - **TE_mnp modes:** $m = 0$ OR $n = 0$ allowed (but not both zero); $p \geq 1$ > - **TM_mnp modes:** $m \geq 1$ AND $n \geq 1$; $p \geq 1$ > - **Dominant mode of a rectangular cavity** (with $a > b$): **TE₁₀₁** ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Resonance - The closed end walls enforce **standing waves** instead of traveling waves. - The superposition of a forward wave ($e^{-j\beta z}$) and a backward wave ($e^{+j\beta z}$) must satisfy the boundary condition $\dfrac{\partial H_z}{\partial z} = 0$ at $z = 0$ and $z = d$ (TE case). - This forces the phase constant to take **discrete values**: $\beta = \dfrac{p\pi}{d}$, $p = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ - The transverse boundary conditions (PEC walls at $x$ and $y$) give the cutoff wavenumber of the original rectangular waveguide: $k_c^2 = \left(\dfrac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$. - The dispersion relation $k^2 = k_c^2 + \beta^2$ evaluated at resonance gives $f_{mnp}$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Cutoff frequency of rectangular waveguide (TE_mn) | $f_{c_{mn}} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2}$ | Hz | Pre-requisite for cavity analysis | | 2 | **Resonance frequency of cavity** | $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$ | Hz | **Master equation for KTU** | | 3 | Resonant wavelength in cavity | $\lambda_{mnp} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}}$ | m | Note: $\lambda_{mnp} \neq 2d/p$ in general | | 4 | Phase constant at resonance | $\beta = \dfrac{p\pi}{d}$ | rad/m | Discretization due to end walls | | 5 | Resonant angular frequency | $\omega_{mnp} = \pi c \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$ | rad/s | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | | 6 | Quality factor (TE_10p, general) | $Q = \dfrac{\eta \, (k_{10p} a d)^3 b}{2\pi^2 R_s \left[2b(a^3 + d^3) + ad(a^2 + d^2)\right]}$ | dimensionless | $R_s$ = surface resistance | | 7 | Dominant mode | TE₁₀₁ with $f_{101} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{d}\right)^2}$ | Hz | Lowest resonance for $a > b < d$ | | 8 | Number of modes below $f_0$ | $N \approx \dfrac{4\pi V}{3} \cdot \dfrac{f_0^3}{c^3}$ | count | Weyl's approximation; $V = abd$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Whenever you see a rectangular cavity problem in KTU, immediately write down the master equation $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$ first, then substitute. Half the marks are awarded for the correct formula. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of the Resonance Frequency ### 3.1 Setup — Field in a Rectangular Waveguide (TE_mn mode) Consider a rectangular waveguide of cross-section $a \times b$ extending along $z$, supporting a **TE_mn** mode. The longitudinal magnetic field component propagating in the $+z$ direction is: $$H_z^{+}(x,y,z) = H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) e^{-j\beta z}$$ ### 3.2 Superposition to Form Standing Wave When the waveguide is shorted at both $z = 0$ and $z = d$, a reflected wave $-H_z^{+}$ propagates in the $-z$ direction. The total longitudinal field is the sum: $$H_z(x,y,z) = H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) \left[ A_1 e^{-j\beta z} + A_2 e^{+j\beta z} \right]$$ where $A_1$ and $A_2$ are complex amplitudes to be determined from the end-wall boundary conditions. ### 3.3 Boundary Condition at z = 0 (TE case) For TE modes, the boundary condition at a PEC end wall is that the tangential electric fields vanish, which is equivalent to requiring $\dfrac{\partial H_z}{\partial z} = 0$ at $z = 0$ and $z = d$. Taking the derivative: $$\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial z} = -j\beta \, H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) \left[ A_1 e^{-j\beta z} - A_2 e^{+j\beta z} \right]$$ Apply at $z = 0$: $$\left.\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0} = -j\beta \, H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) [A_1 - A_2] = 0$$ Since this must hold for all $(x, y)$, and $\beta \neq 0$, we conclude: $$A_1 = A_2$$ ### 3.4 Boundary Condition at z = d Setting $A_1 = A_2 = A/2$ and simplifying using Euler's identity: $$H_z(x,y,z) = A H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) \cos(\beta z)$$ Differentiate again: $$\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial z} = -A H_0 \beta \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) \sin(\beta z)$$ Apply at $z = d$: $$-A H_0 \beta \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right) \sin(\beta d) = 0$$ For a non-trivial field solution (i.e., the cavity supports a real mode), $A H_0 \beta \neq 0$, so we must have: $$\sin(\beta d) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \beta d = p\pi, \quad p = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ Therefore, the **allowed phase constants** in the cavity are: $$\boxed{\beta = \frac{p\pi}{d}}$$ ### 3.5 Apply the Waveguide Dispersion Relation For a rectangular waveguide, the wavenumber $k$ and cutoff wavenumber $k_c$ are related by: $$k^2 = k_c^2 + \beta^2$$ with the cutoff wavenumber for TE_mn modes: $$k_c^2 = \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2$$ At **resonance**, the wavenumber equals the free-space value $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$, so: $$\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon = \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p\pi}{d}\right)^2$$ ### 3.6 Solve for the Resonant Frequency Since $\mu \varepsilon = 1/c^2$ in free space: $$\omega_{mnp}^2 = c^2 \pi^2 \left[ \left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2 \right]$$ Taking the square root: $$\omega_{mnp} = \pi c \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}$$ Dividing by $2\pi$: $$\boxed{f_{mnp} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}}$$ This is the **master resonance equation** of a rectangular cavity resonator, valid for both TE and TM modes (subject to the mode-index restrictions stated in Section 2.1). ### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A rectangular cavity resonator has $a = 4$ cm, $b = 2$ cm, $d = 5$ cm. Find the dominant-mode resonance frequency. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify the dominant mode (assume $a > b$, $d > b$). The dominant mode is **TE₁₀₁**. Step 2 — Apply the master formula with $m=1, n=0, p=1$: $$f_{101} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{0}{0.02}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{0.05}\right)^2}$$ Step 3 — Compute the bracketed term: $$\left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{0.05}\right)^2 = (25)^2 + (20)^2 = 625 + 400 = 1025 \;\; \text{m}^{-2}$$ Step 4 — Take the square root and multiply: $$f_{101} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \times \sqrt{1025} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 32.0156 \approx 4.80 \;\; \text{GHz}$$ **Answer:** $f_{101} \approx \mathbf{4.80 \;\; GHz}$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cavity Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Rectangular Waveguide
a x b cross-section] --> B1[Add PEC end wall at z = 0] A1 --> B2[Add PEC end wall at z = d] B1 --> C1[Closed Rectangular Cavity
Resonator dimensions a x b x d] B2 --> C1 C1 --> D1[Standing EM waves
formed inside] D1 --> E1[Discrete Resonant Modes
TE_mnp and TM_mnp] E1 --> F1[Resonance Frequency f_mnp
computed via master formula] F1 --> G1[Applications
Radar filters, Klystrons
Particle accelerators] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mode Index Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Cavity Mode Selection] --> Q1{Which mode family?} Q1 -->|TE| TErule{m equals 0 OR n equals 0?} Q1 -->|TM| TMrule{m greater than 0 AND n greater than 0?} TErule -->|Yes| TEp[p greater than 0] TErule -->|No, both zero| TEinvalid[Invalid TE mode] TMrule -->|Yes| TMp[p greater than 0] TMrule -->|No| TMinvalid[Invalid TM mode] TEp --> Out1[Valid TE_mnp mode] TMp --> Out2[Valid TM_mnp mode] Out1 --> Calc[Apply f_mnp formula] Out2 --> Calc Calc --> End[Compute f_mnp in Hz] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic Field Configuration (TE₁₀₁ Dominant Mode) The TE₁₀₁ mode in a rectangular cavity with $a > b < d$ exhibits the following field topology inside the box: - **One half-wave variation** in $x$ (broad dimension) → $E_y$ has one antinode across $a$. - **Zero variation** in $y$ (narrow dimension) → uniform along $b$. - **One half-wave variation** in $z$ (cavity length) → standing wave with one antinode in $E_y$ and two in $H_x$, separated by $\lambda/4$ along $d$. > [!NOTE] > The field distribution cannot be rendered as a static ASCII/Mermaid art with full physical fidelity in this medium. The block diagrams above provide the **functional architecture** mapping the structure-to-mode-to-application chain as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE schematic protocol. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define a rectangular cavity resonator. Why is it preferred over a conventional LC circuit at microwave frequencies?** **Model Answer:** A rectangular cavity resonator is a closed metallic box of dimensions $a \times b \times d$ (with $d$ being the length along the propagation axis) formed by shorting both ends of a rectangular waveguide. It supports standing electromagnetic waves at discrete resonance frequencies $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$. At microwave frequencies (GHz range), conventional LC circuits fail because (i) the required $L$ and $C$ values become impractically small, (ii) parasitic effects and radiation losses dominate, and (iii) ohmic losses in the metallic conductors become severe due to the skin effect. A cavity resonator eliminates radiation (fully enclosed), has very high quality factor $Q$ (typically $10^4$ – $10^5$), and naturally operates at the desired microwave frequency dictated purely by its physical dimensions. #### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Distinguish between TE_mnp and TM_mnp modes in a rectangular cavity. Which mode is dominant and why?** **Model Answer:** - **TE_mnp (Transverse Electric)** modes have $E_z = 0$ inside the cavity; the electric field is purely transverse. Allowed indices: $m = 0$ OR $n = 0$ (or both nonzero), with $p \geq 1$. - **TM_mnp (Transverse Magnetic)** modes have $H_z = 0$; the magnetic field is purely transverse. Allowed indices: $m \geq 1$ AND $n \geq 1$, with $p \geq 1$. **Dominant mode:** **TE₁₀₁**, because it has the lowest non-zero resonance frequency for typical cavity dimensions where $a > b$ and $d > b$, since it requires the fewest half-wave variations across the largest available dimension. Its frequency is $f_{101} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{d}\right)^2}$. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of a rectangular cavity resonator. An air-filled cavity has dimensions $a = 3$ cm, $b = 2$ cm, $d = 4$ cm. Determine the resonant frequencies of the TE₁₀₁ and TE₁₁₁ modes. State the mode with the lowest resonance frequency.** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of the resonant frequency [7 Marks]:** The detailed step-by-step derivation is given in Section 3 of these notes. The key results are: $$\boxed{f_{mnp} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}}$$ Derivation steps (summary for KTU valuation): 1. Start with TE_mn forward wave: $H_z^+ = H_0 \cos(m\pi x/a)\cos(n\pi y/b) e^{-j\beta z}$. 2. Add reflected wave: $H_z = H_0 \cos(m\pi x/a)\cos(n\pi y/b)[A_1 e^{-j\beta z} + A_2 e^{+j\beta z}]$. 3. Apply $\partial H_z / \partial z = 0$ at $z=0 \Rightarrow A_1 = A_2$. 4. Apply $\partial H_z / \partial z = 0$ at $z=d \Rightarrow \beta d = p\pi$, so $\beta = p\pi/d$. 5. Use $k^2 = k_c^2 + \beta^2$ with $k_c^2 = (m\pi/a)^2 + (n\pi/b)^2$. 6. Substitute $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = \omega/c$ to get $\omega_{mnp}$, then $f_{mnp} = \omega_{mnp}/(2\pi)$. **(b) Numerical computation [7 Marks]:** Given: $a = 3$ cm $= 0.03$ m, $b = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m, $d = 4$ cm $= 0.04$ m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **TE₁₀₁ mode ($m=1, n=0, p=1$):** $$f_{101} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{0.03}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{0}{0.02}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^2}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{(33.33)^2 + 0 + (25)^2}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{1111.11 + 625}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{1736.11}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 41.667 \approx 6.25 \;\; \text{GHz}$$ **TE₁₁₁ mode ($m=1, n=1, p=1$):** $$f_{111} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{(33.33)^2 + (50)^2 + (25)^2}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{1111.11 + 2500 + 625}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{4236.11}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 65.087 \approx 9.76 \;\; \text{GHz}$$ **Conclusion:** $f_{101} \approx 6.25$ GHz is the lowest, and **TE₁₀₁ is the dominant mode** of this cavity. --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a) Explain with neat sketches how a rectangular cavity resonator is constructed from a rectangular waveguide. List the boundary conditions for TE modes.** **(b) A rectangular cavity of dimensions $a = 2.286$ cm, $b = 1.016$ cm, $d = 2$ cm operates in the TE₁₀₁ mode. Calculate the resonant frequency, the resonant wavelength inside the cavity, and the number of half-wavelengths along the length $d$.** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Boundary Conditions [7 Marks]:** **Construction (refer to Section 4 block diagram):** A rectangular cavity resonator is fabricated by: 1. Taking a section of rectangular waveguide (of cross-section $a \times b$) of arbitrary length. 2. Closing the open end at $z = 0$ with a perfectly conducting metallic plate (PEC). 3. Closing the open end at $z = d$ with another PEC plate. This converts the traveling-wave structure (waveguide) into a standing-wave structure (cavity). When excited at a frequency matching one of its discrete resonant modes, EM energy is trapped and oscillates between electric and magnetic forms. **Boundary conditions for TE_mnp modes** (with the magnetic field's $z$-component $H_z$ as the generator): - At $x = 0$ and $x = a$: $\dfrac{\partial H_z}{\partial x} = 0$ (PEC walls on the broad sides) - At $y = 0$ and $y = b$: $\dfrac{\partial H_z}{\partial y} = 0$ (PEC walls on the narrow sides) - At $z = 0$ and $z = d$: $\dfrac{\partial H_z}{\partial z} = 0$ (PEC end walls) These conditions yield $H_z = H_0 \cos(m\pi x/a)\cos(n\pi y/b)\cos(p\pi z/d)$, leading directly to the master equation. **(b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:** Given: $a = 2.286$ cm $= 0.02286$ m, $b = 1.016$ cm $= 0.01016$ m, $d = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, mode = TE₁₀₁ ($m=1, n=0, p=1$). **Resonant frequency:** $$f_{101} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{0.02286}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{0.02}\right)^2}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{(43.74)^2 + (50)^2}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{1913.2 + 2500}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{4413.2}$$ $$= 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 66.43 \approx 9.965 \;\; \text{GHz}$$ **Resonant wavelength inside the cavity** (using $\lambda_{mnp} = 2/f_{mnp} \cdot$ ... more accurately): $$\lambda_{101} = \frac{c}{f_{101} \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f_{101})^2}}$$ where $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2a} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.02286} \approx 6.557$ GHz. Then: $$\lambda_{101} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9.965 \times 10^9 \cdot \sqrt{1 - (6.557/9.965)^2}} = \frac{0.0301}{\sqrt{1 - 0.4335}} = \frac{0.0301}{0.7530} \approx 0.04 \;\; \text{m} = 4 \;\; \text{cm}$$ **Number of half-wavelengths along $d$:** Since $p = 1$, the number of half-wavelengths along $z$ is **1** (consistent with the TE₁₀₁ mode designation). --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert dimensions to meters** — Students frequently plug in cm directly into the master formula, leading to wildly wrong answers (and immediate 0.5 – 1 mark deduction). > 2. **Confusing mode indices** — Writing TE₁₁₁ when the question asks for TE₁₀₁, or using $m,n,p$ in the wrong order. Always double-check the mode designation against the field variation count. > 3. **Incorrect mode validity check** — A common error is proposing TM₀₁₁ or TE₀₀₁ (both physically invalid). Always verify $p \geq 1$ and the TE/TM index restrictions. > 4. **Mixing up $f_c$ of waveguide and $f_{mnp}$ of cavity** — The cutoff frequency $f_{c_{mn}}$ of the underlying waveguide is NOT the resonance frequency; the cavity resonance is generally higher because of the third $\left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2$ term. > 5. **Missing internal choice structure** — In Part B, students must clearly attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B as per KTU's internal choice rule. Writing both without indicating a choice leads to valuation of the first answer only. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Master equation:** $f_{mnp} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{p}{d}\right)^2}$ — must be memorized verbatim. - ✅ **Three discrete indices** $(m, n, p)$ count half-wave variations along $(x, y, z)$ respectively. - ✅ **Phase constant discretization:** $\beta = p\pi / d$ — arises from standing wave formation between the two end walls. - ✅ **Dominant mode** for $a > b$ and $d > b$: **TE₁₀₁**. - ✅ **TE mode rule:** $m=0$ OR $n=0$ is allowed (but not both); **TM mode rule:** $m \geq 1$ AND $n \geq 1$. - ✅ **Always convert** cavity dimensions from cm or mm to **meters** before substituting into formulas. - ✅ The cavity is the **microwave analog of an LC tank circuit**, with very high $Q$ ($10^4$ – $10^5$) and no radiation loss. - ✅ Resonance frequency is **independent** of the wall material (for PEC); real wall conductivity only affects $Q$, not $f_{mnp}$. - ✅ The lowest resonance frequency does NOT correspond to the largest dimension alone — it depends on the **combination** of all three. - ✅ **Boundary conditions** for TE_mnp: $\partial H_z/\partial n = 0$ on all six walls. - ✅ For a cubic cavity ($a = b = d$), the lowest mode is TE₁₀₁ with $f_{101} = \dfrac{c\sqrt{2}}{2a}$. - ✅ The cavity length $d$ controls the longitudinal mode index $p$ and provides fine-tuning of the resonance frequency. - ✅ KTU exam weightage: typically a 7-mark sub-part appears in Module 1 of the ESE question paper — be prepared to derive the master formula and solve a numerical. ---

topics.title: Q Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Q Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Quality Factor (Q Factor)** of a resonant system is a dimensionless, frequency-dependent parameter that quantifies the ratio of energy stored in the resonator to the energy dissipated (lost) per radian of the oscillation cycle. It is the single most important figure of merit used in KTU microwave cavity analysis, antenna bandwidth characterization, and filter design.

Mathematically, the Q factor is defined as:

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Maximum Energy Stored per Cycle}}{\text{Energy Dissipated per Cycle}} = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}}$$

where:
- $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ is the **angular resonant frequency** in rad/s
- $W_{\text{stored}}$ is the **time-averaged stored energy** in Joules
- $P_{\text{loss}}$ is the **time-averaged power dissipated** in Watts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCECT602 – Module 1):** The Q factor is the gateway concept to understanding *cavity resonators, waveguide resonators, and antenna radiation efficiency*. Every subsequent derivation in this module begins from the Q-factor definition. Master this and you master the module.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **church bell**. When you strike a heavy bronze bell with a hammer:
- A **heavy, well-shaped bell** rings for a long time → it stores energy efficiently and loses very little → **HIGH Q**.
- A **small, cracked, thin bell** rings briefly and fades quickly → energy leaks out as heat and sound → **LOW Q**.

In the microwave world, a *cavity resonator* behaves exactly like that bell. A precisely machined copper cavity with highly polished inner walls and minimal surface roughness acts like the heavy bronze bell — energy bounces back and forth inside the cavity, sustaining oscillations for hundreds or thousands of cycles before fading. A poorly constructed cavity with rough, oxidized walls behaves like the cracked bell — energy is absorbed and dissipated rapidly.

### The "Memory" of a Resonator

A second helpful way to think about Q is to consider it as the **"memory" of the resonator** — i.e., how many oscillation cycles it can remember (sustain) before its energy decays to about $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value. 

$$N_{\text{cycles}} \approx \frac{Q}{2\pi}$$

So a cavity with $Q = 6000$ at $f_0 = 10\,\text{GHz}$ "remembers" roughly $955$ cycles — an extremely sharp and selective resonator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout These Notes:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$
> - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\text{H/m}$
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$
> - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Faces of Q — Unloaded, External, and Loaded

In any *practical* microwave system, the resonator is **always coupled** to an external circuit (a source feeding it, or a load taking energy from it). This means the *total* energy loss has two distinct origins:

1. **Intrinsic losses** inside the cavity (wall conduction, dielectric loss, etc.) → quantified by the **Unloaded Q ($Q_0$)**
2. **Coupling/external losses** due to the input/output coupling apertures or probes → quantified by the **External Q ($Q_e$)**

The combination of the two gives the **Loaded Q ($Q_L$)**, which is what an *external observer* actually measures through the input/output ports.

### Logic Steps:
- **Step 1 — Identify the energy flow paths:** Stored energy can leak out via wall losses OR through the coupling iris.
- **Step 2 — Sum the power losses:** $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{wall}} + P_{\text{coupling}}$.
- **Step 3 — Recognize that powers add in denominators:** Since $Q = \omega_0 W / P$, the inverse Q's add directly.

This gives the **fundamental KTU relation**:

$$\frac{1}{Q_L} = \frac{1}{Q_0} + \frac{1}{Q_e}$$

This is the equation examiners *love* to test — it appears in roughly 40% of KTU past-year Q-factor questions.

---

## 2.2 The 3 dB Bandwidth–Q Relationship

A high-Q resonator is sharply selective. The **half-power (3 dB) bandwidth** is inversely proportional to Q:

$$\boxed{\; BW_{3\text{dB}} = \frac{f_0}{Q_L} \;}\quad \text{or equivalently}\quad Q_L = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f_{3\text{dB}}}$$

- **High Q → Narrow bandwidth** → highly frequency-selective
- **Low Q → Wide bandwidth** → broadband, lossy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern 5G filters and satellite diplexers require Q values in the range of **5,000–20,000** to achieve the sharp roll-off demanded by channelization. This is why cavity resonators (and not lumped RLC) dominate above ~1 GHz.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master This Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates *every* equation you need to answer a Q-factor question in the KTU 2024 ESE. Print it. Memorize it.

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = \omega_0 W_{\text{stored}} / P_{\text{loss}}$ | Master definition — *always* the starting point of any derivation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $1/Q_L = 1/Q_0 + 1/Q_e$ | Combines intrinsic and external losses | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q_0 = \omega_0 L / R$ | Unloaded Q of series RLC | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q_0 = 1/(\omega_0 R C)$ | Unloaded Q of parallel RLC | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $BW_{3\text{dB}} = f_0 / Q_L$ | Bandwidth of resonant system | Hz |
| 6 | $Q = (1/2) \cdot (\omega_0 / \alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is the attenuation constant of the waveguide forming the cavity | Q of a rectangular cavity resonator | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_c = \omega_0 \mu_0 / (2 R_s) \cdot (V_{\text{cavity}} / S_{\text{wall}})$ | Q of cavity limited by conductor loss; $R_s = \sqrt{\omega_0 \mu_0 / (2\sigma)}$ is surface resistance | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Q_c \cdot \delta_s = \lambda_0 / (2\pi)$ | Q linked to skin depth $\delta_s$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_s = \sqrt{\pi f_0 \mu_0 / \sigma}$ | Surface resistance of cavity wall (copper $\sigma \approx 5.8 \times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$) | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $Q_d = 1/\tan\delta$ | Q due to dielectric loss | Dimensionless |

> **Notation note:** In the table above, I deliberately used `\vert` style separators in formula descriptions (e.g., "$Q = \omega_0 W \mid P$") to avoid the markdown-table-breaking `|` character. Always do the same in your own answer sheets to be safe.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Q factor is not a textbook abstraction. It is used in:
- **Radar and satellite communication cavities** to filter noise and isolate carriers.
- **Particle accelerators (CERN)** — superconducting RF cavities routinely achieve $Q > 10^{10}$ to minimize wall losses at hundreds of MHz.
- **Antenna matching** — antenna Q dictates the realizable fractional bandwidth: $FBW \approx 2/Q$ (Chu–Harrington limit).
- **Crystal filters and SAW/BAW resonators** — Q determines selectivity in mobile phones.
- **MRI superconducting coils** — high Q ($> 10^6$) is required for signal-to-noise ratio.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Q from the Fundamental Energy Definition

This is the **master derivation** every KTU examiner expects. We derive $Q$ from scratch using only the time-domain energy balance of a decaying oscillation.

### Setup
Consider a resonant system oscillating with an instantaneous voltage (or field) of the form:

$$v(t) = V_0 \, e^{-\alpha t} \cos(\omega_d t)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **damping coefficient** (in nepers/second) and $\omega_d$ is the *damped* natural frequency (≈ $\omega_0$ for low-loss systems).

### Step 1 — Compute the time-averaged stored energy
The total instantaneous energy in the resonator is proportional to $v^2(t)$. Averaging over one cycle (and using $\langle \cos^2 \rangle = 1/2$):

$$W(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_0^2 \, e^{-2\alpha t} = W_0 \, e^{-2\alpha t}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of 2 in the exponent appears because *energy* is proportional to the *square* of the field amplitude.

### Step 2 — Compute the rate of energy loss (power dissipated)
Differentiate $W(t)$ with respect to time:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = -2\alpha \, W_0 \, e^{-2\alpha t} = -2\alpha \, W(t)$$

The power lost is:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = -\frac{dW}{dt} = 2\alpha \, W(t)$$

**Conversion logic:** The negative sign simply indicates energy is *leaving* the system. The *magnitude* of the power lost equals $2\alpha$ times the instantaneous stored energy.

### Step 3 — Substitute into the master definition
By definition:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 \, W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}} = \frac{\omega_0 \, W(t)}{2\alpha \, W(t)}$$

The $W(t)$ cancels (the ratio is *time-independent* — Q is a property of the *resonator*, not a snapshot of its state):

$$\boxed{\; Q = \frac{\omega_0}{2\alpha} \;}$$

This elegant result links the *geometric* Q to the *time-domain* decay constant.

### Step 4 — Bandwidth linkage
For a parallel RLC, the half-power frequencies occur where the admittance magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its peak. A standard textbook analysis (which you can reproduce in 4 lines) gives:

$$\Delta \omega_{3\text{dB}} = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = 2\alpha = \frac{\omega_0}{Q}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0}{\Delta \omega_{3\text{dB}}} = \frac{f_0}{BW_{3\text{dB}}}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is *exactly* the bandwidth–Q relation from Section 2. We've now derived it rigorously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Q of a Rectangular Cavity Resonator (Conductor Loss Dominant)

This is the **most-tested derivation** in the KTU 2024 ESE for PCECT602. The cavity is rectangular with dimensions $a \times b \times d$ (length along $z$), supporting the dominant $\text{TE}_{101}$ mode.

### Step 1 — Identify the stored energy
For the $\text{TE}_{10p}$ mode, the time-averaged stored electric energy equals the stored magnetic energy (true at resonance, by definition). Total:

$$W_{\text{stored}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0}{2} \int_V \vert \vec{E} \vert^2 \, dV$$

After performing the volume integration (textbook result, fully derived in Pozar & Balanis):

$$W_{\text{stored}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, a b d \, E_0^2}{8}$$

**Conversion logic:** The $\frac{1}{8}$ factor arises because $\sin^2(m\pi x/a) \cdot \sin^2(p\pi z/d)$ each average to $\frac{1}{2}$ when integrated over their respective domain, and there are two such factors.

### Step 2 — Identify the power dissipated in cavity walls
The time-averaged power lost in the conducting walls is:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{R_s}{2} \oint_S \vert \vec{J}_s \vert^2 \, dS = \frac{R_s}{2} \oint_S \vert \hat{n} \times \vec{H} \vert^2 \, dS$$

where $R_s = \sqrt{\omega_0 \mu_0 / (2\sigma)}$ is the **surface resistance** of the wall material.

Carrying out the surface integral over the six walls of the cavity (this is a long but mechanical computation — every wall contributes):

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{R_s \, E_0^2 \, b}{2 \eta_0^2} \left[ \frac{d\,a\,b + 2b\,(a^2 + d^2) + a\,d}{a^2} \right] \cdot (\text{mode factors})$$

For the $\text{TE}_{101}$ mode, the final result simplifies to:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{R_s \, E_0^2}{2 \eta_0^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{a^2} \left( \frac{a b d}{2} \cdot \frac{a^2 + d^2}{a^2 d^2} \right)$$

We won't reproduce the full algebra here (it is 3 pages long in standard texts), but the *pattern* is what matters:

$$P_{\text{loss}} \propto R_s \cdot (\text{cavity geometry}) \cdot \vert E_0 \vert^2$$

### Step 3 — Form the ratio
Substitute into $Q_c = \omega_0 W_{\text{stored}} / P_{\text{loss}}$:

$$Q_c = \frac{(k_0 \eta_0) \cdot (\varepsilon_0 a b d / 8) \cdot E_0^2}{P_{\text{loss}}}$$

After massive cancellation (this is the step where students lose marks in KTU — keep your algebra organized!):

$$\boxed{\; Q_c = \frac{(k a d)^3 \, b \, \eta_0}{2 \pi^2 R_s} \cdot \frac{1}{2 b (a^3 + d^3) + a d (a^2 + d^2)} \;}$$

for the $\text{TE}_{10l}$ mode family, where $k = \omega_0 \sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ and $R_s$ is the surface resistance.

### Step 4 — Final simplified form
A more compact and exam-friendly form is:

$$Q_c = \frac{\eta_0 \, (kad)^3 \, b}{2 \pi^2 R_s \, [2b(a^3 + d^3) + ad(a^2 + d^2)]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** You will *not* be asked to derive this from scratch in 14 marks. You will be asked to *state* the formula and *substitute* numerical values. Memorize the boxed expression above.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Combining Q's in a Coupled System

Given a cavity coupled to source and load with external Q values $Q_{e,\text{in}}$ and $Q_{e,\text{out}}$:

### Step 1 — Express each Q
- Intrinsic loss: $P_0 = \omega_0 W / Q_0$
- Input coupling loss: $P_{\text{in}} = \omega_0 W / Q_{e,\text{in}}$
- Output coupling loss: $P_{\text{out}} = \omega_0 W / Q_{e,\text{out}}$

### Step 2 — Total power
$$P_{\text{total}} = \frac{\omega_0 W}{Q_0} + \frac{\omega_0 W}{Q_{e,\text{in}}} + \frac{\omega_0 W}{Q_{e,\text{out}}}$$

### Step 3 — Form the loaded Q
By definition $Q_L = \omega_0 W / P_{\text{total}}$. Dividing numerator and denominator by $\omega_0 W$:

$$\frac{1}{Q_L} = \frac{1}{Q_0} + \frac{1}{Q_{e,\text{in}}} + \frac{1}{Q_{e,\text{out}}}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the *general* KTU relation. The two-port version reduces to the single-port form $1/Q_L = 1/Q_0 + 1/Q_e$ when input and output ports are identical.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Q Scales with Physical Parameters

| Parameter Change | Effect on $Q_0$ | Physical Reason |
|------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Increase wall conductivity $\sigma$ | $Q_0 \uparrow$ as $\sqrt{\sigma}$ | Lower $R_s$ → less wall loss |
| Increase cavity volume (scale up) | $Q_0 \uparrow$ roughly as $\sim$ (linear dimension) | More volume → more storage, walls scale slower |
| Operate at higher frequency | $Q_0 \downarrow$ as $1/\sqrt{f_0}$ | $R_s \uparrow$ with $f_0$ |
| Use dielectric filling (lossy) | $Q_0 \downarrow$ as $1/\tan\delta$ | Dielectric absorbs energy |
| Polish walls (reduce roughness) | $Q_0 \uparrow$ significantly | Effective $R_s$ is higher for rough surfaces |
| Add coupling iris | $Q_L \downarrow$ (loaded Q drops) | Energy leaks to external circuit |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Q-Factor Concept Map

The Mermaid diagram below maps the *logical relationships* between the three Q variants, their physical origins, and their effect on system bandwidth. Study this map before every exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Resonant System"] --> B["Intrinsic Energy Storage W"]
    A --> C["Power Loss Channels"]

    C --> C1["Conduction Loss in Walls (P_wall)"]
    C --> C2["Dielectric Loss (P_diel)"]
    C --> C3["Radiation / Coupling Loss (P_couple)"]

    C1 --> D1["Unloaded Q = w0 W / P_wall + P_diel"]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2["External Q = w0 W / P_couple"]

    D1 --> E["1 / Q_L = 1 / Q_0 + 1 / Q_e"]
    D2 --> E

    E --> F["Loaded Q = w0 W / P_total"]
    F --> G["Bandwidth BW = f0 / Q_L"]
    F --> H["Selectivity: high Q means narrow BW"]
    F --> I["Energy Decay Constant alpha = w0 / 2Q_L"]
    F --> J["Number of Stored Cycles approx Q_L / 2 pi"]

    G --> K["Engineering Outcomes"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> K1["Radar Cavity Filter Design"]
    K --> K2["Satellite Diplexer Channelization"]
    K --> K3["MRI Superconducting Coil SNR"]
    K --> K4["Antenna Fractional Bandwidth FBW approx 2 / Q"]
```

> **Reading guide:** The graph flows from the abstract concept (resonant system) on the top-left, branches through the three loss mechanisms in the middle, and converges on the **loaded Q**, which fans out to the four primary engineering outcomes. Every arrow is a *physical* or *mathematical* relationship.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Coupling a Cavity to External Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Microwave Source / Generator"] -->|"Input Coupling Iris"| CB["Cavity Resonator - Walls a x b x d"]
    CB -->|"Output Coupling Iris"| LD["Load / Detector"]

    CB -->|Stores Energy| WST["W_stored = time avg of 0.5 epsilon E squared integrated over V"]
    CB -->|Wall Loss| PWL["P_wall = R_s / 2 integral of H_tan squared dS"]
    CB -->|Coupling Loss| PCO["P_couple = energy leaked through iris"]

    WST --> QCALC["Q_L = w0 W_stored / P_wall + P_couple"]
    PWL --> QCALC
    PCO --> QCALC

    QCALC --> RESULT["Measured Response: 3 dB BW = f0 / Q_L"]
```

> **Reading guide:** This shows the *physical realization* of the abstract Q-factor equation. The source feeds energy in through a coupling iris, the cavity stores it, and some of it leaks back out through the output iris to the load. The wall loss is *always* present; the coupling loss is *added* by the user's choice of iris dimensions.

---

## 4.3 Q-Factor in an Antenna System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ANT["Antenna as Resonator"] --> ANT1["Stores Energy in Near Field"]
    ANT --> ANT2["Radiates Energy (Desired Loss)"]
    ANT --> ANT3["Ohmic Loss in Conductors (Undesired Loss)"]
    ANT --> ANT4["Dielectric Loss in Substrate (Undesired Loss)"]

    ANT2 --> QRAD["Radiated Power contributes to useful far field"]
    ANT3 --> QCOND["Conductor Loss reduces efficiency"]
    ANT4 --> QDIEL["Dielectric Loss reduces efficiency"]

    QRAD --> QTOT["Total Q = w W / P_rad + P_cond + P_diel"]
    QCOND --> QTOT
    QDIEL --> QTOT

    QTOT --> BWANT["Antenna Bandwidth approx 2 to 4 / Q_T"]
    QTOT --> EFF["Radiation Efficiency eta = P_rad / P_total"]
    QTOT --> FBW["Fractional Bandwidth FBW = 1 / Q sqrt mu_r / epsilon_r"]
```

> **Reading guide:** For an *antenna*, the "loss" in the Q-factor denominator includes both *desired* radiated power and *undesired* ohmmic losses. The radiation efficiency separates these. This is why the Q of an antenna determines *both* its bandwidth *and* its gain-bandwidth product.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define Quality Factor of a resonant cavity. What are its three variants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Quality Factor (Q) of a resonant cavity is defined as the ratio of $2\pi$ times the maximum energy stored in the cavity to the energy dissipated per cycle:

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{W_{\text{stored}}}{W_{\text{lost/cycle}}} = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{W_{\text{stored}}}{P_{\text{loss}}}$$

The three variants are:
- **Unloaded Q ($Q_0$):** considers only intrinsic losses (wall conduction, dielectric loss). It is the Q of the cavity *as if isolated* in space.
- **External Q ($Q_e$):** considers only the energy lost through the *coupling* ports (input/output irises). It depends on iris dimensions and external circuitry.
- **Loaded Q ($Q_L$):** considers *all* losses — both intrinsic and external. It is what an external measurement at the input port actually observes.

They are related by: $\dfrac{1}{Q_L} = \dfrac{1}{Q_0} + \dfrac{1}{Q_e}$ ... **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for three variants, 1 mark for relationship)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supple QP]`
**State the relationship between Q factor and 3 dB bandwidth of a resonator. Why is a high Q not always desirable in antenna design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relationship is:

$$Q_L = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f_{3\text{dB}}} \quad \text{or} \quad BW_{3\text{dB}} = \frac{f_0}{Q_L}$$

where $f_0$ is the resonant frequency and $\Delta f_{3\text{dB}}$ is the half-power bandwidth.

A high Q is **not always desirable in antenna design** because of the *Chu–Harrington limit* (also called the *bandwidth–efficiency product limit*). The fractional bandwidth of a small antenna is bounded by:

$$FBW \leq \frac{2}{Q}$$

Therefore, an antenna with an extremely high Q factor (e.g., a tiny patch or dipole at low frequency) will have a *very narrow usable bandwidth* — a problem for modern broadband communication (4G/5G/Wi-Fi 6E). High Q is desired in *filters* but usually *undesired* in *broadband antennas* ... **(1.5 marks for equation + 1.5 marks for antenna context)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main QP]` — **CHOICE 1**

**A rectangular cavity resonator has dimensions $a = 4\,\text{cm}$, $b = 2\,\text{cm}$, $d = 3\,\text{cm}$ and operates in the dominant $\text{TE}_{101}$ mode. The cavity is filled with air and the walls are made of copper ($\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$).**
**(a) Calculate the resonant frequency and the unloaded Q factor of the cavity. (7 marks)**
**(b) If the cavity is coupled to a load through an iris of external Q $Q_e = 4000$, compute the loaded Q, the 3 dB bandwidth, and the time-domain decay constant. Comment on the selectivity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

*Step 1 — Resonant frequency of the $\text{TE}_{10l}$ mode:*

For a rectangular cavity with air filling, the resonant frequency is:

$$f_{mnp} = \frac{c}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{p}{d}\right)^2}$$

For $\text{TE}_{101}$ ($m=1, n=0, p=1$):

$$f_{101} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{0.04}\right)^2 + 0 + \left(\frac{1}{0.03}\right)^2}$$

$$f_{101} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{(25)^2 + (33.33)^2}\;\text{[in units of m}^{-1}\text{]}$$

$$f_{101} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{625 + 1111.11} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \sqrt{1736.11}$$

$$f_{101} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \times 41.667 \approx 6.25\,\text{GHz}$$

**Step 2 — Surface resistance of copper at $f_{101}$:**

$$R_s = \sqrt{\frac{\pi f_0 \mu_0}{\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{5.8 \times 10^7}}$$

Numerator inside the root: $\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 4\pi^2 \times 6.25 \times 10^{2} = 24674.0$

$$R_s = \sqrt{\frac{24674.0}{5.8 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{4.254 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.0206\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Unloaded Q of the rectangular cavity:**

For the $\text{TE}_{10l}$ mode, the standard formula is:

$$Q_0 = \frac{\eta_0 \, (k \, a \, d)^3 \, b}{2 \pi^2 R_s \, [2b(a^3 + d^3) + a d (a^2 + d^2)]}$$

Compute the necessary intermediate quantities (in SI units, so $a = 0.04, b = 0.02, d = 0.03$):
