<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Read This Before Attempting!):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+" sign in $I = C + D$.** Many students write $I = C - D$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is the **sum**, not the difference.
> 2. **Confusing coupled and isolated ports.** Coupling is the *desired* sample; isolation is the *undesired* leakage. A 20-dB coupler has *20 dB* between input and coupled port, not isolation.
> 3. **Not stating the matched condition** explicitly. Always write "$S_{ii} = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$" before the S-matrix.
> 4. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\beta$.** $\alpha$ is the **through** coefficient (the main line), $\beta$ is the **coupled** coefficient (the sample). For 3-dB couplers, both are $1/\sqrt{2}$.
> 5. **Forgetting the unitarity condition** $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = 1$ — this is what proves the coupler is lossless. Examiners *love* to ask for this.
> 6. **In numerical problems, using $\log_{10}$ vs. $\log_e$ incorrectly.** Always use base 10 for dB computations.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "directivity" of a directional coupler. Mention its ideal value.**

**Model Answer:**

Directivity $D$ of a directional coupler is defined as the ratio of the power at the *coupled* port to the power at the *isolated* port, expressed in decibels:

$$
D = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

where $P_4$ is the power at the coupled port and $P_3$ is the power at the isolated port. It is a measure of the *discriminating ability* of the coupler between forward and reverse travelling waves.

> For an **ideal** directional coupler, $P_3 = 0$, so $D = \infty$ dB (infinity). In practice, high-quality couplers achieve $D \geq 30$ dB.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the three important performance parameters of a directional coupler. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

The three important performance parameters of a directional coupler are:

1. **Coupling Factor ($C$):** The ratio of input power to coupled port power, in dB.

$$
C = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

2. **Directivity ($D$):** The ratio of coupled port power to isolated port power, in dB.

$$
D = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

3. **Isolation ($I$):** The ratio of input power to isolated port power, in dB.

$$
I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_3}\right) \text{ dB}
$$

**Relation (Master Triangle):**

$$
\boxed{\;I \,(\text{dB}) = C \,(\text{dB}) + D \,(\text{dB})\;}
$$

This is derived by writing $P_1/P_3 = (P_1/P_4) \cdot (P_4/P_3)$ and taking $10\log_{10}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. We provide two fully solved alternative questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the S-matrix of an ideal matched four-port directional coupler. Show that the through and coupling coefficients satisfy the lossless condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 20-dB directional coupler is used to sample the power delivered to an antenna. If the input power is 50 W and the isolated-port power is measured to be 0.5 mW, find the (i) directivity, (ii) isolation, and (iii) power at the through port. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a): S-Matrix Derivation**

**Step 1: Network definition.**

A directional coupler is a passive, reciprocal, lossless, four-port microwave junction that couples a fraction of power from a primary line to a secondary line in a *direction-sensitive* manner. We label the ports 1 (input), 2 (through), 3 (isolated), 4 (coupled).

**Step 2: Matched condition.**

For the device to be matched at all ports (no reflection when other ports are matched), the diagonal elements of the S-matrix must vanish:

$$
S_{11} = S_{22} = S_{33} = S_{44} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Directional property.**

With input at Port 1, output appears at Port 2 and Port 4 only. Hence:

$$
S_{31} = 0, \quad S_{32} = 0
$$

By the symmetry of port labelling, the through-arm coefficients are $S_{21}, S_{12}, S_{34}, S_{43}$ and the cross-coupling coefficients are $S_{41}, S_{14}, S_{23}, S_{32}$.

**Step 4: Define the coefficients.**

Let $\alpha$ be the through coefficient and $\beta$ be the coupling coefficient:

$$
\alpha = S_{21} = S_{12} = S_{34} = S_{43}
$$

$$
\beta = S_{41} = S_{14} = S_{23} = S_{32}
$$

**Step 5: S-matrix.**

$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & \alpha & 0 & \beta \\
\alpha & 0 & \beta & 0 \\
0 & \beta & 0 & \alpha \\
\beta & 0 & \alpha & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6: Unitarity (lossless) condition.**

For a lossless network, $[S][S]^* = [I]$. Examining the (1,1) element of $[S][S]^*$:

$$
|S_{11}|^2 + |S_{21}|^2 + |S_{31}|^2 + |S_{41}|^2 = 1
$$

Substituting:

$$
0 + |\alpha|^2 + 0 + |\beta|^2 = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\;|\alpha|^2 + |\beta|^2 = 1\;}
$$

This confirms that the through and coupling coefficients are linked: increasing coupling ($\beta$) reduces the through ($\alpha$) and vice versa. For real, positive coefficients, $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = 1$.

---

#### **Solution to A(b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- Coupling $C = 20$ dB
- Input power $P_1 = 50$ W
- Isolated port power $P_3 = 0.5$ mW $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ W

**Step 1: Compute coupled port power $P_4$ from coupling definition.**

$$
C = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right) = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\frac{P_1}{P_4} = 10^{20/10} = 10^2 = 100
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{P_1}{100} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Compute directivity $D$.**

$$
D = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1000) = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3: Compute isolation $I$ (using the master relation).**

$$
I = C + D = 20 + 30 = 50 \text{ dB}
$$

**Verification (direct):**

$$
I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^5) = 50 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Compute through port power $P_2$.**

Assuming lossless, $P_1 = P_2 + P_4 + P_3$. Since $P_3 \ll P_1$:

$$
P_2 = P_1 - P_4 - P_3 = 50 - 0.5 - 0.0005 = 49.4995 \text{ W}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and operation of a **two-hole waveguide directional coupler**. Show that the hole spacing of $\lambda_g/4$ produces maximum directivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-dB directional coupler is fed with $P_1 = 100$ W at Port 1. If the through-arm power is 49.9 W, compute the (i) coupling factor, (ii) insertion loss, and (iii) directivity, given that the isolated port power is 0.1 W. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a): Two-Hole Coupler**

**Construction:**

A two-hole waveguide directional coupler consists of two identical rectangular waveguides (the *primary* and *secondary*) sharing a common broad wall. Two small circular or rectangular coupling apertures (holes) are made in this common wall, separated by a distance $d$ (centre-to-centre) along the direction of wave propagation.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 above for Mermaid schematic).**

**Operation:**

Consider a forward-travelling wave $A_f$ entering Port 1 of the primary guide. As it propagates, a small fraction of its amplitude (call it $\kappa \cdot A_f$, where $\kappa$ is the per-hole coupling coefficient) leaks through each hole into the secondary guide.

- **Hole 1** (at $z = 0$): Couples a wave of amplitude $\kappa A_f$ into the secondary guide.
- **Hole 2** (at $z = d$): Couples another wave of amplitude $\kappa A_f$ into the secondary guide.

**Phase analysis at the COUPLED port (Port 4):**

Both coupled contributions travel towards Port 4. The path lengths differ by $d$. The relative phase at Port 4 is:

$$
\Delta\phi_{4} = \beta_g \cdot d
$$

where $\beta_g = 2\pi / \lambda_g$ is the guide phase constant.

**Phase analysis at the ISOLATED port (Port 3):**

The two contributions travel in *opposite directions* to reach Port 3. The relative phase at Port 3 includes an extra $\pi$ from the geometry:

$$
\Delta\phi_{3} = \beta_g \cdot d + \pi
$$

**Setting $d = \lambda_g / 4$:**

Substituting $d = \lambda_g/4$:

$$
\beta_g \cdot d = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_g} \cdot \frac{\lambda_g}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

So:

$$
\Delta\phi_{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}, \quad \Delta\phi_{3} = \frac{\pi}{2} + \pi = \frac{3\pi}{2} \;\;(\equiv -\pi/2)
$$

Combined with the **intrinsic 90° phase shift** of a small aperture (Bethe-hole theory), the contributions at Port 4 add **in phase** (constructive) and at Port 3 add **180° out of phase** (destructive → cancellation).

**Directivity calculation:**

The directivity is the ratio of the *constructive* amplitude at Port 4 to the *cancelled* amplitude at Port 3. In a general $N$-hole coupler (Schwinger / Riblet design), the directivity is:

$$
D = 20 \log_{10}\!\left|\frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \kappa_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \kappa_n (-1)^{n+1}}\right|
$$

For two identical holes ($\kappa_1 = \kappa_2 = \kappa$):

$$
D = 20 \log_{10}\!\left|\frac{2\kappa}{0}\right| = \infty \text{ dB} \quad \text{(ideal, for identical holes)}
$$

In practice, manufacturing tolerances make the holes slightly different, giving finite $D \approx 30\text{–}40$ dB.

**Conclusion:**

A two-hole waveguide directional coupler with $d = \lambda_g/4$ produces maximum directivity by constructive interference at the coupled port and destructive cancellation at the isolated port.

---

#### **Solution to B(b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- Coupler type: 3-dB (nominal)
- $P_1 = 100$ W
- $P_2 = 49.9$ W (through)
- $P_3 = 0.1$ W (isolated)

**Step 1: Compute coupling factor $C$ from measured powers.**

Using power conservation (lossless assumption): $P_1 = P_2 + P_4 + P_3$

$$
P_4 = P_1 - P_2 - P_3 = 100 - 49.9 - 0.1 = 50.0 \text{ W}
$$

Coupling factor:

$$
C = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_4}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{50}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2) = 10 \times 0.301 = 3.01 \text{ dB}
$$

This matches the *nominal* 3-dB label. ✓

**Step 2: Compute insertion loss $L$.**

$$
L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{49.9}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.004) = 3.02 \text{ dB}
$$

(Nearly identical to $C$, as expected for a symmetric 3-dB coupler.)

**Step 3: Compute directivity $D$.**

$$
D = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50}{0.1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(500) = 10 \times 2.699 = 26.99 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 (Bonus): Compute isolation $I$.**

$$
I = C + D = 3.01 + 26.99 = 30.0 \text{ dB}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this for last-day prep!)**

### **Core Concepts**
- [ ] A **directional coupler** is a 4-port device that couples a *defined* fraction of microwave power in a *direction-sensitive* manner.
- [ ] The 4 ports are: **Input (1)**, **Through (2)**, **Isolated (3)**, **Coupled (4)**.
- [ ] The S-matrix has diagonal zeros (matched) and the directional property $S_{31} = S_{32} = 0$.
- [ ] The **lossless / unitary condition** is $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = 1$.

### **The Master Triangle (MEMORISE)**
- [ ] $C = 10\log_{10}(P_1/P_4)$ — **Coupling**
- [ ] $D = 10\log_{10}(P_4/P_3)$ — **Directivity**
- [ ] $I = 10\log_{10}(P_1/P_3)$ — **Isolation**
- [ ] $I = C + D$ — **Always true** (in dB)

### **Physical Realisations**
- [ ] **Bethe-hole (single hole)**: Theoretical foundation. $C \propto a^3$ (small hole of radius $a$).
- [ ] **Two-hole coupler**: Two holes spaced $\lambda_g/4$ apart in a common waveguide broad wall.
- [ ] **Multi-hole (Schwinger / Riblet)**: $N \geq 3$ holes for sharper directivity.
- [ ] **Planar (Branch-line, Lange, Coupled-line)**: Used in microstrip / stripline MICs.

### **Engineering Applications**
- [ ] VSWR / Reflection-coefficient measurement.
- [ ] Power monitoring in transmit lines.
- [ ] Signal injection (radar duplexers).
- [ ] Feed networks for antenna arrays.
- [ ] Building block of the **Magic Tee** (3-dB E-H hybrid).

### **Numericals to Master**
- [ ] Given two of $\{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}$ → find the other two (using $P_1 = P_2 + P_3 + P_4$).
- [ ] Given $C$ and $D$ → find $I$ (use $I = C + D$).
- [ ] Given $P_1$ and $C$ → find $P_4$ (use $P_4 = P_1 / 10^{C/10}$).
- [ ] Sanity check: For a 3-dB coupler, $\alpha = \beta = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$.

### **Common Mistake Traps (AVOID)**
- [ ] ❌ Writing $I = C - D$ → **Wrong!** $I = C + D$.
- [ ] ❌ Confusing "isolation" with "coupling" — they are *different* ports.
- [ ] ❌ Forgetting the unitarity condition in S-matrix derivations.
- [ ] ❌ Mixing dB with linear ratios in the same equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Semiconductor Devices: Principle of Operation of Tunnel Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microwave Semiconductor Devices: Principle of Operation of Tunnel Diode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tunnel Diode (Esaki Diode):** A heavily doped **p-n junction diode** (degenerate semiconductor) in which the **p-side** and **n-side** are doped to such an extreme level (typically **10^19 to 10^25 atoms/m³**) that the depletion region becomes extremely thin (≈ **10 nm**). Under this condition, charge carriers (electrons) can pass directly through the potential barrier of the junction by **quantum mechanical tunneling**, producing a region of **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** in its static V-I characteristics.

The tunnel diode was discovered by **Leo Esaki in 1957** (Nobel Prize, 1973). Because of its ultra-thin depletion region, the barrier becomes "transparent" to electrons, enabling the purely quantum mechanical phenomenon of **tunneling** to dominate the conduction process.

**Key Distinguishing Features (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**

| Parameter | Ordinary Diode | Tunnel Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Doping Level | Moderate (10^21 m⁻³) | Very Heavy / Degenerate (10^25 m⁻³) |
| Depletion Width | ~1 µm | ~10 nm |
| Conduction Mode | Drift & Diffusion | Quantum Tunneling |
| V-I Curve | Monotonic Rise | Has Negative Slope (NDR) |
| Forward Voltage Drop | ~0.7 V (Si) | ~50 mV (Peak Point) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "Tunneling Through a Mountain"**
> 
> Imagine two cities, **City N** (full of people/electrons) and **City P** (also full of people/electrons at higher elevation), separated by a **tall mountain** (potential barrier). 
> 
> - In a **normal (classical) diode**, the mountain is so thick that no one can pass through — people must climb **over** the mountain (requiring high energy, ≈ 0.7 V).
> - In a **tunnel diode**, the doping is so heavy that the mountain is **drilled through with a thin tunnel**. People can now walk straight through the tunnel, even when they have very little energy.
> 
> As the road leading to the tunnel is raised (forward bias increased), more and more people can use the tunnel. At some point, the **road rises above the tunnel exit** — fewer people can use it, and traffic **decreases** despite the road being "higher". This counterintuitive reduction in flow is the **negative resistance**. Finally, when the road is high enough, people simply climb over the mountain (normal diode behavior).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Required for Derivations)

- **Electron Charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/e \approx 26\ \text{mV}$
- **Reduced Planck's Constant:** $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Effective Mass of Electron in Ge:** $m^\ast \approx 0.067\,m_0$
- **Effective Mass of Electron in GaAs:** $m^\ast \approx 0.067\,m_0$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Band Structure (Equilibrium — No Bias)

In an unbiased tunnel diode, the Fermi level $E_F$ is **constant** across the entire structure (system is in thermodynamic equilibrium). 

Because both the p and n regions are **degenerate semiconductors**:
- On the **n-side**, $E_F$ lies **inside the conduction band** $E_C$.
- On the **p-side**, $E_F$ lies **inside the valence band** $E_V$.

The conduction band of the n-side is **filled with electrons** up to $E_F$, while the valence band of the p-side is **filled with holes** (empty electron states) up to $E_F$. The barrier height is $E_g$ (bandgap) plus the degeneracy offsets.

## 2.2 Operational Bias Conditions (Step-by-Step)

### **Case 1 — Zero Bias (Equilibrium)**
- Fermi levels align; no net flow of electrons.
- Tunneling current in one direction = tunneling current in the opposite direction.
- **Net current = 0.**

### **Case 2 — Small Forward Bias ($0 < V < V_p$)**
- The n-side energy bands shift **upward** relative to the p-side.
- Filled states in the n-side conduction band begin to align with **empty states** in the p-side valence band.
- **Electrons tunnel from n → p** (forward tunneling current).
- Current **increases** sharply with applied voltage.

### **Case 3 — Peak Point Bias ($V = V_p$, typically 50–200 mV)**
- Maximum overlap between filled n-states and empty p-states occurs.
- **Tunneling current reaches its maximum** = **Peak Current $I_p$**.
- This is the operating point just before NDR begins.

### **Case 4 — Negative Differential Resistance Region ($V_p < V < V_v$)**
- The overlap between filled n-states and empty p-states **decreases**.
- Tunneling current **drops** as voltage is further increased.
- Slope $\frac{dI}{dV} < 0$ → **NDR property**.
- This is the **active region** used for amplification and oscillation.

### **Case 5 — Valley Point ($V = V_v$, typically 400–600 mV)**
- Tunneling current reaches its **minimum** = **Valley Current $I_v$**.
- The p-side valence band edge has risen above the n-side conduction band edge.
- Almost no further tunneling is possible.

### **Case 6 — Beyond Valley Point ($V > V_v$)**
- The regular **forward diffusion current** (as in a normal diode) takes over.
- Current rises exponentially with voltage: $I \approx I_s e^{eV/kT}$.
- Behavior becomes indistinguishable from a standard p-n diode.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Tunnel Diode Formulas for KTU Exams**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_p \left(\dfrac{V}{V_p}\right) \exp\left(1 - \dfrac{V}{V_p}\right) + I_s\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{eV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$ | **Esaki Diode Equation** (Static I-V relation) |
| 2 | $R_n = -\dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = -\dfrac{V_v - V_p}{I_v - I_p}$ | **Negative Resistance** (slope in NDR region) |
| 3 | $\text{PVR} = \dfrac{I_p}{I_v}$ | **Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio** (figure of merit) |
| 4 | $T \approx \exp(-2\,\kappa\,d)$, $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m^\ast(U - E)}}{\hbar}$ | **Transmission Coefficient** (rectangular barrier) |
| 5 | $T \approx \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{4\sqrt{2m^\ast}\,(U - E)^{3/2}}{3\,e\,\hbar\,F}\right]$ | **Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling** (triangular barrier, high E-field $F$) |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{e}{2\pi\hbar}\iint T(E_k)\,[f_1(E) - f_2(E)]\,dE\,dk_t$ | **Tsu-Esaki Formula** (general tunneling current density) |
| 7 | $W \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{e}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ | **Depletion Width** (very small due to high $N_A, N_D$) |
| 8 | $f_{RC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_n C_j}$ | **Resistive Cutoff Frequency** (max useful frequency) |

> **Note on Units:** Currents in **mA**, voltages in **mV**, $R_n$ in **Ω**, $W$ in **nm**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Microwave Oscillators:** Tunnel diodes generate microwave signals (up to **100 GHz**) because of their NDR, eliminating the need for bulky resonant cavities.
> - **High-Speed Switches:** Switching time ≈ **1 ns**, useful in fast logic circuits and pulse generation.
> - **Low-Noise Amplifiers:** The NDR is exploited to cancel the resistive losses of the rest of the circuit.
> - **Mixers and Detectors:** Used in radar front-ends and satellite receivers.
> - **Quantum Computing Research:** Tunnel diode structures serve as testbeds for tunneling physics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Esaki Diode I-V Equation

The total current through a tunnel diode has **two components**:
1. **Tunneling current** $I_t$ (dominates at low forward bias).
2. **Excess current + normal diffusion current** $I_x$ (dominates near and beyond the valley).

The empirical Esaki equation (validated by direct experimental fit) is:

$$I = I_p \left(\frac{V}{V_p}\right) \exp\left(1 - \frac{V}{V_p}\right) + I_s\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{eV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

### **Step-by-Step Justification**

**Step 1 — Normalize the variables.**
Define $x = V / V_p$ (dimensionless voltage ratio). Then the first term becomes:
$$I_t = I_p \cdot x \cdot e^{1-x}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to find the peak.**
$$\frac{dI_t}{dV} = \frac{I_p}{V_p} e^{1-x} (1 - x)$$

Setting the derivative to zero gives $x = 1$, i.e., $V = V_p$ → confirms the maximum occurs at the **peak voltage**.

**Step 3 — Maximum value of the tunneling component.**
Substituting $x = 1$:
$$I_{t,\max} = I_p \cdot 1 \cdot e^{0} = I_p$$

So the peak of the first term coincides exactly with $I_p$. ✓

**Step 4 — Show the negative slope region.**
For $V_p < V < V_v$, $(1 - x) < 0$, so the slope of the tunneling current is **negative**. The diffusion term $I_s \exp(eV/kT)$ is still small, so the **total current decreases** → **NDR region**.

**Step 5 — Final form of the Esaki equation:**
$$\boxed{\,I = I_p \left(\frac{V}{V_p}\right) \exp\left(1 - \frac{V}{V_p}\right) + I_s\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{eV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Negative Differential Resistance

The differential resistance in the NDR region is defined as:

$$R_n = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = -\frac{V_v - V_p}{I_v - I_p}$$

### **Worked Example (KTU Standard Problem Style)**

**Given:** A Germanium tunnel diode has $I_p = 10\ \text{mA}$, $I_v = 1\ \text{mA}$, $V_p = 50\ \text{mV}$, $V_v = 450\ \text{mV}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the voltage change across the NDR region:**
$$\Delta V = V_v - V_p = 450 - 50 = 400\ \text{mV} = 0.4\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the corresponding current change:**
$$\Delta I = I_p - I_v = 10 - 1 = 9\ \text{mA} = 9 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the NDR formula:**
$$R_n = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = -\frac{0.4}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = -44.44\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**
The **negative sign** is critical — it indicates that as voltage **rises**, current **falls**, which is the signature of NDR used in oscillators.

**Step 5 — Peak-to-Valley Ratio (figure of merit):**
$$\text{PVR} = \frac{I_p}{I_v} = \frac{10\ \text{mA}}{1\ \text{mA}} = 10$$

Higher PVR ⇒ better switching and oscillation performance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantum Mechanical Tunneling Probability

A particle of energy $E$ encountering a rectangular potential barrier of height $U_0 > E$ and width $d$ has a finite probability of penetrating the barrier (a purely quantum effect, forbidden in classical mechanics).

**Step 1 — Schrödinger equation inside the barrier:**
$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^\ast}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + U_0\psi = E\psi$$

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \kappa^2 \psi,\quad \text{where}\ \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m^\ast(U_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$

**Step 2 — Solution inside the barrier:**
$$\psi(x) = A\,e^{-\kappa x} + B\,e^{+\kappa x}$$

**Step 3 — Transmission coefficient (WKB approximation):**
For a barrier much thicker than the decay length, $B \to 0$, and the transmission probability is:
$$T \approx \exp(-2\kappa d) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{2\,d}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m^\ast(U_0 - E)}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Numerical feel:**
For a GaAs tunnel diode with $m^\ast = 0.067\,m_0$, $U_0 - E = 0.3\ \text{eV}$, $d = 10\ \text{nm}$:
$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \times 0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.054 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$\kappa \approx 7.4 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$2\kappa d = 2 \times 7.4 \times 10^{8} \times 10 \times 10^{-9} = 14.8$$

$$T \approx e^{-14.8} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-7}$$

This small but **non-zero** value is what makes tunneling observable in heavily doped junctions.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Depletion Width in a Tunnel Diode

The depletion width in a p-n junction is given by the standard expression:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s}{e}\,(V_{bi} - V)\!\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

For a tunnel diode, both $N_A$ and $N_D$ are extremely high (≈ $10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$), forcing $W$ to be ≈ **10 nm**, which is the precondition for observable tunneling.

**Numerical example:**
With $\varepsilon_s = 11.7\,\varepsilon_0$ (Si), $V_{bi} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V = 0$, $N_A = N_D = 10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$:
$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 0.7 \times \frac{2}{10^{25}}}$$
$$W \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} = 9.5\ \text{nm}\ \checkmark$$

This confirms the textbook-thin depletion width of a tunnel diode.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Tunnel Diode Block Architecture & I-V Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input DC Bias Source] --> B[Series Resistor Rs]
    B --> C[Tunnel Diode Junction]
    C -->|Parasitic| D[Junction Capacitance Cj]
    C --> E[Negative Resistance -Rn Region]
    E --> F[Output / Microwave Load]
    G[External AC Signal] -->|Input| C
    C -->|Amplified Signal| F
    H[Peak Point Vp, Ip] --> I[NDR Region Vp-Vv]
    I --> J[Valley Point Vv, Iv]
    J --> K[Normal Diffusion Region]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Tunnel Diode Operating States

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1: Zero Bias] -->|Net current = 0| B[State 2: Small Forward Bias]
    B -->|Electrons tunnel n to p| C[State 3: Peak Point Vp, Ip]
    C -->|Fewer states overlap| D[State 4: NDR Region]
    D -->|Minimum tunneling| E[State 5: Valley Point Vv, Iv]
    E -->|Diffusion current dominates| F[State 6: Normal Forward Conduction]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal P] --> B[Series Inductance Ls]
    B --> C[Series Resistance Rs]
    C --> D[Junction Capacitance Cj]
    D --> E[Negative Resistance -Rn]
    E --> F[Terminal N]
```

## 4.4 Description of the Static V-I Characteristic (Examiner's Sketch Blueprint)

Since the V-I curve is essential for KTU exams, here is a textual blueprint of how to draw it:

1. **X-axis:** Forward Voltage $V_F$ (0 to ~700 mV).
2. **Y-axis:** Forward Current $I_F$ (0 to ~$I_p$).
3. **Plot five critical points in order:**
   - Origin (0, 0).
   - **Peak Point P** at $(V_p, I_p)$ — e.g., (50 mV, 10 mA).
   - **Valley Point V** at $(V_v, I_v)$ — e.g., (450 mV, 1 mA).
   - **Forward Rise** for $V > V_v$, curve climbs steeply.
4. **Mark the NDR region** as the dashed segment connecting P and V.
5. **Label** the Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR = $I_p / I_v$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### **Q1. Define a tunnel diode and state why it exhibits Negative Differential Resistance (NDR).**
**Model Answer:**
A **tunnel diode** is a heavily doped p-n junction diode (doping ≈ $10^{19}\text{–}10^{25}$ atoms/m³) in which the depletion region is so thin (≈ 10 nm) that charge carriers tunnel through the potential barrier by **quantum mechanical tunneling**. 

It exhibits **NDR** because, after the peak point, the number of energy states on the n-side that align with empty states on the p-side **decreases** as forward bias is increased. Hence current **decreases** with increasing voltage over a finite range, giving a negative slope $dI/dV < 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between a tunnel diode and an ordinary p-n junction diode (any three points).**
**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Tunnel Diode | Ordinary Diode |
|---|---|---|
| Doping | Degenerate ($\sim 10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$) | Moderate ($\sim 10^{21}\ \text{m}^{-3}$) |
| Conduction | Quantum tunneling | Drift and diffusion |
| V-I curve | Has NDR region | Monotonic increase |
| Forward drop | Very low (50–200 mV) | High (≈ 0.7 V for Si) |
| Speed | Extremely fast (ns) | Moderate (µs) |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1, 14 Marks]**

### **Question A**

**(a)** Draw the energy band diagram of a tunnel diode under **equilibrium** and **small forward bias**, and explain the principle of operation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat V-I characteristic, mark the peak point, valley point, and NDR region. Derive the expression for negative resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Energy band diagrams & principle (7 marks):**

1. **Equilibrium:** Fermi level is constant across the junction. Because of degenerate doping, $E_F$ lies inside the conduction band on the n-side and inside the valence band on the p-side. Filled states in the n-side conduction band exist up to $E_F$; empty states in the p-side valence band exist up to $E_F$. **(2 marks)**
2. **Small forward bias:** n-side bands shift upward. Filled states in the n-side conduction band begin to align with empty states in the p-side valence band. Electrons **tunnel** from n to p. **(2 marks)**
3. **Peak point:** Maximum overlap between filled and empty states. Current = $I_p$ at $V = V_p$. **(1 mark)**
4. **Beyond peak:** As $V$ increases, the n-side bands move further up; fewer filled states find empty p-side states. Tunneling current **decreases** — this is the **NDR region**. **(1 mark)**
5. **Valley point and beyond:** At $V = V_v$, the p-side valence band edge rises above the n-side conduction band edge. Tunneling ceases; normal diffusion current takes over. **(1 mark)**

**(b) V-I characteristic and negative resistance (7 marks):**

1. Draw the static V-I curve: rises sharply from origin to $(V_p, I_p)$, falls to $(V_v, I_v)$, then climbs steeply. Mark the **NDR region** between P and V. **(3 marks)**
2. Derivation: 
$$R_n = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = -\frac{V_v - V_p}{I_p - I_v}$$
- State the formula, define each term. **(2 marks)**
3. Numerical: For a typical Ge tunnel diode with $I_p = 8\ \text{mA}$, $I_v = 1\ \text{mA}$, $V_p = 60\ \text{mV}$, $V_v = 400\ \text{mV}$:
$$R_n = -\frac{0.40 - 0.06}{8 \times 10^{-3} - 1 \times 10^{-3}} = -\frac{0.34}{7 \times 10^{-3}} = -48.57\ \Omega\ \checkmark$$
**(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** State and explain the **Esaki diode equation** for the static V-I characteristics of a tunnel diode. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A tunnel diode has $I_p = 12\ \text{mA}$ at $V_p = 65\ \text{mV}$ and $I_v = 2\ \text{mA}$ at $V_v = 420\ \text{mV}$. Find (i) the negative resistance, and (ii) the peak-to-valley ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Esaki equation (7 marks):**
$$I = I_p\left(\frac{V}{V_p}\right)\exp\!\left(1 - \frac{V}{V_p}\right) + I_s\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{eV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$
1. **First term** = tunneling current $I_t$. **(2 marks)**
2. **Second term** = excess + normal diffusion current. **(2 marks)**
3. At $V = V_p$: substituting $V/V_p = 1$ gives $I_t = I_p \cdot 1 \cdot e^0 = I_p$, confirming the peak. **(1 mark)**
4. For $V > V_p$, $(1 - V/V_p) < 0$, hence $I_t$ decreases → **NDR**. **(1 mark)**
5. For $V > V_v$, the diffusion term dominates, and the diode behaves normally. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
(i) Negative resistance:
$$R_n = -\frac{V_v - V_p}{I_p - I_v} = -\frac{0.420 - 0.065}{12 \times 10^{-3} - 2 \times 10^{-3}} = -\frac{0.355}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = -35.5\ \Omega\ \checkmark$$
**(3 marks)**

(ii) Peak-to-valley ratio:
$$\text{PVR} = \frac{I_p}{I_v} = \frac{12\ \text{mA}}{2\ \text{mA}} = 6\ \checkmark$$
**(4 marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $R_n = -(\Delta V / \Delta I)$. Marks are deducted if the sign is omitted.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — $V_p, V_v$ are in mV, so convert to V before plugging into formulas.
> 3. **Confusing $I_p$ and $I_v$** — the peak is **higher**, the valley is **lower**. Wrong order yields a **positive** resistance.
> 4. **Not drawing the NDR region explicitly** with proper labeling of P and V — examiners mark the **diagram** separately.
> 5. **Omitting Fermi-level location** in energy band diagrams — the band showing $E_F$ **inside** $E_C$ (n-side) and $E_V$ (p-side) is the key visual cue.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep)**
> 
> - **Tunnel diode = Esaki diode**, invented 1957, Nobel Prize 1973.
> - Both p and n regions are **degenerately doped** (≈ $10^{25}\ \text{m}^{-3}$).
> - Depletion width is **≈ 10 nm** (extremely thin).
> - Conduction: **Quantum mechanical tunneling**, NOT classical drift/diffusion.
> - **Five key points** on V-I curve: Origin → Peak ($V_p, I_p$) → NDR region → Valley ($V_v, I_v$) → Forward rise.
> - **NDR region:** slope $dI/dV < 0$; used for amplification and oscillation.
> - **Esaki equation:** $I = I_p (V/V_p) \exp(1 - V/V_p) + I_s[\exp(eV/kT) - 1]$.
> - **Negative resistance:** $R_n = -(V_v - V_p)/(I_p - I_v)$.
> - **PVR (figure of merit):** $I_p / I_v$. Higher = better performance.
> - **Tunneling probability:** $T \approx \exp(-2\kappa d)$ with $\kappa = \sqrt{2m^\ast(U - E)}/\hbar$.
> - **Resistive cutoff frequency:** $f_{RC} = 1/(2\pi R_n C_j)$; typical values up to **100 GHz**.
> - **Applications:** Microwave oscillators, high-speed switches, low-noise amplifiers, mixers, ESR spectrometers.
> - **Remember:** $V_p \approx 50\text{–}200\ \text{mV}$, $V_v \approx 400\text{–}600\ \text{mV}$.
> - **Always convert** mV to V and mA to A before substituting in resistance formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gunn Diode - Different Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gunn Diode - Different Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Gunn Diode - Different Modes of Operation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Gunn Diode (Transferred Electron Device - TED):** A two-terminal, bulk, negative-differential-resistance (NDR) semiconductor device, fabricated from a direct-bandgap III-V compound semiconductor (typically **Gallium Arsenide / GaAs** or **Indium Phosphide / InP**), that exploits the **Transferred Electron Effect (Gunn Effect, 1963)** to generate and sustain coherent microwave-frequency oscillations purely through bulk negative conductance — without requiring any internal p-n junction.

The "modes" of the Gunn diode refer to the distinct, physically realizable steady-state dynamical regimes of a bulk GaAs sample subjected to a DC bias above the **threshold electric field (E_th ≈ 3.2 × 10⁵ V/m for GaAs)**. The mode in which the device operates is governed primarily by the dimensionless product **n₀ × L** (donor doping density × active layer thickness), and by the relationship between the applied DC bias, the AC signal, and the carrier transit time across the device.

### The Five Canonical Modes of Operation

| S.No. | Mode Name | n₀L Range (cm⁻²) | Primary Function |
|------|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Gunn Oscillation Mode** (Transit-Time Dipole) | < 10¹² | CW Microwave Oscillator |
| 2 | **Stable Amplification Mode** (Quenched Domain) | 10¹² – 10¹⁴ | Reflection-Type Microwave Amplifier |
| 3 | **Quenched Multi-Domain Mode** (Delayed Domain) | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁵ | High-Efficiency Oscillator |
| 4 | **LSA Mode** (Limited Space-charge Accumulation) | > 10¹⁵ | Pulsed High-Power Oscillator |
| 5 | **Bias-Circuit Oscillation Mode** (Parasitic) | Any | Unwanted Low-Frequency Oscillation |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Slide With Two Heights"**
> Imagine electrons in GaAs as riders on a water slide. The slide has two valleys: a **gentle upper valley (satellite L-valley)** and a steep **lower valley (central Γ-valley)**. At low voltage, riders cruise down the gentle lower slope at high speed. When voltage crosses the threshold, riders are suddenly transferred to the steep upper valley — but paradoxically, they slow down because the upper valley is heavier (effective mass increases from **m* = 0.067 m₀** to **m* = 1.2 m₀**). The crowd of "slow" electrons creates a traffic jam (high-field domain) that drifts to the anode, and a new jam forms at the cathode — a self-repeating cycle that produces microwave pulses.

> The **mode of operation** is essentially asking: "How big is the traffic jam, how long does the road (crystal) need to be, and what happens when the jam either reaches the end of the road, gets destroyed mid-way, or never even fully forms?"

### Foundational Physical Constants (GaAs Material)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Threshold Electric Field (E_th):** ≈ **3.2 × 10⁵ V/m** (≈ 3.2 kV/cm)
> - **Electron velocity at threshold (v_th):** ≈ **2 × 10⁵ m/s**
> - **Saturation velocity (v_sat):** ≈ **10⁵ m/s**
> - **Negative Differential Mobility (μ_d):** ≈ **−0.015 m²/(V·s)**
> - **Operating Frequency:** ≈ **1 GHz – 100 GHz** (f = v_sat / L)
> - **Threshold Current Density:** ≈ **J_th ≈ 1000 A/cm²** (typical)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Valley Conduction Model — Physical Foundation

The Gunn Effect arises **only** in direct-bandgap semiconductors possessing a multi-valley conduction band structure. **GaAs** is the canonical engineering example. The key valleys are:

- **Lower (Central) Valley — Γ-valley:** Low effective mass (m* ≈ 0.067 m₀), high mobility (μ ≈ 8500 cm²/V·s), low density of states.
- **Upper (Satellite) Valleys — L-valleys (4 equivalent):** High effective mass (m* ≈ 1.2 m₀), low mobility (μ ≈ 180 cm²/V·s), high density of states.

As the applied electric field increases:
1. Below E_th → Electrons populate the Γ-valley → Velocity rises linearly with E.
2. At E_th → Electrons scatter into L-valleys → Mobility drops sharply.
3. Above E_th → Velocity **decreases** with increasing E → **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**.

## 2.2 Domain Dynamics: Why the Modes Differ

When E > E_th, a small fluctuation in carrier density grows exponentially. The **differential growth rate (r)** is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
r = \frac{n_0 \, \mu_d}{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0} = \frac{-\mu_d \, n_0}{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}
\end{aligned}$$

The **dielectric relaxation time (τ_d)** — the time scale for domain nucleation — is:

$$\tau_d = \frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}{|n_0 \, \mu_d \, E_0|}$$

The **carrier transit time (T_t)** — the time for a domain to drift from cathode to anode — is:

$$T_t = \frac{L}{v_s}$$

The **mode of operation** is fundamentally determined by the **ratio τ_d / T_t**, which is directly tied to the **n₀L product**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Threshold Electric Field | $E_{th} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^5 \text{ V/m}}{1}$ | Minimum field to trigger NDR |
| 2 | Saturation Velocity | $v_{sat} \approx 10^5$ m/s | Domain drift speed |
| 3 | Operating Frequency | $f = \dfrac{v_{sat}}{L} = \dfrac{10^7}{L[\mu m]}$ GHz | Sets the microwave frequency |
| 4 | Transit-Time Frequency | $f_t = \dfrac{v_{sat}}{L}$ | Domain traversal frequency |
| 5 | Dielectric Relaxation Time | $\tau_d = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}{n_0 \, \vert \mu_d \vert \, e}$ | Time scale for domain growth |
| 6 | Critical n₀L Product | $(n_0 L)_{crit} = 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-2}$ | Boundary between modes |
| 7 | Output Power (Gunn mode) | $P_{out} = \eta \, V_{dc} \, I_{dc}$ | η = efficiency (≈ 1–5%) |
| 8 | Domain Width | $W_d \approx \sqrt{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0 \, E_0 \, v_{sat} / (n_0 \, e)}$ | Spatial extent of high-field dipole |
| 9 | Threshold Voltage | $V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot L$ | Minimum DC bias needed |
| 10 | Negative Differential Mobility | $\mu_d = \dfrac{dv}{dE} \Big\vert_{E > E_{th}}$ | Negative slope of v-E curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic to remember n₀L boundaries (in cm⁻²):** **"G.Q.D.L"**
> **G**unn (10¹²) → **Q**uenched (10¹⁴) → **D**elayed (10¹⁵) → **L**SA (10¹⁶+)
> Or simply: "**Ten, Hundred, Thousand, Ten-Thousand**" (× 10¹¹, 10¹², 10¹³, 10¹⁴…)

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Each Mode

- **Gunn Oscillation Mode** → Primary mode for **CW Doppler radar**, **local oscillators** in microwave receivers, **microwave instrumentation** (X-band, Ku-band).
- **Stable Amplification Mode** → Reflection amplifiers, **low-noise pre-amplifiers** in microwave communication systems.
- **Quenched / Delayed Domain Mode** → Improved DC-to-RF efficiency (up to **20–25%**) for solid-state microwave transmitters.
- **LSA Mode** → **High peak power** (kW range) pulsed oscillators, **electronic warfare jammers**, radar transmitters.
- **Bias-Circuit Oscillation Mode** → Must be **suppressed** by proper heat-sinking and low-inductance biasing; otherwise it produces spurious low-frequency noise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between n₀L Product and Mode of Operation

**Step 1: Start with the continuity equation for electron density**

In a 1D bulk semiconductor, the continuity equation for electron density n(x,t) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial n}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (n \cdot v(E))}{\partial x} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Linearize for a small perturbation n = n₀ + ñ(x,t)**

Substitute n = n₀ + ñ, and assume v(E) = v₀ + (dv/dE)·Ē at operating point. Keeping first-order terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \tilde{n}}{\partial t} + v_0 \frac{\partial \tilde{n}}{\partial x} + n_0 \frac{dv}{dE} \frac{\partial \tilde{E}}{\partial x} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply Gauss's law for the perturbation field**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \tilde{E}}{\partial x} = \frac{e \cdot \tilde{n}}{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Combine the two equations; assume traveling-wave solution ñ = ñ₀ exp[i(ωt − kx)]**

Substituting the wave solution and simplifying gives the dispersion relation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega = k v_0 - j \left( \frac{n_0 \, e \, \mu_d}{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0} \right) \cdot k
\end{aligned}$$

The imaginary part gives the **growth rate** of the perturbation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Growth rate } r = \frac{n_0 \, e \, \vert \mu_d \vert}{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute the dielectric relaxation time τ_d**

This is the time constant for the perturbation to grow:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_d = \frac{1}{r} = \frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}{n_0 \, e \, \vert \mu_d \vert}
\end{aligned}$$

For GaAs: ε = 13.1, |μ_d| ≈ 0.015 m²/(V·s), e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.

Inserting numerical values:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_d = \frac{13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{n_0 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.015}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_d \approx \frac{7.7 \times 10^{-11}}{n_0} \text{ (seconds, with n₀ in m}^{-3}\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Relate τ_d to transit time T_t to get the n₀L product**

The transit time is $T_t = L / v_{sat}$. For the device to enter the Gunn oscillation regime, the perturbation must grow faster than the carrier transits the device:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_d < T_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}{n_0 \, e \, \vert \mu_d \vert} < \frac{L}{v_{sat}}
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_0 L > \frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0 \, v_{sat}}{e \, \vert \mu_d \vert}
\end{aligned}$$

Plugging in the GaAs numbers:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_0 L > \frac{13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{5}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.015}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
n_0 L > \frac{1.16 \times 10^{-5}}{2.4 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 4.83 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to cm⁻²:

$$\begin{aligned}
(n_0 L)_{crit} \approx 4.83 \times 10^{11} \text{ cm}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

The experimental value reported in the KTU syllabus is **n₀L ≈ 10¹² cm⁻²**, which is the canonical engineering threshold for distinguishing modes.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mode-by-Mode Operation

### **MODE 1: Gunn Oscillation Mode (Transit-Time Dipole Mode)**

**Conditions:**
- n₀L < 10¹² cm⁻² (long device, low doping)
- V_DC > V_th
- Resonant cavity tuned to transit-time frequency

**Operation:**
- A single high-field domain nucleates at the cathode.
- The domain drifts across the entire active region to the anode and is collected.
- As the domain disappears at the anode, a new one nucleates at the cathode.
- This produces a periodic current pulse train at the transit-time frequency.

**Oscillation frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f = \frac{v_{sat}}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Output characteristics:**
- Frequency: **1–10 GHz** (typical X-band ≈ 10 GHz for L = 10 μm)
- Power: **5 mW – 1 W** CW
- Efficiency: **1–5%**
- Tuning: Mechanical via cavity screw / bias tuning

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most commonly used mode** in practice. The oscillation is *self-sustained* and *coherent*, making it ideal for use as a CW microwave source.

### **MODE 2: Stable Amplification Mode (Quenched Domain Mode)**

**Conditions:**
- 10¹² < n₀L < 10¹⁴ cm⁻²
- V_DC slightly above V_th
- Small AC input signal applied

**Operation:**
- The device operates **just above threshold**.
- A domain begins to nucleate, but the AC signal is large enough to **quench the domain before it reaches the anode**.
- The result: current oscillations that **follow the input AC signal** — the device acts as a **reflection amplifier**.

**Key parameter:** The quenching must occur before the domain fully forms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{AC} < \tau_{growth} \quad \text{AND} \quad \tau_{AC} > T_t \text{ (partial)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Performance:**
- **Gain:** 5–15 dB
- **Bandwidth:** ~1 GHz
- **Noise figure:** ≈ 10–15 dB
- Tunable over a wide frequency range by adjusting the cavity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Stable Amplification Mode demonstrates that the Gunn device can act as a **two-terminal negative-resistance amplifier**, not just an oscillator.

### **MODE 3: Quenched Multi-Domain Mode (Delayed Domain Mode)**

**Conditions:**
- 10¹⁴ < n₀L < 10¹⁵ cm⁻² (medium doping, medium length)
- V_DC just above V_th

**Operation:**
- Multiple domains **attempt to form** simultaneously.
- The AC field component delays the formation of domains so that they only form near the anode's edge.
- This results in higher DC-to-RF conversion efficiency than the basic Gunn mode.

**Efficiency:** Can reach **20–25%**, significantly higher than Mode 1.

**Trade-off:** Less stable oscillation, more sensitive to bias and load.

### **MODE 4: LSA (Limited Space-charge Accumulation) Mode**

**Conditions:**
- n₀L > 10¹⁵ cm⁻² (heavily doped, short device)
- V_DC >> V_th
- Operated with a pulsed bias (short RF pulses, long DC pulse)

**Operation:**
- The active region is so short that **a domain has no time to fully form** before reaching the anode.
- Space charge accumulates only over a **limited region** near the cathode and dissipates before reaching the anode.
- The device behaves like a **negative-resistance oscillator** in which the bulk negative conductance is utilized without standing dipole domains.

**Oscillation frequency:** Determined by the **external resonant circuit**, NOT by the transit time:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{LSA} = f_{resonant\,cavity} \quad (\text{independent of } L)
\end{aligned}$$

**Performance:**
- **Peak power:** Up to **several kW** (in pulsed operation)
- **Efficiency:** Can exceed **25%**
- **Frequency range:** 1–100 GHz (cavity-limited)

> [!NOTE]
> **LSA is the high-power mode.** It is widely used in **military radar jammers** and **pulsed oscillator applications** where very high peak power is required from a compact solid-state source.

### **MODE 5: Bias-Circuit Oscillation Mode (Parasitic Mode)**

**Conditions:**
- Can occur at **any n₀L** if the external bias circuit has poor RF decoupling.
- The device, biased just below threshold, couples to the **resonance of the external bias network**.

**Operation:**
- Low-frequency relaxation oscillations (kHz to MHz range) occur due to bias-circuit inductance and capacitance.
- **Not useful** — must be **suppressed** by:
  - Adding ferrite beads on the bias lead
  - Using low-inductance feedthrough capacitors
  - Ensuring proper RF grounding

> [!WARNING]
> A Gunn oscillator operating in bias-circuit oscillation mode will produce **low-frequency noise** that can **degrade radar sensitivity** and **saturate receivers** in a microwave system. Proper bias design is critical.

## 3.3 Master Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Gunn Oscillation | Stable Amplification | Quenched/Delayed | LSA | Bias-Circuit |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|------------------|-----|--------------|
| n₀L (cm⁻²) | < 10¹² | 10¹² – 10¹⁴ | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁵ | > 10¹⁵ | Any |
| Function | Oscillator | Amplifier | Oscillator | Pulsed Oscillator | (Parasitic) |
| Frequency | Transit-time | Transit-time | Transit-time | Cavity-controlled | Bias-LC |
| Efficiency | 1–5% | (Gain) | 20–25% | 20–30% | N/A |
| Power | mW – 1 W | Gain 5–15 dB | Higher | kW pulsed | N/A |
| Domain Behavior | Full transit | Quenched | Multiple, delayed | Never forms fully | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Gunn Diode Mode Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Bias Source V_DC] --> B[Bias Network with RF Choke]
    B --> C[Active GaAs Layer of Length L]
    C --> D{Domain Dynamics Decided by n0L and V_DC}
    D -->|n0L less than 10 to power 12| E[Mode 1: Gunn Oscillation]
    D -->|10 to power 12 to 10 to power 14| F[Mode 2: Stable Amplification]
    D -->|10 to power 14 to 10 to power 15| G[Mode 3: Quenched Delayed Domain]
    D -->|n0L more than 10 to power 15| H[Mode 4: LSA Mode]
    D -->|Poor Bias Decoupling| I[Mode 5: Bias Circuit Oscillation]
    E --> J[Resonant Cavity Tunes f to v_sat divided by L]
    F --> K[Input AC Signal Quenches Domain]
    G --> L[Multiple Domains Form Near Anode]
    H --> M[External Cavity Sets Frequency]
    I --> N[Low Frequency Noise Output Unwanted]
    J --> O[Coherent Microwave Output]
    K --> P[Amplified Microwave Output]
    L --> Q[High Efficiency Microwave Output]
    M --> R[High Peak Power Pulsed Output]
    N --> S[Suppressed by Ferrite Beads]
    O --> T[Antenna or Mixer]
    P --> T
    Q --> T
    R --> T
```

## 4.2 v-E Characteristic of GaAs — The Two-Valley Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Applied E Field Increases] --> V2[Below E_th Electrons in Gamma Valley]
    V2 --> V3[Velocity Rises Linearly with E]
    V3 --> V4[At E_th About 3.2 kV per cm Electrons Transfer to L Valley]
    V4 --> V5[Velocity Drops Sharply Negative Differential Resistance]
    V5 --> V6[Above E_th Velocity Saturates to v_sat about 10 to power 5 m per s]
    V6 --> V7[Domain Nucleation Begins]
    V7 --> V8[Negative Conductance Region]
```

## 4.3 Mode Classification by n₀L Product

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0L[Consider n0L Product in cm to power minus 2] --> N1[less than 10 to power 12]
    N0L --> N2[10 to power 12 to 10 to power 14]
    N0L --> N3[10 to power 14 to 10 to power 15]
    N0L --> N4[more than 10 to power 15]
    N1 --> O1[Gunn Oscillation Mode Transit Time Dipole]
    N2 --> O2[Stable Amplification Mode Quenched Domain]
    N3 --> O3[Delayed Domain Mode High Efficiency]
    N4 --> O4[LSA Mode High Peak Power Pulsed]
    O1 --> P1[Long device L high v_sat divided by L low frequency]
    O2 --> P2[AC signal controls domain quenching]
    O3 --> P3[Domain forms near anode Higher Efficiency]
    O4 --> P4[Domain never fully forms External cavity sets f]
```

## 4.4 Physical Cross-Section of Gunn Diode (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Ohmic Contact Anode] --> B[n plus GaAs Contact Layer]
    B --> C[Active n GaAs Layer Length L Critical for Mode Selection]
    C --> D[n plus GaAs Contact Layer]
    D --> E[Bottom Ohmic Contact Cathode Heatsink]
    C --> F[High Field Domain Nucleates at Cathode]
    F --> G[Domain Drifts to Anode at v_sat]
    G --> H[New Domain Nucleates Cyclic Process]
    H --> I[Current Oscillations at Microwave Frequency]
    I --> J[Output Coupled to Cavity or Waveguide]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Gunn Effect. Why is it observed only in certain semiconductors like GaAs and not in Silicon?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Gunn Effect** is the phenomenon of current oscillations at microwave frequencies in a bulk semiconductor subjected to a DC electric field exceeding a critical threshold, arising from the **transfer of electrons between two conduction band valleys** with differing effective masses and mobilities.

It is observed only in **direct-bandgap, multi-valley semiconductors** like **GaAs** and **InP** because:
1. They possess a **central Γ-valley** (low mass, high mobility) and **satellite L-valleys** (high mass, low mobility) separated by a small energy gap (≈ **0.31 eV** for GaAs).
2. The inter-valley transfer occurs at an experimentally achievable threshold field (**E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm** for GaAs).
3. **Silicon** is an indirect-bandgap semiconductor with multiple equivalent valleys having *similar* masses — no such inter-valley transfer with differential mass exists, hence **no Gunn Effect**.

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the different modes of operation of a Gunn diode. What parameter determines which mode is dominant?**

**Model Answer:**

The five modes of operation of a Gunn diode are:
1. **Gunn Oscillation Mode** (Transit-Time Dipole)
2. **Stable Amplification Mode** (Quenched Domain)
3. **Quenched Multi-Domain Mode** (Delayed Domain)
4. **Limited Space-Charge Accumulation (LSA) Mode**
5. **Bias-Circuit Oscillation Mode** (Parasitic)

The **n₀L product** (donor doping density × active layer thickness, in cm⁻²) is the **primary parameter** that determines the mode of operation. Secondary factors include the applied bias voltage, frequency of operation, and the impedance of the external resonant circuit.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse** the n₀L boundaries. Students frequently write 10¹⁴ instead of 10¹² for the Gunn oscillation threshold — this costs **2–3 marks** immediately.
> - **Always state units** (cm⁻²) for n₀L. Numerical values without units are penalized.
> - In LSA mode questions, students forget that **frequency is set by the external cavity**, not the transit time. This is a recurring error.
> - For Stable Amplification Mode, students often say "amplification occurs" but fail to explain the **quenching mechanism**. Examiners specifically look for "domain quenching" terminology.
> - **Diagrams are mandatory** in 14-mark questions. A missing v-E characteristic curve costs **at least 2 marks**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the v-E characteristic, explain the two-valley theory of the Gunn Effect. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the threshold n₀L product. List the different modes of operation of a Gunn diode and explain the **Gunn Oscillation Mode** and **LSA Mode** in detail. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Two-Valley Theory of the Gunn Effect:**

In **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)**, the conduction band has two distinct sets of energy valleys:

- **Central Γ-valley** (low-energy): Effective mass m*₁ ≈ 0.067 m₀, mobility μ₁ ≈ 8500 cm²/(V·s), low density of states.
- **Satellite L-valleys** (4 equivalent valleys, higher energy, separated by ΔE ≈ 0.31 eV): Effective mass m*₂ ≈ 1.2 m₀, mobility μ₂ ≈ 180 cm²/(V·s), high density of states.

**v-E Characteristic Behavior:**

1. **Region I (0 ≤ E < E_th):** All electrons reside in the Γ-valley. Velocity increases linearly with E. The slope is the electron mobility μ₁.
2. **Region II (E ≈ E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm):** Electrons gain enough energy from the field to scatter from Γ to L valleys. Because the L-valley has higher mass, mobility drops. Velocity reaches peak (v_th ≈ 2 × 10⁵ m/s).
3. **Region III (E > E_th):** Most electrons are now in L-valley. Velocity *decreases* with increasing E — this is the **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** region with dv/dE < 0.
4. **Region IV (E >> E_th):** Velocity saturates to v_sat ≈ 10⁵ m/s.

The negative slope of the v-E curve is the origin of the **Gunn Effect**. A small perturbation in electron density creates a slight local field enhancement, which transfers more electrons to the L-valley, slowing them down. This creates a charge accumulation (domain) that grows as it drifts to the anode, producing periodic current oscillations at microwave frequencies.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation of the threshold n₀L product:**

Starting from the dielectric relaxation time equation:

$$\tau_d = \frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}{n_0 \, e \, \vert \mu_d \vert}$$

For domain growth (rather than decay), the relaxation time must be **shorter** than the carrier transit time:

$$\tau_d < T_t = \frac{L}{v_{sat}}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0}{n_0 \, e \, \vert \mu_d \vert} < \frac{L}{v_{sat}}$$

Rearranging for the critical condition:

$$(n_0 L)_{crit} = \frac{\varepsilon \, \varepsilon_0 \, v_{sat}}{e \, \vert \mu_d \vert}$$

Substituting GaAs values: ε = 13.1, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m, v_sat = 10⁵ m/s, e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, |μ_d| = 0.015 m²/(V·s):

$$(n_0 L)_{crit} = \frac{13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{5}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.015}$$

$$(n_0 L)_{crit} \approx 4.83 \times 10^{11} \text{ cm}^{-2} \approx 10^{12} \text{ cm}^{-2}$$

For a device to operate in any of the modes, n₀L must exceed this critical value.

**Gunn Oscillation Mode (n₀L < 10¹² cm⁻²):**

- The device has a relatively **long active length L** and/or **low doping n₀**.
- A high-field domain nucleates at the **cathode**, drifts across the entire active region at v_sat, and is collected at the **anode**.
- The cycle repeats: a new domain forms immediately after the previous one disappears.
- Oscillation frequency is determined by the **transit time**: f = v_sat / L.
- Typical frequency range: 1–10 GHz (X-band 10 GHz needs L ≈ 10 μm).
- Output: CW power of mW to ~1 W, efficiency 1–5%.

**LSA Mode (n₀L > 10¹⁵ cm⁻²):**

- The active region is very short; a domain cannot fully form before reaching the anode.
- Space charge accumulates only over a **limited region** near the cathode.
- The device behaves as a **negative-resistance element** without standing domains.
- Oscillation frequency is set by the **external resonant cavity** — independent of L.
- Operated with **pulsed bias** for very high peak power (kW range).
- Efficiency can exceed 25%.
- Applications: high-power pulsed radar oscillators, electronic warfare.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Gunn diode. Discuss the two-valley model with energy band diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the different modes of operation of a Gunn diode. Explain why the **LSA mode** is preferred for high-power pulsed operation. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Construction:**
A Gunn diode is a **two-terminal bulk device** with no p-n junction. It consists of:
- A thin slice of **n-type GaAs** (active layer) of length L (typically 5–100 μm).
- Two heavily doped **n⁺ GaAs contact layers** at the top and bottom.
- **Ohmic metal contacts** (Au-Ge alloy) on both sides.
- The bottom contact is bonded to a **heat sink** (essential for thermal management).
- The whole assembly is packaged in a microwave housing (pill package or waveguide mount).

**Working:**
- A DC voltage V_DC is applied across the contacts. When E > E_th, the bulk GaAs exhibits NDR.
- A small random fluctuation in electron density near the cathode grows exponentially, forming a high-field domain.
- The domain consists of an electron-accumulation layer and a depletion layer (charge dipole).
- It propagates from cathode to anode at v_sat.
- At the anode, the domain is collected; a new one forms at the cathode.
- This produces a periodic current oscillation at microwave frequencies.

**Two-Valley Model with Energy Band Diagram:**
- The conduction band of GaAs has a central valley (Γ) at k = 0 and four satellite valleys (L) at the Brillouin zone boundary, offset in energy by ΔE ≈ 0.31 eV.
- At low fields, electrons occupy the Γ valley (low mass, high mobility).
- At high fields (E > E_th), electrons transfer to L valleys (high mass, low mobility), creating NDR.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparison Table (as given in Section 3.3 above).**

**Why LSA Mode is preferred for high-power pulsed operation:**

1. **High peak power:** The LSA mode can deliver peak powers in the **kilowatt range**, far exceeding the mW–W range of Gunn oscillation mode.
2. **High efficiency:** DC-to-RF conversion efficiency can exceed **25%**, compared to 1–5% in the basic Gunn mode.
3. **Frequency flexibility:** The frequency is set by the external cavity, not by the device length. This allows the same device to operate over a wide frequency range by changing the cavity.
4. **Pulsed operation suitability:** LSA mode works best with short, high-voltage DC pulses. The device is on briefly, allowing the high peak power without thermal damage. The duty cycle is low (typically 0.1–1%).
5. **No domain formation penalty:** In the Gunn oscillation mode, energy is lost in the formation and collection of domains, reducing efficiency. In LSA mode, since the domain never fully forms, less energy is wasted.
6. **High n₀L product:** The high doping and short length means the device can handle high current densities without breakdown.

These advantages make LSA mode ideal for **military radar systems**, **electronic countermeasures**, and **scientific pulsed-power applications**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gunn Diode Modes**

- **Gunn Effect** = Transferred Electron Effect in direct-bandgap multi-valley semiconductors (GaAs, InP).
- **Threshold Field (E_th) for GaAs:** ≈ **3.2 × 10⁵ V/m** (≈ 3.2 kV/cm).
- **Five modes** of operation: Gunn Oscillation, Stable Amplification, Quenched/Delayed Domain, LSA, and Bias-Circuit Oscillation.
- **Mode selection is primarily determined by the n₀L product** (in cm⁻²).
- **n₀L < 10¹² cm⁻²** → Gunn Oscillation Mode (most common, CW oscillator).
- **10¹² < n₀L < 10¹⁴ cm⁻²** → Stable Amplification Mode (negative-resistance amplifier).
- **10¹⁴ < n₀L < 10¹⁵ cm⁻²** → Quenched/Delayed Domain Mode (higher efficiency oscillator).
- **n₀L > 10¹⁵ cm⁻²** → LSA Mode (pulsed high-power oscillator, kW range).
- **Bias-Circuit Oscillation Mode** = parasitic; suppressed with proper RF choke and decoupling.
- **Operating frequency formula:** $f = v_{sat} / L$ (Gunn mode); cavity-controlled (LSA mode).
- **Dielectric relaxation time:** $\tau_d = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 / (n_0 \, e \, \vert \mu_d \vert)$.
- **Threshold condition for any domain mode:** $\tau_d < T_t \Rightarrow n_0 L > 10^{12}$ cm⁻².
- **Two-valley model:** Γ-valley (m* = 0.067 m₀) and L-valley (m* = 1.2 m₀) separated by ΔE ≈ 0.31 eV.
- **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** is the root cause of the Gunn Effect — dv/dE < 0 in the region E > E_th.
- **Gunn diode has NO p-n junction** — it is a **bulk, two-terminal, majority-carrier device**.
- **LSA mode** is the only mode where frequency is **independent of device geometry** (cavity-controlled).
- **Silicon does not exhibit Gunn Effect** because it is indirect-bandgap with valleys of similar effective mass.
- **Remember the 5-mode mnemonic:** **"G-S-Q-D-L"** (Gunn-Stable-Quenched-Delayed-LSA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Klystrons (Types)

A **Klystron** is a *linear-beam* (O-type) vacuum electron device that uses the principle of **velocity modulation** followed by **density (current) modulation** of an electron beam to amplify or generate microwave RF energy. Klystrons are broadly classified as:

| Type | No. of Cavities | Primary Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Two-Cavity Klystron** | 2 (Buncher + Catcher) | RF Amplifier, Oscillator (with feedback) |
| **Multi-Cavity Klystron** | 3, 4, or 5 (cascaded) | High-power CW pulsed amplifiers (Radar, TV) |
| **Reflex Klystron** | 1 (single cavity + reflector) | Low-power tunable microwave oscillator (LO) |
| **Floating Drift Tube Klystron** | 2 (with modulating anode) | Used in broadband applications |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module-2 Focus: **Two-Cavity Klystron** — its **construction**, **velocity modulation**, and **bunching** principles are the principal examinable areas.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Two-Cavity Klystron

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Cavity Klystron** — *A linear-beam microwave amplifier consisting of an electron gun, an input resonant cavity (the **buncher**), a field-free **drift space**, an output resonant cavity (the **catcher**), and a beam collector, in which the input RF signal velocity-modulates the electron beam; subsequent bunching in the drift space converts this velocity modulation into density modulation, inducing amplified RF power into the catcher cavity.*

## 1.3 Structural Components (Construction)

A Two-Cavity Klystron consists of the following sub-assemblies:

1. **Electron Gun** — Heated cathode, focusing electrode, and accelerating anode producing a narrow, high-velocity electron beam.
2. **Input Cavity (Buncher / Buncher Gap)** — Re-entant cavity resonator through which the beam first passes; the low-power RF input signal is applied across its gap.
3. **Drift Space** — A field-free, equipotential region where electrons travel at velocities imparted by the buncher. No RF field exists here.
4. **Output Cavity (Catcher / Catcher Gap)** — Tuned re-entrant cavity that intercepts the bunched beam and extracts RF power via an output coupling.
5. **Beam Collector (Anode)** — Collects the *spent* (decelerated) electrons and dissipates their remaining kinetic energy as heat.
6. **Solenoid / Focusing Magnet** — Provides a strong axial magnetic field (typically **B ≈ 0.1 – 0.5 Tesla**) to confine the electron beam and prevent transverse spreading.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Relay Race" Picture

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a **relay race** where runners start evenly spaced on a circular track. The starter's pistol (the RF input) causes some runners to speed up and others to slow down. After one lap, the fast runners catch up to the slow ones ahead — the runners **bunch** together. As this "bunched group" crosses the finish line (catcher cavity), the sudden surge in current delivers enormous "power" (the amplified RF output) to the stadium. The two-cavity klystron operates on precisely this principle — **velocity modulation → drift → bunching → power extraction**.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- Electron charge: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- Electron mass: **m = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- Charge-to-mass ratio: **e/m = 1.759 × 10¹¹ C/kg**
- Beam voltage range: **V₀ = 300 V to 100 kV** (typical)
- Operating frequency: **f = 0.5 GHz to 40 GHz**
- Theoretical max efficiency (2-cavity): **~ 58 %**
- Practical efficiency: **15 % – 40 %**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation (Five-Stage Process)

- **Stage 1 — Beam Formation:** A dc accelerating voltage **V₀** between cathode and anode imparts a uniform axial velocity
  $$v_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2eV_0}{m}} \approx 0.593 \times 10^6 \sqrt{V_0}\ \text{m/s}$$

- **Stage 2 — Velocity Modulation (at Buncher):** The RF input signal
  $$v_{in}(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t)$$
  applied across the buncher gap accelerates some electrons and decelerates others — their **velocities are modulated** but the **current density remains uniform** as the beam leaves the gap.

- **Stage 3 — Bunching (in Drift Space):** Faster electrons catch up with slower ones ahead. The previously uniform beam **condenses into packets (bunches)** — velocity modulation is converted to **density (current) modulation**.

- **Stage 4 — Power Extraction (at Catcher):** The bunched beam crossing the catcher gap induces a pulsating current in the catcher. The catcher, tuned to the same resonant frequency, develops a large RF voltage and delivers amplified output power to the load.

- **Stage 5 — Collection:** Spent electrons impinge on the collector and dissipate their remaining energy as heat.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The klystron is essentially a **transducer of energy** — it converts the **kinetic energy** of a velocity-modulated beam into **electromagnetic energy** in the catcher cavity.

## 2.2 Velocity Modulation — Physical Picture

Let an electron enter the buncher gap at time **t = t₁** when the instantaneous RF voltage is
$$v_{rf}(t_1) = V_1 \sin(\omega t_1)$$

Applying **conservation of energy** between the entry and exit of the gap:
$$\tfrac{1}{2}mv_2^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2 = eV_1 \sin(\omega t_1)$$

Solving for the exit velocity **v₂**:
$$v_2 = v_0 \sqrt{1 + \frac{eV_1}{mv_0^2}\sin(\omega t_1)}$$

Defining the **bunching parameter coefficient**:
$$\beta \equiv \frac{eV_1}{mv_0^2} = \frac{V_1}{2V_0}\quad\left(\text{since } \tfrac{1}{2}mv_0^2 = eV_0\right)$$

The exit velocity becomes:
$$v_2 = v_0\sqrt{1 + \beta \sin(\omega t_1)}$$

For typical small-signal conditions (**β ≪ 1**, V₁ ≪ V₀), the binomial expansion yields the **linearised velocity-modulation equation**:
$$v_2 \approx v_0 \left[1 + \frac{\beta}{2}\sin(\omega t_1)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> Electrons arriving when **sin(ωt₁) = +1** (positive RF half-cycle) are **accelerated** → faster.
> Electrons arriving when **sin(ωt₁) = –1** (negative RF half-cycle) are **decelerated** → slower.
> Electrons arriving when **sin(ωt₁) = 0** (zero crossing) are **unaffected** — these are the **reference electrons** with velocity v₀.

## 2.3 Bunching — Formation of Electron Packets

In the field-free **drift space**, no further force acts on the electrons. The electron that entered the buncher at time **t₁** with velocity **v₂** continues at that velocity:
$$z(t) = v_2\,(t - t_1)$$

Consider the **reference electron** (entering at **t₁ = 0**) — it travels with velocity **v₀**:
$$z_{ref}(t) = v_0\, t$$

Consider an electron that entered the buncher **1/4 cycle LATER** (at **t₁ = +T/4**) — it has been **accelerated** (faster):
$$v_f = v_0\!\left[1 + \tfrac{\beta}{2}\right],\quad z_f(t) = v_0\!\left(1 + \tfrac{\beta}{2}\right)\!\left(t - \tfrac{T}{4}\right)$$

Consider an electron that entered **1/4 cycle EARLIER** (at **t₁ = –T/4**) — it has been **decelerated** (slower):
$$v_s = v_0\!\left[1 - \tfrac{\beta}{2}\right],\quad z_s(t) = v_0\!\left(1 - \tfrac{\beta}{2}\right)\!\left(t + \tfrac{T}{4}\right)$$

Setting **z_f = z_ref** yields the **optimum catch-up (bunching) distance**:
$$L_{opt} = \frac{v_0\, T}{2\beta} = \frac{v_0}{2\beta f} = \frac{3 v_0}{2\beta\omega}$$

At this distance, all electrons entering the buncher within a half-cycle have **converged to the same point** — the **bunch centre**.

## 2.4 The Bunching Parameter (X)

A dimensionless quantity that characterises the degree of bunching at any drift distance **z**:
$$X = \frac{\beta \omega z}{v_0}$$

- For the reference electron, X reaches its **optimum value** at the catcher:
$$X_0 = \frac{\beta \omega L_{opt}}{v_0} = \pi$$
(in the analytical convention; in many textbooks X = 1.841 is the optimum for maximum fundamental current).

## 2.5 Current Induced at the Catcher

The induced current at the catcher, derived from the conservation of charge, has the Fourier series form:
$$i_{ind}(t) = I_0\left[1 + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_n(nX)\sin(n\omega t)\right]$$

The **fundamental (n = 1) component** is:
$$i_1(t) = 2I_0 J_1(X)\sin(\omega t)$$

The peak of **J₁(X) is 0.582 at X = 1.841** — the **optimum bunching condition** for maximum RF output.

## 2.6 Output Power and Efficiency

Output power delivered to the catcher (matched load):
$$P_{out} = \frac{1}{2} V_2\, I_1 = I_0^2 J_1^2(X)\, R_{sh}$$

DC input power: **P_dc = V₀ I₀**

Overall efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{dc}} = \frac{I_0 J_1^2(X) R_{sh}}{V_0}$$

Theoretical maximum efficiency of the two-cavity klystron:
$$\boxed{\eta_{max} \approx 58\%}$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | DC beam velocity | $v_0 = \sqrt{2eV_0/m} = 0.593\times10^6\sqrt{V_0}$ | m/s |
| 2 | Bunching coefficient | $\beta = eV_1/(mv_0^2) = V_1/(2V_0)$ | dimensionless, β ≪ 1 |
| 3 | Velocity-modulated exit velocity | $v_2 = v_0\sqrt{1+\beta\sin\omega t_1}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Linearised exit velocity | $v_2 \approx v_0\bigl[1+(\beta/2)\sin\omega t_1\bigr]$ | m/s, small-signal |
| 5 | Optimum bunching distance | $L_{opt} = v_0/(2\beta f) = 3v_0/(2\beta\omega)$ | metres |
| 6 | Bunching parameter | $X = \beta\omega z / v_0$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Optimum X (max J₁) | $X_{opt} = 1.841$ | radian-less |
| 8 | Induced fundamental current | $I_1 = 2I_0 J_1(X)$ | amperes |
| 9 | Maximum J₁ value | $J_1(X)_{max} = 0.582$ at X = 1.841 | — |
| 10 | Output power | $P_{out} = I_0^2 J_1^2(X) R_{sh}$ | watts |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta = I_0 J_1^2(X) R_{sh}/V_0$ | dimensionless (≤ 58%) |
| 12 | Frequency of operation | $f = 1/(2\pi)\sqrt{1/LC}$ (cavity) | Hz |
| 13 | Buncher coupling coefficient | $M = \sin(\beta_q d/2)/(\beta_q d/2)$ | dimensionless, 0 ≤ M ≤ 1 |
| 14 | Period of RF cycle | $T = 2\pi/\omega = 1/f$ | seconds |

> [!TIP]
> Memorise rows 1, 4, 5, 7, 8, and 11 — these alone cover **> 80%** of KTU numerical/derivation marks for this topic.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radar transmitters** (pulsed two-cavity klystrons for surveillance and tracking).
- **Satellite communication ground stations** as final-stage high-power amplifiers.
- **TV broadcast transmitters** (UHF bands).
- **Particle accelerators** (klystrons provide multi-megawatt pulsed RF to accelerate charged particles).
- **Troposcatter & over-the-horizon (OTH) radar** systems.
