Therefore: $$v_p = \frac{p}{T} = \frac{pc}{\sqrt{(\pi D)^2 + p^2}} = c \sin\psi$$ (Detailed algebra: factor out $\pi D$ to get $v_p = c \cdot (p/\pi D) / \sqrt{1+(p/\pi D)^2} = c \tan\psi \cos\psi = c \sin\psi$.) **Mark split:** 1 mark pitch-angle definition, 1 mark setup, 1 mark final algebra. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Module Internal Choice: Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a helix-type traveling-wave tube. Explain the role of the slow-wave structure in achieving amplification. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction (3 marks):** A helix TWT consists of the following parts: 1. **Electron gun:** Comprises a heated cathode (oxide or dispenser), a focus electrode (Wehnelt cylinder), and an accelerating anode. It produces a high-velocity, well-focused electron beam. 2. **Helix slow-wave structure:** A wire (or tape) wound in the form of a helix around a glass or ceramic beam tunnel. The helix is held in place by three or more dielectric support rods (BN, BeO, or alumina) inside the vacuum envelope. 3. **Input and output RF couplers:** Coaxial-to-helix or waveguide-to-helix transitions match the external circuit to the helix impedance at the entry and exit. 4. **Internal attenuator (sever):** A lossy coating (e.g., aquadag, carbon, or AIN) on part of the helix or on a separate ceramic rod. It absorbs reflected waves, suppresses backward-wave oscillation, and improves stability. 5. **Beam collector:** A water- or radiation-cooled electrode that captures the spent electron beam after interaction. 6. **Periodic Permanent Magnet (PPM) stack:** A series of alternating-polarity magnets surrounding the helix, providing the axial magnetic field that confines the electron beam against space-charge blow-up. 7. **RF input and output windows:** Hermetic, low-loss dielectric windows (typically alumina or BeO) that pass the RF signal in and out of the vacuum envelope. **Working (3 marks):** The RF input signal is applied to the helix near the gun end. It propagates along the helix as a slow wave with axial phase velocity $v_p = c \sin\psi$. The electron gun emits a beam with velocity $v_0 \gtrsim v_p$ (synchronism condition). As beam and wave travel together along the helix: - The axial $E$-field of the wave velocity-modulates the beam. - Velocity modulation converts to density modulation (bunching) within a few helix turns. - The bunches sit near the decelerating phase of the wave and lose kinetic energy. - This energy is delivered to the wave via the electromagnetic field of the helix, causing the wave amplitude to grow. - The process is cumulative and grows exponentially. - The amplified wave is extracted at the output end through the output coupler, and the spent beam is collected. **Role of the slow-wave structure (1 mark):** The SWS reduces the wave's axial velocity from $c$ to a value close to the beam velocity, enabling continuous, cumulative energy transfer. It is also non-resonant, giving the TWT its characteristic octave-band bandwidth. --- **Q.A (b)** A helix TWT has the following parameters: mean helix diameter $D = 3.2 \text{ mm}$, pitch $p = 0.8 \text{ mm}$, helix length $L = 30 \text{ cm}$, beam voltage $V_0 = 2800 \text{ V}$, and operating frequency $f = 6 \text{ GHz}$. Calculate: 1. The pitch angle ψ 2. The axial phase velocity $v_p$ 3. The number of turns $N$ 4. The synchronism ratio $v_0 / v_p$ **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (1) — Pitch angle:** $$\tan\psi = \frac{p}{\pi D} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 3.2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.8}{10.053} = 0.0796$$ $$\psi = \arctan(0.0796) \approx 4.55°$$ **(1 mark)** **Part (2) — Axial phase velocity:** $$v_p = c \sin\psi = 3 \times 10^8 \times \sin(4.55°) = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.0793 \approx 2.379 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s}$$ **(2 marks)** **Part (3) — Number of turns:** $$N = \frac{L}{p} = \frac{0.30}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 375 \text{ turns}$$ **(1 mark)** **Part (4) — Synchronism ratio:** $$v_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2eV_0}{m_0}} = \sqrt{3.517 \times 10^{11} \times 2800} = \sqrt{9.85 \times 10^{14}} \approx 3.139 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\frac{v_0}{v_p} = \frac{3.139 \times 10^7}{2.379 \times 10^7} \approx 1.320$$ **(3 marks)** **Conclusion:** $v_0 / v_p \approx 1.32$, indicating the beam is ~32% faster than the wave — initial design. For optimized gain, $V_0$ would be tuned down to bring this ratio close to 1.05–1.15. The result confirms a properly scaled helix SWS suitable for X-band operation. --- ### **Module Internal Choice: Question B (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 2 — Alternative]** **Q.B (a)** Explain the amplification process in a helix TWT using the concepts of velocity modulation, density modulation, and energy extraction. Why does the wave grow exponentially rather than linearly? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Velocity modulation (2 marks):** When a small RF signal is applied to the helix, an axial electric field $E_z$ is set up along the beam axis. As the electron beam passes through, electrons that encounter the **decelerating** phase of $E_z$ lose kinetic energy and slow down; those that encounter the **accelerating** phase gain energy and speed up. This is **velocity modulation** of the beam — the velocities of individual electrons vary with the phase of the RF signal at the moment they entered the helix. **Density modulation / bunching (2 marks):** Faster electrons catch up to slower electrons ahead of them. After a short distance (typically a few helix turns), the originally uniform beam becomes a series of **electron bunches** separated by half an RF period. This is **density modulation** — the charge density now contains a strong component at the RF frequency. **Energy extraction (2 marks):** The phase of the bunches is such that they sit near the **decelerating phase** of the RF wave. The wave does negative work on the bunches, extracting their kinetic energy. This energy is delivered to the wave through the electromagnetic field of the helix, increasing the wave's amplitude. **Why exponential growth (1 mark):** The growing wave, in turn, produces a stronger $E_z$, which produces stronger bunching, which induces more charge on the helix (image-charge coupling), which strengthens $E_z$ still further. This **positive feedback** is what makes the amplitude grow exponentially, not linearly. The growth rate is set by Pierce's gain parameter $C$: $$A(z) = A(0)\, e^{\beta_e C z}$$ where $\beta_e = \omega/v_0$. The total gain is approximately $G_{dB} \approx 47.3\, C N - L$ in decibels, where $N$ is the number of turns and $L$ is the cold loss. --- **Q.B (b)** Compare the helix SWS with the coupled-cavity SWS used in TWTs. Mention at least four comparative points and state two applications where each is preferred. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Comparative Point | Helix SWS | Coupled-Cavity SWS | |---|---|---| | **Bandwidth** | Very wide (octave or more, ~1–2 octaves) | Moderate (10–20%) | | **Power handling** | Limited (~hundreds of W CW) | Very high (kW to MW pulsed) | | **Efficiency** | 20–30% typical | 30–50% typical | | **Interaction impedance** | High (better coupling) | Lower (less coupling) | | **Construction** | Simple wire/tape helix, fragile at high frequency | Complex machined cavity chain, robust | | **Size at mm-wave** | Difficult (very fine pitch) | Easier to fabricate at high frequencies | | **Susceptibility to BWO** | Higher; needs sever | Lower; inherently stable | | **Voltage / current** | Lower voltage, lower current | Higher voltage and current | **(4 marks for table content, 1 mark per comparative point × 4 minimum = 4 marks; consolidate to 4 marks.)** **Applications (3 marks):** **Helix TWT preferred in:** 1. **Satellite communication transponders** (Ku-band, Ka-band) — wideband, moderate power, high reliability. 2. **Electronic warfare (EW) jammers** — instantaneous bandwidth > 1 octave is essential. **Coupled-cavity TWT preferred in:** 1. **Long-range pulsed radar transmitters** — high peak power (MW class) and high efficiency are critical. 2. **Particle accelerator RF drivers** — narrowband, high-power pulsed operation with good phase stability. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the units in $v_p$:** Always state the final phase velocity in m/s or as a fraction of $c$. Examiners explicitly check for unit consistency. > 2. **Confusing the small-angle approximation with the exact result:** If you write $v_p = c \tan\psi$ and ψ is **not** small, you will lose a mark. Use the exact $v_p = c \sin\psi$ form unless the problem specifies $\psi \ll 1$. > 3. **Mixing up phase velocity and group velocity:** In the helix, the group velocity is the *energy* velocity along the wire, $v_g = c \cos\psi$, which is **different** from $v_p = c \sin\psi$. The KTU exam will explicitly test this distinction. > 4. **Using relativistic electron velocity:** For $V_0 < 30 \text{ kV}$, the non-relativistic formula is sufficient. If $V_0 > 50 \text{ kV}$, relativistic correction is required. > 5. **Skipping the BWO (backward-wave oscillation) discussion:** Many students forget that the helix is prone to backward-wave oscillation. The **internal attenuator (sever)** is mandatory to break the feedback loop — always mention it. > 6. **Missing the synchronism condition:** When asked to "design" or "select" a beam voltage for a given helix, you must explicitly state that $v_0 \gtrsim v_p$ and compute $V_0$ from it, not just plug numbers in. > 7. **Confusing Pierce parameters $A$ and $B$ with the $A$ and $B$ in the gain equation $G = A + BCN - L$:** They are numerical constants from Pierce's theory, not arbitrary symbols. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Helix TWT Module** - [x] A **TWT** is a broadband vacuum electronic microwave amplifier using distributed beam–wave interaction on a **slow-wave structure (SWS)**. - [x] An SWS is needed because **RF waves in free space travel at $c$**, while electron beams travel at only **5–20% of $c$** — they cannot interact without velocity matching. - [x] A **simple waveguide cannot** serve as an SWS — its phase velocity is *greater* than $c$ (anomalous dispersion), and it would be below cutoff at the desired slow-wave frequency. - [x] Common SWS types: **helix**, **coupled-cavity**, **ring-loop**, **folded-waveguide**, **ring-bar**. - [x] **Helix parameters:** pitch $p$, mean diameter $D$, pitch angle $\psi$, where $\tan\psi = p / (\pi D)$. - [x] **Axial phase velocity on helix:** $v_p = c \sin\psi$ (exact); $v_p \approx c \cdot p/(\pi D)$ for small $\psi$. - [x] **Group velocity on helix** (energy velocity): $v_g = c \cos\psi$. Note that $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2 \cos\psi \sin\psi$ — not a simple product. - [x] **Synchronism:** $v_0 \gtrsim v_p$, where $v_0 = \sqrt{2eV_0 / m_0}$ is the non-relativistic beam velocity. - [x] **Amplification process:** velocity modulation → density modulation (bunching) → energy extraction → exponential growth via positive feedback. - [x] **Pierce's gain parameter:** $C = (K I_0 / 4V_0)^{1/3}$, where $K$ is the interaction impedance (Ω). - [x] **Small-signal gain:** $G_{dB} \approx 47.3\, C N - L$ (for $b \approx 0$). - [x] **Internal attenuator (sever)** is essential to suppress **backward-wave oscillation (BWO)** and prevent regenerative instabilities. - [x] **PPM focusing** is used to confine the electron beam over the long interaction length (against space-charge repulsion). - [x] **Helix TWTs excel in bandwidth** (octave+) and **moderate power** (hundreds of W CW); **coupled-cavity TWTs excel in peak power** (MW pulsed) but with narrower bandwidth. - [x] Applications: **satellite transponders (TWTA), EW jammers, radar transmitters, deep-space probes**. - [x] Typical operating frequency range: **1 GHz to 100 GHz**; typical gain: **30–60 dB**; typical efficiency: **20–30%**. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** When a numerical TWT problem appears, always start by computing $\psi$, then $v_p$, then $v_0$ from $V_0$, then check synchronism. The "design" question almost always reduces to solving $v_0 \approx v_p$ for $V_0$ given the helix geometry. Master the four-step routine above and you will handle 80% of numerical questions on this topic.

topics.title: Magnetron Oscillator - Cylindrical Magnetron, Cyclotron Angular Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetron Oscillator - Cylindrical Magnetron, Cyclotron Angular Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Cylindrical Magnetron — Formal KTU Definition

A **Cylindrical Magnetron** is a high-power, vacuum-electronic cross-field microwave oscillator in which a central cylindrical cathode is concentrically surrounded by a cylindrical anode block (containing multiple re-entrant resonant cavities). A static axial magnetic field **B** and a radial DC electric field **E** (established by anode voltage **V₀**) act simultaneously (perpendicular to one another) on electrons emitted from the cathode, producing a *crossed-field* configuration. By carefully tuning the ratio of **B/B_c** (where **B_c** is the Hull cutoff), the device is forced into bunched (phase-focused) electron spokes that transfer kinetic energy to a chosen cavity mode — typically the **π-mode** — yielding continuous wave (CW) or pulsed microwave power from the L-band up through the X-band.

### 1.2 Cyclotron Angular Frequency — Formal KTU Definition

The **Cyclotron Angular Frequency** ($\omega_c$) is the natural angular velocity with which a free charged particle (here, an electron of charge magnitude **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** and mass **m = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**) gyrates in a uniform static magnetic field **B**, in the plane perpendicular to **B**, under the sole influence of the Lorentz force. It is given by:

$$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m}$$

Its equivalent *cyclotron frequency* in hertz is **f_c = eB / (2πm)**, and the radius of the circular trajectory is the *Larmor (cyclotron) radius* **r_L = v_⊥ / ω_c**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 (Microwave Tubes)**
> Crossed-field tubes (Magnetrons) are explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 Scheme. The Hull cutoff, the Buneman–Hartree threshold, the cyclotron frequency, and the conditions for π-mode oscillation are **high-yield topics** that appear in 2-mark concepts, 3-mark short derivations, and 14-mark full problems.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "Electron Skater in a Magnetic Ice-Rink"**
> Imagine a tiny ice-skater (the electron) launched outward from a central post (the cathode) on a circular ice-rink (the anode). A strong vertical magnetic field **B** acts like a constant sideways "nudge" — the faster the skater moves, the stronger the sideways push, and the tighter becomes the circular curve. The DC voltage from anode to cathode acts like a *radial* push pulling the skater outward. The **Cyclotron Angular Frequency** is simply how many times per second the skater completes a circle when the magnetic field alone is acting.
>
> Now, if you tune the magnetic field and the anode voltage just right, instead of skating aimlessly, the skater joins a *conga-line* of other skaters, all circling in synchronism. That synchronized spiral is called an **electron spoke** — and it is this organized motion that "kicks" the resonant cavities (the slots cut into the anode) to oscillate at microwave frequencies, producing useful RF power.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Electron charge:** **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Electron rest mass:** **m = 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
- **Charge-to-mass ratio:** **e/m = 1.759 × 10¹¹ C·kg⁻¹**
- **Free-space permittivity:** **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- **Speed of light (vacuum):** **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electron Dynamics in a Cylindrical Magnetron — Structured Logic

A cylindrical magnetron consists of a cylindrical cathode of radius **r_c** and a coaxial cylindrical anode of radius **r_a**, with **r_a > r_c**. Anode voltage **V₀** is applied positive with respect to the cathode, and an axial uniform magnetic field **B** is provided by a solenoid or permanent magnet.

1. **Radial Electric Field (Static, No Space Charge):**
   The Laplacian equation in cylindrical coordinates between concentric cylinders gives a **1/r** radial field:
   $$E_r(r) = \frac{V_0}{r \, \ln(r_a / r_c)}$$
   This field points outward and pulls electrons from cathode to anode.

2. **Axial Magnetic Field (Uniform):**
   $$\vec{B} = B \, \hat{a}_z \quad \text{(uniform, axial)}$$
   This field is perpendicular to **E**, giving the *crossed-field* configuration.

3. **Lorentz Force on an Electron (charge –e):**
   $$\vec{F} = -e\left[\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B}\right]$$
   The **v × B** term continuously deflects the radial motion into a curved path, producing circular/cycloidal trajectories.

4. **Three Operating Regimes (governed by B):**
   - **B = 0:** Electrons travel radially cathode → anode (conventional diode).
   - **B < B_c (Hull cutoff):** Electrons follow curved paths but still reach the anode (current flows).
   - **B = B_c:** Electron just *grazes* the anode — anode current is theoretically zero (critical cutoff).
   - **B > B_c:** Electron trajectory is confined; the electron loops back without striking the anode — **magnetron cutoff** condition.

5. **Cyclotron Motion (the Fundamental Gyration):**
   With **E = 0**, an electron injected with velocity **v_⊥** in the **x–y** plane circles at angular frequency:
   $$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m}$$
   This is the **cyclotron angular frequency**, independent of particle velocity and radius — a key KTU 2024 concept.

6. **Bunching & π-mode Oscillation:**
   When the magnetron is operated in a dynamic regime (Buneman–Hartree condition), electrons bunch into rotating *spokes*. As the spoke passes each cavity slot, it induces RF currents. In the **π-mode**, the RF phase alternates 180° between adjacent cavities — the most efficient and commonly used mode.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Equations

- **Why is ω_c independent of v?** Because the magnetic Lorentz force is always perpendicular to velocity. Faster electrons experience a *larger* centripetal force, but their *larger* inertia compensates exactly → the gyration period is fixed by B alone.
- **Why does increasing B cutoff the anode current?** Stronger B tightens the Larmor radius. Above **B_c**, the Larmor radius is smaller than **r_a**, so electrons can never reach the anode — they spiral back to the cathode.
- **Why π-mode?** Adjacent cavities oscillating 180° out of phase couple strongly through the slot openings, producing the highest group velocity and the most stable oscillation, enabling high efficiency (~70–80%).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cyclotron angular frequency | $\omega_c = eB/m$ | rad/s | Independent of v and r |
| 2 | Cyclotron frequency (Hz) | $f_c = eB/(2\pi m)$ | Hz |  |
| 3 | Larmor radius | $r_L = v_\perp / \omega_c$ | m | Radius of gyration |
| 4 | Static radial E-field | $E_r(r) = V_0 / [r \ln(r_a / r_c)]$ | V/m | Between coaxial cylinders |
| 5 | Hull cutoff magnetic field | $B_c = \dfrac{\sqrt{8mV_0/e}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (r_c / r_a)^2}}$ | Tesla (T) | Static (DC) cutoff |
| 6 | Hull cutoff voltage | $V_c = \dfrac{e\,B^2\,r_a^2}{8m}\left(1 - \tfrac{r_c^2}{r_a^2}\right)^2$ | V | Alternate form |
| 7 | Buneman–Hartree threshold | $B_0 = \dfrac{1}{r_a}\sqrt{\dfrac{8mV_0}{e(1-r_c^2/r_a^2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - (\omega/\omega_c)^2}}$ | T | Dynamic oscillation threshold |
| 8 | Hull cutoff relation (B² vs V₀) | $V_0 = \dfrac{e\,r_a^2\,B_c^2}{8m}\left(1 - \dfrac{r_c^2}{r_a^2}\right)^2$ | V | Parabolic cutoff boundary |
| 9 | Anode current condition | $I_a > 0$ if $B < B_c$; $I_a = 0$ if $B \geq B_c$ | A | Static magnetron |
| 10 | Electron charge-to-mass ratio | $e/m = 1.759 \times 10^{11}$ | C/kg | Numerical constant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The Hull cutoff and cyclotron-frequency relations are the *two pillars* of every magnetron problem. Memorize them in both the B-form and the V-form; many KTU problems flip the question (give V, find B, or vice-versa).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radar Transmitters (S-band, C-band, X-band):** Magnetrons are the workhorse pulsed power source for civilian and military radar.
- **Microwave Ovens:** A 2.45 GHz CW magnetron heats food via dielectric loss in water molecules.
- **Industrial Heating:** RF drying, plasma generation, sintering of ceramics.
- **Electronic Countermeasure (ECM) Jammers:** Tunable magnetrons provide broadband noise.
- **Particle Accelerators & Plasma Physics:** The cyclotron-frequency concept is foundational for cyclotrons, synchrocyclotrons, tokamaks, and Hall-effect thrusters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cyclotron Angular Frequency (ω_c)

**Starting Point:** A free electron of charge **−e** and mass **m** moving in a uniform magnetic field **B** (along **+z**) with velocity **v_⊥** in the x–y plane.

**Step 1 — Lorentz Force Equation:**
The magnetic component of the Lorentz force on the electron is the only force acting (no E-field in this derivation):
$$\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 2 — Set Up the Equation of Motion (Newton's Second Law):**
$$m \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 3 — Resolve Into Cartesian Components:**
With **B = B ẑ** and **v = v_x x̂ + v_y ŷ**:
$$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = (v_x \hat{x} + v_y \hat{y}) \times (B \hat{z}) = v_x B (\hat{x} \times \hat{z}) + v_y B (\hat{y} \times \hat{z})$$
Using **x̂ × ẑ = −ŷ** and **ŷ × ẑ = x̂**:
$$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = -v_x B \hat{y} + v_y B \hat{x}$$

**Step 4 — Write Scalar Equations:**
$$m \frac{dv_x}{dt} = -e(v_y B) = -eBv_y$$
$$m \frac{dv_y}{dt} = -e(-v_x B) = +eBv_x$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate the First Equation Again:**
$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dt^2} = -\frac{eB}{m}\frac{dv_y}{dt} = -\frac{eB}{m}\left(+\frac{eB}{m}v_x\right) = -\left(\frac{eB}{m}\right)^2 v_x$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the SHM Form:**
$$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dt^2} + \left(\frac{eB}{m}\right)^2 v_x = 0$$

This is the simple harmonic motion equation with **ω² = (eB/m)²**, giving the cyclotron angular frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m}}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**
- Period of gyration: **T = 2πm / (eB)**
- Frequency: **f_c = eB / (2πm) = 2.799 × 10¹⁰ B [Hz] (for B in Tesla)**
- At **B = 0.1 T**: f_c ≈ **2.8 GHz** (microwave range!) — *this is why magnetrons work at microwave frequencies.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hull Cutoff Magnetic Field (B_c) for Cylindrical Magnetron

**Physical Condition:** Hull cutoff is the *minimum* magnetic field at which an electron just barely fails to reach the anode — i.e., it grazes the anode surface tangentially at **r = r_a** with zero radial velocity.

**Step 1 — Energy Conservation (No E-Field Work Beyond r = r_a):**
An electron emitted at rest from the cathode at **r = r_c** and reaching **r = r_a** with tangential velocity **v_θ** (radial = 0) has kinetic energy equal to the work done by the radial E-field:
$$\frac{1}{2}m v_\theta^2 = e V_0$$

**Step 2 — Angular Momentum Conservation:**
The magnetic force has zero torque about the axis (it is purely radial in cylindrical coordinates, or it acts perpendicular to **r**). Hence, the canonical angular momentum is conserved:
$$p_\theta = m r v_\theta - \tfrac{1}{2} e B r^2 = \text{constant}$$
At the cathode (**r = r_c**, **v_θ = 0**): constant = **−½ e B r_c²**.
At the anode (**r = r_a**): **m r_a v_θ − ½ e B r_a² = −½ e B r_c²**.

**Step 3 — Solve for v_θ at r = r_a:**
$$m r_a v_\theta = \frac{1}{2} e B (r_a^2 - r_c^2)$$
$$v_\theta = \frac{eB}{2m r_a}(r_a^2 - r_c^2)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Into Energy Equation:**
$$\frac{1}{2}m \left[\frac{eB}{2m r_a}(r_a^2 - r_c^2)\right]^2 = e V_0$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**
$$\frac{m}{2} \cdot \frac{e^2 B^2 (r_a^2 - r_c^2)^2}{4 m^2 r_a^2} = e V_0$$
$$\frac{e^2 B^2 (r_a^2 - r_c^2)^2}{8 m r_a^2} = e V_0$$
$$B^2 (r_a^2 - r_c^2)^2 = \frac{8 m r_a^2 V_0}{e}$$

**Step 6 — Take the Square Root (B > 0):**
$$B_c (r_a^2 - r_c^2) = r_a \sqrt{\frac{8 m V_0}{e}}$$

**Step 7 — Final Form of Hull Cutoff Field:**

$$\boxed{B_c = \frac{1}{r_a \left[1 - (r_c / r_a)^2\right]} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8 m V_0}{e}} \;\; \text{or equivalently} \;\; B_c = \frac{\sqrt{8 m V_0 / e}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (r_c / r_a)^2}}}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Form (using e/m = 1.759 × 10¹¹):**

$$B_c = \frac{6.74 \times 10^{-6} \sqrt{V_0}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (r_c / r_a)^2}} \;\; \text{[Tesla]}$$

when V₀ is in volts and r_a, r_c are in meters.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A cylindrical magnetron has the following operating parameters: anode radius **r_a = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m**, cathode radius **r_c = 0.5 cm = 0.005 m**, anode voltage **V₀ = 2000 V**, magnetic flux density **B = 0.05 T**.

**(a)** Find the Hull cutoff magnetic field **B_c**.

**(b)** Find the cyclotron angular frequency **ω_c** and the cyclotron frequency **f_c**.

**(c)** State whether the magnetron is operating above or below cutoff.

**Solution (a):**

Using **B_c = √(8mV₀/e) / [r_a √(1 − (r_c/r_a)²)]**:

**Sub-step i:** Compute **(r_c/r_a)² = (0.5)² = 0.25**

**Sub-step ii:** Compute **1 − 0.25 = 0.75**

**Sub-step iii:** Compute **8mV₀/e = 8 × 9.109 × 10⁻³¹ × 2000 / 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹**

$$= \frac{8 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 2 \times 10^{3}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$= \frac{1.45744 \times 10^{-26}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 9.097 \times 10^{-8} \;\; \text{T}^2\cdot\text{m}^2$$

**Sub-step iv:** Compute **√(9.097 × 10⁻⁸) = 3.016 × 10⁻⁴ T·m**

**Sub-step v:** Compute denominator **r_a √0.75 = 0.01 × 0.8660 = 8.660 × 10⁻³ m**

**Sub-step vi:** Final result:

$$B_c = \frac{3.016 \times 10^{-4}}{8.660 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0348 \;\; \text{T} \approx 34.8 \;\; \text{mT}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.05}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}}$$

$$= \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-21}}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}} = 8.79 \times 10^{9} \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{8.79 \times 10^{9}}{2\pi} = 1.40 \times 10^{9} \;\; \text{Hz} = 1.40 \;\; \text{GHz}$$

**Solution (c):**

Since **B = 0.05 T > B_c = 0.0348 T**, the magnetron is operating **above the Hull cutoff**. Anode current is theoretically zero in the static model. To produce oscillation, we must operate in the dynamic regime, satisfying the **Buneman–Hartree condition**:

$$B_0 = \frac{\sqrt{8mV_0/(e(1-r_c^2/r_a^2))}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (\omega/\omega_c)^2}}$$

with **ω = 2πf**, where **f** is the desired RF oscillation frequency. The denominator shows that as **ω → ω_c**, the required **B_0 → ∞** — this defines the *Hartree line* of oscillation thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Cylindrical Magnetron Architecture and Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC High-Voltage Source V0] -->|anode voltage| B[Anode Block at radius ra]
    C[Heated Filament Cathode at radius rc] -->|thermionic emission| D[Electron Cloud]
    E[External Solenoid or PM] -->|axial B field| F[Crossed E x B Region]
    G[Resonant Cavities N slots] -->|RF output| H[Output Coupler Waveguide]
    D --> F
    B --> F
    F -->|cycloidal motion| I[Electron Spokes Phase Bunching]
    I -->|induces RF currents| G
    G -->|pi mode oscillation| J[High Power Microwave Output]
    H --> J
    I -.energy transferred.-> G

    subgraph INSIDE_VACUUM_ENVELOPE
        C
        D
        B
        F
        G
        I
    end

    subgraph EXTERNAL_CIRCUIT
        A
        E
        H
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Cyclotron Angular Frequency Operating Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operating Point r_a V0 fixed] --> B{Magnetic Field B}
    B -->|B = 0| C[Radial electron flow, conventional diode]
    B -->|0 less than B less than Bc| D[Curved trajectories still reach anode]
    B -->|B equal to Bc Hull cutoff| E[Electron grazes anode, I_a approx 0]
    B -->|B greater than Bc| F[Electrons confined, magnetron cutoff]
    B -->|B greater than B0 Hartree threshold| G[Dynamic regime phase bunching]
    G -->|spoke rotates at sync speed| H[pi mode RF oscillation]
    H --> I[Antenna or Waveguide Output]
    F -->|anode current = 0| J[No useful RF output]

    subgraph CROSSED_FIELD_REGION
        C
        D
        E
        F
        G
        H
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Signal-Flow of Cyclotron Frequency Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[User Input: B in Tesla] --> K2[Lookup Physical Constant e and m]
    K2 --> K3[Compute omega_c = eB divided by m]
    K3 --> K4[Compute f_c = omega_c divided by 2 pi]
    K4 --> K5{Compare f_c with operating band}
    K5 -->|L band 1 to 2 GHz| K6[B approx 0.036 to 0.071 T]
    K5 -->|X band 8 to 12 GHz| K7[B approx 0.286 to 0.428 T]
    K5 -->|Ku band 12 to 18 GHz| K8[B approx 0.428 to 0.643 T]
    K6 --> K9[Output Cyclotron Frequency]
    K7 --> K9
    K8 --> K9
    K9 --> K10[Compare with Hull Cutoff B_c]
    K10 --> K11[Decide operating regime: below / at / above cutoff]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams are block-level, not physical:** Mermaid cannot accurately render concentric 3-D cylindrical structures with radial electric field lines. The block diagrams above capture the *functional* architecture — what feeds what, which regime applies, and how cyclotron frequency is determined from **B** — which is exactly what KTU 2024 examiners test.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Cyclotron Angular Frequency** of an electron. Derive its expression in terms of charge **e**, mass **m**, and magnetic flux density **B**.

**Model Answer:**
The cyclotron angular frequency is the natural angular velocity at which a free electron gyrates in a uniform magnetic field under the sole action of the magnetic Lorentz force. From Newton's second law applied to **m(dv/dt) = −e(v × B)**, the components give simple harmonic motion with angular frequency:

$$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m}$$

For **B = 0.1 T**, $\omega_c \approx 1.76 \times 10^{10}$ rad/s, corresponding to a cyclotron frequency of about **2.8 GHz** — squarely in the microwave region. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Hull cutoff condition** in a cylindrical magnetron? Write the expression for the Hull cutoff magnetic field.

**Model Answer:**
The Hull cutoff condition defines the *minimum* axial magnetic field **B_c** at which electrons emitted from the cathode just fail to reach the anode (anode current ≈ 0). It is found by combining energy conservation (½mv² = eV₀) with conservation of canonical angular momentum. The expression is:

$$B_c = \frac{\sqrt{8mV_0/e}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (r_c/r_a)^2}}$$

Equivalently, the Hull cutoff anode voltage is:

$$V_c = \frac{e r_a^2 B_c^2}{8m}\left(1 - \frac{r_c^2}{r_a^2}\right)^2$$

Operation **above B_c** (i.e., B > B_c) is required for magnetron oscillation. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 3 | 14 Marks]**
> **(A)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and principle of operation of a **cylindrical cavity magnetron**. Derive the Hull cutoff condition and the expression for the **cyclotron angular frequency**. Explain the role of the **π-mode** in efficient microwave generation. *(14 Marks)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Construction (≈ 3 Marks)**
- Cylindrical cathode (oxide-coated, indirectly heated) at the center.
- Anode block (OFHC copper for good thermal conductivity) with **N** re-entrant slot-and-hole cavities drilled around its inner periphery.
- Output waveguide coupled via a slot to one of the cavities.
- External permanent magnet or solenoid producing axial **B**.
- Vacuum envelope, cooling fins, and a getter to maintain vacuum.

**(b) Principle of Operation (≈ 3 Marks)**
- Electrons are thermionically emitted from the cathode.
- Radial DC E-field (from V₀) pulls them outward.
- Axial B-field (crossed-field) deflects them into cycloidal paths.
- Above the Hull cutoff, electrons bunch into rotating *spokes* due to RF fields of the cavities.
- The rotating spoke is phase-locked with the RF wave traveling around the anode.
- Energy is transferred from the spoke to the RF field; electrons finally return to the cathode.

**(c) Hull Cutoff Derivation (≈ 5 Marks)** — *see Section 3.2 above* for the full step-by-step derivation. Arrive at:

$$B_c = \frac{\sqrt{8mV_0/e}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (r_c/r_a)^2}}$$

**(d) Cyclotron Angular Frequency (≈ 2 Marks)** — *see Section 3.1 above*:

$$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m}$$

**(e) π-Mode (≈ 1 Mark)**
- Adjacent cavities oscillate 180° out of phase.
- Strongest mutual coupling through slot openings.
- Highest group velocity → most stable against mode-jumping.
- Highest efficiency (≈ 70–80%).

---

> **(B)** *Internal choice for the same 14 marks:*
> **(a)** Derive the **Hull cutoff magnetic field** for a cylindrical magnetron, clearly stating the assumptions. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** A cylindrical magnetron operates with **r_a = 8 mm**, **r_c = 3 mm**, **V₀ = 3000 V**, and **B = 0.12 T**. Compute the **Hull cutoff field B_c** and the **cyclotron frequency f_c**. Is the device operating above or below cutoff? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hull Cutoff Derivation (7 Marks)** — *refer to Section 3.2 above for the complete 8-step derivation.* Key steps to include in the answer:
1. State the two conservation laws (energy + canonical angular momentum).
2. Apply them between cathode (**r = r_c, v_θ = 0**) and anode grazing point (**r = r_a, v_r = 0**).
3. Combine to eliminate **v_θ**.
4. Solve for **B = B_c**.
5. Final boxed expression: **B_c = √(8mV₀/e) / [r_a √(1 − (r_c/r_a)²)]**.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio **r_c/r_a = 3/8 = 0.375**, so **(r_c/r_a)² = 0.1406** and **1 − 0.1406 = 0.8594**.

**Step 2:** Compute **8mV₀/e**:
$$8 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 3000 / 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 1.365 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{T}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Numerator: **√(1.365 × 10⁻⁷) = 3.694 × 10⁻⁴ T·m**

**Step 4:** Denominator: **r_a √(1 − (r_c/r_a)²) = 0.008 × √0.8594 = 0.008 × 0.9270 = 7.416 × 10⁻³ m**

**Step 5:** Hull cutoff:
$$B_c = \frac{3.694 \times 10^{-4}}{7.416 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0498 \;\; \text{T} \approx 49.8 \;\; \text{mT}$$

**Step 6:** Cyclotron angular frequency:
$$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.12}{9.109 \times 10^{-31}} = 2.11 \times 10^{10} \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 7:** Cyclotron frequency:
$$f_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{2.11 \times 10^{10}}{2\pi} = 3.36 \times 10^{9} \;\; \text{Hz} = 3.36 \;\; \text{GHz}$$

**Step 8:** Operating regime: **B = 0.12 T > B_c = 0.0498 T**, so the device is **operating above Hull cutoff** — necessary for magnetron (oscillator) operation, but it must also satisfy the **Buneman–Hartree threshold** for the chosen π-mode frequency to actually oscillate.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cylindrical Magnetron Problems (KTU Valuation)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the r_c/r_a correction:** Many students write **B_c = √(8mV₀/e) / r_a** — this is the *parallel-plate* (Cartesian) form, not the *cylindrical* form. The cylindrical magnetron requires the **√(1 − (r_c/r_a)²)** factor in the denominator. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Mixing ω_c with ω_m (modulating frequency):** ω_c is the *electron's* gyrofrequency; ω_m is the *RF modulating* frequency. They are related through the Buneman–Hartree equation but are not the same. Conflating them loses **1–2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Dropping the negative sign on −e:** The electron has charge −e. The Lorentz force is **−e(v × B)**, not **+e(v × B)**. Sign errors in derivation propagate and lose **1 mark** in the differential equation setup.
>
> 4. **Using e/m in C/kg vs e × m confusion:** Always keep the **e/m ratio (≈ 1.759 × 10¹¹ C/kg)** in one form; mixing e and m separately is error-prone.
>
> 5. **Numerical unit mistakes:** r_a must be in **meters** (not cm) when substituted into the SI Hull formula. A factor-of-100 error is the most common numerical mistake.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention operating regime:** KTU examiners explicitly award **1 mark** for the final statement "above cutoff / below cutoff / at cutoff". Don't skip this concluding remark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Module 2 — Magnetron & Cyclotron Frequency: 60-Second Recap**

- **Cylindrical Magnetron** = crossed-field (E radial, B axial) vacuum tube with **N** re-entrant anode cavities.
- **Cyclotron angular frequency** is **ω_c = eB/m**; *independent of velocity* — purely a function of **B**.
- **Cyclotron frequency** in Hz: **f_c = eB/(2πm) = 2.8 × 10¹⁰ · B [Hz]**, with B in Tesla.
- **Larmor (cyclotron) radius:** **r_L = v_⊥ / ω_c**.
- **Hull cutoff field (cylindrical):**
  $$B_c = \frac{\sqrt{8mV_0/e}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (r_c/r_a)^2}}$$
- **Equivalent Hull cutoff voltage:**
  $$V_c = \frac{e r_a^2 B_c^2}{8m}\left(1 - \frac{r_c^2}{r_a^2}\right)^2$$
- **Operating regimes:** B < B_c → current flows (diode-like); B = B_c → grazing cutoff; B > B_c → electrons confined (magnetron mode).
- **Two conservation laws** drive the Hull derivation: **kinetic energy** (½mv² = eV₀) and **canonical angular momentum** (mrv_θ − ½eBr² = const).
- **Buneman–Hartree threshold** governs the *dynamic* oscillation condition (the Hartree line):
  $$B_0 = \frac{\sqrt{8mV_0/[e(1-r_c^2/r_a^2)]}}{r_a \sqrt{1 - (\omega/\omega_c)^2}}$$
- **π-mode** is the workhorse mode: 180° phase shift between adjacent cavities, highest efficiency (70–80%), most stable.
- **Electron spokes** form by phase-focusing in the crossed-field region; they rotate at the synchronous angular velocity **ω = ω_m** of the RF wave.
- **Sign convention:** Force on electron = **−e(E + v × B)**; do not drop the negative sign.
- **Numerical value to memorize:** **e/m = 1.759 × 10¹¹ C/kg**.
- **Real-world link:** Microwave oven (2.45 GHz), radar (X-band 8–12 GHz), satellite communication uplinks (C-band 4–8 GHz).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Measurements: Measurement of Power, VSWR, Frequency, Wavelength, Insertion Loss, Impedance and Attenuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Microwave Measurements: Measurement of Power, VSWR, Frequency, Wavelength, Insertion Loss, Impedance and Attenuation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Microwave Measurements — The Formal Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PCECT602), **Microwave Measurements** refers to the standardized experimental techniques used to quantify the physical and electrical properties of signals and components operating in the **microwave frequency range** (typically **300 MHz to 300 GHz**, corresponding to free-space wavelengths from **1 m to 1 mm**). Because conventional lumped-element test instruments (voltmeters, ammeters) fail at these frequencies due to parasitic reactances, radiation losses, and transmission-line effects, specialized methods are employed. The seven major measurement categories in this module are: 1. **Power Measurement** — quantifying the energy delivered per unit time. 2. **VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio) Measurement** — characterizing the mismatch between a source and a load. 3. **Frequency Measurement** — determining the spectral location of the signal. 4. **Wavelength Measurement** — finding the spatial periodicity of the wave. 5. **Insertion Loss** — quantifying the power lost when a device is inserted into a transmission line. 6. **Impedance Measurement** — finding the complex load impedance presented to a line. 7. **Attenuation Measurement** — measuring the deliberate reduction of signal amplitude. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Measurement of **Power, VSWR, Frequency, Wavelength, Insertion Loss, Impedance, and Attenuation** constitutes a high-weightage module (typically 12–15 % of the entire syllabus), and is consistently tested as either a full 14-mark question or a pair of 7-mark sub-parts in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the end of a long swimming pool and shouting toward the opposite wall: - **Power** is how loud your shout is (measured with a sound-level meter at the wall). - **VSWR** is the difference between the loudest and quietest spots along the pool's edge — caused by the echo bouncing back and interfering with your voice. - **Frequency** is whether you are shouting a low, deep "BOOO" or a high-pitched "HEEE". - **Wavelength** is the physical distance between two consecutive crests of that sound wave in the pool. - **Insertion Loss** is the muffling effect of a foam wall you place across the pool. - **Impedance** is how much the opposite wall "pushes back" against your shout. - **Attenuation** is the gradual fading of your voice as it travels through water. At microwave frequencies, the "shout" becomes an electromagnetic wave, the "pool" becomes a **waveguide or coaxial line**, and the "echo" becomes a **reflected wave** that produces standing waves. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:** > - Speed of light in free space: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** > - Intrinsic impedance of free space: **η₀ = 377 Ω ≈ 120π Ω** > - Characteristic impedance of standard waveguide: **Z₀ = 50 Ω (coaxial)** or **TE₁₀-mode waveguide impedance** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Microwave Power Levels Microwave power measurement is divided by the magnitude being measured: | Power Regime | Range | Typical Instruments | |--------------|-------|---------------------| | **Low Power** | < 10 mW | Bolometer (Barretter), Thermistor, Thermocouple | | **Medium Power** | 10 mW – 1 W | Calorimetric with dummy load, bridge circuits | | **High Power** | > 1 W | Water calorimeter, Dry calorimeter (flow type) | ### 2.1.1 Bolometer Method (Barretter) A **bolometer** is a small temperature-sensitive resistive element (typically a platinum or tungsten wire) whose resistance varies with the dissipated microwave power. It is placed in a **Wheatstone bridge** that is initially balanced with DC bias. When microwave power is applied, the bolometer's resistance changes, unbalancing the bridge. The DC bias power is then reduced by ΔP until the bridge re-balances — this ΔP is the microwave power absorbed. ### 2.1.2 Thermistor Method A **thermistor** (negative temperature coefficient bead) replaces the barretter. It is preferred for low-power (< 1 mW) measurements because of higher sensitivity. ### 2.1.3 Calorimetric Method (High Power) The microwave power is dissipated in a known load (water or oil), and the resulting temperature rise is measured. ## 2.2 The Slotted Line — The Master Instrument The **slotted line** is the single most important instrument in this entire module. It is a section of waveguide (or rigid coaxial line) with a precision longitudinal slot through which a small **electric-field probe** is inserted. The probe samples the E-field, and a crystal detector + VSWR meter measures the relative amplitude as the probe slides along the slot. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why slotted lines dominate KTU questions:** Every measurement of VSWR, impedance, frequency, and wavelength can be performed with a slotted line setup. It is the universal microwave testbench. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Microwave Power (Calorimetric) | $P = \dfrac{m \, c \, \Delta T}{t}$ | W (mass m in kg, c = 4186 J/kg·°C for water) | | 2 | VSWR | $S = \dfrac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | Dimensionless, always ≥ 1 | | 3 | Reflection Coefficient Magnitude | $\vert \Gamma \vert = \dfrac{S - 1}{S + 1}$ | $0 \le \vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ | | 4 | Return Loss | $R_L = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB (always ≥ 0) | | 5 | Guide Wavelength | $\lambda_g = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{f_c}{f}\right)^2}}$ | meters | | 6 | Free-Space Wavelength | $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | meters | | 7 | Cut-off Wavelength (TE₁₀) | $\lambda_c = 2a$ | meters (a = broad wall dimension) | | 8 | Slotted-Line Frequency | $f = \dfrac{c}{\lambda_0}$, with $\lambda_0 = \lambda_g \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ | Hz | | 9 | Insertion Loss | $IL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ | dB | | 10 | Load Impedance from VSWR | $Z_L = Z_0 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ (matched line) | Ω | | 11 | Attenuation | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | dB | | 12 | Double-Minimum Shift | $Z_L = Z_0 \dfrac{1 - j \, S \tan(\beta d)}{S - j \tan(\beta d)}$ | Ω (d = shift of V_min from load) | | 13 | Bolometer Substitution | $P_{\mu} = P_{DC1} - P_{DC2}$ | W (DC bias difference) | | 14 | Standing Wave Detector Output | $V_{\text{det}} \propto E^2$ | V (square-law detector) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **dB** units. Insertion loss and attenuation use **10 log(P-ratio)**, while voltage-ratio quantities (reflection coefficient, return loss) use **20 log(V-ratio)**. Students routinely lose 2–3 marks here. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **VSWR measurement** is the universal health check for radar antennas, satellite transponders, and base-station feeds. - **Power measurement** at microwaves is critical for radar safety, EMI/EMC compliance, and link-budget verification in 5G/6G networks. - **Insertion loss** of filters and diplexers determines the noise figure and selectivity of receivers. - **Attenuation measurement** is fundamental in calibrating variable attenuators used in vector network analyzers (VNAs). - **Frequency/wavelength measurement** underpins the design of cavity filters, wavemeters, and microwave oscillators. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — VSWR from Reflection Coefficient Starting from the boundary condition at a load mismatch, the voltage on a lossless transmission line is the sum of an incident wave $V^+ e^{-j\beta z}$ and a reflected wave $V^- e^{+j\beta z}$: $$ V(z) = V^+ e^{-j\beta z} + \Gamma \, V^+ e^{+j\beta z} $$ The reflection coefficient at the load (z = 0) is: $$ \Gamma = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j \phi} $$ Take the magnitude squared of V(z): $$ \vert V(z) \vert^2 = \vert V^+ \vert^2 \left[ 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2 \vert \Gamma \vert \cos(2\beta z - \phi) \right] $$ **Step 1 — Identify the maximum.** The cosine term is maximum (= +1) when $2\beta z - \phi = 0$: $$ V_{\max} = \vert V^+ \vert \left(1 + \vert \Gamma \vert \right) $$ **Step 2 — Identify the minimum.** The cosine term is minimum (= −1) when $2\beta z - \phi = \pi$: $$ V_{\min} = \vert V^+ \vert \left(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert \right) $$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio (this is the definition of VSWR):** $$ S = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\vert \Gamma \vert$ in terms of S:** $$ S(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert $$ $$ S - S\vert \Gamma \vert = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert $$ $$ S - 1 = \vert \Gamma \vert (S + 1) $$ $$ \boxed{\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1}} $$ This is the form you will use in **every** KTU numerical on VSWR. ## 3.2 Derivation — Guide Wavelength in a Rectangular Waveguide The phase velocity in a hollow waveguide exceeds c, and the wavelength along the guide is correspondingly stretched. Starting from the **transcendental relation** between free-space, cutoff, and guided wavelengths: $$ \frac{1}{\lambda_g^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda_0^2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_c^2} $$ **Step 1 — Invert both sides:** $$ \lambda_g^2 = \frac{\lambda_0^2 \, \lambda_c^2}{\lambda_c^2 - \lambda_0^2} $$ **Step 2 — Factor out $\lambda_0^2$ in the denominator:** $$ \lambda_g^2 = \frac{\lambda_0^2}{1 - \dfrac{\lambda_0^2}{\lambda_c^2}} $$ **Step 3 — Take the square root:** $$ \boxed{\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\lambda_0}{\lambda_c}\right)^2}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{f_c}{f}\right)^2}}} $$ **Step 4 — Special case for the dominant TE₁₀ mode of a rectangular waveguide** (where a is the broad-wall dimension): $$ \lambda_c = 2a \quad \text{and} \quad f_c = \frac{c}{2a} $$ Substitute to obtain the most common KTU form: $$ \lambda_g = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\lambda_0}{2a}\right)^2}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation — Calorimetric Power Measurement (High Power) A water-flow calorimeter uses a flowing water stream that absorbs the microwave power in a matched load. Apply **conservation of energy** — the microwave power dissipated equals the rate of heat gained by the water: $$ P_{\mu} = \dot{m} \, c \, \Delta T $$ where: - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of water (kg/s) = mass $m$ divided by collection time $t$ - $c$ = specific heat of water = **4186 J/(kg·°C)** - $\Delta T$ = temperature rise across the load (°C or K) **Step 1 — If water is collected for time t and mass m is measured:** $$ \dot{m} = \frac{m}{t} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute back:** $$ P_{\mu} = \frac{m}{t} \, c \, \Delta T $$ **Step 3 — In terms of volumetric flow rate Q (litres/s) and density ρ (kg/m³):** $$ P_{\mu} = \rho \, Q \, c \, \Delta T $$ **Sample KTU Numerical Setup:** If 2 kg of water is collected in 60 s with a temperature rise of 5.7 °C, then: $$ P_{\mu} = \frac{2 \times 4186 \times 5.7}{60} = \frac{47720.4}{60} \approx 795.3 \; \text{W} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation — Double-Minimum Method for Impedance The **double-minimum method** is the KTU-favourite technique for measuring unknown load impedance using only a slotted line and a VSWR meter. The procedure is: **Step 1 — Measure VSWR (S) of the unknown load directly.** **Step 2 — Replace the unknown load with a movable short. Note the shift in voltage-minimum positions when the short is moved. From this, the guide wavelength $\lambda_g$ is found.** **Step 3 — Re-attach the unknown load. Measure the distance $d$ from the V_min of the unknown load to the nearest V_min obtained with the short.** **Step 4 — Apply the formula** (derivable from transmission-line theory at the V_min point): $$ Z_L = Z_0 \cdot \frac{1 - j \, S \tan(\beta d)}{S - j \tan(\beta d)} $$ where $\beta = 2\pi / \lambda_g$ is the phase constant inside the guide. **Step 5 — Normalize and read off the Smith chart** (or expand into R + jX). **Physical interpretation:** The double-minimum method is preferred over direct measurement of V_min position because the V_min is much sharper and easier to locate than the V_max, especially for high VSWR (S > 10). ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Measurement Techniques | Quantity | Primary Method | Secondary Method | Instrument Used | |----------|---------------|------------------|-----------------| | Low Power (< 10 mW) | Bolometer bridge | Thermistor bridge | Wheatstone bridge + galvanometer | | High Power (> 1 W) | Water calorimeter | Dry calorimeter (oil) | Thermometer, flow meter | | VSWR | Slotted line | Reflectometer, VNA | Crystal detector + VSWR meter | | Frequency | Cavity wavemeter | Slotted-line λ-measurement | Tunable cavity, micrometer | | Wavelength | Slotted line + short | — | Slotted line with detector | | Insertion Loss | Substitution (RF / IF) | Power ratio | Signal generator, attenuator | | Impedance | Slotted line (Smith chart) | VNA, reflectometer | Slotted line + probe | | Attenuation | RF/IF/Audio substitution | Power ratio | Calibrated attenuator | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Microwave Testbench (Slotted Line Setup) The block diagram below represents the standard bench arrangement used for **VSWR, impedance, frequency, and wavelength** measurement in the KTU laboratory. Every student is expected to draw this from memory. ```mermaid graph LR A[Microwave Source
Klystron or Gunn Oscillator] --> B[Isolator or Ferrite Circulator] B --> C[Frequency Meter
Cavity Wavemeter] C --> D[Variable Attenuator] D --> E[Slotted Section
with Movable Probe] E --> F[Crystal Detector Mount] F --> G[VSWR Meter or
Standing Wave Indicator] E --> H[Matched Termination
or Unknown Load] I[Power Supply
and Modulation] -.-> A style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Flow logic of the diagram:** - Source → Isolator (protects source from reflections) → Frequency meter (pre-set λ) → Attenuator (level control) → Slotted line (probe samples E-field) → Detector → VSWR meter display. - The load (matched or unknown) terminates the slotted section. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Calorimetric High-Power Measurement ```mermaid graph TD A[High Power Microwave Source] --> B[Directional Coupler] B --> C[Water-Flow Calorimeter Load] C --> D[Thermometer T1 at Inlet] C --> E[Thermometer T2 at Outlet] D --> F[Flow Meter and
Collection Tank] E --> F F --> G[Compute Power
P = m c ΔT / t] style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Insertion Loss / Attenuation Measurement (RF Substitution Method) ```mermaid graph LR A[Signal Generator] --> B[Precision Calibrated
Attenator A1] B --> C[DUT Device
Under Test] C --> D[Detector] D --> E[Indicator dB Meter] F[Reference Path
Without DUT] --> E style A fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Operating principle:** With the DUT in, the calibrated attenuator is adjusted to restore the same detector reading as the reference. The change in attenuator reading (in dB) equals the **insertion loss** of the DUT. ## 4.4 Schematic Logic — Standing Wave Formation on a Slotted Line ```mermaid graph LR A[Incident Wave
V+ travels towards load] --> B[Load Impedance Mismatch] B --> C[Reflected Wave
V- travels back to source] C --> D[Superposition of V+ and V-] D --> E[Standing Wave Pattern
Vmax and Vmin along the line] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` **Why it matters:** The position and ratio of V_max to V_min give, respectively, the **phase of the reflection coefficient** (and hence load impedance) and the **magnitude of the reflection coefficient** (and hence VSWR). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define VSWR. Show that VSWR = (1 + |Γ|) / (1 − |Γ|).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR) is defined as the ratio of the maximum to minimum voltage amplitudes on a transmission line in the presence of standing waves: $$ S = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}} $$ **Derivation (2 marks):** Consider a lossless line terminated by a load with reflection coefficient $\Gamma = \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j\phi}$. The total voltage is: $$ V(z) = V^+ e^{-j\beta z} + \Gamma V^+ e^{+j\beta z} $$ At $V_{\max}$: $\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$ At $V_{\min}$: $\vert V \vert_{\min} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$ Dividing: $$ \boxed{S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}} $$ --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **With a neat block diagram, explain the measurement of microwave power using a bolometer.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **bolometer (barretter)** is a temperature-sensitive resistive element whose resistance changes when microwave power is dissipated in it. The bolometer is placed in one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge** initially balanced by a DC bias. **Block diagram (1 mark):** ``` Microwave Source → Isolator → Bolometer (in bridge arm) → Matched Load | +--- DC Bias Supply (Wheatstone bridge) | +--- Galvanometer / null detector ``` **Working (2 marks):** When microwave power is applied, the bolometer's resistance changes, unbalancing the bridge. The DC bias power is then reduced by $\Delta P$ until balance is restored. The **substituted DC power** $\Delta P$ equals the absorbed microwave power. Thermistors and barretters are the two common bolometer types. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice (OR). Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR a single 14-mark question with multiple steps. ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the setup for measuring VSWR and impedance using a slotted line. Derive the expression for the reflection coefficient in terms of VSWR.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. Microwave source → isolator → frequency meter → attenuator → slotted line with probe and detector → VSWR meter, with unknown load terminating the line. **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Energize the source and tune to the desired frequency using the cavity wavemeter. 2. Slide the probe along the slotted section and record $V_{\max}$ and $V_{\min}$ from the VSWR meter. 3. Compute $S = V_{\max} / V_{\min}$. 4. Mark the position of $V_{\min}$ and the next $V_{\min}$ (distance = $\lambda_g / 2$). 5. Replace the load with a movable short; the V_min shift gives the phase reference. **Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from the standing-wave voltage expression: $$ V(z) = V^+ \left( e^{-j\beta z} + \vert \Gamma \vert e^{+j(\beta z + \phi)} \right) $$ The maxima and minima occur at $\cos(2\beta z - \phi) = \pm 1$, giving: $$ V_{\max} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert), \quad V_{\min} = \vert V^+ \vert (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) $$ Dividing and solving: $$ \boxed{\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1}} $$ This is the magnitude of the reflection coefficient. The phase is obtained from the position of the V_min. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **A 10 GHz signal is propagated through a rectangular waveguide with broad wall a = 2.286 cm. Calculate (i) the cutoff frequency, (ii) the cutoff wavelength, (iii) the guide wavelength, and (iv) the phase velocity.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $f = 10$ GHz = $10 \times 10^9$ Hz, $a = 2.286$ cm = $0.02286$ m, dominant mode TE₁₀. **Step 1 (1 mark) — Cutoff frequency for TE₁₀:** $$ f_c = \frac{c}{2a} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.02286} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.04572} \approx 6.562 \text{ GHz} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark) — Cutoff wavelength:** $$ \lambda_c = 2a = 2 \times 0.02286 = 0.04572 \text{ m} = 4.572 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks) — Free-space wavelength:** $$ \lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Guide wavelength:** $$ \lambda_g = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{1 - (6.562/10)^2}} $$ $$ = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{1 - 0.4306}} = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{0.5694}} = \frac{0.03}{0.7546} \approx 0.03976 \text{ m} \approx 3.976 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark) — Phase velocity:** $$ v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.7546} \approx 3.976 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} $$ **Summary box (final 1 mark):** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $f_c$ | 6.562 GHz | | $\lambda_c$ | 4.572 cm | | $\lambda_0$ | 3.0 cm | | $\lambda_g$ | 3.976 cm | | $v_p$ | 3.976 × 10⁸ m/s | --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2021] **Explain the RF substitution method for measuring insertion loss of a microwave component. Mention its advantages.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Concept (2 marks):** The **insertion loss (IL)** of a device under test (DUT) is defined as the ratio of power delivered to the load with the DUT inserted to the power delivered to the load without the DUT: $$ IL = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_{\text{without DUT}}}{P_{\text{with DUT}}} \right) \; \text{dB} $$ **RF Substitution Method (3 marks):** 1. Connect the microwave source to the detector through a **calibrated variable attenuator** $A_1$ (no DUT in line). Note the detector reading $I_0$. 2. Insert the **DUT** between the source and the detector. 3. The detector reading drops to $I_1$ because the DUT has absorbed/transmitted less power. 4. **Remove the DUT** and instead **increase the calibrated attenuator** $A_1$ by $\Delta A$ dB until the detector reads $I_1$ again. 5. The value of $\Delta A$ in dB is the **insertion loss** of the DUT. **Block diagram reference:** Section 4.3. **Advantages (2 marks):** - High accuracy (depends only on the calibrated attenuator). - Independent of detector law (square-law or linear). - Wide dynamic range. - Suitable for both narrow-band and broadband DUTs. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: [KTU University Exam — June 2022] **Describe the working of a cavity wavemeter. How is it used to measure the frequency of a microwave signal?** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Construction (2 marks):** A **cavity wavemeter** is a high-Q cylindrical or rectangular resonant cavity with a movable short-circuit plunger (or tuning screw). A small **input iris** couples microwave energy from the source into the cavity, and a **detector output** monitors the cavity response. The cavity is tuned by adjusting the plunger position, calibrated directly in frequency. **Working (3 marks):** When the microwave source frequency matches the cavity's resonant frequency $f_r$, energy is absorbed by the cavity and the detector shows a sharp **dip (or peak)** in the output meter. The plunger position is read against the calibrated scale, giving the frequency directly. Resonant frequency formula for TE₁₁₁ cylindrical mode: $$ f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{ \left( \frac{2.62}{a} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\pi}{h} \right)^2 } $$ where $a$ is the cavity radius and $h$ is its height. **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Couple the wavemeter loosely to the microwave line. 2. Sweep the plunger slowly across the operating band. 3. At the resonant dip, read the calibrated micrometer. 4. Convert the plunger reading to frequency using the calibration chart. **Advantages:** High Q (typically 5000–50,000), excellent frequency resolution, and direct digital readout. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** a = 2.286 cm must be converted to metres before using in $c/(2a)$. > 2. **Confusing dB conventions:** Insertion loss uses **10 log**, voltage ratios use **20 log**. > 3. **Wrong sign in return loss:** RL = −20 log|Γ| is always non-negative; do not drop the minus. > 4. **Slotted-line questions:** Always state the dominant mode (TE₁₀) and identify whether the line is waveguide or coaxial before plugging into $\lambda_g$ formula. > 5. **Bolometer questions:** The microwave power is the **difference** of DC biases, not the final DC bias. > 6. **Missing block diagram:** A 7-mark question without a labelled block diagram loses at least 2 marks. > 7. **Cavity wavemeter:** Forgetting to mention **coupling (loose/through iris)** causes loss of 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **VSWR = V_max / V_min = (1 + |Γ|)/(1 − |Γ|).** This is THE formula. - ⭐ **Reflection coefficient magnitude:** $\vert \Gamma \vert = (S − 1)/(S + 1)$. - ⭐ **Return loss:** $R_L = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ dB, always ≥ 0. - ⭐ **Guide wavelength:** $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — only valid for $f > f_c$. - ⭐ **Cutoff wavelength for TE₁₀:** $\lambda_c = 2a$. - ⭐ **Calorimetric power:** $P = m c \Delta T / t$; c = 4186 J/(kg·°C) for water. - ⭐ **Bolometer:** P_microwave = (DC bias before) − (DC bias after re-balancing). - ⭐ **Slotted line** measures V_max, V_min, and V_min position → gives VSWR, |Γ|, phase, and $\lambda_g$. - ⭐ **Double-minimum method** is preferred for measuring unknown impedance from a slotted line. - ⭐ **Cavity wavemeter** gives frequency from the plunger position at the resonance dip. - ⭐ **Insertion loss & attenuation** in dB: use **10 log(P-ratio)**. - ⭐ **Voltage quantities in dB:** use **20 log(V-ratio)**. - ⭐ **Three attenuation-measurement methods:** RF substitution, IF substitution, and audio substitution. - ⭐ **Standard testbench components:** Source → Isolator → Frequency meter → Attenuator → Slotted line → Detector → VSWR meter → Load. - ⭐ **Real-world link:** All these measurements are the daily bread of **vector network analyser (VNA)** calibration, **antenna range testing**, and **radar cross-section measurement**. ---

topics.title: Basic Concept of Network Analyzer and Anechoic Chamber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Network Analyzer and Anechoic Chamber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)

A **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA)** is a precision electronic test and measurement instrument used in the microwave and radio-frequency (RF) domain that characterizes the linear behavior of electrical networks by measuring both the **magnitude** and the **phase** of transmitted and reflected signals at its calibrated test ports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Vector"?** Unlike a scalar analyzer (which only measures amplitude), the VNA measures the **complex (vector) scattering parameters (S-parameters)** of a Device Under Test (DUT). Phase information is what allows engineers to fully characterize how a network transforms an input signal.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine shouting into a long, twisted hallway and listening to the echoes. By analyzing how the echo returns to you (loud? soft? quick? delayed?), you can deduce the **shape, size, and material** of the hallway. A VNA does the same thing at the speed of light:
- It sends a known microwave "shout" (stimulus signal) into the DUT.
- It listens to what comes back (**reflection**) and what passes through (**transmission**).
- The ratio of these waves forms the **S-parameters**.

## 1.2 Anechoic Chamber

An **Anechoic Chamber** is a specially engineered enclosed room designed to completely absorb electromagnetic (EM) reflections from its internal walls, floor, and ceiling, thereby simulating **free-space** (infinite, reflection-free) propagation conditions inside a finite, controlled laboratory environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **An-echoic = "No-echo."** The Greek root *an-* (without) + *ēkhō* (echo). It is the EM equivalent of an optical darkroom or an anechoic audio chamber used for speaker testing.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture an indoor swimming pool where the walls, floor, and ceiling are covered in soft, black, pointy foam. If you clap your hands inside, the sound dies instantly — no echoes, no reverberation. The electromagnetic version of that foam (typically **carbon-loaded polyurethane pyramidal foam**) covers the walls of an anechoic chamber. Combined with a **Faraday-cage** of metal shielding, the chamber becomes a "perfectly empty" bubble of space where engineers can accurately test antennas without interference from outside signals or indoor reflections.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Frequency Range of VNA:** Modern VNAs cover **10 MHz to 110 GHz** (mm-wave extenders push this beyond 1 THz).
- **Dynamic Range:** Typically **> 100 dB** (i.e., can measure signals 1 part in 10¹⁰ of the input).
- **Chamber Absorber Reflectivity:** Modern foam absorbers achieve **< –50 dB** reflectivity at the design frequency.
- **Shielding Effectiveness:** RF-shielded rooms provide **> 100 dB** of external signal isolation.
- **Far-Field Boundary (Fraunhofer Distance):** $R = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$, where $D$ is the largest antenna dimension.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a VNA

A modern VNA is built around four internal sub-systems:

1. **Stimulus / Source Block:** A synthesized sweep generator produces a known, stable RF signal (typically from **10 MHz** to tens of GHz). The signal is split into two paths.
2. **Signal-Separation Block:** A directional coupler or bridge separates the incident wave ($a_n$) from the reflected wave ($b_n$) at each test port. This is the heart of the VNA — without it, we could not isolate reflections.
3. **Receiver / Detector Block:** Tuned, narrowband receivers down-convert and digitize the separated waves using mixers and ADCs. Vector (I/Q) detection preserves both magnitude and phase.
4. **Display / Processing Block:** A digital processor computes S-parameters in real time and displays them as **rectangular** (real/imaginary) or **polar/Smith-chart** formats.

### Why S-Parameters and not Z, Y, or ABCD?
At microwave frequencies, **open-circuit** and **short-circuit** conditions are practically impossible — they radiate, and leads have parasitic inductance/capacitance. Therefore, Z, Y, and ABCD parameters are unmeasurable above ~1 GHz. S-parameters solve this by using **matched 50 Ω terminations** and measuring travelling waves directly.

## 2.2 S-Parameter Definitions (Two-Port Network)

For a two-port network, the four S-parameters are defined as ratios of outgoing to incoming voltage waves:

$$S_{11} = \left.\frac{b_1}{a_1}\right|_{a_2 = 0} \quad \text{(Input Reflection Coefficient)}$$

$$S_{21} = \left.\frac{b_2}{a_1}\right|_{a_2 = 0} \quad \text{(Forward Transmission / Gain)}$$

$$S_{12} = \left.\frac{b_1}{a_2}\right|_{a_1 = 0} \quad \text{(Reverse Transmission / Isolation)}$$

$$S_{22} = \left.\frac{b_2}{a_2}\right|_{a_1 = 0} \quad \text{(Output Reflection Coefficient)}$$

The condition $a_2 = 0$ means **port 2 is terminated in a matched 50 Ω load**, ensuring no reflection returns from that port.

## 2.3 Anechoic Chamber — Engineering Deep-Dive

An anechoic chamber is **two engineering systems fused into one room**:

### A. The RF-Shielded Enclosure (Faraday Cage)
A welded steel or copper-clad shell that:
- Blocks external RFI/EMI from corrupting measurements.
- Prevents the chamber's internal test signal from escaping (regulatory compliance for EMC testing).
- Typical shielding effectiveness: **> 100 dB** from 1 MHz to 18 GHz.

### B. The Anechoic Interior
The walls, floor, and ceiling are lined with **electromagnetic absorbers**, primarily:
- **Pyramidal Foam Absorbers** (carbon-loaded polyurethane): Operate by gradually tapering the impedance of free space (377 Ω) down to that of the lossy material, forcing the wave to be absorbed with minimal reflection.
- **Ferrite Tile Absorbers** (for low frequencies, < 1 GHz): Operate on magnetic-loss principles.
- **Hybrid Ferrite + Foam** walls: Cover the entire 30 MHz – 40 GHz range.

### Quiet Zone
The **Quiet Zone** is the cylindrical volume within the chamber over which the field distribution approximates a plane wave within a specified amplitude/phase tolerance (typically ±0.5 dB amplitude, ±5° phase). The **Antenna Under Test (AUT)** must be placed entirely within this zone for valid far-field measurement.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|----------|---------|---------------|
| Input Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma_{in} = S_{11}$ | Dimensionless complex number |
| Return Loss | $RL = -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert$ | **dB** (always positive) |
| Insertion Loss | $IL = -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{21} \vert$ | **dB** (positive for loss) |
| VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1+\vert S_{11} \vert}{1-\vert S_{11} \vert}$ | Ratio (≥ 1) |
| Transducer Power Gain | $G_T = \dfrac{\vert S_{21} \vert^2 (1-\vert \Gamma_S \vert^2)(1-\vert \Gamma_L \vert^2)}{\vert (1-S_{11}\Gamma_S)(1-S_{22}\Gamma_L)-S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_S\Gamma_L \vert^2}$ | Dimensionless |
| Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance | $R_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | **meters** |
| Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$ | **dB** |
| Absorber Reflectivity | $R = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_{ref}}{E_{inc}}\right)$ | **dB** (negative) |
| Shielding Effectiveness | $SE = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{inc}}{P_{trans}}\right)$ | **dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All logarithms above are **base-10** since the results are expressed in **decibels (dB)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VNAs** are indispensable in designing 5G/6G handset antennas, satellite transponders, radar front-ends, and filter fabrication.
- **Anechoic chambers** are mandatory in every accredited antenna measurement lab and EMC compliance test house (e.g., BIS, FCC, CE-marked product certification).
- Together, they form the **gold-standard validation chain**: the VNA measures the S-parameters of a device, and the anechoic chamber validates that the antenna built from that device actually performs as predicted.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: S-Parameters from Voltage Wave Definitions

At high frequencies, voltage and current are ill-defined distributed quantities. We instead work with **normalized travelling voltage waves**.

### Step 1 — Define Incident and Reflected Waves
For a port terminated in a reference impedance $Z_0$ (typically 50 Ω), we define the **incident wave** $a_n$ and the **reflected wave** $b_n$ at port $n$ as:

$$a_n = \frac{V_n^{+} }{\sqrt{Z_0}} = \frac{V_n + I_n Z_0}{2\sqrt{Z_0}}$$

$$b_n = \frac{V_n^{-}}{\sqrt{Z_0}} = \frac{V_n - I_n Z_0}{2\sqrt{Z_0}}$$

**Reasoning:** The linear combination $V + IZ_0$ extracts the wave travelling **toward** the port, while $V - IZ_0$ extracts the wave travelling **away** from it.

### Step 2 — Express in Matrix Form
For a two-port network, by linearity:

$$\begin{aligned} b_1 &= S_{11}\, a_1 + S_{12}\, a_2 \\
b_2 &= S_{21}\, a_1 + S_{22}\, a_2
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Reflection Coefficient
If port 2 is matched (so $a_2 = 0$), then:

$$b_1 = S_{11}\, a_1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S_{11} = \frac{b_1}{a_1} = \Gamma_{in}$$

This is the complex reflection coefficient at the input port. Its magnitude is bounded by $\vert S_{11} \vert \le 1$ for a passive network.

### Step 4 — Derive Return Loss from $S_{11}$
By definition, return loss (in dB) is:

$$\begin{aligned} RL &= -20 \log_{10} \vert S_{11} \vert \\
&= -20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{b_1}{a_1} \right\vert
\end{aligned}$$

For a perfectly matched load, $\vert S_{11} \vert = 0$, giving $RL \to \infty$ dB (perfect absorption). For a perfect open or short circuit, $\vert S_{11} \vert = 1$, giving $RL = 0$ dB (total reflection).

### Step 5 — Derive VSWR from $S_{11}$
The standing-wave ratio relates to the reflection coefficient by:

$$VSWR = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} = \frac{1 + \vert S_{11} \vert}{1 - \vert S_{11} \vert}$$

**Derivation logic:** The maximum and minimum voltages on a lossless line are $V_{max} = V^{+}(1+\vert\Gamma\vert)$ and $V_{min} = V^{+}(1-\vert\Gamma\vert)$. Their ratio yields the formula.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance

The far-field boundary is set by requiring the **maximum phase error** between a spherical wavefront and its plane-wave approximation to be no more than $\pi/8$ rad (22.5°).

### Step 1 — Geometry
Consider the AUT of largest dimension $D$ radiating from origin. The center of the aperture is at distance $R$. The edge of the aperture is at distance $R_{edge}$:

$$R_{edge} = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2}$$

### Step 2 — Phase-Error Constraint
The extra path length to the edge must satisfy:

$$R_{edge} - R \le \frac{\lambda}{16}$$

(The $\lambda/16$ rule corresponds to a phase error of $2\pi \cdot \lambda/16 / \lambda = \pi/8$ rad.)

### Step 3 — Apply Binomial Approximation
For $R \gg D$, expand $R_{edge}$ using Taylor series:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{edge} &= R \sqrt{1 + \frac{D^2}{4R^2}} \\
&\approx R \left(1 + \frac{D^2}{8R^2}\right) \\
&= R + \frac{D^2}{8R}
\end{aligned}$$

So the extra path is $\Delta R = \dfrac{D^2}{8R}$.

### Step 4 — Solve for R
Set $\dfrac{D^2}{8R} = \dfrac{\lambda}{16}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{D^2}{8R} &= \frac{\lambda}{16} \\
R &= \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Fraunhofer distance**, the minimum separation required between AUT and probe antenna in the anechoic chamber for valid far-field measurement.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A microwave test uses a VNA to characterize a horn antenna of aperture $D = 30$ cm operating at $f = 10$ GHz. Compute the minimum chamber length required for far-field measurement, and the return loss if $S_{11} = 0.1 \, \angle\!-\!45^{\circ}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Wavelength
$\lambda = c / f = (3 \times 10^8) / (10 \times 10^9) = 0.03$ m = **3 cm**.

### Step 2 — Apply Fraunhofer Formula
$R_{ff} = 2D^2 / \lambda = 2(0.3)^2 / 0.03 = 2(0.09)/0.03 = \mathbf{6\ m}$.

### Step 3 — Compute Return Loss
$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.1) = -20(-1) = \mathbf{20\ dB}$.

### Step 4 — Compute VSWR
$VSWR = (1+0.1)/(1-0.1) = 1.1/0.9 = \mathbf{1.222}$.

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
- The anechoic chamber must be at least **6 m** long (between probe and AUT), with extra room for the probe's own far-field distance and absorber lining.
- A return loss of **20 dB** indicates that 99% of the incident power is absorbed by the antenna; only 1% is reflected. This is excellent for most radar and communication antennas (typical spec ≥ 10 dB).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Two-Port Vector Network Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["STIMULUS SOURCE\nSYNTHESIZED SWEEP"] --> sp1["POWER SPLITTER"]
    sp1 --> ref["REFERENCE RECEIVER\nR1"]
```
