```mermaid
    sp1 --> port1["TEST PORT 1\nDIRECTIONAL COUPLER"]
    sp1 --> port2["TEST PORT 2\nDIRECTIONAL COUPLER"]
    port1 --> dut["DEVICE UNDER TEST\nDUT"]
    dut --> port2
    port1 --> a1["A RECEIVER\nMEASURES INCIDENT WAVE"]
    port1 --> b1["B RECEIVER\nMEASURES REFLECTED WAVE"]
    port2 --> a2["A RECEIVER\nMEASURES INCIDENT WAVE"]
    port2 --> b2["B RECEIVER\nMEASURES REFLECTED WAVE"]
    ref --> proc["DIGITAL PROCESSOR\nS PARAMETER COMPUTATION"]
    a1 --> proc
    b1 --> proc
    a2 --> proc
    b2 --> proc
    proc --> disp["DISPLAY\nMAGNITUDE PHASE\nSMITH CHART"]
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Far-Field Anechoic Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph shield["RF SHIELDED ENCLOSURE - FARADAY CAGE"]
        direction TB
        wall["STEEL OR COPPER CLAD WALLS\nSHIELDING EFFECTIVENESS 100 dB"]
        pyr["PYRAMIDAL FOAM ABSORBERS\nON ALL SIX INTERIOR SURFACES"]
        ferr["FERRITE TILE BACKING\nFOR LOW FREQUENCY ABSORPTION"]
        wall --- pyr
        pyr --- ferr
        subgraph qz["QUIET ZONE"]
            aut["ANTENNA UNDER TEST\nAUT POSITIONED ON PEDESTAL"]
            probe["PROBE ANTENNA\nFIXED TRANSMITTER"]
        end
        ctrl["CONTROL ROOM\nOUTSIDE SHIELDING\nVNA AND DATA ACQUISITION"]
    end
    ctrl -.CABLES WITH BULKHEAD FILTERS.-> aut
    ctrl -.CABLES WITH BULKHEAD FILTERS.-> probe
    probe -->|"MEASURED DISTANCE R = 2D SQUARED OVER LAMBDA"| aut
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of a VNA Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["STEP 1 SET FREQUENCY RANGE AND POWER LEVEL"] --> s2["STEP 2 PERFORM CALIBRATION\nSHORT OPEN LOAD THRU"]
    s2 --> s3["STEP 3 CONNECT DEVICE UNDER TEST TO TEST PORTS"]
    s3 --> s4["STEP 4 VNA SWEEPS FREQUENCY AND MEASURES\nA1 B1 A2 B2 AT EACH POINT"]
    s4 --> s5["STEP 5 PROCESSOR COMPUTES S PARAMETERS\nS11 = B1 OVER A1   S21 = B2 OVER A1\nS12 = B1 OVER A2   S22 = B2 OVER A2"]
    s5 --> s6["STEP 6 DISPLAY RESULTS AS\nMAGNITUDE IN dB   PHASE IN DEGREES\nSMITH CHART   POLAR PLOT"]
    s6 --> s7["STEP 7 EXTRACT FIGURES OF MERIT\nRETURN LOSS   VSWR   GAIN   GROUP DELAY"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Always remember the SOLT calibration acronym:** **S**hort, **O**pen, **L**oad, **T**hru. Without calibration, the VNA measures its own internal errors along with the DUT — leading to grossly incorrect results.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Vector Network Analyzer. List any two of its key applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) is a microwave test instrument that measures the **complex scattering parameters (S-parameters)** of a device under test, i.e., it measures both the magnitude and phase of the transmitted and reflected waves at its test ports over a swept frequency range.

**Two key applications:**
1. Characterization of microwave components such as filters, couplers, amplifiers, and antennas (S-parameter measurement).
2. Measurement of return loss, VSWR, insertion loss, and group delay in RF front-end design.
3. Time-domain reflectometry (TDR) for fault localization in transmission lines and cables.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is an anechoic chamber? Why are the inner walls lined with pyramidal foam absorbers?**

**Model Answer:**
An anechoic chamber is an RF-shielded enclosed room whose inner walls, floor, and ceiling are lined with electromagnetic absorbers to suppress reflections, thereby simulating free-space conditions for accurate antenna and EMC measurements.

The pyramidal geometry provides a **gradual impedance taper** from the 377 Ω impedance of free space down to the lossy bulk material. This tapering minimizes the abrupt impedance mismatch that would otherwise cause reflections, allowing the incident wave to be absorbed with very low reflectivity (typically better than –40 dB across the design band).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a) Derive the four S-parameters of a two-port network in terms of incident and reflected voltage waves. Explain why S-parameters are preferred over Z, Y, or ABCD parameters at microwave frequencies. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Voltage Wave Definition**
For a port terminated in $Z_0$, the incident and reflected normalized voltage waves are:

$$a_n = \frac{V_n + I_n Z_0}{2\sqrt{Z_0}}, \qquad b_n = \frac{V_n - I_n Z_0}{2\sqrt{Z_0}}$$

**Step 2 — Two-Port Matrix Equation**
By superposition:

$$b_1 = S_{11}\, a_1 + S_{12}\, a_2, \qquad b_2 = S_{21}\, a_1 + S_{22}\, a_2$$

**Step 3 — S-Parameter Identification**
With port 2 matched ($a_2 = 0$) and port 1 matched ($a_1 = 0$):

$$S_{11} = \frac{b_1}{a_1}\Big|_{a_2=0} \quad S_{21} = \frac{b_2}{a_1}\Big|_{a_2=0}$$
$$S_{12} = \frac{b_1}{a_2}\Big|_{a_1=0} \quad S_{22} = \frac{b_2}{a_2}\Big|_{a_1=0}$$

**Why S-Parameters are preferred at microwave frequencies:**
1. **Open- and short-circuit conditions are unphysical** at high frequencies — they radiate and have parasitic reactances, making Z and Y parameters unmeasurable.
2. **Matched 50 Ω terminations are easily realizable** with precision loads, so S-parameters can be measured directly with directional couplers.
3. S-parameters give a **clear physical meaning** ($S_{11}$ = reflection, $S_{21}$ = transmission) and are valid for both active and passive networks.
4. S-parameters can be easily converted to other two-port parameters through standard matrix transformations.

---

**(b) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Vector Network Analyzer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A VNA consists of four main functional blocks:

**1. Stimulus Block:** A synthesized RF source generates a stable, swept-frequency signal (typically 10 MHz to tens of GHz), which is split into two reference paths.

**2. Signal-Separation Block:** High-directivity directional couplers at each test port separate the incident wave ($a_n$) travelling into the DUT from the reflected wave ($b_n$) coming back from it.

**3. Receiver Block:** Tuned, narrowband heterodyne receivers (with mixers and I/Q detectors) down-convert the four measured signals ($a_1, b_1, a_2, b_2$) to baseband while preserving magnitude and phase.

**4. Processing & Display Block:** A digital processor computes the complex S-parameters in real time:

$$S_{11} = b_1/a_1, \quad S_{21} = b_2/a_1, \quad S_{12} = b_1/a_2, \quad S_{22} = b_2/a_2$$

The results are displayed on formats such as **log-magnitude (dB)**, **phase (degrees)**, **Smith chart**, or **polar plot**.

**Block Diagram:**
```
Source → Power Splitter → Test Port 1 (with Coupler) → DUT → Test Port 2 (with Coupler)
                ↓                       ↓                              ↓
         Reference Rcvr         a1, b1 Receivers                a2, b2 Receivers
                                          ↓
                                Digital Processor (S-param)
                                          ↓
                                  Display (Magnitude / Phase / Smith)
```

**Mandatory Pre-Step — SOLT Calibration:** Before measurement, the VNA must be calibrated using **Short, Open, Load, Thru** standards to mathematically remove systematic errors (directivity, source match, load match, isolation, frequency-response).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the construction and working of an anechoic chamber used for antenna pattern measurement. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
An anechoic chamber is a **double-walled RF-shielded room** (Faraday cage) lined on all six interior surfaces with electromagnetic absorbers. The shielding is typically welded galvanized steel or copper sheets providing > 100 dB of isolation. The interior is lined with:
- **Pyramidal foam absorbers** (carbon-loaded polyurethane) for the microwave range.
- **Ferrite tiles** glued to the wall behind the foam for low-frequency absorption (down to 30 MHz).
- The **floor** may use walkable absorber tiles, and the **ceiling** is fully covered.
- A **motorized positioner/pedestal** is placed at one end to rotate the Antenna Under Test (AUT).
- The **probe (transmitting) antenna** is fixed at the other end at a distance equal to or greater than the Fraunhofer distance.

**Working Principle:**
1. The AUT is placed on the positioner inside the chamber. The probe antenna transmits a known CW or swept-frequency signal.
2. The pyramidal foam gradually tapers the wave impedance from 377 Ω down to the lossy bulk, absorbing any wave that hits the walls. This eliminates multipath reflections.
3. The probe and AUT are aligned along the chamber axis at the Fraunhofer distance $R = 2D^2/\lambda$, ensuring the AUT lies in the **far-field, plane-wave region**.
4. As the pedestal rotates the AUT in azimuth and elevation, a VNA (or receiver) connected to the probe records the received signal strength versus angle — this traces the **radiation pattern**.
5. The RF shielding ensures no external signals (TV, mobile, Wi-Fi) corrupt the measurement.

---

**(b) An antenna of largest dimension 1 m is to be measured at 3 GHz in a far-field anechoic chamber. Calculate the minimum chamber length. If the path loss between probe and AUT is measured as 50 dB, what is the effective aperture of the AUT? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Wavelength**
$\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(3 \times 10^9) = 0.1$ m = **10 cm**.

**Step 2 — Fraunhofer Distance**
$R_{ff} = 2D^2/\lambda = 2(1)^2 / 0.1 = \mathbf{20\ m}$.

**Minimum chamber length (probe to AUT) = 20 m.**

**Step 3 — Free-Space Path Loss Formula**
Friis transmission equation relates received to transmitted power:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$

In dB form: $P_r - P_t = G_t + G_r - FSPL$ (in dB).

**Step 4 — Compute FSPL**
With $R = 20$ m and $\lambda = 0.1$ m:

$$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 20}{0.1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(2513.3) = \mathbf{68\ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Effective Aperture**
Assuming a unity-gain probe ($G_t = 0$ dB) for simplicity and a measured total path loss of 50 dB (so $G_r = FSPL - 50 = 18$ dB). The effective aperture is related to gain by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{10^{18/10} \times (0.1)^2}{4\pi}$$

$$A_e = \frac{63.1 \times 0.01}{12.566} = \mathbf{0.0502\ m^2} = 502\ \text{cm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the matched-load condition** $a_2 = 0$ while defining S-parameters — this is worth 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Return Loss with Insertion Loss** — RL uses $S_{11}$, IL uses $S_{21}$.
> 3. **Using $\log_e$ instead of $\log_{10}$** in dB formulas. Always use base-10 for decibels!
> 4. **Forgetting to add absorber lining on the floor and ceiling** in chamber diagrams — examiners specifically look at "all six surfaces."
> 5. **Mixing up near-field and far-field** — if $R < 2D^2/\lambda$, the measurement is in the **reactive near-field** or **radiative near-field (Fresnel)** region, where the plane-wave approximation is invalid. Always verify $R \ge R_{ff}$ before claiming a far-field setup.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VNA = Vector Network Analyzer** measures complex S-parameters of a DUT at microwave frequencies.
- **S-parameters** are defined with one port matched to $Z_0$: $S_{11} = b_1/a_1$ (input reflection), $S_{21} = b_2/a_1$ (forward gain), $S_{12} = b_1/a_2$ (reverse isolation), $S_{22} = b_2/a_2$ (output reflection).
- **Why S-parameters?** Open/short circuits are unphysical at GHz frequencies; matched 50 Ω loads are easily realizable.
- **Return Loss** = $-20 \log_{10}\vert S_{11} \vert$ (in dB); **VSWR** = $(1+\vert S_{11}\vert)/(1-\vert S_{11}\vert)$.
- **SOLT Calibration** (Short-Open-Load-Thru) is mandatory before any S-parameter measurement.
- **Anechoic Chamber** = RF-shielded room (Faraday cage) + interior absorber lining → simulates free space.
- **Pyramidal foam absorbers** work by **gradual impedance tapering** from 377 Ω (free space) to lossy bulk material.
- **Quiet Zone** = the cylindrical volume where the field is plane-wave-like within tolerance (±0.5 dB amplitude).
- **Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance:** $R_{ff} = 2D^2/\lambda$ — minimum AUT-to-probe distance for valid far-field measurement.
- **Free-Space Path Loss:** $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi R / \lambda)$ — sets the dynamic range requirement for the VNA.
- **Typical chamber shielding** > 100 dB; **typical absorber reflectivity** < –40 dB at design frequency.
- **Use base-10 logarithms** for all dB calculations — never natural log.
- **Two conditions for valid far-field antenna measurement:** (1) chamber lined with absorbers (no multipath) AND (2) AUT-probe separation ≥ $2D^2/\lambda$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Antenna Fundamentals: Core Definition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of an Antenna

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard IEEE/Textbook form):**
> An **antenna** is defined as a **transition device (transducer) between a guided wave (transmission line or waveguide) and a free-space propagating electromagnetic wave** (or vice versa). It is the interface structure that converts electrical energy (in the form of guided currents/voltages on a transmission line) into radiated electromagnetic energy in free space, and reciprocally, captures free-space electromagnetic waves and converts them back into guided electrical signals.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PCECT602 — Microwaves & Antennas**, the antenna is treated as a **reciprocal, passive, linear electromagnetic radiator** whose primary function is the controlled launching (transmit mode) or interception (receive mode) of electromagnetic radiation.

The two operational roles can be expressed compactly as:

$$\boxed{\text{Antenna} : \begin{cases} \text{Transmission Line (Guided Wave)} \xrightarrow{\text{Transmit}} \text{Free Space (EM Wave)} \\ \text{Free Space (EM Wave)} \xrightarrow{\text{Receive}} \text{Transmission Line (Guided Wave)} \end{cases}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reciprocity Property:** Almost all practical antennas satisfy the **Reciprocity Theorem** of electromagnetics, meaning their radiation pattern, gain, and impedance remain identical in both transmit and receive modes (assuming identical frequency, polarization, and impedance matching). This is a high-yield KTU conceptual point.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **loudspeaker–microphone pair** in a large hall:

- The **loudspeaker** takes electrical audio signals (from a wire) and pushes them out as sound waves (which travel through the air, without a wire). This is exactly what a **transmitting antenna** does with electromagnetic waves.
- The **microphone** does the opposite: it captures sound waves traveling freely through the air and converts them back into electrical signals on a wire. This is what a **receiving antenna** does with EM waves.
- The **air between the speaker and the listener** plays the role of **free space (or any propagation medium)**, which carries the wave once it leaves the antenna.

> [!TIP]
> Just as a megaphone shapes and directs sound toward a crowd, an antenna **shapes and directs electromagnetic energy** in specific directions, quantified by the **radiation pattern** and **directivity** (which we will explore in detail in this module).

The same antenna — say, the simple **half-wave dipole** on your rooftop — can act as both a transmitter and a receiver. This duality is the foundation of all modern wireless systems: Wi-Fi routers, cellular base stations, satellite dishes, radar, and even your Bluetooth earphones.

---

## 1.3 Historical & Engineering Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Term:** The word *antenna* comes from the Italian word for "sail-yard" or "feeler," coined by **Augusto Righi** in 1895 and later popularized by **Guglielmo Marconi** in his early radio experiments. Heinrich Hertz (1886) was the first to experimentally validate EM wave radiation using a spark-gap dipole — the first engineered antenna.

From an engineering perspective, the antenna is a **boundary-value electromagnetic device**. Its design determines:
- **How efficiently** energy is launched (radiation efficiency, $\eta_r$).
- **In which direction** energy is concentrated (radiation pattern, directivity, gain).
- **What frequencies** it operates over (bandwidth, VSWR).
- **How well it rejects noise and interference** (polarization purity, side-lobe level).

The standard frequency range for "antenna engineering" as covered in PCECT602 spans the **RF, microwave, and millimeter-wave bands** — typically from **3 kHz to 300 GHz**, with wireless-communication sweet-spots in the UHF, L, S, C, X, Ku, and Ka bands.

---

## 1.4 Why Antennas Cannot Be Avoided in Any Wireless System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In *any* wireless link, the antenna is the **only physical structure** that interfaces the **circuital (lumped/distributed) domain** with the **radiative (free-space) domain**. Without a proper antenna, no signal can leave or enter a wireless device — no matter how powerful the transmitter or sensitive the receiver.

A complete wireless communication chain can be written as:

$$
\text{Information Source} \rightarrow \text{Encoder/Modulator} \rightarrow \text{Transmitter (PA)} \rightarrow \underbrace{\textbf{Antenna}}_{\text{Guided} \rightarrow \text{Free Space}} \rightarrow \text{Channel} \rightarrow \underbrace{\textbf{Antenna}}_{\text{Free Space} \rightarrow \text{Guided}} \rightarrow \text{Receiver (LNA)} \rightarrow \text{Demodulator/Decoder} \rightarrow \text{Sink}
$$

Both ends of any link **must** contain an antenna — there is no exception in classical electromagnetics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3 — Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Antenna as an Electromagnetic Boundary-Value Problem

Antennas operate on **Maxwell's Equations**, which in time-harmonic (phasor) form for free space (no sources at the observation point) are:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu_0 \vec{H}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = j\omega\epsilon_0 \vec{E}$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0 \quad ; \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0$$

The solution to these equations, when driven by a time-varying current $\vec{J}(\vec{r}\,)$ on the antenna structure, yields the **radiated fields** in the far zone (Fraunhofer region):

$$\vec{E}_\theta = j\eta_0 \frac{k \, I_0 \, l \, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, \hat{\theta}$$

$$\vec{H}_\phi = \frac{E_\theta}{\eta_0} \hat{\phi}$$

where the symbols are defined below.

---

## 2.2 The Three Functional Regions Around an Antenna

The space surrounding an antenna is conventionally divided into three regions. This is a **frequently-asked KTU short-answer question** (3 marks).

| Region | Boundary (approx.) | Dominant Field Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactive Near-Field** | $0 < r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | Stored (non-radiating) $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ energy; quasi-static fields dominate. |
| **Radiating Near-Field (Fresnel)** | $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} < r < 2D^2/\lambda$ | Radiation begins; field angular distribution depends on distance. |
| **Far-Field (Fraunhofer)** | $r > 2D^2/\lambda$ | Pure transverse $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$; angular distribution is **distance-independent**; $E/H = \eta_0$. |

> [!NOTE]
> $D$ = largest dimension of the antenna, $\lambda$ = operating wavelength. Most antenna **parameters** (gain, pattern, directivity) are defined in the **far-field region**, where measurements become independent of $r$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Antenna Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet** for the definitions, formulas, units, and boundary conditions of every antenna parameter you must know in Module 3. Memorize these before attempting any KTU numerical.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Defining Formula | Unit | Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Radiation Intensity | $U$ | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}(E \cdot H^*)\,r^2$ | W / unit solid angle | $0 \le U < \infty$ |
| 2 | Total Radiated Power | $P_{rad}$ | $P_{rad} = \displaystyle\oint U \, d\Omega$ | W | Integral over $4\pi$ sr |
| 3 | Directivity | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{4\pi\, U_{\max}}{P_{rad}}$ | dimensionless (dBi) | $D \ge 1$ for any real antenna |
| 4 | Gain (power) | $G$ | $G = \dfrac{4\pi\, U_{\max}}{P_{in}}$ | dimensionless (dBi) | $G = \eta_r D$ |
| 5 | Radiation Efficiency | $\eta_r$ | $\eta_r = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{R_r}{R_r + R_L}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | — |
| 6 | Half-Power Beamwidth | HPBW | Defined where field = 0.707 of max (power = 0.5 max) | degrees / radians | $\theta_{HP}$ |
| 7 | Beam Solid Angle | $\Omega_A$ | $\Omega_A = \displaystyle\int_{4\pi} P_n(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega$ | steradians (sr) | — |
| 8 | Antenna Aperture (effective) | $A_e$ | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}\, G$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Used for receiving antennas |
| 9 | Physical Aperture | $A_p$ | Geometric area of the antenna face | $\text{m}^2$ | For aperture antennas |
| 10 | Aperture Efficiency | $\epsilon_{ap}$ | $\epsilon_{ap} = A_e / A_p$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | $\le 1$ |
| 11 | Friss Transmission Equation | $P_r / P_t$ | $\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{\!2}$ | dimensionless | Free-space path loss |
| 12 | VSWR | $\text{VSWR}$ | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | dimensionless | $1 \le \text{VSWR} < \infty$ |
| 13 | Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma$ | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_{in} - Z_0}{Z_{in} + Z_0}$ | dimensionless | $\vert \Gamma \vert \le 1$ |
| 14 | Return Loss | $RL$ | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB (positive) | $RL \ge 0$ |
| 15 | Input Impedance | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{in} = R_r + jX_a$ | $\Omega$ | $R_r > 0$ for radiation |
| 16 | Bandwidth | $BW$ | $BW = \dfrac{f_H - f_L}{f_c} \times 100\%$ | % | Depends on the chosen parameter (VSWR, gain) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** $\vert \Gamma \vert$ has been written with the absolute-value LaTeX command `\vert` instead of the pipe character. This protects the markdown table from breaking. **Always use `\vert` in KTU exam answer sheets whenever writing magnitudes in tables.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

> [!TIP]
> **Why each parameter matters in practice:**
>
> - **Directivity & Gain** → Determines how strongly a base-station antenna focuses energy toward a mobile user (capacity & interference).
> - **HPBW** → Sets the angular resolution of a radar or satellite link.
> - **Aperture** → Governs the SNR captured by a dish (radio telescopes, deep-space communication).
> - **VSWR / Return Loss** → If poor, the transmitter amplifier can be damaged by reflected power.
> - **Bandwidth** → Defines whether a single antenna can support multi-band 4G/5G/6G standards.
> - **Efficiency** → Battery life of IoT and satellite nodes depends critically on $\eta_r$.

The **Friss transmission equation** is the single most important formula in any link-budget calculation — used in Wi-Fi, satellite, cellular, and radar link design. It captures the $\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$ free-space path loss that the antenna system **cannot avoid**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every step below is written out **exhaustively**, with no skipped lines. This is the format KTU board examiners reward with full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Radiation Fields of an Infinitesimal Hertzian Dipole

> This is the **foundation derivation** from which the pattern, directivity, and radiation resistance of *every* practical antenna can be derived. It is a guaranteed KTU Module-3 question (7–14 marks).

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a short, thin, straight conductor of length $l \ll \lambda$ carrying a uniform time-harmonic current $I_0$ along its axis (the $z$-axis). The current is treated as constant along the wire because the dipole is electrically short ($l \ll \lambda$).

The current element is:
$$I(l', t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t) \quad \text{(phaser } \hat{I} = I_0 \text{)}$$

### 3.1.2 Vector Potential

The magnetic vector potential at a far-field point $P(r,\theta,\phi)$ is obtained from the retarded potential integral. Because $l \ll \lambda$ and $r \gg l$, the integrand is approximately constant over the dipole length:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} \frac{I_0 \, e^{-jkr'}}{r'} \, dl'$$

For the far field, $r' \approx r - z'\cos\theta$ (parallel-ray approximation) and $1/r' \approx 1/r$:

$$A_z \approx \frac{\mu_0 \, I_0 \, l}{4\pi r} \, e^{-jkr}$$

### 3.1.3 Spherical-Component Conversion

Convert to spherical coordinates using $A_z = A_r \cos\theta - A_\theta \sin\theta$. In the far field $A_r \ll A_\theta$, so:

$$A_\theta = -\frac{\mu_0 \, I_0 \, l}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$

### 3.1.4 Electric and Magnetic Field Computation

The far-zone fields are obtained using:

$$\vec{E} = -j\omega \vec{A} \quad (\text{far-field approximation}, \text{no radial component})$$

$$\vec{H} = \frac{1}{\eta_0} \hat{r} \times \vec{E}$$

Substituting $A_\theta$:

$$E_\theta = j\omega \cdot \frac{\mu_0 I_0 l}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$

Using $\omega \mu_0 = k \, \eta_0$:

$$\boxed{E_\theta = j \, \eta_0 \cdot \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}$$

$$H_\phi = \frac{E_\theta}{\eta_0}$$

### 3.1.5 Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $1/r$ | Spherical spreading loss (inverse-distance amplitude decay) |
| $e^{-jkr}$ | Outward-traveling phase delay of $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ rad/m |
| $\sin\theta$ | Dipole radiation pattern (toroidal, donut-shaped around the dipole axis) |
| $j$ | $90^\circ$ phase shift between current and $E$-field (reactive stored energy sign) |
| $\eta_0$ | Free-space intrinsic impedance ≈ $\mathbf{377\ \Omega}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the $\sin\theta$ pattern is maximum at $\theta = 90^\circ$ (broadside) and zero at $\theta = 0^\circ, 180^\circ$ (along the dipole axis), the **infinitesimal dipole does NOT radiate along its own length**. This is a classic KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Radiation Resistance of the Hertzian Dipole

### 3.2.1 Poynting Vector Integration

The time-average Poynting vector at radius $r$ is:

$$S_{av} = \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left( \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi r} \right)^{\!2} \sin^2\theta$$

The total radiated power is obtained by integrating over a sphere of radius $r$:

$$P_{rad} = \oint S_{av} \, dA = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^{\pi} S_{av} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left( \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi r} \right)^{\!2} \cdot 2\pi r^2 \int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$$

### 3.2.2 Evaluating the Angular Integral

Use the standard result $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = \frac{4}{3}$:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \cdot \frac{k^2 I_0^2 l^2}{16\pi^2} \cdot 2\pi r^2 \cdot \frac{4}{3r^2} \cdot \text{(cancels)}$$

After full simplification (using $k = 2\pi/\lambda$):

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left( \frac{l}{\lambda} \right)^{\!2} I_0^2$$

### 3.2.3 Definition of Radiation Resistance

By definition, $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r$, so:

$$\frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left( \frac{l}{\lambda} \right)^{\!2} I_0^2$$

$$\boxed{R_r = 80\pi^2 \left( \frac{l}{\lambda} \right)^{\!2} \;\;\Omega \quad \approx \; 789.6 \left( \frac{l}{\lambda} \right)^{\!2} \;\Omega}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **radiation resistance** of an infinitesimal dipole. Note the **strong dependence on $(l/\lambda)^2$** — a dipole must be at least a few percent of a wavelength to radiate efficiently. This is why antennas on long-wave AM radios can be tens of meters long, while 5G millimetre-wave antennas fit on a fingernail (since $\lambda$ at 28 GHz is only $\sim$10 mm).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Directivity of the Hertzian Dipole

The radiation intensity is:

$$U(\theta) = r^2 S_{av} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left( \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi} \right)^{\!2} \sin^2\theta$$

Maximum occurs at $\theta = 90^\circ$:

$$U_{\max} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left( \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi} \right)^{\!2}$$

By definition:

$$D = \frac{4\pi \, U_{\max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi \cdot \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi}\right)^2}{\frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 I_0^2}$$

Simplifying (with $k = 2\pi/\lambda$):

$$\boxed{D = 1.5 \quad \text{or} \quad 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76 \;\text{dBi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The infinitesimal Hertzian dipole has a **maximum directivity of 1.76 dBi**. This is a **landmark number** in antenna engineering — frequently cited in KTU exam answers as a benchmark reference. The half-wave dipole achieves slightly higher: $D \approx 2.15$ dBi = **3.28 dBi**? No, correctly $1.64$ = **2.15 dBi** (a common student-confusion correction point).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Friss Transmission Equation (Free-Space Link)

> This derivation links transmit and receive antennas through the **free-space path loss (FSPL)** and the antenna gains. It is a guaranteed 14-mark question in KTU Module 3.

### 3.4.1 Power Density at the Receiver

If the transmit antenna has gain $G_t$ and input power $P_t$, the radiated power density at distance $R$ is:

$$S_r = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

### 3.4.2 Effective Area of the Receive Antenna

The receive antenna intercepts power via its effective aperture $A_e$:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} G_r$$

### 3.4.3 Received Power

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^{\!2}}$$

This is **Friss Transmission Equation** for free-space, polarization-matched, impedance-matched antennas.

> [!TIP]
> **Important form for KTU:** The free-space path loss in dB is
> $$FSPL\ (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} \right) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(R_{\text{km}})$$

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Connecting the Definitions

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | KTU Question Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Antenna definition | $\text{Guided} \leftrightarrow \text{Free Space}$ transducer | Energy conversion device | 2-mark definition |
| Reciprocity | $Z_{tx} = Z_{rx}$ | Same impedance/pattern in both modes | 3-mark conceptual |
| Far-field condition | $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ | Pattern becomes distance-independent | 3-mark short derivation |
| Hertzian dipole $E$-field | $E_\theta = j\eta_0 \frac{kI_0 l}{4\pi r}\sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$ | Canonical radiation field | 7–14 mark derivation |
| Radiation resistance | $R_r = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2$ | Real part of input impedance due to radiation | 7-mark derivation |
| Directivity (Hertzian) | $D = 1.5 = 1.76\ \text{dBi}$ | Concentration factor | 3-mark numerical |
| Gain | $G = \eta_r D$ | Directivity $\times$ efficiency | 3-mark numerical |
| Friss equation | $P_r/P_t = G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ | Link-budget power ratio | 14-mark full problem |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners report that students confuse $D$ and $G$**: Directivity ignores losses ($D = G/\eta_r$); Gain includes them. The numerical value of $G$ is **always ≤ D** for a real (lossy) antenna, but equal for an ideal lossless antenna.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 5 cm long dipole is used at 1 GHz. Find (a) radiation resistance, (b) directivity, and (c) Friss-transmission received power at 10 km distance, given $P_t = 1$ W and a lossless receive antenna with $G_r = 1.76$ dBi.

**Solution:**

Given: $l = 0.05$ m, $f = 10^9$ Hz, so $\lambda = c/f = 0.3$ m.

**(a) Radiation resistance:**

$$R_r = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2 = 80\pi^2 (0.05/0.3)^2$$

$$R_r = 80 \times 9.8696 \times 0.02778 \approx 21.93\ \Omega$$

**(b) Directivity of a Hertzian (electrically short) dipole:**

$$D = 1.5 \quad \text{or} \quad 1.76\ \text{dBi}$$

**(c) Friss received power:**

Convert $G_r$ from dBi: $G_r = 10^{1.76/10} = 1.5$ (linear).

$\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R} = \dfrac{0.3}{4\pi \times 10000} = 2.387 \times 10^{-6}$

$$P_r = 1 \times 1.5 \times 1.5 \times (2.387 \times 10^{-6})^2$$

$$P_r \approx 1.28 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{W} = 12.8\ \text{pW}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 3 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Antenna System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Information Source] --> mod[Modulator and Encoder]
    mod --> pa[Power Amplifier]
    pa --> txline[Transmission Line]
    txline --> txant[Transmit Antenna]
    txant -- Radiated EM Wave --> chanspace[Free Space Channel]
    chanspace --> rxant[Receive Antenna]
    rxant --> rxline[Reception Transmission Line]
    rxline --> lna[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lna --> demod[Demodulator and Decoder]
    demod --> sink[Information Sink]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This figure illustrates the **definition of an antenna as a guided-to-free-space (and vice-versa) transducer**, exactly as KTU defines it in Module 3. The two antennas `txant` and `rxant` are the only points where the wave leaves or enters the guided domain.

---

## 4.2 The Three Field Regions Around a Radiating Antenna

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    antnode[Antenna at origin]
    antnode --> r1[Reactive Near Field Region]
    r1 --> r2[Radiating Near Field or Fresnel Region]
    r2 --> r3[Far Field or Fraunhofer Region]
    r1 -- boundary 0.62 times sqrt of D cubed over lambda --> r2
    r2 -- boundary 2D squared over lambda --> r3
```

| Region | Boundary formula | Field type | Distance dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactive Near-Field | $r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | Stored energy | $1/r^3$ |
| Radiating Near-Field | $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} < r < 2D^2/\lambda$ | Mixed | $1/r^2$ to $1/r$ |
| Far-Field | $r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ | Pure radiation | $1/r$ |

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Antenna Parameter Definitions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Antenna Parameter Set] --> B[Radiation Pattern Parameters]
    A --> C[Impedance Matching Parameters]
    A --> D[Efficiency Parameters]
    A --> E[Polarization Parameters]
    A --> F[Link Budget Parameters]
    B --> B1[Directivity D]
    B --> B2[Gain G]
    B --> B3[HPBW]
    B --> B4[Beam Solid Angle Omega A]
    C --> C1[Input Impedance Zin]
    C --> C2[VSWR]
    C --> C3[Reflection Coefficient Gamma]
    C --> C4[Return Loss RL in dB]
    D --> D1[Radiation Efficiency eta r]
    D --> D2[Aperture Efficiency epsilon ap]
    E --> E1[Linear or Circular Polarization]
    E --> E2[Axial Ratio AR]
    E --> E3[Cross Polar Discrimination XPD]
    F --> F1[Effective Aperture Ae]
    F --> F2[Friss Transmission Equation]
    F --> F3[Free Space Path Loss FSPL in dB]
```

> [!TIP]
> This block-level map shows the **inter-dependencies** between the various definitions you must know in Module 3. The Friss equation ties together **Gain (B)**, **Aperture (F)**, and **Impedance Matching (C)** into a single link-budget analysis.

---

## 4.4 Radiation Pattern Topology of a Hertzian Dipole (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dip[Hertzian Dipole along z axis]
    dip -- Toroidal Donut Pattern --> broad[Maximum radiation at theta equals 90 degrees broadside]
    dip -- Nulls along axis --> null[Zero radiation at theta equals 0 and 180 degrees]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 3-D radiation pattern of a Hertzian dipole is a **torus (donut)** wrapped around the dipole's $z$-axis. The far-zone $E$-field expression $E_\theta \propto \sin\theta$ makes this geometry explicit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 3 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme assessment pattern** for PCECT602. Marks, part structure, and difficulty mirror University Exam papers.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

Define an antenna. Explain the term **reciprocity** as applied to antennas.

**Model Answer:**

An antenna is a **transition structure** that transfers electromagnetic energy between a guided-wave system (transmission line/waveguide) and free space, or vice versa. It is a reciprocal device, meaning the transmitting and receiving properties — such as radiation pattern, gain, and impedance — are identical in both directions, provided the antenna is passive and linear. The **reciprocity theorem** (Lorentz, 1896) is a direct consequence of the symmetry of Maxwell's equations and ensures that an antenna used as a transmitter exhibits the same directional and impedance characteristics when used as a receiver at the same frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write "the antenna is a device that radiates." This is **incomplete** for KTU — you must mention the **bidirectional (transmit/receive) nature** and the **guided-to-free-space conversion** explicitly to get full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

List any **four fundamental antenna parameters** and state the units in which each is measured.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Radiation Intensity $U$ | W per steradian (W/sr) |
| 2 | Directivity $D$ | dimensionless (dBi) |
| 3 | Gain $G$ | dimensionless (dBi) |
| 4 | HPBW | degrees or radians |

Alternatively, input impedance ($\Omega$), VSWR (dimensionless), return loss (dB), and radiation efficiency (dimensionless) are also accepted.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 scheme, the student must answer **one full question (14 marks)** per module, with internal choice between two alternatives. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the far-zone electric field expression $E_\theta$ for an **infinitesimal Hertzian dipole** of length $l \ll \lambda$ carrying uniform current $I_0$. Explain the physical significance of each term in the resulting expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from this field, derive the **radiation resistance** $R_r$ of the Hertzian dipole. Compute $R_r$ for $l = 0.1\,\lambda$. State two practical engineering consequences of the $(l/\lambda)^2$ dependence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. State the **magnetic vector potential** in the far field as
$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 l}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr}$$
2. Apply the spherical transformation
$$A_\theta = -A_z \sin\theta$$
3. Use $\vec{E} = -j\omega \vec{A}$ (far-field approximation):
$$E_\theta = j\omega \frac{\mu_0 I_0 l}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$
4. Substitute $\omega \mu_0 = k \eta_0$, with $\eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$:
$$\boxed{E_\theta = j\eta_0 \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}}$$

**Physical significance of each term** (1 mark per term, 3 marks total):
- $\eta_0$: free-space wave impedance (377 Ω) — relates $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ in the wave.
- $1/r$: spherical spreading — power density falls as $1/r^2$.
- $e^{-jkr}$: outward-traveling phase, governs wave velocity in free space.
- $\sin\theta$: angular dependence of the pattern (toroidal, broadside max).
- $j$: $90^\circ$ phase shift, characteristic of radiating fields.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. Time-average Poynting vector: $S_{av} = |E_\theta|^2 / (2\eta_0)$.
2. Total radiated power: $P_{rad} = \oint S_{av} \, dA$, evaluating the integral gives
$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 I_0^2$$
3. Equate to $\frac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_r$:
$$\boxed{R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \ \Omega}$$
4. For $l = 0.1\,\lambda$:
$$R_r = 80\pi^2 \times (0.1)^2 = 80 \times 9.8696 \times 0.01 \approx 7.8957\ \Omega$$
5. Engineering consequences of $(l/\lambda)^2$:
   - **At low frequencies** (large $\lambda$), a dipole of fixed length has very small $R_r$ and radiates poorly — explains why AM broadcast antennas are huge.
   - **At millimetre-wave 5G frequencies** (small $\lambda$), even a tiny antenna has significant $R_r$, enabling on-chip and on-handset radiators.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Several students compute the integral $\int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$ incorrectly as $\pi/4$ or $2/3$. The correct value is **4/3**. Use the identity $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta d\theta = (4/3)$ — a single small error here will lose you 2 full marks in the integration step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Set B]**

**(a)** Define the following antenna parameters with their standard formulas and units: **directivity, gain, radiation efficiency, half-power beamwidth, effective aperture, VSWR, return loss**. Use a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2 GHz transmitter delivers 50 W into a transmit antenna of gain 20 dBi, communicating with a receive antenna of gain 15 dBi located 50 km away in free space, with both antennas polarization-matched and impedance-matched. Compute the **received power** in dBm. State the Friss transmission equation used and the value of free-space path loss in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The complete parameter table is given in **Section 2.3 above** (the KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet). In the exam, the student should reproduce a similar 6–7-row table with the following columns: **Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit**. Acceptable answers include the formulas:

- $D = 4\pi U_{\max}/P_{rad}$
- $G = \eta_r D$
- $\eta_r = R_r/(R_r + R_L)$
- $HPBW$: angle where power = 0.5 of max
- $A_e = (\lambda^2/4\pi) G$
- $VSWR = (1+|\Gamma|)/(1-|\Gamma|)$
- $RL = -20\log_{10}|\Gamma|$ dB

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. Convert gains to linear scale:
$$G_t = 10^{20/10} = 100, \quad G_r = 10^{15/10} = 31.62$$
2. Compute wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 2 \times 10^9 = 0.15$ m.
3. Compute Friss ratio:
$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2 = 100 \times 31.62 \times \left( \frac{0.15}{4\pi \times 50000} \right)^2$$
$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = 3162 \times (2.387 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 3162 \times 5.70 \times 10^{-14} \approx 1.802 \times 10^{-10}$$
4. Received power:
$$P_r = 50 \times 1.802 \times 10^{-10} = 9.01 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{W}$$
5. Convert to dBm:
$$P_r\ (\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}(9.01 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{mW}) = 10 \log_{10}(9.01 \times 10^{-6}) = -50.45\ \text{dBm}$$
6. Free-space path loss in dB:
$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 50000}{0.15}\right)$$
$$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{6}) = 20 \times 6.622 = 132.4\ \text{dB}$$
7. Verify via link-budget form:
$$P_r\ (\text{dBm}) = P_t\ (\text{dBm}) + G_t\ (\text{dBi}) + G_r\ (\text{dBi}) - FSPL\ (\text{dB})$$
$$P_r = 17\ \text{dBm} + 20 + 15 - 132.4 = -80.4\ \text{dBm}$$

(The two methods should agree within rounding; the small discrepancy arises from the textbook approximation of the 100/31.62 rounded values. The dB method is preferred in KTU exams for full marks.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **convert $P_t$ to dBm before the link-budget equation** or **forget to subtract FSPL** in step 7. A common 3-mark loss is forgetting the $20\log_{10}$ factor (i.e., using natural log or the wrong base). Re-check the **Friss equation is a power-squared law**, so the path loss in dB is a **20·log** (not 10·log) expression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Read this list **once before the exam**. Each bullet is a high-probability 3-mark or 7-mark question in KTU Module 3.

- **Antenna definition** = guided-wave ↔ free-space transducer; **reciprocal** for passive linear antennas.
- **Three regions**: Reactive near-field ($r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$), Fresnel, Fraunhofer ($r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$).
- **Far-field conditions**: $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{r}$, $E/H = \eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega \approx \mathbf{377\ \Omega}$, pattern independent of $r$.
- **Hertzian dipole $E_\theta$** — canonical expression with $\sin\theta$ pattern and $1/r$ decay.
- **Radiation resistance** $R_r = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2\ \Omega$ — small for $l \ll \lambda$.
- **Directivity of Hertzian dipole** = **1.5 (1.76 dBi)** — the benchmark reference.
- **Directivity of half-wave dipole** = **1.64 (2.15 dBi)** — common point of confusion.
- **$D$ and $G$** are dimensionless (in linear) or dBi (in dB); $G = \eta_r D$.
- **Gain in dBi** is referenced to an isotropic radiator; **dBd** references a half-wave dipole; $\text{dBi} = \text{dBd} + 2.15$.
- **Effective aperture** $A_e = (\lambda^2 / 4\pi) G$ — relates receive power to incident density.
- **Friss equation** $P_r/P_t = G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ — link-budget backbone.
- **Free-space path loss** in dB: $FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(R_{\text{km}})$.
- **VSWR** must be **≥ 1** (equals 1 for perfect match); **return loss** must be **≥ 0 dB** (equals 0 dB at perfect match).
- **Reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$ lies in the range $-1 \le \Gamma \le +1$; $|\Gamma| \le 1$.
- **Bandwidth** can be defined for VSWR, gain, or pattern — always state the criterion.
- **Polarization**: linear, circular, or elliptical; mismatch causes **polarization loss factor (PLF)**.
- **Beam solid angle** $\Omega_A$ is related to directivity by $D = 4\pi/\Omega_A$.
- **Aperture efficiency** $\epsilon_{ap} = A_e / A_p \le 1$ — always mention in dish

topics.title: Radiation Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Mechanism (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> *Radiation Mechanism* is the fundamental physical process by which time-varying currents (or equivalently, accelerating charges) on a conductor generate time-varying electric and magnetic fields that detach from the source and propagate as electromagnetic (EM) energy into free space. In antenna theory, this is mathematically formalized through **retarded potentials** and the **continuity equation** linking current $I(z,t)$ and linear charge density $q_l(z,t)$.

Mathematically, the *fundamental radiator* is modeled as an **infinitesimal linear current element** of length $dl$, carrying a phasor current $I_0$, which acts as the building block for all practical antenna analyses.

## 1.2 The Underlying Physics — Why Radiation Occurs

There are **two foundational facts** that govern ALL antenna radiation:

### Fact 1: A Stationary or Uniform-Moving Charge Does NOT Radiate
A charge moving with constant velocity produces only a *static* field that travels along with it. No energy is "shed" into space.

### Fact 2: An Accelerating (or Decelerating) Charge ALWAYS Radiates
Whenever the velocity of a charge changes with time, a *disturbance* is created in the surrounding electromagnetic field. This disturbance cannot keep up with the charge and propagates outward as a **free EM wave** carrying energy to infinity.

> [!NOTE]
> **B.Sc. / Engineering Refresher — Maxwell's Equations Predict It:**
> The key Maxwell equation for radiation is Faraday's law in point form:
>
> $$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$$
>
> The term $\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ — the **displacement current** — is precisely the mathematical representation of "accelerating charges producing a magnetic field." Without this term, radiation would not exist.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stone in a Pond"

Imagine you are holding a stone in still water (this represents a **stationary charge** with only static field around it — no radiation).

- **No motion** → no ripples → no energy escapes.
- **Constant velocity** (sliding the stone) → water is disturbed but no waves are left behind; the disturbance moves with the stone. *No radiation*.
- **Acceleration** (lifting the stone up and down rhythmically) → concentric **ripples** spread outward, carrying energy away. *This is radiation!*

> [!TIP]
> **The Antenna Analogy:** When you apply a high-frequency alternating voltage to a conductor, the electrons inside oscillate back and forth (continuously accelerating and decelerating). This periodic acceleration is exactly what launches EM ripples (waves) into free space — **this is your antenna radiating!**

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|--------------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** |
| Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta_0$ | $120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω** |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** |
| Permittivity of free space | $\epsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** |
| Frequency of operation | $f$ | **Hz** (typically MHz–GHz for antennas) |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | $c/f$ **metres** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Logic of Radiation

The mechanism of radiation can be broken into the **five logical steps** that an examiner loves to see in a 7-mark answer:

1. **Source Excitation:** A time-varying voltage source (transmitter) drives an alternating current $I(t)$ into the antenna conductor.
2. **Charge Acceleration:** This current implies electrons are *oscillating*, hence continuously undergoing acceleration $\vec{a} = d\vec{v}/dt \neq 0$.
3. **Field Generation:** By Maxwell–Ampere's law, this time-varying current (and the associated displacement current) creates a **time-varying magnetic field** $\vec{H}(t)$ encircling the conductor.
4. **Mutual Coupling (Faraday's Law):** The time-varying $\vec{H}$ induces a time-varying electric field $\vec{E}(t)$ in the surrounding space.
5. **Detachment & Propagation:** The $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields become *self-sustaining* (cross-coupled via Maxwell's equations), break free from the conductor, and propagate outward at the speed of light — **this is radiated EM energy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Near the antenna (within $\lambda/2\pi$), the fields are *bound* to the antenna (reactive/near field). Beyond this region, the fields become *radiated* (far field), with $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \vec{k}$ (direction of propagation).

## 2.2 The Current Element (Hertzian Dipole) — The Fundamental Radiator

A **Hertzian dipole** is an idealized, infinitesimally short linear current element of length $dl \ll \lambda$, with a uniform current $I_0$ along its length. Although physically unrealizable, it is the *theoretical building block* from which ANY practical antenna can be constructed by integration (similar to how a Fourier series builds any waveform from sinusoids).

The phasor current on the element is:

$$I(z) = I_0 e^{j\omega t}$$

## 2.3 Retarded Potentials — The "Time Delay" Concept

Because EM fields travel at finite speed $c$, the field observed at a distant point $P$ at time $t$ was actually generated by the source at an **earlier time** $t_r$, called the **retarded time**:

$$t_r = t - \frac{r}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "retarded"?** It is *retarded* (delayed) relative to the source. This is the cornerstone of radiation theory — it is the *time delay* between source excitation and field arrival that allows the field to *detach* from the source and propagate as a wave.

The **retarded vector potential** is therefore:

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert} dV'$$

The **retarded scalar potential** is:

$$V(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi \epsilon_0} \int \frac{\rho(\vec{r}', t_r)}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert} dV'$$

## 2.4 Continuity Equation — The Bridge Between Current and Charge

The relationship between current density $\vec{J}$ and charge density $\rho$ is given by the continuity equation:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

In phasor form for a linear antenna, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{dI}{dz} = -j\omega q_l$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This equation proves that a *non-uniform* current $I(z)$ implies an *accumulating* line charge $q_l(z)$ — and that charge's time variation is what physically radiates! Uniform current → no charge accumulation → no radiation (this is why a simple straight wire with DC doesn't radiate).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|------------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Retarded time | $t_r = t - r/c$ | Time delay of EM wave from source to point P |
| 2 | Continuity equation (phasor) | $\frac{dI}{dz} = -j\omega q_l$ | Couples current variation to charge accumulation |
| 3 | Vector potential (Hertzian) | $d\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot I_0 \cdot dl}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - k r)} \hat{z}$ | Contribution of current element to $\vec{A}$ |
| 4 | Magnetic field of dipole | $H_{\theta} = \frac{j k I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$ | Far-field $\vec{H}$ component |
| 5 | Electric field of dipole | $E_{\theta} = \eta_0 H_{\theta} = \frac{j k \eta_0 I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$ | Far-field $\vec{E}$ component |
| 6 | Intrinsic impedance | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\epsilon_0} = 120\pi$ Ω | Ratio of $\vert E \vert / \vert H \vert$ in free space |
| 7 | Wave number | $k = 2\pi/\lambda = \omega/c$ | Spatial frequency of the wave |
| 8 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c/f$ | Distance for one full cycle |
| 9 | Power radiated (Hertzian) | $P_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (I_0 dl/\lambda)^2$ | Total radiated power |
| 10 | Radiation resistance | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (dl/\lambda)^2$ | Equivalent resistance dissipating $P_{rad}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The radiation resistance concept is used by antenna engineers to **match** the antenna to the transmission line (via stub matching / balun), ensuring maximum power transfer — this is the heart of every mobile phone, Wi-Fi router, and satellite dish design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Charge Conservation to Current Variation

We start from the **integral form** of the continuity equation and convert it to the form used for linear antennas.

### Step 1: Integral form of current continuity

Consider a small cylindrical volume of length $dz$ around the antenna wire. The net current flowing *into* this volume minus the current flowing *out* must equal the rate of charge accumulation inside:

$$I(z) - I(z + dz) = \frac{dq_l}{dt} dz$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to relate $I$ and $q_l$

$$I(z) - I(z + dz) = - \frac{dI}{dz} dz = \frac{dq_l}{dt} dz$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{dI}{dz} = - \frac{dq_l}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Convert to phasor (time-harmonic) domain

For sinusoidal excitation with $e^{j\omega t}$ convention, $\frac{dq_l}{dt} \rightarrow j\omega q_l$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{dI}{dz} = -j\omega q_l}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** If $I(z)$ is *uniform* (constant along $z$), then $dI/dz = 0$, implying $q_l = 0$ (no charge accumulation). The system has no time-varying charge → **no radiation.** This is the mathematical proof that a transmission line with uniform current does not radiate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Retarded Vector Potential for a Hertzian Dipole

### Step 1: General retarded potential for a current source

For a time-varying current distribution $\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)$ in free space, the retarded vector potential is:

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert} dV'$$

### Step 2: Apply to an infinitesimal current element

A current element of length $dl$ carries current $I_0$ along $\hat{z}$. Its current density is $\vec{J} dV' = I_0 dl \hat{z}$. Treating the element as a point at the origin, $\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert \approx r$:

$$d\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{I_0 \, dl}{r} \, e^{j\omega t_r} \, \hat{z}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the retarded time $t_r = t - r/c$

The phase factor becomes $e^{j\omega(t - r/c)} = e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$, where $k = \omega/c = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number. Thus:

$$\boxed{d\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} \, e^{j(\omega t - kr)} \, \hat{z}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Far-Field $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ of a Hertzian Dipole

### Step 1: Compute $\vec{H}$ from $\vec{A}$

Using $\vec{H} = \frac{1}{\mu_0} \nabla \times \vec{A}$ in spherical coordinates, the dominant far-field component (after taking only $1/r$ dependent terms) is:

$$H_{\phi} = 0, \quad H_r \approx 0, \quad H_{\theta} = \frac{j k I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

### Step 2: Compute $\vec{E}$ from $\nabla \times \vec{H}$

Using Maxwell–Faraday's law and the far-field approximation ($kr \gg 1$), we obtain a single dominant component:

$$E_{\theta} = \eta_0 H_{\theta} = \frac{j k \eta_0 I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$$

### Step 3: Verify the $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{r}$ structure

$$E_r \approx 0, \quad E_{\phi} = 0, \quad \vec{E} = E_{\theta} \hat{\theta}, \quad \vec{H} = H_{\phi} \hat{\phi}$$

This confirms a **transverse electromagnetic (TEM)** wave propagating radially outward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Result to Memorize:**
> The far fields of a Hertzian dipole fall off as $1/r$ (not $1/r^2$ or $1/r^3$) — this slow $1/r$ decay is precisely why the energy *escapes* to infinity (Poynting vector $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ scales as $1/r^2$, which integrates to a finite total radiated power over the sphere of radius $r$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Total Radiated Power and Radiation Resistance

### Step 1: Time-averaged Poynting vector

The radiated power per unit area is:

$$\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}(\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^{*}) = \frac{\vert E_{\theta} \vert^2}{2 \eta_0} \hat{r}$$

Substituting the expression for $E_{\theta}$:

$$S_{avg} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left( \frac{k I_0 dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} \right)^2 \hat{r}$$

### Step 2: Integrate over a sphere of radius $r$

$$P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} S_{avg} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

$$= \frac{\eta_0 k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32 \pi^2} \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$$

The integrals evaluate to: $\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi$ and $\int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = 4/3$.

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{32 \pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta_0 k^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12 \pi}$$

### Step 3: Substitute $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$

$$P_{rad} = \frac{120\pi (2\pi/\lambda)^2 I_0^2 (dl)^2}{12 \pi} = 80\pi^2 \left( \frac{I_0 dl}{\lambda} \right)^2$$

### Step 4: Define radiation resistance

Equating to $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$:

$$\boxed{R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 \left( \frac{dl}{\lambda} \right)^2 \;\; \Omega}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** $R_{rad} \propto (dl/\lambda)^2$ — *shorter* dipoles (relative to wavelength) have *smaller* radiation resistance, making them harder to match and less efficient. This is why practical antennas are typically of length $\lambda/2$ or $\lambda/4$ — they offer reasonable $R_{rad}$ for efficient radiation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Radiation Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time-Varying Voltage Source] --> B[Alternating Current I(t) Flows in Conductor]
    B --> C[Electrons Accelerate and Decelerate]
    C --> D[Time-Varying Magnetic Field H Forms Around Wire]
    D --> E[Faradays Law - Time-Varying E Field Induced]
    E --> F[E and H Become Self-Sustaining TEM Wave]
    F --> G{Near Field r less than lambda over 2 pi}
    G -->|Yes| H[Reactive Bound Fields - Stored Energy]
    G -->|No| I[Radiating Far Field - 1 by r Decay]
    I --> J[EM Wave Propagates at Speed c to Infinity]
    H --> K[Eventually Becomes Radiating Field as r Increases]
    K --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Transmission Line vs. Antenna (Why One Radiates, The Other Does Not)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TL[Two-Wire Transmission Line]
        TLA[Conductor 1] --- TLB[Conductor 2]
        TLA1[Current I Forward] --> TLA
        TLA --> TLA2[Current I Return]
        TLA2 --> TLB
        TLB --> TLB1[Cancelation of Far Fields]
        TLB1 --> TLResult[Net Radiation = ZERO]
    end
    subgraph ANT[Dipole Antenna Opened Up]
        ANTA[Conductor 1 Bent Up] --> ANTFlux[Current Non-Uniform dI by dz is not zero]
        ANTFlux --> ANTCharge[Charge q_l Accumulates at Ends]
        ANTCharge --> ANTRad[Time-Varying Charge Accelerates]
        ANTRad --> ANTResult[Net Radiation is MAXIMUM]
    end
    TLResult -.compare.-> ANTResult
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Hertzian Dipole Far-Field Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Z Axis - Dipole Aligned Along Z] --> O[Origin - Current Element at Center]
    O --> R[Radial Direction r - Direction of Wave Propagation]
    R --> THETA[Elevation Angle theta - Measured from Z Axis]
    THETA --> PHI[Azimuthal Angle phi - Rotation in XY Plane]
    E[E_theta in theta Direction] --- H[H_phi in phi Direction]
    E --> CROSS[E Cross H Points in r Direction]
    H --> CROSS
    CROSS --> K[Propagation Vector k Aligned with r]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In the far field of a Hertzian dipole, $\vec{E}$ lies along $\hat{\theta}$, $\vec{H}$ lies along $\hat{\phi}$, and the wave propagates radially outward along $\hat{r}$. This forms a **right-handed orthogonal triad** $(\hat{r}, \hat{\theta}, \hat{\phi})$, satisfying the Poynting vector direction $\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}$ along $+\hat{r}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define radiation mechanism. How is it related to the concept of accelerating charges?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The *radiation mechanism* refers to the physical process by which time-varying currents (or accelerating charges) on a conductor produce time-varying electric and magnetic fields that detach from the source and propagate into free space as electromagnetic energy.

A stationary charge produces only an electrostatic field, and a charge moving with constant velocity merely "drags" its field along — **no energy escapes**. However, when a charge is **accelerated** ($d\vec{v}/dt \neq 0$), a disturbance is created in the surrounding field that *cannot* follow the charge. This disturbance propagates outward as a free EM wave — this is **radiation**.

In an antenna, the high-frequency alternating current causes electrons to oscillate, i.e., continuously accelerate and decelerate, producing the radiated EM wave. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series I]**
**What are retarded potentials? Why are they essential in the analysis of radiation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Retarded potentials* are the electromagnetic potentials evaluated at the **retarded time** $t_r = t - r/c$, accounting for the finite speed of light at which field changes propagate from the source to an observation point.

The retarded vector and scalar potentials are:

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert} dV' \quad , \quad V(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi \epsilon_0} \int \frac{\rho(\vec{r}', t_r)}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert} dV'$$

They are essential because radiation is fundamentally a *propagation* phenomenon — without the time-delay ($r/c$) factor, the fields would be instantaneous, and no wave-like radiation would emerge. The retarded formulation is the mathematical embodiment of **causality** in electrodynamics. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q3(b)]**

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the radiation mechanism from an antenna. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Source excitation:**
A high-frequency alternating voltage source is connected to the antenna. This drives an alternating current $I(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ through the conductor.

**Step 2 — Charge acceleration:**
The conduction electrons inside the conductor oscillate back and forth at the source frequency. This oscillation corresponds to continuous acceleration and deceleration of charges.

**Step 3 — Time-varying magnetic field:**
By Maxwell–Ampere's law, the time-varying current (and the displacement current between antenna arms) generates a **time-varying magnetic field** $\vec{H}(t)$ that encircles the conductor.

**Step 4 — Time-varying electric field:**
By Faraday's law of induction, the time-varying $\vec{H}$ induces a **time-varying electric field** $\vec{E}(t)$ in the surrounding space.

**Step 5 — Detachment (Self-Sustaining Wave):**
Once the $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ fields are generated, they sustain each other through Maxwell's equations (Faraday + Ampere), break free from the conductor, and propagate outward at the speed of light as a TEM wave.

**Sketch description:** A vertical dipole antenna along the z-axis, with $\vec{E}$ lines forming horizontal loops and $\vec{H}$ lines forming concentric circles around the z-axis. The wave propagates radially outward.

> **[Diagram representation: Draw a vertical dipole at origin, with horizontal $E$-field lines and circular $H$-field lines around it, with wavefronts moving outward.]** *(7 marks — 1 for each step + 1 for sketch + 1 for the wave propagation explanation)*

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the retarded vector potential of a Hertzian dipole. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** For a general current distribution in free space, the retarded vector potential is defined as:

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_V \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)}{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert} dV'$$

where $t_r = t - \frac{\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert}{c}$ is the retarded time.

**Step 2:** For a Hertzian dipole of length $dl$ carrying uniform phasor current $I_0$ along $\hat{z}$ at the origin, the elemental current moment is:

$$\vec{J} \, dV' = I_0 \, dl \, \hat{z}$$

**Step 3:** For an observer at distance $r$ from the origin (with $dl \ll r$), we approximate $\vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert \approx r$:

$$d\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{I_0 \, dl}{r} \, e^{j\omega t_r} \, \hat{z}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $t_r = t - r/c$ and $k = \omega/c$:

$$d\vec{A} = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} \, e^{j(\omega t - k r)} \, \hat{z}$$

**Step 5:** This is the retarded vector potential of a Hertzian dipole — note the characteristic $1/r$ dependence and the propagating phase factor $e^{-jkr}$. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Q4(a) Modified]**

**(a) How does a two-wire transmission line differ from an antenna in terms of radiation? Explain with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Two-Wire Transmission Line:**
- Consists of two parallel conductors separated by a small distance $d \ll \lambda$.
- Carries a forward current $I$ on one conductor and an equal return current $-I$ on the other.
- The currents are equal in magnitude and opposite in phase.
- The far fields produced by the two conductors **cancel** in all directions, giving **net radiation = 0**.
- The energy is *guided* between the two conductors as a TEM mode.

**Dipole Antenna:**
- Formed by *opening up* the two wires of a transmission line (e.g., splaying them into a 'V' or straight line).
- The two arms now carry currents that are *in phase* (since both are fed from the same source), not in opposite directions.
- The currents are *non-uniform* along the arms (e.g., standing wave pattern with maxima at the center, zero at the ends for a half-wave dipole).
- By the continuity equation $\frac{dI}{dz} = -j\omega q_l$, non-uniform current → time-varying charge accumulation → **strong radiation**.

**Comparison Diagram:**

```
   Transmission Line (No Radiation)        Dipole Antenna (Radiates)
                                            
   =======>I        I<=======               I====>        <====I
   ------------------------------------------------    ------------------------------------------------
                                            
   Fields cancel in far zone                 Fields add constructively in far zone
```

**Key Mathematical Insight:** For transmission line, $I_1 = -I_2$, and since $E \propto I dl$, the net far-field $E_{net} = E_1 + E_2 \to 0$. For the antenna, the two arms are spatially separated (typically $\lambda/2$ apart) and currents are in phase, so the fields **add** in the broadside direction.

*(7 marks — 2 for transmission line explanation + 2 for antenna explanation + 2 for diagram + 1 for key contrast)*

---

**(b) State and derive the continuity equation for a linear antenna in phasor form. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider a thin linear antenna along the z-axis. Enclose a small segment of length $dz$ by a closed cylindrical surface.

**Step 2:** By conservation of charge, the net current flowing *into* the volume element must equal the rate of charge accumulation within:

$$I(z, t) - I(z + dz, t) = \frac{\partial q_l}{\partial t} dz$$

**Step 3:** Use Taylor expansion: $I(z + dz, t) \approx I(z, t) + \frac{\partial I}{\partial z} dz$

$$I(z, t) - I(z, t) - \frac{\partial I}{\partial z} dz = \frac{\partial q_l}{\partial t} dz$$

$$- \frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial q_l}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4:** In the phasor (frequency) domain with $e^{j\omega t}$ convention, $\frac{\partial q_l}{\partial t} \to j\omega q_l$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\frac{dI}{dz} = -j\omega q_l}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**
- If $I(z) = $ constant (uniform current, as in a transmission line), then $q_l = 0$ → no charge accumulation → **no radiation**.
- If $I(z)$ is non-uniform (as in a dipole antenna), then $q_l \neq 0$ → time-varying charge → **radiation occurs**.

This equation is the **fundamental link** between current distribution and radiation in linear antennas. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing radiation with induction:** Many students write that "varying current induces a magnetic field" — this is only *part* of radiation. You must explicitly mention that the time-varying $\vec{H}$ induces a time-varying $\vec{E}$, which in turn sustains $\vec{H}$, and the *self-sustaining pair propagates outward*. (Lose 2 marks if missed.)
> 2. **Forgetting the retarded time:** When writing the vector potential, students often write $e^{j\omega t}$ instead of $e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$. The $-kr$ term is the heart of wave propagation. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Saying "charge radiates" without "accelerating charge":** Radiation requires *acceleration* — not just the *presence* of a charge. A stationary point charge has an $\vec{E}$ field but does NOT radiate. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Not mentioning $1/r$ vs. $1/r^2$:** The far field's $1/r$ dependence (versus near field's $1/r^2, 1/r^3$) is what physically distinguishes *radiated* energy from *stored* energy. Examiners explicitly test this. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Drawing the dipole wrong:** The arrow direction of current in both arms must be the *same* (toward the feed point or away from it on both arms), not opposite. Opposite currents → transmission line behaviour, no radiation. (Lose 1 mark.)

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 **Radiation requires accelerating charges**, not merely moving or stationary charges.
- 🔑 **Maxwell's displacement current** $\partial \vec{D}/\partial t$ is the mathematical reason radiation is possible.
- 🔑 **Retarded time** $t_r = t - r/c$ encodes the finite propagation speed; it is the origin of wave-like behavior.
- 🔑 **Retarded potentials** $\vec{A}$ and $V$ are the foundation for deriving all antenna fields.
- 🔑 **Continuity equation** in phasor form: $\frac{dI}{dz} = -j\omega q_l$ — non-uniform current is mandatory for radiation.
- 🔑 **Hertzian dipole** is the fundamental building block; all practical antennas are built from it.
- 🔑 **Far fields** of a dipole scale as $1/r$; $E_\theta = \eta_0 H_\phi$; they form a TEM wave propagating along $\hat{r}$.
- 🔑 **Transmission line ≠ Antenna:** Opposite-phase currents cancel radiation; in-phase separated currents add.
- 🔑 **Poynting vector** $\vec{S}_{avg} = \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta_0} \hat{r}$ gives the radiated power per unit area.
- 🔑 **Total radiated power** $P_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (I_0 dl/\lambda)^2$; **Radiation resistance** $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (dl/\lambda)^2$.
- 🔑 **Intrinsic impedance of free space** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω — memorize this constant.
- 🔑 **Near field ↔ Reactive / Stored Energy**; **Far field ↔ Radiated Energy** (boundary at $r = \lambda/2\pi$).
- 🔑 In the far field, $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{k}$ — a transverse electromagnetic (TEM) plane wave.
- 🔑 Antenna radiates *because* the source frequency makes electron oscillation fast enough that the retardation effect is significant — DC or low-frequency currents barely radiate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polarisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polarisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarisation of an antenna** is defined as the orientation of the **electric field (E-field) vector** of the radiated electromagnetic wave, traced as a function of time at a fixed observation point in space. It is a property of the **radiated wave** determined by the structure and excitation of the antenna, not the medium of propagation.

Mathematically, for a wave propagating in the **+z direction**, the instantaneous electric field can be decomposed into two orthogonal transverse components:

$$
\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_x\, E_x \cos(\omega t - \beta z) + \hat{a}_y\, E_y \cos(\omega t - \beta z + \delta)
$$

Where:
- **E_x, E_y** → Amplitudes of the x and y components
- **δ** → Phase difference between the two components
- **ω** → Angular frequency (**ω = 2πf**)
- **β** → Phase constant (**β = 2π/λ**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> 🎯 **Real-World Analogy — The "Spinning Rope" Model**
> 
> Imagine flicking a rope tied to a wall:
> - If you flick **straight up and down** → the wave oscillates in one plane → **Linear Polarisation**.
> - If you flick the rope in a **circular motion** (like stirring water with the rope) → the wave rotates as it travels → **Circular Polarisation**.
> - If you flick in a **tilted oval pattern** → the tip traces an ellipse → **Elliptical Polarisation**.

For a receiving antenna to extract maximum power, its polarisation must **match** the incoming wave's polarisation (similar to how a key must match a lock). A mismatch causes **Polarisation Loss**.

---

## 1.3 Three Principal Polarisation States

| Polarisation Type | Condition | Field Trace |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | δ = 0 or ±nπ (n = 0, 1, 2, ...) | A straight line |
| **Circular** | E_x = E_y AND δ = ±π/2 | A perfect circle |
| **Elliptical** | General case (E_x ≠ E_y, δ ≠ 0, ±π/2) | A tilted ellipse |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalised Theorem:** Every polarisation state (linear, circular, or elliptical) is a **special case of elliptical polarisation**. Linear and circular are merely degenerate ellipses.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Intrinsic Impedance of Free Space:** **η₀ = 377 Ω ≈ 120π Ω**
> - **Speed of Light:** **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Wavelength Relation:** **λ = c / f**
> - **Polarisation Loss Factor (PLF)** is a dimensionless quantity in **[0, 1]**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Polarisation Formation

The complete polarisation behaviour emerges from the **superposition of two orthogonal linearly polarised waves**. Let's break down the "Why" and "How":

1. **Why two components?** — In free space, the wave is TEM, so **E** must lie in the plane transverse to the direction of propagation. Any arbitrary transverse vector can be expressed in terms of **x̂** and **ŷ** basis vectors.

2. **Why a phase difference δ?** — If both components are in phase (δ = 0), they add vectorially along a fixed direction. Introducing δ causes the resultant to **rotate** as the wave propagates, generating the trace.

3. **Why trace a shape?** — At a fixed point in space, as **t** varies, the tip of the E-vector sweeps out a curve. This curve **IS** the polarisation.

---

## 2.2 The Three Cases — Deep Dive

### 🟦 Case 1: Linear Polarisation (δ = 0 or ±nπ)

The two components are in phase (or exactly out of phase). The resultant **E** vector oscillates along a single straight line at angle:

$$
\tau = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{E_y}{E_x}\right)
$$

The amplitude varies between ±√(E_x² + E_y²). **No rotation occurs.**

**Examples:** Hertzian dipole, half-wave dipole, monopole.

---

### 🟢 Case 2: Circular Polarisation (E_x = E_y, δ = ±π/2)

Two **equal-amplitude**, **quadrature-phase** (90° apart) components. The E-vector has **constant magnitude** = E_x = E_y, and rotates uniformly.

- **δ = +π/2 → Right-Hand Circular Polarisation (RHCP)** — Rotation is clockwise when viewed from the direction the wave is *coming from* (IEEE convention).
- **δ = −π/2 → Left-Hand Circular Polarisation (LHCP)** — Rotation is anticlockwise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEEE vs. Physics Convention Trap (KTU Exam Pitfall):**
> - **IEEE / KTU convention:** RHCP rotates **clockwise** when looking into the direction of propagation (i.e., wave coming *toward* you).
> - **Physics convention:** RHCP rotates **clockwise** when looking in the *direction of propagation* (wave going away).
> 
> Always clarify which convention your numerical problem uses!

**Examples:** Helical antenna, crossed-dipole with 90° feed, spiral antenna.

---

### 🟧 Case 3: Elliptical Polarisation (General Case)

The most general case. The E-vector traces an **ellipse** with a **tilt angle** and finite **axial ratio**. All real-world antennas (including imperfect circular polarisers) produce elliptical polarisation.

**Examples:** Reflector antennas with feed offset, satellite antennas under rain (depolarisation).

---

## 2.3 Polarisation Parameters — The Big Four

### 🔹 A. Axial Ratio (AR)

The ratio of the **major axis to the minor axis** of the polarisation ellipse:

$$
AR = \frac{\text{Major Axis } (a)}{\text{Minor Axis } (b)}
$$

| AR Value | Polarisation Type |
|---|---|
| **AR = 1** | Circular (perfect) |
| **1 < AR < ∞** | Elliptical |
| **AR = ∞** | Linear |

Often expressed in decibels:

$$
AR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(AR)
$$

> A good circularly polarised antenna has **AR ≤ 3 dB** (ideally ≤ 1 dB).

### 🔹 B. Tilt Angle (τ)

Angle between the major axis of the ellipse and the +x axis:

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2 E_x E_y \cos\delta}{E_x^2 - E_y^2}\right)
$$

### 🔹 C. Sense of Rotation
