Determined by the sign of **δ**:
- **δ > 0** (with E_y leading) → RHCP / RH elliptic (IEEE)
- **δ < 0** → LHCP / LH elliptic

### 🔹 D. Polarisation Efficiency / Loss Factor (PLF)

When Tx and Rx antennas are not perfectly polarisation-matched:

$$
PLF = \left\vert \hat{\rho}_w \cdot \hat{\rho}_a^* \right\vert^2 = \cos^2(\psi_p)
$$

Where:
- **ρ̂_w** → Unit polarisation vector of the incoming wave
- **ρ̂_a** → Unit polarisation vector of the receiving antenna
- **ψ_p** → Polarisation mismatch angle

The **received power** is:

$$
P_r = PLF \cdot P_{\max} = P_{\max} \cos^2(\psi_p)
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{E} = \hat{x} E_x \cos(\omega t - \beta z) + \hat{y} E_y \cos(\omega t - \beta z + \delta)$ | General E-field of a TEM wave | V/m |
| 2 | $AR = \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{E_{max}}{E_{min}}$ | Axial Ratio of polarisation ellipse | dimensionless |
| 3 | $AR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(AR)$ | AR in decibels | dB |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2 E_x E_y \cos\delta}{E_x^2 - E_y^2}\right)$ | Tilt angle of polarisation ellipse | radians/degrees |
| 5 | $PLF = \cos^2(\psi_p)$ | Polarisation Loss Factor | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | $P_r = P_{max}\cos^2(\psi_p)$ | Received power (Friis-modified) | Watts |
| 7 | $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Phase constant | rad/m |
| 8 | $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | Ohms |

> ⚠️ **KTU Table Format Reminder:** All absolute-value bars have been written using the inline notation (e.g., $\vert \hat{\rho}_w \cdot \hat{\rho}_a^* \vert^2$) to keep them inside the LaTeX math environment and out of the markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Satellite Communications:** GPS uses **RHCP** to avoid the **Faraday rotation** effect in the ionosphere.
> - **Radar:** Cross-polarisation measurement (sending RHCP, receiving LHCP) reveals **target shape and rainfall rate**.
> - **Mobile/Wi-Fi:** Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) systems exploit different polarisations to **multiply channel capacity**.
> - **Radio Astronomy:** Linear polarisation mapping reveals **magnetic field orientation** in celestial sources.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Equation of the Polarisation Ellipse

We start from the two orthogonal components of the electric field at a fixed point (z = 0 for simplicity):

$$
E_x(t) = E_x \cos(\omega t)
$$
$$
E_y(t) = E_y \cos(\omega t + \delta)
$$

**Step 1:** Expand E_y using the cosine addition identity.

$$
E_y(t) = E_y [\cos(\omega t)\cos\delta - \sin(\omega t)\sin\delta]
$$

**Step 2:** From the first equation, isolate $\cos(\omega t)$ and $\sin(\omega t)$.

$$
\cos(\omega t) = \frac{E_x(t)}{E_x}
$$

**Step 3:** Derive $\sin(\omega t)$ by substituting back into the E_x² relation. We use:

$$
\cos^2(\omega t) + \sin^2(\omega t) = 1
$$

Which gives:

$$
\sin(\omega t) = \pm \sqrt{1 - \frac{E_x^2(t)}{E_x^2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\cos(\omega t)$ and $\sin(\omega t)$ into the E_y equation.

$$
E_y(t) = E_y \left[ \frac{E_x(t)}{E_x}\cos\delta - \sin(\omega t)\sin\delta \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\sin(\omega t)$ explicitly.

$$
E_y(t) = E_y \left[ \frac{E_x(t)}{E_x}\cos\delta \mp \sqrt{1 - \frac{E_x^2(t)}{E_x^2}}\,\sin\delta \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate the square-root term on one side.

$$
E_y(t) - \frac{E_y E_x(t)\cos\delta}{E_x} = \mp E_y\sin\delta \sqrt{1 - \frac{E_x^2(t)}{E_x^2}}
$$

**Step 7:** Square both sides to eliminate the square root.

$$
\left[ E_y - \frac{E_y E_x \cos\delta}{E_x} \right]^2 = E_y^2 \sin^2\delta \left[ 1 - \frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2} \right]
$$

**Step 8:** Expand and divide through by the common factor $E_y^2$ (assuming E_y ≠ 0).

$$
\left[ 1 - \frac{E_x \cos\delta}{E_x} \right]^2 = \sin^2\delta \left[ 1 - \frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2} \right]
$$

**Step 9:** Multiply out both sides.

$$
1 - \frac{2 E_x \cos\delta}{E_x} + \frac{E_x^2 \cos^2\delta}{E_x^2} = \sin^2\delta - \frac{E_x^2 \sin^2\delta}{E_x^2}
$$

**Step 10:** Collect terms containing $E_x^2 / E_x^2$ and $E_x / E_x$, and apply the identity $\sin^2\delta = 1 - \cos^2\delta$.

$$
\frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2}(\cos^2\delta + \sin^2\delta) - \frac{2 E_x \cos\delta}{E_x} + (1 - \sin^2\delta) = \sin^2\delta
$$

**Step 11:** Simplify using $\cos^2\delta + \sin^2\delta = 1$.

$$
\frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2} - \frac{2 E_x \cos\delta}{E_x} + 1 - \sin^2\delta = \sin^2\delta
$$

**Step 12:** Subtract $\sin^2\delta$ from both sides.

$$
\frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2} - \frac{2 E_x \cos\delta}{E_x} + 1 = 2\sin^2\delta
$$

**Step 13:** Using $2\sin^2\delta = 1 - \cos 2\delta$, write the final ellipse equation in canonical form:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2} - \frac{2 E_x E_y \cos\delta}{E_x E_y} + \frac{E_y^2}{E_y^2} = \sin^2\delta\;}
$$

> This is the **equation of a tilted ellipse** in the E_x–E_y plane — the **polarisation ellipse**. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Axial Ratio (AR)

The polarisation ellipse can be characterised by its major axis **a**, minor axis **b**, and tilt **τ**. The Axial Ratio is:

$$
AR = \frac{a}{b}
$$

Using the relationship between the ellipse parameters and the field components:

$$
AR = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}} = \frac{E_x^2 + E_y^2 + \sqrt{E_x^4 + E_y^4 + 2 E_x^2 E_y^2 \cos(2\delta)}}{E_x^2 + E_y^2 - \sqrt{E_x^4 + E_y^4 + 2 E_x^2 E_y^2 \cos(2\delta)}}
$$

> This is the **general form** used in KTU numericals when E_x ≠ E_y.

**Special Cases:**

- **Linear:** $\delta = 0$ → $E_{min} = 0$ → $AR = \infty$ ✅
- **Circular:** $E_x = E_y$ and $\delta = \pm \pi/2$ → numerator = denominator → $AR = 1$ ✅
- **Elliptical:** $1 < AR < \infty$ ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Polarisation Loss Factor (PLF)

Consider a transmit antenna with unit polarisation vector $\hat{\rho}_w$ (polarisation of the wave) and a receive antenna with $\hat{\rho}_a$. The induced voltage in the receive antenna is proportional to the **dot product** of these vectors:

$$
V_{oc} \propto \hat{\rho}_a^* \cdot \hat{\rho}_w
$$

**Step 1:** Maximum power transfer happens when $\hat{\rho}_a = \hat{\rho}_w$ (perfect match).

$$
P_{max} \propto \left\vert \hat{\rho}_a^* \cdot \hat{\rho}_w \right\vert^2_{max} = 1
$$

**Step 2:** Define the **polarisation mismatch angle** $\psi_p$ as the angle between $\hat{\rho}_a$ and $\hat{\rho}_w$ on the Poincaré sphere.

**Step 3:** The dot product simplifies to $\cos(\psi_p)$:

$$
\hat{\rho}_a^* \cdot \hat{\rho}_w = \cos(\psi_p)
$$

**Step 4:** The Polarisation Loss Factor is the squared magnitude:

$$
\boxed{\;PLF = \cos^2(\psi_p)\;}
$$

**Step 5:** The effective Friis transmission equation with polarisation loss:

$$
P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2 \cdot \cos^2(\psi_p)
$$

> For matched polarisation: $\psi_p = 0$ → PLF = 1 (no loss). For orthogonal polarisation: $\psi_p = 90°$ → PLF = 0 (total loss, **polarisation diversity** principle).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An antenna radiates with E_x = 3 V/m, E_y = 4 V/m, and δ = 60°. Determine the **Axial Ratio (AR)** and the **tilt angle τ**.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $E_x^2 + E_y^2 = 9 + 16 = 25$ V²/m².

Step 2: Compute $E_x^4 + E_y^4 = 81 + 256 = 337$ V⁴/m⁴.

Step 3: Compute $2 E_x^2 E_y^2 \cos(2\delta) = 2(9)(16)\cos(120°) = 288 \times (-0.5) = -144$.

Step 4: Sum under the square root: $337 - 144 = 193$.

Step 5: $\sqrt{193} \approx 13.89$.

Step 6: Numerator: $25 + 13.89 = 38.89$. Denominator: $25 - 13.89 = 11.11$.

Step 7: $AR = 38.89 / 11.11 \approx 3.50$ → $AR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3.50) \approx 10.88$ dB.

Step 8: Tilt angle:

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2(3)(4)\cos 60°}{9 - 16}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{-7}\right)
$$

Since the argument is negative, the tilt is in the second quadrant:

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{2}(180° - 59.74°) \approx 60.13°
$$

✅ **Result:** AR ≈ 3.5 (elliptical), τ ≈ 60.1°.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Tree: Classifying Polarisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Antenna Radiating TEM Wave] --> B{Phase Difference delta}
    B -->|delta = 0 or n pi| C[LINEAR Polarisation]
    B -->|delta = plus or minus 90 deg| D{Equal Amplitudes?}
    B -->|General Case| E[ELLIPTICAL Polarisation]
    D -->|E_x = E_y| F[CIRCULAR Polarisation]
    D -->|E_x not equal E_y| E
    C --> C1[Examples: Dipole, Monopole]
    F --> F1{Sign of delta}
    F1 -->|delta = plus 90| G[RHCP - IEEE]
    F1 -->|delta = minus 90| H[LHCP - IEEE]
    G --> G1[Example: Helical Antenna RH Mode]
    H --> H1[Example: Helical Antenna LH Mode]
    E --> E1[Example: Reflector with Offset Feed]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4cc
    style F fill:#d4f4dd
    style E fill:#ffd6d6
    style G fill:#cce5ff
    style H fill:#ffccff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Polarisation Loss in a Communication Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transmit Antenna] -->|Polarisation rho_w| CH[Channel / Free Space]
    CH --> RX[Receive Antenna]
    RX -->|Polarisation rho_a| RL[Receiver Load]
    TX -.->|P_t, G_t| M1[Matched: PLF = 1]
    RX -.->|G_r| M1
    TX -.->|Mismatched| M2[PLF = cos squared of psi_p]
    M1 --> OUT1[Maximum Power Delivered]
    M2 --> OUT2[Reduced Power: P_r = P_max times cos squared psi_p]
    style TX fill:#b3e5fc
    style RX fill:#b3e5fc
    style M1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style M2 fill:#ffccbc
    style OUT1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style OUT2 fill:#ffccbc
```

## 4.3 The Polarisation Ellipse — Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Field Vector] --> B[Traces Path Over One Cycle]
    B --> C{Tip of E vector}
    C --> D[Major Axis a]
    C --> E[Minor Axis b]
    C --> F[Tilt Angle tau from x axis]
    C --> G[Sense of Rotation: RH or LH]
    D --> AR[Axial Ratio AR = a over b]
    E --> AR
    AR --> AR1[AR = 1: Circular]
    AR --> AR2[1 less than AR less than infinity: Elliptical]
    AR --> AR3[AR = infinity: Linear]
    style A fill:#fff9c4
    style AR fill:#e1bee7
    style AR1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style AR2 fill:#fff59d
    style AR3 fill:#ffcdd2
```

## 4.4 Mapping to the Poincaré Sphere (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Poincare Sphere Representation] --> B[Each Point: One Polarisation State]
    B --> C[North Pole: RHCP]
    B --> D[South Pole: LHCP]
    B --> E[Equator: All Linear Polarisations]
    E --> E1[0 deg: Horizontal Linear]
    E --> E2[90 deg: Vertical Linear]
    E --> E3[45 deg: Diagonal Linear]
    C --> F[Latitude: Sense of Rotation]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Longitude: Tilt Angle of Linear]
    B --> H[Angular Distance 2 times psi_p Between Two Points]
    H --> I[PLF = cos squared psi_p]
    style A fill:#d1c4e9
    style C fill:#ffccbc
    style D fill:#b2dfdb
    style E fill:#fff9c4
    style I fill:#c8e6c9
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Poincaré sphere** is a powerful geometric tool. The angular distance between any two points on the sphere equals **twice the polarisation mismatch angle ψ_p**. The shorter the arc, the better the match.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definitions)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **Define polarisation of an antenna. What is meant by linear polarisation?**

**Model Answer:**
Polarisation of an antenna is the orientation of the **electric field vector** of the radiated electromagnetic wave, observed as a function of time at a fixed point in space. It describes the locus traced by the tip of the E-vector.

In **linear polarisation**, the electric field oscillates along a single straight line. This occurs when the two orthogonal components of the E-field are either in phase ($\delta = 0$) or exactly $180°$ out of phase ($\delta = \pm n\pi$). Examples: a half-wave dipole, a monopole antenna.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
> **What is the Axial Ratio (AR)? What does AR = 1 signify?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Axial Ratio (AR)** is defined as the ratio of the major axis to the minor axis of the polarisation ellipse traced by the E-field vector:

$$
AR = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{E_{max}}{E_{min}}
$$

- **AR = 1** → The major and minor axes are equal → the E-field traces a **circle** → perfect **circular polarisation**.
- **AR = ∞** → The minor axis vanishes → the field is **linearly polarised**.
- **1 < AR < ∞** → **Elliptical polarisation** (general case).

AR is also expressed in dB: $AR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(AR)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🟦 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Derive the equation of the polarisation ellipse for a plane wave propagating in the +z direction. From the result, identify the conditions for **linear** and **circular** polarisation. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A wave has E_x = 5 V/m, E_y = 5 V/m, δ = 90°. Identify the polarisation. Compute its Axial Ratio in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a):

**Step 1:** Write the two orthogonal field components at a fixed point in space (z = 0):

$$
E_x(t) = E_x \cos(\omega t) \quad ; \quad E_y(t) = E_y \cos(\omega t + \delta)
$$

**Step 2:** Eliminate $\omega t$ by solving the E_x equation for $\cos(\omega t) = E_x(t)/E_x$ and using the identity $\sin^2(\omega t) = 1 - \cos^2(\omega t)$ in the E_y expansion:

$$
E_y(t) = E_y \left[ \frac{E_x(t)}{E_x}\cos\delta \mp \sqrt{1-\frac{E_x^2(t)}{E_x^2}}\,\sin\delta \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides and simplify using $\sin^2\delta + \cos^2\delta = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{E_x^2}{E_x^2} - \frac{2 E_x E_y \cos\delta}{E_x E_y} + \frac{E_y^2}{E_y^2} = \sin^2\delta\;}
$$

This is the **equation of a tilted ellipse** in the E_x–E_y plane — the **polarisation ellipse**.

**Conditions:**
- **Linear:** $\delta = 0$ or $\pm n\pi$ → $\sin^2\delta = 0$ → equation reduces to a straight line: $E_y = (E_y/E_x) E_x$.
- **Circular:** $E_x = E_y$ AND $\delta = \pm \pi/2$ → the ellipse becomes a circle of radius E_x.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Step 1:** Identify the polarisation.
Since E_x = E_y = 5 V/m and δ = 90°, the wave is **circularly polarised**.
- δ = +90° → **Right-Hand Circular Polarisation (RHCP)** (IEEE convention).

**Step 2:** Compute the Axial Ratio.
For circular polarisation, the major axis = minor axis:

$$
AR = 1
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to dB:

$$
AR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1) = 0\ \text{dB}
$$

✅ **Result:** RHCP, AR = 0 dB.

---

### 🟩 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Polarisation Loss Factor (PLF)**. Derive the expression $P_r = P_{max}\cos^2(\psi_p)$ for a polarisation-mismatched link. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A linearly polarised transmit antenna (oriented at +45° to the x-axis) illuminates a linearly polarised receive antenna (oriented at +30° to the x-axis). Find the polarisation loss in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Step 1:** Define PLF.
The **Polarisation Loss Factor (PLF)** quantifies the loss in received power due to mismatch between the polarisation of the incoming wave ($\hat{\rho}_w$) and the polarisation of the receiving antenna ($\hat{\rho}_a$).

**Step 2:** Open-circuit induced voltage in receive antenna is proportional to the dot product:

$$
V_{oc} \propto \hat{\rho}_a^* \cdot \hat{\rho}_w
$$

**Step 3:** When perfectly matched ($\hat{\rho}_a = \hat{\rho}_w$), the maximum power delivered is $P_{max}$. With mismatch, the delivered power is reduced by the squared magnitude of the dot product.

**Step 4:** Define the **polarisation mismatch angle** $\psi_p$ as the angle between $\hat{\rho}_a$ and $\hat{\rho}_w$. The dot product equals $\cos(\psi_p)$:

$$
PLF = \left\vert \hat{\rho}_a^* \cdot \hat{\rho}_w \right\vert^2 = \cos^2(\psi_p)
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the received power is:

$$
\boxed{\;P_r = P_{max} \cos^2(\psi_p)\;}
$$

**Special cases:**
- $\psi_p = 0°$ → $P_r = P_{max}$ (perfect match).
- $\psi_p = 90°$ → $P_r = 0$ (orthogonal polarisations — total loss).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Step 1:** The polarisation mismatch angle is the difference in tilt:

$$
\psi_p = 45° - 30° = 15°
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Polarisation Loss Factor:

$$
PLF = \cos^2(15°) = (0.9659)^2 \approx 0.9330
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to dB:

$$
PLF_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.9330) \approx -0.30\ \text{dB}
$$

✅ **Result:** The polarisation loss is approximately **0.30 dB**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in Polarisation Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing IEEE vs. Physics Convention for Handedness** — A student loses 1–2 marks for writing "δ = +90° → LHCP" instead of "RHCP (IEEE)". **Always state the convention explicitly.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert AR to dB** — The question often asks "AR in dB" but the student writes only the linear ratio. **Step 3 conversion is mandatory.**
> 
> 3. **Sign Error in Tilt Angle Formula** — The argument of $\tan^{-1}$ can be negative, putting the angle in the **second quadrant** (τ > 45°). Don't blindly use the calculator's principal value.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the Squared Term in PLF** — Writing $P_r = P_{max}\cos(\psi_p)$ (missing the square) is a 1-mark error. The dot product is squared to yield a power ratio.
> 
> 5. **Mixing AR with Tilt** — They are two different ellipse parameters. AR describes the *shape*; τ describes the *orientation*. Don't substitute one for the other.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> **🧠 Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> - ⭐ Polarisation is a **wave property**, defined by the **E-field trace** at a fixed point.
> - ⭐ Three types: **Linear (δ = nπ)**, **Circular (E_x = E_y, δ = ±π/2)**, **Elliptical (general)**.
> - ⭐ **Axial Ratio (AR):** $AR = a/b$. AR = 1 → circular, AR = ∞ → linear.
> - ⭐ **Tilt angle τ:** Orientation of the major axis of the polarisation ellipse.
> - ⭐ **PLF:** $PLF = \cos^2(\psi_p)$. Maximum power transfer requires ψ_p = 0.
> - ⭐ **IEEE Convention:** δ = +90° → RHCP (clockwise when looking *into* the direction of propagation).
> - ⭐ **GPS, satellite links** use **RHCP** to bypass ionospheric Faraday rotation.
> - ⭐ The general **polarisation ellipse equation** is the heart of derivations — know every step.
> - ⭐ **PLF = 0** (orthogonal polarisations) is the basis of **polarisation diversity** in MIMO.
> - ⭐ **Cross-polarisation discrimination (XPD)** measures how well an antenna rejects the unwanted orthogonal component — critical in dual-polarised systems.
> - ⭐ A perfect circular polarisation has **AR = 0 dB**; practical antennas aim for **AR ≤ 3 dB** within their bandwidth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Antenna Engineering**, "Types" refers to the systematic classification of radiating structures based on their **geometry, operating frequency band, radiation mechanism, and directivity characteristics**. Antennas are broadly categorized into **Wire Antennas, Aperture Antennas, Microstrip (Patch) Antennas, Array Antennas, Reflector Antennas, and Lens Antennas**. Each type is engineered to optimize a specific set of electromagnetic parameters such as **gain, bandwidth, polarization, and beamwidth** for a designated application (e.g., cellular base stations, satellite communication, radar). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes understanding the **physical construction**, **radiation pattern**, and **field equations** of the major antenna families, with specific stress on **Dipole, Loop, Helical, Horn, Parabolic Reflector, and Microstrip Patch** antennas. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you need to **shine a flashlight beam** in a dark room. You can use different types of bulbs and reflectors: - A **bare bulb (dipole antenna)** sends light everywhere equally (omnidirectional). - A **bulb in a small parabolic dish (horn/reflector antenna)** focuses light into a narrow, powerful beam (high gain, directional). - A **strip of LEDs on a flexible strip (microstrip antenna)** is flat, cheap, and good for mounting on a phone's circuit board. - **Stacking many bulbs in a row (array antenna)** allows you to electronically steer and focus the beam. Antenna "Types" are essentially the engineer's toolbox, where each design is a specialized "light source" tailored for a specific electromagnetic environment. > [!NOTE] > **Core Principle:** Every antenna, regardless of type, obeys the **conservation of energy** and **reciprocity theorem**. If a structure is a good transmitter, it is also a good receiver at the same frequency. --- ### 1.3 Classification Tree (High-Level Overview) 1. **Wire Antennas** — constructed from conducting wires/rods. 2. **Aperture Antennas** — radiate through an opening/aperture. 3. **Reflector Antennas** — use a reflecting surface to focus EM waves. 4. **Lens Antennas** — use a lens to collimate EM waves. 5. **Microstrip (Patch) Antennas** — printed on a dielectric substrate. 6. **Array Antennas** — combination of multiple radiating elements. 7. **Broadband/Special Antennas** — frequency-independent designs (Log-periodic, Spiral). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Wire Antennas #### 2.1.1 Infinitesimal Dipole An **infinitesimal dipole** is a hypothetical, vanishingly short linear antenna ($L \ll \lambda$) with a uniform current distribution. It is the building block for analyzing all wire antennas. **Key Parameters:** - **Radiation Resistance ($R_{rad}$):** $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2\left(\dfrac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$ **Ω** - **Directivity ($D$):** $D = 1.5$ (or **1.76 dBi**) - **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW):** $90°$ #### 2.1.2 Half-Wave Dipole A **half-wave dipole** has a total length $L = \lambda/2$. It is the most widely used wire antenna. - **Input Impedance:** $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j42.5$ **Ω** (resonant at $L=0.47\lambda$ to remove reactance). - **Directivity:** $D = 1.64$ (or **2.15 dBi**). - **HPBW:** $78°$ (in the E-plane). - **Radiation Pattern:** Figure-8 in the E-plane, omnidirectional in the H-plane. #### 2.1.3 Quarter-Wave Monopole A **quarter-wave monopole** ($L = \lambda/4$) erected over a perfectly conducting ground plane. - **Input Impedance:** $Z_{in} \approx 36.5 + j21$ **Ω** (half of dipole due to image theory). - **Directivity:** $D = 3.28$ (or **5.15 dBi**). - **Gain over isotropic in upper hemisphere:** ~**5.15 dBi** (since image doubles effective radiated power). > [!NOTE] > **Image Theory:** A monopole over a perfect ground plane is mathematically equivalent to a full dipole. The ground plane "mirrors" the monopole, creating a virtual second arm. #### 2.1.4 Loop Antenna A **small loop antenna** has circumference $C \ll \lambda$ (typically $C < 0.1\lambda$). - **Radiation Resistance:** $R_{rad} = 31{,}200\left(\dfrac{A}{\lambda^2}\right)^2$ **Ω** - **Far-Field Pattern:** Same shape as an infinitesimal dipole (figure-8) but with **E and H fields interchanged** (electrically dual). - **Polarization:** Magnetic in H-plane, linear in all other planes. #### 2.1.5 Helical Antenna A **helical antenna** consists of a conducting wire wound in a helix. It operates in two modes: - **Normal Mode ($D \ll \lambda$):** Broadside radiation, elliptical polarization, low gain. - **Axial Mode ($C_\lambda \approx 1$, $S \approx \lambda/4$):** End-fire radiation, **circular polarization**, high gain. **Gain (Axial Mode):** $$G \approx 15\left(\frac{C_\lambda^2 N S_\lambda}{\lambda}\right)$$ **HPBW (Axial Mode):** $$HPBW \approx \frac{52}{C_\lambda \sqrt{N S_\lambda}} \text{ degrees}$$ where $C_\lambda$ = circumference in wavelengths, $N$ = number of turns, $S_\lambda$ = spacing in wavelengths. --- ### 2.2 Aperture Antennas #### 2.2.1 Horn Antenna A **horn antenna** is a flared waveguide. It is the most common aperture antenna. - **Gain ($G$):** $$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi \varepsilon_{ap} A_p}{\lambda^2}$$ where $A_e$ = effective aperture, $A_p$ = physical aperture, $\varepsilon_{ap}$ = **aperture efficiency** (typically 0.5–0.6 for pyramidal horns). - **Directivity of Pyramidal Horn:** $$D_0 \approx \frac{10.7}{0.001\, a\, b / \lambda^2}$$ where $a, b$ are horn aperture dimensions in wavelengths. - **HPBW (E-plane):** $HPBW_E \approx 51°\, \lambda/a$ - **HPBW (H-plane):** $HPBW_H \approx 68°\, \lambda/b$ #### 2.2.2 Reflector Antennas (Parabolic Dish) A **parabolic reflector** focuses parallel rays to a single focal point. - **Gain:** $$G = \frac{4\pi A \varepsilon_{ap}}{\lambda^2} = \varepsilon_{ap} \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$$ where $D$ = dish diameter. - **3 dB Beamwidth:** $$\theta_3 \approx 70° \frac{\lambda}{D}$$ - **Focal Length:** $f = \dfrac{D^2}{16 d}$, where $d$ is the depth. --- ### 2.3 Microstrip (Patch) Antennas A **microstrip patch antenna** is a low-profile, planar antenna consisting of a metallic patch on a grounded dielectric substrate. - **Resonant Length (for $TM_{10}$ mode):** $$L \approx \frac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}}$$ - **Effective Dielectric Constant:** $$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2} \left[1 + 12\frac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2}$$ - **Width:** $$W = \frac{c}{2 f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$$ - **Bandwidth:** Narrow, typically **2–5%** of center frequency. - **Polarization:** Linear (default), circular (with truncated corners or dual feed). --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Antenna Type | Key Formula | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Infinitesimal Dipole | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2(dl/\lambda)^2$ | $D = 1.5$ (1.76 dBi) | | Half-Wave Dipole | $Z_{in} \approx 73$ **Ω** | $D = 1.64$ (2.15 dBi) | | Quarter-Wave Monopole | $Z_{in} \approx 36.5$ **Ω** | $D = 3.28$ (5.15 dBi) | | Small Loop | $R_{rad} = 31{,}200(A/\lambda^2)^2$ | $D = 1.5$ | | Helical (Axial) | $G \approx 15(C_\lambda^2 N S_\lambda)$ | High, CP | | Horn | $G = 4\pi \varepsilon_{ap} A_p / \lambda^2$ | $D = 10$–$25$ dBi | | Parabolic Reflector | $G = \varepsilon_{ap}(\pi D/\lambda)^2$ | $D = 20$–$50$ dBi | | Microstrip Patch | $L \approx \lambda/2\sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}$ | $D \approx 6$–$8$ dBi | --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wire antennas** dominate **HF/VHF broadcast, mobile handset, and Wi-Fi** applications. - **Horn antennas** are used as **feed elements for reflectors** and in **microwave test benches** as standard-gain references. - **Parabolic reflectors** are the workhorse of **satellite ground stations, radio astronomy (e.g., GBT), and deep-space communication (DSN)**. - **Microstrip patches** are critical in **aerospace (low-profile), GPS receivers, and 5G mmWave arrays** due to their planar, low-cost nature. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Radiation Resistance of an Infinitesimal Dipole **Step 1 — Starting from the far-field expression** The far electric field of an $z$-oriented infinitesimal dipole of length $dl$ carrying current $I_0$ is: $$E_\theta = j \frac{\eta_0 I_0 \, dl \, k}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ **Step 2 — Magnetic field using Faraday's Law in the far field** $$H_\phi = \frac{E_\theta}{\eta_0} = j \frac{I_0 \, dl \, k}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \, e^{-jkr}$$ **Step 3 — Time-average Poynting vector** $$S_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\{E_\theta H_\phi^*\} = \frac{\eta_0}{2}\left(\frac{I_0 \, dl \, k}{4\pi r}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta$$ **Step 4 — Total radiated power (integrate over a sphere of radius $r$)** $$P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} S_{avg} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$ $$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 (I_0 \, dl \, k)^2}{32\pi^2} \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the angular integral** The integral $\int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = \dfrac{4}{3}$. **Step 6 — Substitute $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ **Ω** and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$** $$P_{rad} = \frac{120\pi \cdot I_0^2 \, dl^2 \cdot 4\pi^2}{32\pi^2 \lambda^2} \cdot \frac{4}{3}$$ $$P_{rad} = \frac{120\pi \cdot I_0^2 \, dl^2 \cdot 4}{32\lambda^2} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 40\pi^2 I_0^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$$ **Step 7 — Equate to the standard $I^2 R$ form** $$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$$ Solving for $R_{rad}$: $$\boxed{R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \;\; \Omega}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This small value (a few ohms) explains why a very short dipole is a **poor radiator**. Lengthening the dipole to $\lambda/2$ increases $R_{rad}$ to ~73 Ω, making it efficient and easier to match to standard transmission lines. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Half-Power Beamwidth of a Parabolic Reflector **Step 1 — Standard approximation from antenna theory** For a circular aperture with uniform illumination, the 3 dB beamwidth (in radians) is: $$\theta_3 \approx 0.886 \frac{\lambda}{D}$$ **Step 2 — Real-world correction (typical aperture taper $\approx -10$ dB)** A tapered illumination broadens the beam slightly. The empirical formula widely used in practice is: $$\theta_3 \approx 70° \frac{\lambda}{D}$$ **Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example** For a **3-meter parabolic dish** operating at **10 GHz** ($\lambda = 0.03$ m): $$\theta_3 = 70° \times \frac{0.03}{3} = 0.7°$$ **Interpretation:** A 3 m dish at 10 GHz produces a pencil beam only **0.7° wide**, which is sharp enough to track geostationary satellites in the Clarke belt (36,000 km altitude). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Antenna? | Application | Frequency Band | Recommended Antenna | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | AM Broadcast | MF (530–1700 kHz) | Monopole mast | Vertical polarization, omnidirectional | | FM Radio / TV | VHF/UHF | Half-wave dipole / Yagi-Uda array | High gain, directional | | Wi-Fi Router | 2.4 / 5.8 GHz | Microstrip patch / dipole array | Compact, planar, low cost | | Satellite TV (DTH) | 11.7–12.2 GHz | Parabolic reflector | High gain required for downlink | | Radar Tracking | 1–40 GHz | Parabolic dish / phased array | Sharp beam, high power handling | | Spacecraft | UHF–Ka band | Horn / helical | Reliable, radiation-hardened | | GPS | 1.575 GHz | Circularly polarized patch | Matches RHCP satellite signal | | Radio Astronomy | 1–100 GHz | Large parabolic dish | Extreme gain, low noise | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Antenna Classification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Antenna Types"] --> B["Wire Antennas"] A --> C["Aperture Antennas"] A --> D["Reflector Antennas"] A --> E["Microstrip Patch Antennas"] A --> F["Array Antennas"] A --> G["Broadband Antennas"] B --> B1["Infinitesimal Dipole"] B --> B2["Half Wave Dipole"] B --> B3["Monopole"] B --> B4["Loop Antenna"] B --> B5["Helical Antenna"] C --> C1["Horn Antenna"] C --> C2["Slot Antenna"] C --> C3["Open Ended Waveguide"] D --> D1["Parabolic Reflector"] D --> D2["Cassegrain Feed"] D --> D3["Corner Reflector"] E --> E1["Rectangular Patch"] E --> E2["Circular Patch"] E --> E3["Array of Patches"] F --> F1["Linear Array"] F --> F2["Planar Array"] F --> F3["Phased Array"] G --> G1["Log Periodic"] G --> G2["Spiral Antenna"] G --> G3["Biconical"] ``` --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Parabolic Reflector System ```mermaid flowchart LR TX["Transmitter / LNA"] --> FEED["Feed Horn
at Focal Point"] FEED --> WAVE["Spherical Wavefront"] WAVE --> DISH["Parabolic Reflector
Diameter D"] DISH --> PLANE["Planar Wavefront
Collimated Beam"] PLANE --> TARGET["Target / Satellite"] TARGET --> PLANE2["Incoming Plane Wave"] PLANE2 --> DISH2["Parabolic Reflector"] DISH2 --> WAVE2["Converging Wavefront"] WAVE2 --> FEED2["Feed Horn"] FEED2 --> RX["Receiver"] style DISH fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style DISH2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style FEED fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style FEED2 fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.3 Microstrip Patch Antenna Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP["Patch Length L
Width W"] --> SUB["Substrate
Thickness h
Dielectric eps_r"] SUB --> GND["Ground Plane"] GND --> FEED["Feed Line
50 Ohm Microstrip"] FEED --> INS["Insulation Layer"] INS --> TOP RAD["Radiation Pattern"]:::rad TOP -.->|"TM10 Mode"| RAD classDef rad fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ### 4.4 Helical Antenna Mode Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR H1["Normal Mode Helix
D much less than lambda"]:::nm --> P1["Broadside Radiation
Elliptical Polarization
Low Gain"] H2["Axial Mode Helix
C approx lambda
S approx lambda by 4"]:::am --> P2["End Fire Radiation
Circular Polarization
High Gain"] classDef nm fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:2px classDef am fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Distinguish between **normal mode** and **axial mode** of operation of a helical antenna. Which mode is preferred for satellite communication and why? **Model Answer:** | Feature | Normal Mode | Axial Mode | |---|---|---| | Helix diameter | $D \ll \lambda$ | $D \approx \lambda/\pi$ | | Radiation direction | Broadside (perpendicular to helix axis) | End-fire (along helix axis) | | Polarization | Elliptical | Circular | | Gain | Low (typically < 5 dBi) | High (up to ~20 dBi) | | Bandwidth | Narrow | Wide (nearly 2:1) | **Axial mode is preferred for satellite communication** because of its **high gain, end-fire radiation, and circular polarization** (CP). CP is essential for satellite links because it mitigates Faraday rotation effects in the ionosphere, ensuring stable signal reception regardless of satellite orientation. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** A **microstrip patch antenna** is designed to resonate at 2.4 GHz. State any **three** reasons why its bandwidth is inherently limited, and mention one technique to enhance it. **Model Answer:** **Reasons for narrow bandwidth:** 1. **High Q-factor:** The resonant nature of the patch (analogous to a parallel-plate cavity) gives a very high quality factor, $Q \approx 100$–$200$, leading to a narrow impedance bandwidth (typically 2–5%). 2. **Surface wave excitation:** Power is trapped as surface waves in the dielectric substrate, reducing radiation efficiency and limiting the usable bandwidth. 3. **Probe/feed inductance:** The coaxial probe feed introduces a series inductance that further narrows the matched impedance bandwidth. **Technique to enhance bandwidth:** Use a **stacked patch configuration** (multiple patches on different substrate layers) or **aperture-coupled feeding**, which can extend bandwidth to **15–25%**. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) > **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts) from a choice of two. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and operation of a **half-wave dipole antenna**. Derive its **radiation resistance** and mention the values of its **directivity** and **HPBW**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the construction and radiation mechanism of a **parabolic reflector antenna**. Derive the **gain expression** in terms of the dish diameter $D$, operating wavelength $\lambda$, and aperture efficiency $\varepsilon_{ap}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** A **half-wave dipole** is a straight conducting wire of total length $L = \lambda/2$, fed at the center by a two-wire transmission line. **Construction & Operation:** - The antenna is excited by a source at the feed-point gap. - The current distribution follows a **sinusoidal standing-wave pattern**: $I(z) = I_0 \cos(\pi z / L)$, with a **maximum at the center** and **zero at the ends**. - The far-field electric field in the E-plane is: $$E_\theta = j \frac{\eta_0 I_0}{2\pi r} \left[ \frac{\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}\right] e^{-jkr}$$ **Radiation Resistance Derivation:** The time-average Poynting vector is integrated over a sphere: $$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2}|I_0|^2 R_{rad}$$ After integration (using $\int_0^\pi \dfrac{\cos^2(\pi/2 \cos\theta)}{\sin\theta} d\theta = 1.218$): $$R_{rad} = 60 \times 1.218 \times 2 = 73.08 \;\; \Omega$$ **Key Parameters:** - **Directivity:** $D = 1.64$ (or **2.15 dBi**) - **HPBW (E-plane):** **78°** - **Input Impedance (resonant):** **73 Ω** (real) - **Polarization:** Linear (along the dipole axis) **Part (b):** A **parabolic reflector antenna** consists of a **paraboloidal dish** (a surface of revolution generated by rotating a parabola about its axis) and a **feed antenna** (typically a horn or dipole) placed at the **focus**. **Radiation Mechanism:** 1. The feed radiates spherical waves. 2. Every ray emanating from the focus is reflected such that its **path length to any plane perpendicular to the axis is constant** (equal to $2f$). 3. This converts the spherical wavefront into a **collimated planar wavefront**, producing a highly directional beam. **Gain Derivation:** The **physical aperture area** of a circular dish is: $$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ The **effective aperture** is: $$A_e = \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot A_p = \varepsilon_{ap} \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ The gain of any antenna is related to effective aperture by: $$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^2} \cdot \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ $$\boxed{G = \varepsilon_{ap} \left( \frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2}$$ For a typical aperture efficiency of $\varepsilon_{ap} \approx 0.55$–$0.65$, the gain is: $$G \approx 0.55 \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$$ **Example:** A 5 m dish at 6 GHz ($\lambda = 0.05$ m) gives $G = 0.6 \times (\pi \times 100)^2 = 0.6 \times 98{,}696 \approx 59{,}217 \approx 47.7$ dBi. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the design equations of a **rectangular microstrip patch antenna** for the $TM_{10}$ mode. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With diagrams, explain the two modes of operation of a **helical antenna** and derive the **axial-mode gain** expression. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Design Equations of Rectangular Microstrip Patch** A rectangular microstrip patch has dimensions $L \times W$ on a substrate of thickness $h$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. **Step 1 — Width:** $$W = \frac{c}{2 f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r + 1}}$$ where $f_r$ = resonant frequency, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **Step 2 — Effective Dielectric Constant:** $$\varepsilon_{re} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left[1 + 12\frac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2}$$ **Step 3 — Effective Length (accounting for fringing):** $$L_{eff} = L + 2\Delta L$$ where the extension is: $$\Delta L = 0.412 h \frac{(\varepsilon_{re} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{re} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.8)}$$ **Step 4 — Resonant Length for $TM_{10}$ mode:** $$\boxed{L = \frac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{re}}} - 2\Delta L}$$ **Step 5 — Substrate thickness constraint** (to avoid surface waves): $$h \le \frac{0.3 c}{2\pi f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$ **Practical Example:** For $f_r = 2.4$ GHz, $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$ (FR-4), $h = 1.6$ mm: - $W \approx 38.0$ mm - $\varepsilon_{re} \approx 4.3$ - $L \approx 28.6$ mm **Part (b): Helical Antenna Modes and Axial Gain** (As discussed in Section 2.1.5) **Axial Mode Gain Derivation:** The beam solid angle of an axial-mode helix can be approximated as: $$\Omega_A \approx \frac{152}{C_\lambda^2 N S_\lambda} \text{ (steradians)}$$ Using $G = 4\pi / \Omega_A$: $$G = \frac{4\pi \cdot C_\lambda^2 N S_\lambda}{152}$$ $$\boxed{G \approx 15 \left(\frac{C_\lambda^2 N S_\lambda}{\lambda}\right)}$$ **Design conditions for axial mode:** - $3/4 \le C_\lambda \le 4/3$ - $S_\lambda \approx 0.12$–$0.15$ (optimal) - $N \ge 3$ for sufficient gain **HPBW of axial mode:** $$HPBW = \frac{52°}{C_\lambda \sqrt{N S_\lambda}}$$ --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > 1. **Confusing units in $R_{rad}$:** Forgetting that the formula $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2(dl/\lambda)^2$ yields a result in **ohms**, not dimensionless. Students often forget to express $dl/\lambda$ as a pure number. > 2. **Monopole vs Dipole confusion:** The monopole's input impedance is **half** that of a dipole (image theory), and its directivity is **double** (5.15 dBi vs 2.15 dBi). Mixing these up costs easy marks. > 3. **Microstrip patch fringing:** Forgetting the extension $\Delta L$ leads to a resonant length off by **5–10%**, which is a very common error. Always include fringing corrections for accurate design. > 4. **Parabolic gain formula:** Writing $G = 4\pi A/\lambda^2$ without the **aperture efficiency** $\varepsilon_{ap}$ is treated as incomplete by KTU examiners — they expect $\varepsilon_{ap} \in [0, 1]$ explicitly. > 5. **Helical antenna units:** Mixing $C_\lambda$ (dimensionless) with $C$ (in meters) is a frequent error. Always normalize to wavelengths. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Half-wave dipole** is the **reference** antenna: $Z_{in} \approx 73$ **Ω**, $D = 2.15$ dBi, HPBW = $78°$. - **Quarter-wave monopole** has **half the impedance** and **double the directivity** of a dipole due to image theory. - **Small loop** is the **magnetic dual** of a dipole — same pattern shape but interchanged E/H fields. - **Helical antenna in axial mode** gives **circular polarization** — critical for satellite and space communication. - **Horn antenna** is essentially a **flared waveguide**; gain scales with aperture area. - **Parabolic reflector gain** = $\varepsilon_{ap}(\pi D/\lambda)^2$ — large dishes give very high gain. - **Microstrip patch** is **low-profile and cheap** but suffers from **narrow bandwidth (2–5%)** and low efficiency. - **Image theory** underpins the monopole-over-ground-plane equivalence to a full dipole. - For **5G mmWave and aerospace**, microstrip patches and phased arrays dominate. - Always **include units** and **state assumptions** (e.g., perfect conductor, far-field) in KTU derivations.

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are "Applications" in Microwaves & Antennas?

In the context of **PCECT602 – Microwaves & Antennas (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, the topic *Applications* consolidates the practical engineering deployment of the theoretical antenna principles, microwave propagation phenomena, and radar/communication theory discussed in the earlier sub-modules. It is the *bridge* between abstract electromagnetic theory and the real-world wireless systems that form the backbone of modern civilization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Applications of Microwaves and Antennas* refer to the engineered deployment of electromagnetic wave systems operating in the microwave frequency range (**300 MHz to 300 GHz**) and the antenna structures used to launch, receive, or process these waves for purposes such as radar detection, satellite communication, navigation, remote sensing, industrial heating, medical therapy, and wireless networking.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voice in a Stadium" Intuition

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a massive cricket stadium with **100,000 people**. You want your friend sitting in the very last row to hear you clearly.

- **Without a microphone/PA system** → Your voice disperses in all directions, loses energy, and becomes inaudible. This is like an **isotropic radiator** wasting energy everywhere.
- **With a directional horn or parabolic speaker pointed at your friend** → Almost all acoustic energy is funneled toward a narrow direction. Your friend hears you perfectly even in a noisy stadium. This is the role of a **directional antenna** (like a parabolic dish or horn antenna).
- **The stadium's echoes and timing** → If your friend throws the ball back, you can judge *how far* they are by timing the round trip. This is the fundamental principle of **RADAR** (RAdio Detection And Ranging).

Microwaves are the "voice" of the electromagnetic spectrum, and antennas are the "megaphones and ears" that direct and capture this voice for practical use.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

| Engineering Domain | Real-World Microwave/Antenna Application | Frequency Band (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Aviation & Defense** | Airborne Radar, Missile Guidance | 8–12 GHz (X-band) |
| **Telecommunications** | 5G/6G cellular base stations | 0.7–39 GHz |
| **Broadcasting** | Direct-to-Home (DTH) TV | 12 GHz (Ku-band) |
| **Navigation** | GPS, GLONASS, NavIC | 1.2–1.6 GHz (L-band) |
| **Medical** | Microwave Hyperthermia, MRI | 2.45 GHz, 64 MHz |
| **Industrial** | Microwave ovens, drying, vulcanization | 2.45 GHz (ISM band) |
| **Space Research** | Deep-space telemetry, radiotelescopes | 1.4 GHz – 300 GHz |
| **Remote Sensing** | SAR, weather radar | 5–95 GHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Module 3 frequently features 14-mark questions that test a student's ability to *map a specific antenna type or microwave device to its real-world application and justify the choice using performance parameters* (gain, bandwidth, beamwidth, polarization). Memorizing at least 8–10 application scenarios is a high-yield strategy.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Applications — A Bird's-Eye View

The "Applications" topic can be cleanly partitioned into **four engineering families**:

1. **Communication Applications** → Satellite, terrestrial, point-to-point links
2. **Radar & Remote Sensing Applications** → Surveillance, weather, imaging
3. **Industrial & Scientific Applications** → Heating, spectroscopy, plasma generation
4. **Medical & Biological Applications** → Diagnostics, therapy, imaging

Each family is governed by a specific subset of antenna types and microwave source/transmission considerations, as detailed in the sections that follow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How an Application is Engineered

Every microwave/antenna application follows a **five-stage engineering chain**:

1. **Requirement Definition** → Range, resolution, data rate, mobility, environment
2. **Frequency Band Selection** → Regulatory allocation (ITU-R), propagation behavior
3. **Antenna Selection** → Gain, beamwidth, polarization, form factor
4. **Transmitter/Receiver Chain Design** → TWTA/SSPA, LNA, mixer, filter
5. **Link Budget Verification** → Using Friis / Radar Range equation to validate feasibility

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" behind each step:** A satellite link requires **high gain, narrow beam** (→ parabolic reflector) because the path loss over 36,000 km is enormous (~200 dB). A Wi-Fi router uses **moderate gain, wide beam** (→ patch or monopole) because the user moves around a room. **Frequency band selection follows from the application's range and bandwidth needs, not the other way around.**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation | Watts (W) | Communication link budget |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Antenna Gain from Effective Aperture | Dimensionless (linear) / dBi | Selecting antenna size for given gain |
| 3 | $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k T_s \, B \, (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$ | Radar Range Equation (monostatic) | meters (m) | Radar detection range |
| 4 | $\theta_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ | Half-Power Beamwidth (degrees) | degrees (°) | Antenna resolution / design |
| 5 | $BW = \dfrac{f_H - f_L}{f_c} \times 100\%$ | Fractional Bandwidth | % | Broadband antenna design |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$ | Directivity from Beam Solid Angle | Dimensionless | Comparing antenna concentration |
| 7 | $HPBW = \dfrac{0.886 \, \lambda}{D}$ | HPBW for circular aperture | radians | Parabolic dish sizing |
| 8 | $\eta_{aperture} = \dfrac{A_e}{A_{physical}}$ | Aperture Efficiency | 0 to 1 (or %) | Real antenna performance |
| 9 | $PL_{FS}(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-Space Path Loss | dB | Quick link budget |
| 10 | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_c}{c}$ | Doppler Frequency (radar) | Hz | MTI radar, speed guns |
| 11 | $P_{densed} = \dfrac{P_{abs}}{m} = 4.186 \times 10^6} \quad [W/kg]$ | Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) | W/kg | Medical/biomedical safety |
| 12 | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{58.4 \lambda}{D}$ | Reflector diameter approximation | meters | Satellite dish sizing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Integrity Rule:** Notice how the absolute-value-style notation and fractions have been written using `\vert`, `\dfrac`, and `\left( \right)` to avoid breaking the table cell syntax. The vertical bar character `|` is **strictly forbidden** inside table cells.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility Map

| Engineering Field | Why Microwave/Antenna Engineering is Indispensable |
|---|---|
| **5G/6G Networks** | Massive MIMO and mmWave (28/39 GHz) require precise beam-steering patch/array antennas |
| **ISRO / Space Missions** | Deep-space communication uses **high-gain parabolic reflectors** (e.g., 34-m beam waveguide at IDSN) with **X-band (8.4 GHz) and Ka-band (32 GHz)** |
| **Air Traffic Control** | Primary radar (2.7–2.9 GHz) and secondary radar (1030/1090 MHz) require **slotted waveguide arrays** with cosecant-squared patterns |
| **Medical Oncology** | Microwave hyperthermia uses **applicator antennas** at 434 MHz / 915 MHz to selectively heat tumor tissue |
| **Remote Sensing** | Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) uses antenna motion to synthesize a *virtual* huge aperture, achieving sub-meter resolution from space |
| **Automotive** | 77 GHz FMCW radar (Bosch, Continental) for adaptive cruise control and collision avoidance — *every modern car has 3–5 microwave radars* |
| **Defence** | Phased-array radar (AESA) on Su-30MKI, Rafale — electronically steered beams with microsecond reorientation |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Radar Range Equation (Monostatic)

The radar range equation is the **single most important formula** for any radar-related application question. It links transmitted power, antenna characteristics, target size, and receiver sensitivity to the maximum detectable range.

**Starting Assumptions (KTU Board Standard):**
- Isotropic radiator replaced by a directive antenna of gain $G$
- Target modeled as a point scatterer with radar cross-section $\sigma$ (in m²)
- Propagation is line-of-sight in free space
- Receiver minimum detectable signal is $S_{min} = k T_s B \cdot (S/N)_{min}$

**Step 1 — Power Density at Target Distance $R$:**

A transmit antenna of gain $G_t$ radiates total power $P_t$ isotropically first, then concentrates it into a beam. The power density (W/m²) at distance $R$ is:

$$S_t = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

*Logic:* The denominator $4\pi R^2$ is the surface area of a sphere of radius $R$. The numerator $P_t G_t$ is the effective radiated power in the beam direction.

**Step 2 — Power Intercepted by Target:**

The target intercepts power equal to the incident density times its radar cross-section $\sigma$:

$$P_{intercepted} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G_t \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

*Logic:* By definition, $\sigma$ is the area that *re-radiates* the captured power isotropically. It is *not* the physical area of the target — a small metal sphere and a stealthy aircraft can have radically different $\sigma$ values.

**Step 3 — Power Density Back at the Radar:**

The target re-radiates the intercepted power isotropically. The back-scattered density at the radar (distance $R$ again) is:

$$S_r = \frac{P_{intercepted}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2} = \frac{P_t G_t \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4}$$

**Step 4 — Power Captured by Receive Antenna:**

The receive antenna (same as transmit in monostatic radar, so $G_r = G_t = G$) captures this density over its effective aperture $A_e$:

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

*Substitution used:* $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ from the gain-aperture relation.

**Step 5 — Equating to Minimum Detectable Signal:**

For detection, $P_r$ must equal or exceed the receiver's noise floor:

$$P_{min} = k \, T_s \, B \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{min}$$

Therefore:

$$k T_s B \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{min} = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R_{max}^4}$$

**Step 6 — Solving for Maximum Range $R_{max}$:**

$$\boxed{R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, k \, T_s \, B \, (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice the **fourth-root** dependence on transmitted power. To *double* the radar range, you must increase $P_t$ by a factor of $2^4 = 16$. This is why real-world radar systems invest in **higher antenna gain** (a square-law improvement) rather than brute-force power amplification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Parabolic Reflector Diameter from Gain Requirement

**Problem archetype:** *A satellite ground station requires a receive antenna gain of 50 dBi at 12 GHz. Find the minimum parabolic dish diameter assuming 60% aperture efficiency.*

**Step 1 — Convert dBi to Linear Gain:**

$$G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(G_{linear}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_{linear} = 10^{G_{dBi}/10}$$

$$G_{linear} = 10^{50/10} = 10^5 = 100{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gain-Aperture Formula:**

$$G = \frac{4\pi \, \eta \, A_p}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi \, \eta \, (\pi D^2 / 4)}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \, \eta \, D^2}{\lambda^2}$$

*Logic:* For a circular dish, physical aperture $A_p = \pi D^2 / 4$, and effective aperture $A_e = \eta A_p$.

**Step 3 — Solve for Diameter D:**

$$D^2 = \frac{G \lambda^2}{\pi^2 \eta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{\lambda}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{G}{\eta}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Wavelength at 12 GHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \, m/s}{12 \times 10^9 \, Hz} = 0.025 \, m = 2.5 \, cm$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$D = \frac{0.025}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{10^5}{0.60}}$$

$$D = \frac{0.025}{3.1416} \times \sqrt{166{,}666.67}$$

$$D = 0.007958 \times 408.248$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 3.25 \, \text{meters}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world cross-check:** INSAT/GSAT DTH ground stations in India typically use **1.8 m to 3.7 m dishes** in the Ku-band. Our 3.25 m result falls squarely in this range, validating the engineering.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Antenna Type vs. Application

| Antenna Type | Key Electrical Property | Primary Application | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Half-wave Dipole** | $G = 2.15$ dBi, omnidirectional in H-plane | FM broadcast, reference antenna | Simple, cheap, broadband |
| **Yagi-Uda** | $G = 7\text{–}15$ dBi, highly directional | Terrestrial TV reception, RFID gates | High front-to-back ratio, low cost |
| **Helical (Axial Mode)** | Circular polarization, $G \approx 10$ dBi | Satellite telemetry, GNSS, deep space | CP rejects Faraday rotation in ionosphere |
| **Parabolic Reflector** | Very high gain (30–60 dBi) | Earth stations, radio telescopes, radar | Frequency-independent scaling |
| **Horn Antenna** | $G = 10\text{–}25$ dBi, wideband | Feed for reflectors, EMC test chambers | Handles high power, easy to fabricate |
| **Microstrip Patch** | Low profile, $G \approx 6$–9 dBi | Aircraft conformal arrays, 5G base stations | Can be etched on PCB, planar |
| **Slot Antenna** | Complementary to dipole, $G \approx 3$ dBi | Flush-mounted on aircraft fuselages | Conformal, no protrusion |
| **Log-Periodic Dipole** | Ultra-wideband, $G \approx 6$–8 dBi | EMC measurements, TV receive-only | 10:1 or wider bandwidth |
| **Lens Antenna** | $G = 20\text{–}40$ dBi | Millimeter-wave 5G, automotive radar | Low loss at mmWave vs reflector |
| **Phased Array** | Electronic beam steering | AESA radar, 5G MIMO, SATCOM-on-move | No mechanical inertia, microsecond re-steering |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Microwave Oven Design Logic

A **microwave oven** is the most ubiquitous microwave application. KTU 2024 often asks students to explain its working principle.

- **Frequency chosen:** **2.45 GHz** (ISM band, internationally allocated)
- **Wavelength inside oven:** $\lambda = c/f = 0.122$ m = **12.2 cm**
- **Oven cavity dimensions:** Typically ~30 cm × 30 cm × 25 cm — these are **2λ to 3λ** in each direction, producing a complex standing-wave mode pattern.
- **Mode stirrer (rotating fan):** Ensures the field is statistically uniform so all parts of the food receive equal exposure.
- **Penetration depth in water:** ~1.5 cm at 2.45 GHz → heating occurs throughout the food's volume, not just on the surface (unlike conventional heating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2.45 GHz specifically?** Because it is the **center of the ISM (Industrial, Scientific, Medical) band**, and at this frequency water has a peak in its dielectric loss factor, maximizing the absorption of microwave energy into food (mostly water content).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Satellite Communication Link

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end signal flow** in a typical Ku-band DTH satellite TV application. Each block represents a subsystem with a specific function, from baseband encoding on the ground to LNB output at the user's dish.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Studio[Studio Encoder\nMPEG-2/4] --> UplinkMod[Uplink Modulator\nQPSK/8PSK 14 GHz]
    UplinkMod --> HPA[High Power Amplifier\nTWTA 2 kW]
    HPA --> EarthAnt[Earth Station Antenna\n9-13 m Parabolic]
    EarthAnt -->|Uplink 14 GHz| Sat[Geostationary Satellite\nTransponder 36,000 km]
    Sat -->|Downlink 12 GHz| Beam[Footprint Beam\nEIRP 52 dBW]
    Beam --> UserDish[User Dish 60-90 cm\nParabolic Offset]
    UserDish --> LNB[Low Noise Block\nLNA + Mixer]
    LNB --> IFStage[IF Stage 950-2150 MHz]
    IFStage --> STB[Set Top Box\nQPSK Decoder]
    STB --> TV[HDMI Output to TV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from left to right. The ground segment (left side) encodes, modulates, amplifies, and transmits. The space segment (middle) receives, frequency-translates, amplifies, and re-radiates. The user segment (right side) captures, down-converts, demodulates, and decodes for display.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Monostatic Pulse Radar

The following diagram maps the **transmit-receive signal chain** inside a primary surveillance radar (PSR), as used at airports.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Trigger[Pulse Trigger\nPRF 1 kHz] --> Modulator[Modulator\nCharges PFN]
    Modulator --> Magnetron[Magnetron\nX-band 9.4 GHz]
    Magnetron --> Circulator[3-Port Circulator\nDuplexer]
    Circulator -->|TX pulse| AntSw[Antenna Feed\nHorn or Slotted WG]
    AntSw -->|Beam to sky| Target[Target Aircraft\nsigma = 1 m^2]
    Target -->|Echo return| AntSw
    AntSw --> Circulator
    Circulator --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier\nNF 2 dB]
    LNA --> Mixer[Mixer with STALO\n9.4 GHz local]
    Mixer --> IFamp[IF Amplifier 30 MHz]
    IFamp --> MatchFilter[Matched Filter\nPulse Compression]
    MatchFilter --> Detector[Envelope Detector]
    Detector --> Threshold[Threshold Comparator]
    Threshold --> Display[PPI Display / Plot Extractor]
    Trigger --> Display
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key engineering insight:** The **circulator** is the heart of monostatic radar — it allows a *single antenna* to both transmit a high-power pulse (MW) and receive a weak echo (μW) with >60 dB isolation. Without it, the transmit pulse would destroy the receiver.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Microwave Hyperthermia for Cancer Treatment

A modern **microwave hyperthermia system** for oncology uses multiple phased-array applicators to focus energy into a tumor while sparing surrounding healthy tissue.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Synth[Signal Synthesizer\n60-915 MHz] --> Div[1 to 8 Power Divider]
    Div --> Ch1[Channel 1 Amp\nPhase Shift 0 deg]
    Div --> Ch2[Channel 2 Amp\nPhase Shift 45 deg]
    Div --> Ch3[Channel 3 Amp\nPhase Shift 90 deg]
    Div --> Ch4[Channel 4 Amp\nPhase Shift 135 deg]
    Ch1 --> App1[Applicator 1\nWaveguide Slot]
    Ch2 --> App2[Applicator 2\nWaveguide Slot]
    Ch3 --> App3[Applicator 3\nWaveguide Slot]
    Ch4 --> App4[Applicator 4\nWaveguide Slot]
    App1 --> Tissue[Patient Tissue\nTumor at Focus]
    App2 --> Tissue
    App3 --> Tissue
    App4 --> Tissue
    Tissue --> Probes[Thermal Probes\nFeedback Sensors]
    Probes --> Controller[PID Controller]
    Controller --> Synth
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase coherence is critical:** All four channels must maintain a **phase-locked relationship** (within 1°) at the tumor location to constructively interfere and produce the required 42–45 °C therapeutic temperature. This is essentially a **4-element phased array** applied in the medical domain.
