# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** *List any three applications of microwaves in the medical field and state the frequency used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Diagnostic MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging):** Uses RF pulses in the range **15–80 MHz** to excite hydrogen nuclei in body tissue; the resulting resonance signal is reconstructed into 3D anatomical images.
2. **Microwave Hyperthermia for Cancer Therapy:** Uses applicator antennas in the range **434 MHz / 915 MHz / 2.45 GHz** to selectively heat malignant tumors to 42–45 °C, enhancing the efficacy of chemotherapy and radiotherapy.
3. **Diathermy (Deep Tissue Heating):** Uses **27.12 MHz** (shortwave) and **2.45 GHz** (microwave) to relieve muscle pain, arthritis, and accelerate wound healing through controlled internal heating.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** *Why is a parabolic reflector preferred over a horn antenna for deep-space communication, even though both can provide high gain?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher achievable gain for a given physical size:** A parabolic reflector's gain scales with the square of the dish diameter; dishes 30–70 m are practical (e.g., the 70-m NASA Deep Space Network), whereas a horn of equivalent gain would be impractically long.
2. **Better main-lobe to side-lobe ratio:** Reflectors can achieve side-lobe levels of −30 dB or below, which is critical to avoid picking up cosmic noise and to comply with ITU coordination.
3. **Frequency scalability:** A reflector is essentially *frequency-independent* (the geometry stays the same); only the feed horn needs replacement when migrating from S- to X- to Ka-band. A horn's dimensions must change with wavelength.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a **monostatic pulse radar system**. Derive the **radar range equation** and explain how the maximum detection range depends on transmitted power, antenna gain, wavelength, target radar cross-section, and receiver sensitivity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ground-based surveillance radar operates at **10 GHz** with peak transmitted power **$P_t$ = 1 MW**, antenna gain **$G$ = 40 dBi**, pulse width **$\tau$ = 2 μs**, receiver noise figure **$NF$ = 4 dB**, system temperature **$T_0$ = 290 K**, and minimum SNR **$(S/N)_{min}$ = 13 dB**. The target RCS is $\sigma = 2 \, m^2$. Calculate the **maximum detectable range**. Use Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):**
The monostatic pulse radar consists of: (i) a pulse trigger and modulator that forms a high-voltage rectangular pulse, (ii) a magnetron (or klystron) that oscillates at microwave frequency during the pulse, (iii) a duplexer/circulator that routes the transmit pulse to the antenna and the received echo to the receiver, (iv) an antenna (typically a slotted waveguide array or parabolic dish) that both transmits the beam and collects the echo, (v) a low-noise receiver chain (LNA → mixer → IF amplifier → matched filter), (vi) an envelope detector, (vii) a threshold comparator, and (viii) a PPI (Plan Position Indicator) display.

**Operating Principle:** The radar transmits a short, high-power RF pulse (e.g., 2 μs wide) along a narrow beam. If the beam intercepts a target, a small fraction of the energy is back-scattered toward the radar. The round-trip time $t_R$ gives the range as $R = c t_R / 2$. The echo power is computed using the radar range equation.

**Derivation of Radar Range Equation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

$$\boxed{R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

**Dependence Analysis:**

| Parameter | Exponent on $R_{max}$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $P_t$ | $1/4$ | To double range, increase power 16× — *very inefficient* |
| $G$ | $1/2$ | Doubling range requires 4× gain (or 2× diameter) — *more cost-effective* |
| $\lambda$ | $1/2$ | Longer wavelength → larger range, but poorer resolution |
| $\sigma$ | $1/4$ | Stealth targets (small $\sigma$) drastically reduce range |
| $k T_s B$ | $-1/4$ | Sensitive receivers with low noise temperature extend range |

---

#### **Model Solution to (b)**

**Step 1 — Convert all dB quantities to linear:**

$$G_{dBi} = 40 \Rightarrow G_{linear} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

$$(S/N)_{min,dB} = 13 \Rightarrow (S/N)_{min} = 10^{13/10} = 19.95$$

$$NF_{dB} = 4 \Rightarrow F = 10^{0.4} = 2.512$$

**Step 2 — Compute Receiver Bandwidth B from Pulse Width:**

For a matched filter, the optimal bandwidth is:

$$B = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^5 \, Hz = 500 \, kHz$$

**Step 3 — Compute System Noise Temperature:**

$$T_s = T_0 \cdot (F - 1) + T_{antenna} \approx T_0 \cdot F \quad (\text{assuming } T_{ant} \ll T_0 F)$$

$$T_s = 290 \times 2.512 = 728.5 \, K$$

**Step 4 — Compute Wavelength at 10 GHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \, m = 3 \, cm$$

**Step 5 — Compute Denominator:**

$$(4\pi)^3 = (12.566)^3 = 1984.4$$

$$k T_s B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 728.5 \times 5 \times 10^5$$

$$k T_s B = 1.38 \times 728.5 \times 5 \times 10^{-18} = 5027.7 \times 10^{-18} = 5.028 \times 10^{-15} \, W$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by (S/N)$_{min}$:**

$$k T_s B \cdot (S/N)_{min} = 5.028 \times 10^{-15} \times 19.95 = 1.003 \times 10^{-13} \, W$$

**Step 7 — Compute Numerator:**

$$P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma = (1 \times 10^6) \times (10^4)^2 \times (0.03)^2 \times 2$$

$$= 10^6 \times 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 1.8 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Ratio:**

$$\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B (S/N)_{min}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{12}}{1.003 \times 10^{-13}} = 1.795 \times 10^{25}$$

**Step 9 — Take Fourth Root:**

$$R_{max} = (1.795 \times 10^{25})^{1/4}$$

Compute by taking square root twice:

$$(1.795 \times 10^{25})^{1/2} = \sqrt{1.795} \times 10^{12.5} = 1.340 \times 10^{12.5}$$

$$10^{12.5} = 10^{12} \times \sqrt{10} = 10^{12} \times 3.162 = 3.162 \times 10^{12}$$

$$\sqrt{1.795} = 1.340 \quad (\text{since } 1.34^2 = 1.7956)$$

So $(1.795 \times 10^{25})^{1/2} = 1.340 \times 3.162 \times 10^{12} = 4.237 \times 10^{12}$

Now take square root again:

$$R_{max} = \sqrt{4.237 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{4.237} \times 10^6 = 2.058 \times 10^6 \, m$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} \approx 2058 \, km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert dB to linear, or use $T_s = T_0$ instead of $T_s = T_0 \cdot F$. They also frequently mis-compute $(4\pi)^3$ (the correct value is ≈ 1984.4, **not** 64π³ ≈ 2010 — close enough, but using the wrong *order of magnitude* loses full marks). Always show the intermediate value of $(4\pi)^3$ explicitly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **principle of operation and applications of a parabolic reflector antenna**. With neat sketches, explain: (i) the focus-fed configuration, (ii) the Cassegrain configuration. Compare their merits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Cassegrain antenna is to be designed for a satellite earth station operating at **6 GHz** with a required gain of **48 dBi**. The aperture efficiency is **65%** and the blockage factor is **0.85**. Determine: (i) the main reflector diameter, (ii) the half-power beamwidth in degrees, (iii) the 3-dB beamwidth in radians.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

**Principle of Operation:**
A parabolic reflector antenna uses a **paraboloid of revolution** (or a parabolic cylinder for a linear feed) to convert a *spherical wavefront* emanating from a feed horn placed at its focus into a *plane wavefront* propagating along the antenna's boresight axis. This is based on the geometric optics property of the parabola: every ray emanating from the focus reflects off the surface parallel to the axis. The reverse is also true — incoming plane waves are all focused back to the focal point.

**Key Parameters:**
- $f$ = focal length
- $D$ = diameter of the dish
- $f/D$ ratio (typically 0.3 to 0.5)
- Depth $d = D^2 / (16f)$

**Focus-Fed Configuration:**
The feed horn (or dipole + reflector assembly) is placed at the focal point, in front of the dish. The feed radiates toward the dish, and the dish collimates the rays into a parallel beam. The feed is connected to the receiver/transmitter via a waveguide run, and the feed assembly is supported by three or four struts (tripod). This is the simplest configuration but has two disadvantages: (i) the feed and its support struts cause **aperture blockage** (typically 5–10% area loss), and (ii) the long waveguide run from the equipment room to the feed introduces **RF losses**.

**Cassegrain Configuration:**
To overcome the drawbacks of focus-fed, a **secondary hyperbolic reflector** (sub-reflector) is placed between the main paraboloid and the prime focus. The feed is placed at the **Cassegrain focus** (behind the main dish), illuminating the convex side of the hyperboloid. The hyperboloid reflects the rays to the main paraboloid, which then collimates them. The Cassegrain geometry is equivalent to placing a virtual feed at the parabola's prime focus. Advantages: (i) **no aperture blockage from struts** (the sub-reflector is small and supported on a tripod from behind), (ii) **shorter waveguide run** (feed is at the vertex of the main dish), (iii) **lower noise temperature** (feed is in the shadow of the dish), and (iv) ability to add **beam waveguide systems** for very large antennas.

**Comparative Table:**

| Feature | Focus-Fed | Cassegrain |
|---|---|---|
| Aperture blockage | High (struts in path) | Low (small sub-reflector) |
| Waveguide length | Long (to front) | Short (at vertex) |
| Complexity | Low | High (two reflectors to align) |
| Spillover loss | Moderate | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Application | TVRO, small earth stations | Large earth stations, deep-space |

---

#### **Model Solution to (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Frequency: $f = 6 \, GHz$
- Required gain: $G_{dBi} = 48$
- Aperture efficiency: $\eta_a = 0.65$
- Blockage factor: $\eta_b = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Convert Gain to Linear:**

$$G = 10^{48/10} = 10^{4.8} = 63{,}096$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Efficiency:**

Total effective efficiency: $\eta_{eff} = \eta_a \times \eta_b = 0.65 \times 0.85 = 0.5525$

**Step 3 — Compute Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \, m = 5 \, cm$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Dish Diameter D:**

Using $G = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 \eta_{eff}}{\lambda^2}$:

$$D^2 = \frac{G \lambda^2}{\pi^2 \eta_{eff}}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{63{,}096 \times (0.05)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.5525}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{63{,}096 \times 0.0025}{9.8696 \times 0.5525}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{157.74}{5.453} = 28.93 \, m^2$$

$$\boxed{D = \sqrt{28.93} = 5.379 \, m \approx 5.38 \, m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW) in Degrees:**

For a parabolic dish, $HPBW \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D}$ degrees (Balanis approximation):

$$HPBW = \frac{70 \times 0.05}{5.38} = \frac{3.5}{5.38} = 0.651°$$

**Step 6 — Compute HPBW in Radians:**

$$HPBW_{rad} = HPBW_{deg} \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.651 \times 0.01745 = 0.01136 \, rad$$

Or alternatively, $HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886 \lambda}{D}$ (Stutzman):

$$HPBW = \frac{0.886 \times 0.05}{5.38} = \frac{0.0443}{5.38} = 0.00823 \, rad$$

$$\boxed{HPBW \approx 0.65° \approx 0.0114 \, rad}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students use the *physical* aperture area $\pi D^2/4$ with the *full* gain formula $G = 4\pi A_e / \lambda^2$ but forget that the **blockage factor multiplies the effective aperture** — leading to an under-sized dish. Always check whether the problem statement says "aperture efficiency" alone or "aperture efficiency AND blockage factor". A 5% error in $D$ can be the difference between 6 and 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Applications**

### A. Core Application Families (must memorize)
- [ ] **Communication:** Satellite (DTH, VSAT), terrestrial microwave links, 5G/6G base stations, point-to-point radio relays
- [ ] **Radar:** Air defense, ATC, marine, weather, SAR, automotive ACC, missile guidance
- [ ] **Industrial:** Microwave ovens (2.45 GHz), RF drying of textiles/paper, rubber vulcanization, plasma generation, food processing
- [ ] **Medical:** MRI (15–80 MHz), hyperthermia (434/915 MHz), diathermy (27.12 MHz / 2.45 GHz), ablation
- [ ] **Scientific:** Radio astronomy, deep-space tracking, spectroscopy, remote sensing

### B. Frequency Allocations (high-yield)
- [ ] **L-band (1–2 GHz):** GPS, GLONASS, mobile communications
- [ ] **S-band (2–4 GHz):** Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz), ATC radar, microwave ovens (2.45 GHz)
- [ ] **C-band (4–8 GHz):** Satellite downlink, weather radar
- [ ] **X-band (8–12 GHz):** Military radar, satellite uplink, marine radar
- [ ] **Ku-band (12–18 GHz):** DTH TV (12 GHz), VSAT
- [ ] **Ka-band (26–40 GHz):** 5G mmWave, satellite high-throughput
- [ ] **ISM bands:** 433/868/915 MHz, 2.4 GHz, 5.8 GHz, 24 GHz

### C. Antenna-to-Application Mapping (top 8)
- [ ] **Dipole** → FM broadcast, reference
- [ ] **Yagi-Uda** → Domestic TV reception
- [ ] **Helical** → Satellite TT&C, GPS receivers
- [ ] **Horn** → Feed element, EMC testing
- [ ] **Parabolic dish** → Earth stations, radio telescopes
- [ ] **Microstrip patch** → Mobile phones, 5G arrays
- [ ] **Log-periodic** → EMC, broadband surveillance
- [ ] **Phased array** → AESA radar, MIMO, SATCOM-on-move

### D. Must-Know Formulas (formula-sheet ready)
- [ ] Friis equation: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$
- [ ] Radar range: $R_{max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_s B (S/N)_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$
- [ ] Gain-aperture: $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$
- [ ] HPBW (parabolic): $\theta_{3dB} \approx \dfrac{70\lambda}{D}$ degrees
- [ ] Doppler: $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r f_c}{c}$

### E. KTU Board-Exam Survival Tips
- [ ] Always state *assumptions* before solving a numerical (free space, monostatic, matched filter, etc.)
- [ ] Always show *unit conversions* explicitly: dB ↔ linear, km ↔ m, GHz ↔ Hz
- [ ] Always draw a *labeled block diagram* before deriving any system-level formula
- [ ] Always mention the *frequency band* and *justification* when assigning an antenna to an application
- [ ] Avoid using `|` inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table corruption

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Antenna Parameters: Radiation Pattern
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Antenna Parameters: Radiation Pattern

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 MODULE 3 — Basic Antenna Parameters: Radiation Pattern

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Pattern (Antenna Radiation Pattern):**
> A **radiation pattern** of an antenna is defined as a *mathematical function* (or a graphical representation) of the **far-field (Fraunhofer region) radiation properties** of the antenna as a function of the **spatial coordinates (θ, φ)**. In simpler terms, it is a plot describing how the antenna **radiates (or receives) electromagnetic energy** as a function of direction in 3-dimensional space.

By IEEE standard definition, the radiation pattern is a *"graphical representation of the radiation characteristics of an antenna as a function of direction in space."* It is the most fundamental and visually intuitive descriptor of an antenna's performance.

The radiation pattern is generally plotted in the **far-field region** because:
- The angular distribution becomes **independent of distance** $r$ from the antenna.
- The field components become purely **transverse** (no radial components of $E$ or $H$).
- The power flow is **entirely radial** (purely real power, no reactive power).

Mathematically, the radiation pattern is represented as:

$$F(\theta, \phi) = \text{normalized field strength or power intensity as a function of } (\theta, \phi)$$

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lamp Post" Intuition

Imagine a **lamp post** standing in the middle of a dark circular field at night:
- A **bare bulb** (without a reflector) radiates light **equally in all directions** → this is like an *isotropic radiator*.
- A **spotlight** concentrates light in a narrow beam in one direction → this is like a *directional antenna* (e.g., dish antenna).
- A **streetlight with a curved reflector** throws light in a particular lobe pattern, with some light spilling to the back → this is like a *directional antenna with side and back lobes*.

The **radiation pattern** is essentially the **"luminous map"** of how an antenna distributes its energy into 3D space — where the antenna "shines brightly" and where it "stays dark." Just as you would not want a flashlight that spreads light in all directions when you are reading a book, an antenna engineer carefully designs the radiation pattern to direct energy exactly where it is needed (e.g., toward a satellite or a base station).

### 📐 Key Classification of Radiation Patterns

| Type | Description | Real-World Example |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Isotropic Pattern** | Hypothetical pattern — radiates equally in **all directions** | Idealized reference only |
| **Omnidirectional Pattern** | Non-uniform in elevation (θ), but **uniform in azimuth (φ)** | AM broadcast antenna, dipole |
| **Directional (Pencil-Beam) Pattern** | Concentrated in a single narrow lobe | Parabolic dish, horn antenna |
| **Fan-Beam Pattern** | Narrow in one plane, wide in the orthogonal plane | Radar antennas |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The radiation pattern is always a *far-field* property. Near-field patterns are described separately using Fresnel zone concepts. Always ensure the student specifies "far-field" when discussing radiation patterns in exams.

### 🎯 Fundamental Pattern Quantities

A radiation pattern may be represented in terms of:
1. **Field intensity** (linear scale): $E(\theta, \phi)$ in V/m
2. **Power intensity** (logarithmic scale): Often in **dB** for clarity
3. **Phase** (for array analysis): $\angle E(\theta, \phi)$

Two primary plots are conventionally used:
- **Rectangular Plot** — useful for examining minor lobes and nulls in detail.
- **Polar Plot** — gives a more realistic, intuitive 3D-feel representation.

### 🔑 Isotropic Radiator — The Reference Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isotropic Radiator / Isotropic Antenna:**
> A *hypothetical* antenna that radiates **equally in all directions** in 3D space. It is **lossless**, with **100% radiation efficiency**, and serves as a **mathematical reference** for defining antenna parameters like **Directivity (D)** and **Gain (G)**.

The total radiated power by an isotropic radiator with field strength $E_0$ at distance $r$ is:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{E_0^2}{\eta} \cdot 4\pi r^2$$

where $\eta$ is the **intrinsic impedance of free space** = $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω**, and the surface area of the sphere of radius $r$ is $4\pi r^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> The isotropic radiator **does not physically exist** — it is a pure mathematical construct used solely as a reference benchmark.

### 🧭 Field Regions Around an Antenna (Mandatory KTU Topic)

The space surrounding an antenna is conventionally divided into **three regions** based on the dominant field behavior. For an antenna of **largest dimension $D$** operating at **wavelength $\lambda$**:

| Region | Boundary | Physical Characteristics |
|--------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Reactive Near-Field Region** | $0 < r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | E and H are **out of phase by 90°** — energy oscillates back and forth; **stored energy dominates**. |
| **Radiating Near-Field (Fresnel) Region** | $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} < r < 2D^2/\lambda$ | Radiation dominates but angular distribution still varies with $r$. |
| **Far-Field (Fraunhofer) Region** | $r > 2D^2/\lambda$ | Angular pattern is **independent of $r$**; $E \perp H \perp \hat{r}$; fields fall off as $1/r$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The far-field condition is universally written as:
> $$r_{ff} \geq \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$
> where $D$ is the **largest physical dimension** of the antenna and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength. Always state this when asked "where is the radiation pattern measured?"

### 🧩 The Three Pattern "Flavors"

1. **E-Plane Pattern**: The plane containing the **electric field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation**.
2. **H-Plane Pattern**: The plane containing the **magnetic field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation**.
3. **Composite (3D) Pattern**: The complete 3D representation combining E and H-plane patterns.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **half-wave dipole**, the **E-plane** is the plane containing the dipole axis (typically the xy-plane if the dipole is along z-axis), and the **H-plane** is the plane perpendicular to it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 📋 Structured Logic Breakdown of Radiation Pattern Theory

- **Step 1 — Define the source**: An antenna radiates time-varying currents that produce electromagnetic waves governed by **Maxwell's equations**.
- **Step 2 — Define the observation point**: Use the **standard spherical coordinate system** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ with the antenna at the origin. The far-field $E$-field is of the form:
$$E_\theta(\theta, \phi) = j\eta \frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi r} e^{-jkr} \sin\theta \cdot F(\theta, \phi)$$
- **Step 3 — Separate radial and angular dependence**: Note that $1/r$ is the radial decay (universal in far-field), and $F(\theta, \phi)$ is the *pattern function* of interest.
- **Step 4 — Normalize the pattern**: Divide by the maximum value to obtain the **normalized field pattern** $F_n(\theta, \phi) = F(\theta, \phi)/F_{max}$.
- **Step 5 — Compute the power pattern**: Square the field pattern: $P_n(\theta, \phi) = \vert F_n(\theta, \phi) \vert^2$.
- **Step 6 — Display in dB for clarity**: $\text{Pattern (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}[P_n(\theta, \phi)]$.

### 🧠 Why Each Step Matters

- **Normalization** makes patterns from different antennas directly comparable.
- **Power pattern** is more meaningful for system-level analysis (link budget, SNR).
- **dB scale** compresses the dynamic range, making weak lobes and nulls visible.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Equations — Radiation Pattern**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Normalized Field Pattern | $F_n(\theta, \phi) = \dfrac{E(\theta, \phi)}{E_{max}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq F_n \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Normalized Power Pattern | $P_n(\theta, \phi) = \vert F_n(\theta, \phi) \vert^2$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq P_n \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Power Pattern in dB | $P(\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}[P_n]$ | dB, negative values for non-peak directions |
| 4 | Radiation Intensity | $U(\theta, \phi) = r^2 \cdot S(\theta, \phi) = \dfrac{r^2}{2\eta} \vert E(\theta, \phi) \vert^2$ | W/Unit solid angle |
| 5 | Total Radiated Power | $P_{rad} = \oint_S U(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} U(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$ | W (Watts) |
| 6 | Isotropic Radiator Power Density | $U_0 = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{4\pi}$ | W/Unit solid angle |
| 7 | Directivity | $D(\theta, \phi) = \dfrac{U(\theta, \phi)}{U_0} = \dfrac{4\pi U(\theta, \phi)}{P_{rad}}$ | Dimensionless (or in dBi) |
| 8 | Half-Power Beamwidth | $F_n(\theta_{HP}/2) = 1/\sqrt{2} = 0.707$ | Degrees or Radians |
| 9 | First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW) | $2\theta_{FN}$: angle between first nulls on either side of main lobe | Degrees or Radians |
| 10 | Beam Solid Angle ($\Omega_A$) | $\Omega_A = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} P_n(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$ | Steradians (sr) |
| 11 | Directivity in terms of $\Omega_A$ | $D_0 = \dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$ | Dimensionless (or dBi) |
| 12 | Approximate Directivity (for HPBW in both planes) | $D_0 \approx \dfrac{41253}{\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2}$ (degrees²) | Use only for pencil-beam antennas |
| 13 | Beam Efficiency | $\eta_B = \dfrac{\Omega_{MB}}{\Omega_A}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 14 | Front-to-Back Ratio (F/B) | $\text{F/B} = 10 \log_{10} \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{back}}$ | dB |
| 15 | Side-Lobe Level (SLL) | $\text{SLL} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{\vert E_{sl} \vert}{\vert E_{max} \vert}$ | dB (negative) |
| 16 | Far-Field Distance | $r_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | meters (D = max antenna dimension) |
| 17 | Spherical Surface Element | $dA = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$ | m² |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** When writing absolute value bars or $\vert E \vert$ in markdown tables, **never** use the pipe `|` symbol — it breaks the table. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ instead, as shown above.

### 🌎 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless Communication**: Engineers use radiation patterns to design **cell tower sectors** that target specific neighborhoods, avoiding interference with adjacent cells.
- **Satellite Ground Stations**: Use highly directional parabolic dish patterns with very narrow beamwidths (e.g., 0.5° for geostationary satellites at 36,000 km).
- **Radar Systems**: Pencil-beam patterns are used to pinpoint targets; shaped-beam patterns (e.g., $\csc^2$ pattern) are used for surveillance radars to compensate for Earth's curvature.
- **GNSS (GPS)**: Hemispherical patterns are used in handheld GPS receivers to receive signals from multiple satellites.
- **Medical Imaging (Microwave Imaging)**: Ultra-wideband antenna patterns are designed for breast tumor detection.
- **5G mmWave Beamforming**: Phased array antennas electronically steer narrow beams — the radiation pattern is the cornerstone of beam-steering algorithms.

### 🔬 The Anatomy of a Radiation Pattern (Lobe Terminology)

A typical directional radiation pattern has the following features:

- **Main Lobe (Major Lobe)**: The lobe containing the **direction of maximum radiation**. This is the *primary beam* of the antenna.
- **Side Lobes (Minor Lobes)**: All lobes *other than the main lobe*. They represent **wasted or undesired radiation** in unintended directions.
- **Back Lobe**: The lobe that points in the **opposite direction (180°)** from the main lobe. Ideally, back lobe should be **as small as possible**.
- **Minor Lobes (in some texts)**: Specifically refers to side lobes adjacent to the main lobe (not the back lobe).
- **Nulls**: Directions of **zero radiation** — $F_n(\theta, \phi) = 0$. They occur between lobes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition — Null:**
> A null in the radiation pattern is a direction in space where the **radiated field strength is zero (or approaches zero)**. Mathematically, $F_n(\theta_{null}, \phi_{null}) = 0$. Nulls are very useful in radar and direction-finding systems because they provide **precise angular discrimination**.

### 🧭 Pattern Regions and Definitions Summary

| Pattern Term | Definition | Typical Numerical Range |
|--------------|------------|-------------------------|
| **Main Beam (Boresight)** | Direction of maximum radiation | $\theta = 0$ (by convention) |
| **HPBW (Half-Power Beamwidth)** | Angular width between -3 dB points | 10°–120° typical |
| **FNBW (First-Null Beamwidth)** | Angular width between first nulls | Larger than HPBW |
| **Side-Lobe Level (SLL)** | Ratio of side-lobe peak to main-lobe peak | -10 dB to -30 dB (good antennas) |
| **Front-to-Back Ratio** | Ratio of main-lobe peak to back-lobe peak | 20–40 dB typical |
| **Beam Solid Angle** | Solid angle through which all power would flow if pattern were uniform at peak value | 0.01–4π steradians |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 DERIVATION 1: Power Radiated by an Isotropic Antenna from Surface Integration

**Goal:** Derive the total radiated power $P_{rad}$ of an isotropic radiator given the far-field strength $E_0$ at distance $r$.

**Step 1 — Start with the time-average Poynting vector (far-field):**

The time-average power density radiated by an antenna in the far-field is:

$$S(r, \theta, \phi) = \frac{\vert E(r, \theta, \phi) \vert^2}{2\eta_0}$$

where $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ **Ω** is the intrinsic impedance of free space.

**Step 2 — For an isotropic antenna, the field magnitude is the same in all directions at the same distance $r$:**

$$E(r, \theta, \phi) = E_0 \cdot \frac{1}{r} \quad \text{(for } r \text{ in the far-field)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Poynting vector expression:**

$$S(r) = \frac{\vert E_0 / r \vert^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0 r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Total power crossing a closed spherical surface of radius $r$:**

The total radiated power is the surface integral of the power density over a sphere of radius $r$:

$$P_{rad} = \oint_S \vec{S} \cdot d\vec{A} = \oint_S S(r) \, dA$$

**Step 5 — Parametrize the sphere using spherical coordinates:**

On a sphere of radius $r$, the differential surface element is:

$$dA = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and integrate over the full sphere (θ from 0 to π, φ from 0 to 2π):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rad} &= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} S(r) \cdot r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0 r^2} \cdot r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \\
&= \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integrals:**

The integral of $\sin\theta$ from 0 to π is 2, and the integral of $d\phi$ from 0 to 2π is 2π. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rad} &= \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} \cdot (2\pi) \cdot (2) \\
&= \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} \cdot 4\pi \\
&= \frac{4\pi E_0^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{2\pi E_0^2}{\eta_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{rad} = \frac{4\pi E_0^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{2\pi E_0^2}{\eta_0} = \frac{E_0^2 \cdot 4\pi r^2}{2\eta_0 r^2} = 4\pi r^2 \cdot S(r)}
$$

This derivation justifies the standard formula for the **radiation intensity** of an isotropic radiator:

$$U_0 = \frac{P_{rad}}{4\pi} = S(r) \cdot r^2 = \frac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0} \quad \text{(W/unit solid angle)}$$

---

### 🧮 DERIVATION 2: Directivity from Beam Solid Angle

**Goal:** Show that $D_0 = \dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$ for a generic antenna.

**Step 1 — Define Directivity (from the formal definition):**

$$D(\theta, \phi) = \frac{U(\theta, \phi)}{U_0} = \frac{4\pi \cdot U(\theta, \phi)}{P_{rad}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$:**

The beam solid angle is the integral of the normalized power pattern over the entire sphere:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega_A &= \oint_S P_n(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} P_n(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Note that the total radiated power is also related to the radiation intensity:**

$$P_{rad} = \oint_S U(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega = U_{max} \cdot \oint_S P_n(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega = U_{max} \cdot \Omega_A$$

**Step 4 — Now compute the maximum directivity (in the direction of $U_{max}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= \frac{4\pi \cdot U_{max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi \cdot U_{max}}{U_{max} \cdot \Omega_A} \\
&= \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed result:**

$$
\boxed{D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This shows that a *narrower* beam (smaller $\Omega_A$) yields a *higher* directivity. A pencil-beam with $\Omega_A = 0.01$ sr gives $D_0 = 1257$ (or 31 dBi), while a hemispherical pattern with $\Omega_A = 2\pi$ sr gives $D_0 = 2$ (or 3 dBi).

---

### 🧮 DERIVATION 3: Approximate Directivity from HPBW (Kraus's Approximation)

**Goal:** Derive Kraus's formula: $D_0 \approx \dfrac{41253}{\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2}$ (degrees²).

**Step 1 — Start with the beam solid angle approximation:**

For a *pencil-beam* antenna with HPBW $\theta_1$ in one plane and $\theta_2$ in the orthogonal plane (both in **radians**), the beam solid angle can be approximated as:

$$\Omega_A \approx \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \theta_1 \cdot \theta_2$$

The factor $\pi/4$ arises because the equivalent "rectangular" beam defined by -3 dB points actually captures approximately 1/($\pi$/4) of the power.

**Step 2 — Apply the directivity-beam solid angle relation:**

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \theta_1 \cdot \theta_2} = \frac{16}{\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2}$$

where $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are in **radians**.

**Step 3 — Convert from radians to degrees:**

Using $1$ radian $= \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ degrees, we have $\theta_{deg} = \theta_{rad} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi}$.

Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0 &= \frac{16}{\left(\theta_{1,rad}\right) \cdot \left(\theta_{2,rad}\right)} \\
&= \frac{16}{\left(\theta_{1,deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot \left(\theta_{2,deg} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}\right)} \\
&= \frac{16 \cdot 180^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \theta_{1,deg} \cdot \theta_{2,deg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{16 \cdot 180^2}{\pi^2} &= \frac{16 \cdot 32400}{9.8696} = \frac{518400}{9.8696} \approx 52527
\end{aligned}
$$

A more refined version (using 70.4% beam efficiency factor) gives the more famous Kraus constant:

$$
\boxed{D_0 \approx \frac{41253}{\theta_{1,deg} \cdot \theta_{2,deg}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** This approximation is valid **only for pencil-beam antennas** where $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are both small. For fan-beam or omnidirectional patterns, the formula **must not** be used.

---

### 🧮 DERIVATION 4: Half-Power Beamwidth from a Sinusoidal Pattern

**Goal:** Show that for a pattern of the form $F_n(\theta) = \cos^n(\theta)$, the HPBW is related to $n$.

**Step 1 — Set the pattern expression:**

$$F_n(\theta) = \cos^n(\theta), \quad -\pi/2 \leq \theta \leq \pi/2$$

**Step 2 — Set the half-power condition:**

At the HPBW edge, $F_n^2(\theta_{HP}) = \dfrac{1}{2}$:

$$\cos^{2n}(\theta_{HP}) = \frac{1}{2} \implies \cos(\theta_{HP}) = 2^{-1/(2n)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_{HP}$:**

$$\theta_{HP} = \arccos\left(2^{-1/(2n)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Special Case n = 1 (Half-Wave Dipole):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{HP} &= \arccos\left(2^{-1/2}\right) = \arccos\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = 45° \\
\text{HPBW} &= 2 \theta_{HP} = 90°
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher $n$ gives narrower beamwidths. For a parabolic reflector of diameter $D$, the HPBW is approximately $\theta_{HP} \approx 70\lambda/D$ degrees. This is a critical KTU design equation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📊 Figure 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radiation Pattern Generation and Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA_AntennaStructure] --> B[StepB_TimeVaryingCurrents]
    B --> C[StepC_ElectromagneticWaves]
    C --> D{StepD_RegionCheck}
    D -->|r less than 0.62D3-2-lambda| E[StepE_ReactiveNearField]
    D -->|r between Reactive and Fraunhofer| F[StepF_FresnelNearField]
    D -->|r greater than 2D2-lambda| G[StepG_FraunhoferFarField]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]**

---

**Q1. Define radiation pattern. What is the difference between an isotropic pattern and an omnidirectional pattern?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A **radiation pattern** of an antenna is a graphical or mathematical representation of the far-field (Fraunhofer region) radiation properties of the antenna as a function of the spatial coordinates $(\theta, \phi)$. It describes how the antenna radiates energy in different directions of space.

| Feature | Isotropic | Omnidirectional |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| Directional Variation | **None** (uniform in all directions) | **Uniform in azimuth (φ)**, varies with elevation (θ) |
| 3D Shape | Perfect sphere | Doughnut-shaped (toroidal) |
| Physical Existence | **Hypothetical** (does not exist) | **Practical** (e.g., half-wave dipole) |
| Directivity | $D_0 = 1$ (0 dBi) | $D_0 = 1.5$ (1.76 dBi for ideal dipole) |

The isotropic pattern is a *theoretical reference*; the omnidirectional pattern is a *realistic design* that is uniform only in one plane.

---

**Q2. List the three field regions around an antenna. Write the boundary condition for the far-field region.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The three field regions around an antenna, along with their boundaries, are:

| Field Region | Boundary (for antenna of max dimension D) |
|--------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Reactive Near-Field Region** | $0 \leq r < 0.62 \sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ |
| **Radiating Near-Field (Fresnel) Region** | $0.62 \sqrt{D^3/\lambda} \leq r < 2D^2/\lambda$ |
| **Far-Field (Fraunhofer) Region** | $r \geq 2D^2/\lambda$ |

The far-field condition $r_{ff} \geq \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ is the criterion for measuring the radiation pattern, because in this region the angular field distribution becomes *independent of the distance $r$* and the field components are purely transverse ($E \perp H \perp \hat{r}$).

---

### 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each question carries 14 marks split as 7+7.*

---

## ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q(A). (a)** Explain the concept of radiation pattern with a neat sketch. Define and distinguish between E-plane and H-plane patterns. What are main lobe, side lobes, and back lobe? **`[7 Marks]`**

**(b)** A 10 GHz parabolic dish antenna has a diameter of $D = 0.5$ m. Compute:
  1. The far-field distance.
  2. The approximate HPBW in degrees (using $\theta_{HP} \approx 70\lambda/D$).
  3. The approximate maximum directivity in dBi (using Kraus's formula $D_0 \approx 41253/(\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2)$, assuming symmetric pattern).
  4. The beam solid angle $\Omega_A$.

**`[7 Marks]`**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q(A)(a)

**Definition of Radiation Pattern:**
The radiation pattern of an antenna is defined as a *mathematical function or graphical representation* of the far-field radiation properties of the antenna as a function of the spatial coordinates $(\theta, \phi)$. It shows how the antenna distributes its radiated energy in different directions of 3-dimensional space.

The radiation pattern is measured in the **Fraunhofer (far-field) region** where the angular distribution is independent of distance $r$.

**Sketch Description (Verbal Description for KTU Board Exams):**

A standard radiation pattern in polar coordinates shows:
- A **main lobe** pointing in the boresight direction ($\theta = 0$),
- **Side lobes** at various angles, with decreasing amplitudes,
- A **back lobe** at $\theta = 180°$,
- **Nulls** between consecutive lobes,
- The **HPBW** marked between the -3 dB points,
- The **FNBW** marked between the first nulls on either side of the main lobe.

**E-Plane vs H-Plane Patterns:**

| Parameter | E-Plane | H-Plane |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Definition | Plane containing the **electric field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation** | Plane containing the **magnetic field vector** and the **direction of maximum radiation** |
| Typical Orientation for Dipole | Contains the dipole axis | Perpendicular to dipole axis |
| Field Shown | E-field strength vs angle | H-field strength vs angle (proportional to E-field) |

For a half-wave dipole along the z-axis:
- The E-plane is typically the **xz-plane** (or any plane containing the z-axis).
- The H-plane is the **xy-plane** (perpendicular to the dipole).

**Lobe Definitions:**

- **Main Lobe (Major Lobe):** The radiation lobe containing the direction of **maximum radiation** (the principal direction in which the antenna radiates maximum power).
- **Side Lobes (Minor Lobes):** All radiation lobes *other than the main lobe*. They represent radiation in unintended directions and are usually **undesirable** (a source of interference and wasted power).
- **Back Lobe:** The radiation lobe that points in the direction **opposite (180°)** to the main lobe. A good directional antenna has a very small back lobe (high front-to-back ratio).

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q(A)(b)

**Given:**
- Frequency $f = 10$ GHz $= 10 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Diameter $D = 0.5$ m
- Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 — Compute the wavelength:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Far-Field Distance:**

$$
r_{ff} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (0.5)^2}{0.03} = \frac{2 \times 0.25}{0.03} = \frac{0.5}{0.03} \approx 16.67 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate HPBW:**

$$
\theta_{HP} \approx \frac{70\lambda}{D} = \frac{70 \times 0.03}{0.5} = \frac{2.1}{0.5} = 4.2° \text{ (in each principal plane)}
$$

So HPBW in both planes: $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 4.2°$.

**Step 4 — Maximum Directivity in dBi (Kraus's Approximation):**

$$
D_0 \approx \frac{41253}{\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2} = \frac{41253}{4.2 \times 4.2} = \frac{41253}{17.64} \approx 2338.6
$$

Converting to dBi:

$$
D_0 \text{ (dBi)} = 10 \log_{10}(2338.6) \approx 33.69 \text{ dBi}
$$

**Step 5 — Beam Solid Angle:**

$$
\Omega_A = \frac{4\pi}{D_0} = \frac{4\pi}{2338.6} = \frac{12.566}{2338.6} \approx 0.005374 \text{ sr}
$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Wavelength $\lambda$ | 0.03 m (3 cm) |
| Far-field distance $r_{ff}$ | **16.67 m** |
| HPBW | **4.2°** (in both principal planes) |
| Directivity $D_0$ (linear) | 2338.6 |
| Directivity $D_0$ (dBi) | **33.69 dBi** |
| Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$ | **0.005374 sr** |

---

## ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q(B). (a)** Derive the relationship between the directivity and the beam solid angle of an antenna. What is the physical significance of beam solid angle? **`[7 Marks]`**

**(b)** A half-wave dipole has a normalized power pattern given by $P_n(\theta) = \cos^2(\theta)$ in the elevation plane. Compute:
  1. The 3 dB beamwidth (HPBW) in degrees.
  2. The beam solid angle $\Omega_A$.
  3. The maximum directivity $D_0$ in linear units and in dBi.
  4. The Half-Power Beamwidth of the pattern in the H-plane (azimuth plane).

**`[7 Marks]`**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q(B)(a)

**Derivation of $D_0 = 4\pi/\Omega_A$:**

**Step 1 — Define Directivity:**

Directivity in any direction $(\theta, \phi)$ is defined as the ratio of the radiation intensity $U(\theta, \phi)$ to the average radiation intensity $U_0$:

$$D(\theta, \phi) = \frac{U(\theta, \phi)}{U_0} = \frac{4\pi U(\theta, \phi)}{P_{rad}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Beam Solid Angle:**

The beam solid angle $\Omega_A$ is defined as the integral of the *normalized* power pattern over the entire sphere:

$$
\Omega_A = \oint_S P_n(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} P_n(\theta, \phi) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

**Step 3 — Express Total Radiated Power:**

By definition, the total radiated power is the integral of the actual radiation intensity over all directions:

$$
P_{rad} = \oint_S U(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Relate $U$ to the Normalized Pattern:**

Since $P_n(\theta, \phi) = U(\theta, \phi)/U_{max}$, we have $U(\theta, \phi) = U_{max} \cdot P_n(\theta, \phi)$. Substituting:

$$
P_{rad} = U_{max} \oint_S P_n(\theta, \phi) \, d\Omega = U_{max} \cdot \Omega_A
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Maximum Directivity:**

In the direction of maximum radiation, $U = U_{max}$, so:

$$
D_0 = \frac{4\pi U_{max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi U_{max}}{U_{max} \cdot \Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}}
$$

**Physical Significance of Beam Solid Angle:**

1. The beam solid angle $\Omega_A$ is the **equivalent cone of radiation** — a fictitious solid angle through which *all the radiated power would flow uniformly* if the antenna radiated at its peak intensity in every direction within that cone.
2. A **smaller** $\Omega_A$ corresponds to a **narrower, more focused beam** and thus a **higher directivity**.
3. For an **isotropic** antenna, $\Omega_A = 4\pi$ sr (full sphere) and $D_0 = 1$ (0 dBi).
4. For a **highly directional** antenna (e.g., a satellite dish), $\Omega_A$ is very small (millisteradians) and $D_0$ is very large (30-60 dBi).
5. The beam solid angle is a **measure of the antenna's "directional concentration"** of radiated power.

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q(B)(b)

**Given:**
The normalized power pattern of a half-wave dipole in the elevation plane is:

$$P_n(\theta) = \cos^2(\theta), \quad 0 \leq \theta \leq \pi$$

**Step 1 — Compute the HPBW (3 dB Beamwidth):**

The half-power condition is $P_n(\theta_{HP}) = 1/2$:

$$
\cos^2(\theta_{HP}) = \frac{1}{2} \implies \cos(\theta_{HP}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \implies \theta_{HP} = 45°
$$

The HPBW is the total angle between the two -3 dB points:

$$
\boxed{\text{HPBW} = 2 \times 45° = 90°}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$:**

Since the pattern is independent of $\phi$ (omnidirectional in azimuth), the beam solid angle is:

$$
\Omega_A = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \cos^2(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

First integrate over $\phi$:

$$
\Omega_A = 2\pi \int_0^{\pi} \cos^2(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta
$$

Let $u = \cos\theta$, $du = -\sin\theta \, d\theta$. When $\theta = 0$, $u = 1$; when $\theta = \pi$, $u = -1$:

$$
\Omega_A = 2\pi \int_1^{-1} u^2 (-du) = 2\pi \int_{-1}^{1} u^2 \, du = 2\pi \left[\frac{u^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{1} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{4\pi}{3}
$$

So the beam solid angle is:

$$
\boxed{\Omega_A = \frac{4\pi}{3} \approx 4.189 \text{ sr}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Maximum Directivity $D_0$:**

$$
D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{4\pi/3} = 3
$$

Converting to dBi:

$$
D_0 \text{ (dBi)} = 10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77 \text{ dBi}
$$

**Step 4 — HPBW in the H-Plane (Azimuth Plane):**

The half-wave dipole is **omnidirectional** in the azimuth (H-plane). This means the pattern is constant (equal to 1) for all $\phi$ at the peak ($\theta = 90°$). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{HPBW (H-plane)} = 360° \text{ (omnidirectional)}}
$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| HPBW (E-plane) | **90°** |
| Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$ | $4\pi/3$ sr ≈ **4.189 sr** |
| Directivity $D_0$ (linear) | **3** (or 1.5 if integrated properly with the dipole's $1.64$ factor) |
| Directivity $D_0$ (dBi) | **4.77 dBi** (theoretical half-wave dipole) |
| HPBW (H-plane) | **360°** (omnidirectional) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Half-Wave Dipole:** The actual maximum directivity of an ideal half-wave dipole is $D_0 = 1.64$ (or 2.15 dBi), not exactly 3, because the more accurate normalized field pattern is $F_n(\theta) = \cos(\pi/2 \cdot \cos\theta)/\sin\theta$, not simply $\cos\theta$. However, the simplified $\cos^2(\theta)$ approximation yields $D_0 = 3$ as a useful first-order estimate, which is widely used in KTU textbook examples.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify "far-field"** when defining the radiation pattern — *loses 1 mark* instantly.
> 2. **Confusing "isotropic" with "omnidirectional"** — these are different. Isotropic = uniform in 3D; omnidirectional = uniform in azimuth only.
> 3. **Using the wrong units** in Kraus's formula — $D_0 \approx 41253/(\theta_1 \theta_2)$ requires **degrees**, not radians. Mixing them gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in HPBW** — students often compute $\theta_{HP}$ and forget to multiply by 2 to get the total beamwidth. Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Conflating "null" with "minimum"** — a null is a *zero* of the pattern ($F_n = 0$), not just a local minimum. Be precise.
> 6. **Skipping the justification for the far-field boundary** — examiners want to see the *reason* (pattern independence of $r$, transverse fields), not just the formula.
> 7. **Mixing up E-plane and H-plane** — for a dipole along z-axis, the E-plane contains z-axis; the H-plane is perpendicular to it.
> 8. **Forgetting to convert linear directivity to dBi** — when asked "in dB," always use $10\log_{10}(D_0)$. Lose 0.5 mark if missing.

---

### 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Key things to remember before the KTU exam:**

- 📌 **Radiation Pattern Definition**: A *mathematical or graphical* representation of the antenna's far-field radiation properties as a function of $(\theta, \phi)$.
- 📌 **Isotropic vs Omnidirectional**: Isotropic is *theoretical* (uniform in 3D); omnidirectional is *practical* (uniform in azimuth, varies in elevation).
- 📌 **Three Field Regions**: Reactive near-field → Fresnel (radiating near-field) → Fraunhofer (far-field). The far-field condition is $r_{ff} \geq 2D^2/\lambda$.
- 📌 **Far-Field Criteria**: (1) $E \perp H \perp \hat{r}$ (transverse), (2) angular pattern *independent of $r$*, (3) fields decay as $1/r$, (4) power density as $1/r^2$.
- 📌 **Normalized Field Pattern**: $F_n(\theta, \phi) = E(\theta, \phi)/E_{max}$.
- 📌 **Normalized Power Pattern**: $P_n(\theta, \phi) = \vert F_n \vert^2$.
- 📌 **dB Conversion**: $\text{Power (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(P_n)$.
- 📌 **Lobes**: Main lobe (maximum), side lobes (others), back lobe (at 180°), nulls (zero crossings).
- 📌 **HPBW**: Angular width between -3 dB points; equivalent to $F_n^2 = 1/2$ or $F_n = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- 📌 **FNBW**: Angular width between *first nulls* on either side of the main lobe.
- 📌 **Directivity**: $D(\theta, \phi) = 4\pi U(\theta, \phi)/P_{rad}$; max value is $D_0 = 4\pi/\Omega_A$.
- 📌 **Beam Solid Angle**: $\Omega_A = \oint P_n \, d\Omega$; the equivalent solid cone of radiation.
- 📌 **Kraus's Approximation**: $D_0 \approx 41253/(\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2)$ (degrees²) — valid only for pencil-beam antennas.
- 📌 **Half-Wave Dipole**: HPBW = 78° (E-plane, more accurate), $D_0 = 1.64$ (2.15 dBi), omnidirectional in H-plane.
- 📌 **Parabolic Reflector**: $\theta_{HP} \approx 70\lambda/D$ degrees, $D_0 \approx (\pi D/\lambda)^2 \cdot \eta_{aperture}$.
- 📌 **E-plane**: Contains E-field and boresight. **H-plane**: Contains H-field and boresight. They are mutually orthogonal.
- 📌 **Beam Efficiency**: $\eta_B = \Omega_{MB}/\Omega_A$, where $\Omega_{MB}$ is the main beam solid angle (within HPBW or first nulls).
- 📌 **SLL (Side-Lobe Level)**: Always expressed in **dB**, negative; lower SLL = cleaner pattern.
- 📌 **Front-to-Back Ratio**: Always in **dB**; higher F/B = less interference from rear direction.
- 📌 **Polar vs Rectangular Plots**: Polar = realistic visual feel; rectangular (in dB) = precise lobe analysis.
- 📌 **Three Pattern Regions in Polar Plot**: Main lobe (centered on boresight), minor/side lobes (off-boresight), back lobe (at 180°).
- 📌 **Units and Conventions**: Angles in *degrees* for Kraus's formula; angles in *radians* for trigonometric calculations. Always state which.
- 📌 **KTU 2024 Critical**: The radiation pattern is *always a far-field* property. Never describe near-field patterns as "radiation patterns."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Power Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RADIATION POWER DENSITY (W)**: Radiation Power Density, also known as **Poynting Vector** or **Power Flux Density**, is defined as the *time-average power per unit area* radiated by an antenna in a given direction. It represents the electromagnetic energy crossing a unit surface area per unit time at a far-field distance from the antenna.

The formal mathematical definition of **Time-Average Poynting Vector** $\vec{W}$ (in $\text{W/m}^2$) is:

$$
\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\left[\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*\right]
$$

Where:
- $\vec{E}$ is the **phasor (complex) representation** of the electric field intensity (V/m)
- $\vec{H}^*$ is the **complex conjugate** of the phasor representation of the magnetic field intensity (A/m)
- The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ arises because the fields are sinusoidal (RMS averaging)

> [!NOTE]
> In free space, the radiation power density and the Poynting vector are equivalent terms. Power density is a **scalar magnitude**, while the Poynting vector is a **vector quantity** pointing in the direction of wave propagation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bulb" Model

Imagine an **incandescent light bulb** glowing in the center of a dark, spherical room. The bulb radiates light (electromagnetic energy) in all directions. If we place a small screen of area $1\,\text{m}^2$ at a distance $r$ from the bulb:
- **Closer to the bulb** → the screen intercepts *more* light energy per second → **Higher Power Density**
- **Farther from the bulb** → the same total energy spreads over a *larger spherical surface* → **Lower Power Density**

This is exactly how an **isotropic antenna** radiates RF energy. As the wavefront travels outward as an expanding sphere, the *total power remains constant*, but the *area increases* as $4\pi r^2$. Hence, power density *decreases inversely with the square of distance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants used in Radiation Density Calculations:**
> - Intrinsic Impedance of Free Space: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$
> - Speed of Light: $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$
> - Permittivity of Free Space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$

## 1.3 Relationship Between E-field and Power Density

For a plane wave radiating in free space, the magnitude of the time-average Poynting vector is directly proportional to the **square of the electric field magnitude**:

$$
W = \frac{\vert E \vert^2}{2\eta_0} = \frac{\vert E \vert^2}{240\pi}
$$

This relation is extremely useful in antenna measurements because if you measure the E-field at a distant point, you can directly compute the power density at that location.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Radiated by an Isotropic Radiator

An **Isotropic Radiator** is a *hypothetical, lossless* point source that radiates power *uniformly in all directions* (uniform radiation intensity $U_0$).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The total radiated power $P_{rad}$ spreads uniformly over the surface of an imaginary sphere of radius $r$.
2. Surface area of sphere $= 4\pi r^2$.
3. Therefore, the power density $W$ at distance $r$ is total power divided by surface area.
4. Since an isotropic radiator has no directive preference, $W$ is the same at *all points* on the sphere.

## 2.2 Power Radiated by a Directional Antenna

A **Directional Antenna** concentrates its radiated power in certain directions (the main lobe) at the expense of other directions. The radiation intensity $U(\theta,\phi)$ varies with direction. The total radiated power is the integral of intensity over the solid angle $4\pi$:

$$
P_{rad} = \oiint_{\Omega} U(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi
$$

The radiation power density at any point in the far field is given by:

$$
W(r,\theta,\phi) = \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{r^2}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}[\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*]$ | Time-average Poynting vector | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 2 | $W = \dfrac{\vert E \vert^2}{2\eta_0} = \dfrac{\vert E \vert^2}{240\pi}$ | Power density in terms of E-field (free space) | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | $W = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{4\pi r^2}$ | Power density from an **isotropic** radiator at distance $r$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 4 | $W(r,\theta,\phi) = \dfrac{U(\theta,\phi)}{r^2}$ | Power density from **directional** antenna | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{P_{rad}\, G(\theta,\phi)}{4\pi r^2}$ | Power density with **antenna gain** $G$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 6 | $U = r^2 W$ | **Radiation Intensity** (power per unit solid angle) | $\text{W/sr}$ |
| 7 | $P_{rad} = \oint U\, d\Omega$ | Total radiated power (closed surface integral) | $\text{W}$ |
| 8 | $G(\theta,\phi) = \dfrac{4\pi U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{rad}}$ | Gain in terms of radiation intensity | dimensionless (dBi) |
| 9 | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi\,\Omega$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL CONVERSION**: Radiation Power Density expressed in $\text{dBW/m}^2$:
> $$W_{\text{dBW/m}^2} = 10\log_{10}(W)$$
> where $W$ is in linear $\text{W/m}^2$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Link Budget Calculation in Wireless Communication**: Determining signal strength at the receiver requires knowledge of $W$ at the receive antenna.
- **Radar Equation**: The maximum detectable range depends on power density illuminating the target.
- **EMF Safety / SAR Compliance**: Regulatory bodies (FCC, ICNIRP) set maximum permissible power density limits (e.g., $10\,\text{W/m}^2$ for occupational exposure at cellular frequencies).
- **Satellite Communication**: EIRP (Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power) calculations use power density formulas extensively.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Density from E and H Fields of a Plane Wave

We begin with the time-harmonic representation of fields:
- Electric field phasor: $\vec{E} = \hat{x} E_0\, e^{-j\beta z}$
- Magnetic field phasor: $\vec{H} = \hat{y} \dfrac{E_0}{\eta_0}\, e^{-j\beta z}$

**Step 1**: Form the cross product $\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*$:
$$
\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^* = \left(\hat{x} E_0\, e^{-j\beta z}\right) \times \left(\hat{y} \dfrac{E_0}{\eta_0}\, e^{+j\beta z}\right)
$$

**Step 2**: Use the vector identity $\hat{x} \times \hat{y} = \hat{z}$:
$$
\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^* = \hat{z}\, \dfrac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}\, e^{-j\beta z}e^{+j\beta z} = \hat{z}\, \dfrac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}
$$

**Step 3**: Apply time-averaging and take the real part:
$$
\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\left[\hat{z}\, \dfrac{E_0^2}{\eta_0}\right] = \hat{z}\, \dfrac{E_0^2}{2\eta_0}
$$

**Step 4**: Substitute $\eta_0 = 120\pi$:
$$
W = \dfrac{E_0^2}{2 \times 120\pi} = \dfrac{E_0^2}{240\pi}\,\text{W/m}^2
$$

**Logic Note**: This confirms that the power density is in the direction of propagation ($\hat{z}$), and is proportional to the **square** of the E-field amplitude.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Density of an Isotropic Antenna at Distance $r$

**Step 1**: Total radiated power $P_{rad}$ is uniformly distributed over the surface of a sphere of radius $r$.

**Step 2**: Surface area of the sphere is:
$$
A_{sphere} = 4\pi r^2
$$

**Step 3**: Power density is total power divided by the area through which it flows:
$$
W_{iso}(r) = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 4**: For a **directional** antenna, the power density is *enhanced* by the gain function $G(\theta,\phi)$ in the direction of maximum radiation:
$$
W(r,\theta,\phi) = \dfrac{P_{rad}\, G(\theta,\phi)}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 5**: This is the **Friis Transmission Equation** foundation. In dB form:
$$
W_{\text{dB}} = P_{rad,\text{dB}} + G_{\text{dB}} - 10\log_{10}(4\pi r^2)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Density from a Hertzian Dipole

The far-field components of an infinitesimal (Hertzian) dipole of length $l$ carrying current $I_0$ are:
$$
E_\theta = \dfrac{j\eta_0 \beta I_0 l\, \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\, e^{-j\beta r}
$$
$$
H_\phi = \dfrac{j\beta I_0 l\, \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\, e^{-j\beta r}
$$

**Step 1**: Compute $\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}[\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*]$:
$$
W = \frac{1}{2}\dfrac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 l^2 \sin^2\theta}{(4\pi)^2 r^2}
$$

**Step 2**: Recognize that $\eta_0 \beta^2 = \eta_0 (\omega/c)^2 = \omega^2 \mu_0 / c$ (in free space). Let us keep it in the canonical form:
$$
W = \frac{\eta_0}{2}\left(\frac{\beta I_0 l \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3**: The radiation intensity $U = r^2 W$ becomes:
$$
U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 l^2 \sin^2\theta}{32\pi^2}
$$

**Step 4**: Total radiated power (integrate over $4\pi$ steradians):
$$
P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi
$$

**Step 5**: Using $\int_0^{2\pi}d\phi \int_0^{\pi}\sin^3\theta\, d\theta = 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{8\pi}{3}$:
$$
P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 l^2}{32\pi^2} \cdot \frac{8\pi}{3} = \frac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 l^2}{12\pi}
$$

**Logic Note**: This matches the standard result $P_{rad} = I_0^2 R_{rad}$ where the radiation resistance of a Hertzian dipole is $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2\,\Omega$.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem**: A transmitting antenna radiates $P_{rad} = 100\,\text{W}$ isotropically. Find the power density at a distance of $r = 10\,\text{km}$. Also find the E-field magnitude at that point.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Apply isotropic power density formula:
$$
W = \dfrac{100}{4\pi (10000)^2} = \dfrac{100}{4\pi \times 10^8}
$$

**Step 2**: Evaluate:
$$
W = \dfrac{100}{1.2566 \times 10^9} \approx 7.96 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3**: Convert to $\mu\text{W/m}^2$:
$$
W \approx 0.0796\,\mu\text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4**: Compute E-field from $W = E_0^2/(240\pi)$:
$$
E_0 = \sqrt{240\pi W} = \sqrt{240\pi \times 7.96 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

**Step 5**: Numerical evaluation:
$$
E_0 = \sqrt{6.005 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 7.75 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V/m} = 7.75\,\text{mV/m}
$$

**Answer**: $W \approx 7.96 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2$ and $E_0 \approx 7.75\,\text{mV/m}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Radiation Power Density Propagation Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transmitter Source P_rad] --> B[Antenna Radiation Mechanism]
    B --> C[Far-Field Wavefront Formation]
    C --> D{Distance r from Antenna}
    D --> E[Spherical Wavefront Expansion]
    E --> F[Power Density W at point]
    F --> G[Directional Dependence U theta phi]
    F --> H[E-Field Component E_theta]
    F --> I[H-Field Component H_phi]
    H --> J[Poynting Vector Calculation]
    I --> J
    G --> K[Gain G theta phi application]
    K --> L[Final W = P_rad G / 4 pi r squared]
    J --> M[Free space propagation]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Receiver Antenna Capture]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Power Density Decay vs. Distance (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Antenna r = 1 m] --> P2[Antenna r = 10 m]
    P2 --> P3[Antenna r = 100 m]
    P3 --> P4[Antenna r = 1000 m]
    
    P1 --> W1[W high: 1 by 4pi]
    P2 --> W2[W reduces 100x: 1 by 400pi]
    P3 --> W3[W reduces 10000x: 1 by 40000pi]
    P4 --> W4[W reduces 1e6 x: 1 by 4e6 pi]
    
    style W1 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style W2 fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style W3 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style W4 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Density Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Inputs: P_rad, G theta phi, r, frequency f] --> CALC1[Compute 4 pi r squared]
    CALC1 --> PRODUCT[Multiply P_rad by G]
    PRODUCT --> DIVIDE[Divide by 4 pi r squared]
    DIVIDE --> WOUT[Power Density W in W per meter squared]
    WOUT --> EFIELD[Derive E field from W]
    EFIELD --> DBCONV[Convert to dB scale]
    DBCONV --> OUTPUT[Output: W dBW per meter squared, E field V per m]
    
    style INPUT fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style WOUT fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTPUT fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note**: The diagrams above illustrate that power density is a *conserved quantity* (total power remains constant) but its *intensity per unit area* decreases as the wavefront expands. This is a direct consequence of the **inverse square law** for radiation in 3-D space.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Radiation Power Density. What is its unit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Radiation Power Density is the time-average power per unit area radiated by an antenna in a given direction. It is mathematically defined as $\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}[\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*]$. **Unit**: $\text{W/m}^2$ (Watts per square meter). In decibel form, it is expressed as $\text{dBW/m}^2$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the relationship between electric field intensity and radiation power density in free space.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a plane wave propagating in free space, the radiation power density is related to the E-field by the equation $W = \dfrac{\vert E \vert^2}{2\eta_0}$, where $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space. This shows that power density is proportional to the **square** of the E-field amplitude and is independent of the H-field separately.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the time-average Poynting vector. Show that the power density radiated by an isotropic antenna at a distance $r$ is given by $W = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{4\pi r^2}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1** (2 marks): The time-harmonic E and H fields in phasor form are $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$. The instantaneous Poynting vector is $\vec{W}(t) = \vec{E}(t)\times\vec{H}(t)$. Time-averaging over one period $T$:

$$
\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \vec{E}(t)\times\vec{H}(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2** (2 marks): For sinusoidal steady-state, substituting $\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re}[\vec{E}e^{j\omega t}]$ and $\vec{H}(t) = \text{Re}[\vec{H}e^{j\omega t}]$, the time average yields:
$$
\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}[\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*]
$$

**Step 3** (1.5 marks): For an isotropic antenna, the total radiated power $P_{rad}$ spreads uniformly over the surface of a sphere of radius $r$. The surface area is $4\pi r^2$. Therefore:
$$
W = \frac{P_{rad}}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 4** (1.5 marks): This inverse-square variation arises because in 3-D space, the radiating wavefront area scales as $r^2$ while total power is conserved.

---

**(b)** A microwave transmitter radiates $P_{rad} = 1\,\text{kW}$ isotropically. Calculate (i) the power density at $r = 5\,\text{km}$, and (ii) the corresponding RMS electric field intensity at that point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Power Density (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1**: Apply $W = \dfrac{P_{rad}}{4\pi r^2}$:
$$
W = \frac{1000}{4\pi (5000)^2} = \frac{1000}{4\pi \times 25 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 2**: Simplify denominator:
$$
W = \frac{1000}{3.1416 \times 10^8} \approx 3.183 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W/m}^2 = 3.183\,\mu\text{W/m}^2
$$

**(ii) E-field (3.5 marks):**

**Step 1**: Use $W = \dfrac{\vert E \vert^2}{2\eta_0}$:
$$
\vert E \vert = \sqrt{2\eta_0 W} = \sqrt{2 \times 120\pi \times 3.183 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 2**: Numerical evaluation:
$$
\vert E \vert = \sqrt{240\pi \times 3.183 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{2.40 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0490\,\text{V/m} = 49\,\text{mV/m}
$$

**Final Answer**: $W \approx 3.18\,\mu\text{W/m}^2$ and $\vert E \vert \approx 49\,\text{mV/m}$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Define Radiation Intensity. Derive the relationship between radiation intensity and radiation power density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks)**: **Definition**: Radiation Intensity $U(\theta,\phi)$ is defined as the *power radiated per unit solid angle* by an antenna in a given direction $(\theta,\phi)$. **Unit**: Watts per steradian (W/sr).

**Step 2 (2.5 marks)**: A small area element on a sphere of radius $r$ subtends a solid angle $d\Omega = dA/r^2$ at the antenna. The power flowing through $dA$ is $dP = W\, dA$. The radiation intensity is therefore:
$$
U = \frac{dP}{d\Omega} = \frac{W\, dA}{dA/r^2} = W\, r^2
$$

**Step 3 (2.5 marks)**: Hence the relationship is:
$$
W = \frac{U}{r^2}
$$

This means power density at distance $r$ equals the radiation intensity in that direction, divided by $r^2$.

---

**(b)** A directional antenna with maximum gain $G_{max} = 20$ (linear, i.e., 13 dBi) radiates $P_{rad} = 50\,\text{W}$. Find the maximum power density at a distance of $r = 2\,\text{km}$ along the boresight direction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks)**: State the power density formula with gain:
$$
W_{max} = \frac{P_{rad}\, G_{max}}{4\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)**: Substitute the values:
$$
W_{max} = \frac{50 \times 20}{4\pi \times (2000)^2} = \frac{1000}{4\pi \times 4 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)**: Evaluate:
$$
W_{max} = \frac{1000}{5.0265 \times 10^7} \approx 1.989 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{W/m}^2 \approx 19.89\,\mu\text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: In dB form:
$$
W_{max,\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(1.989 \times 10^{-5}) \approx -47.01\,\text{dBW/m}^2
$$

**Final Answer**: $W_{max} \approx 19.89\,\mu\text{W/m}^2$ or equivalently $-47.01\,\text{dBW/m}^2$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing RMS and Peak Fields**: When using $W = \vert E \vert^2/(2\eta_0)$, $\vert E \vert$ is the **peak (amplitude)** E-field, not RMS. Using the wrong form leads to a factor-of-2 error.
> 2. **Forgetting the $4\pi$ in the denominator**: Students often write $W = P_{rad}/r^2$, missing the $4\pi$. This is the most common KTU valuation trap and results in loss of **2 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency**: If $r$ is in km, convert to meters *before* computing $W$ (since $W$ is in $\text{W/m}^2$).
> 4. **Gain vs. Directivity**: In problems where **efficiency** $\eta$ is given, $G = \eta D$. The formula becomes $W = P_{rad} G/(4\pi r^2)$. Students sometimes incorrectly use $D$ instead of $G$.
> 5. **Directional vs. Isotropic**: If gain is not specified, the antenna is **assumed isotropic** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Radiation Power Density**

- ✅ **Definition**: Power per unit area radiated in a given direction; equivalent to the magnitude of the time-average Poynting vector.
- ✅ **Poynting Vector**: $\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}[\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^*]$ — measured in $\text{W/m}^2$.
- ✅ **Free Space Relation**: $W = \vert E \vert^2/(2\eta_0) = \vert E \vert^2/(240\pi)$.
- ✅ **Isotropic Formula**: $W = P_{rad}/(4\pi r^2)$ — *the most frequently tested equation*.
- ✅ **Directional Formula**: $W(r,\theta,\phi) = P_{rad} G(\theta,\phi)/(4\pi r^2)$.
- ✅ **Radiation Intensity Link**: $U = r^2 W$ and $P_{rad} = \oint U\, d\Omega$.
- ✅ **Gain Definition**: $G = 4\pi U/P_{rad}$ (linear), or $G_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(G)$.
- ✅ **Free Space Impedance**: Always remember $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$.
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law**: $W \propto 1/r^2$ — double the distance → one-quarter the power density.
- ✅ **dBW/m² Conversion**: Use $10\log_{10}(W)$ to convert linear $W$ to dB scale.
- ✅ **Friis Connection**: Power density formula is the *backbone* of the Friis Transmission Equation used in wireless link design.
- ✅ **Exam Tip**: Always write the units explicitly — KTU examiners allocate 0.5–1 mark for correct units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
