**Step 2 — Convert Return Loss to Reflection Coefficient:**

$$\text{RL (dB)} = -20\log_{10}(\vert\Gamma\vert)$$

$$12 = -20\log_{10}(\vert\Gamma\vert)$$

$$\log_{10}(\vert\Gamma\vert) = -\frac{12}{20} = -0.6$$

$$\vert\Gamma\vert = 10^{-0.6} = 0.2512$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\vert\Gamma\vert^2$:**

$$\vert\Gamma\vert^{2} = (0.2512)^{2} = 0.0631$$

**Step 4 — Total Efficiency:**

$$e_0 = e_{rad} \cdot (1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^{2}) = 0.85 \times (1 - 0.0631) = 0.85 \times 0.9369 = 0.7964$$

$$e_0 \approx 0.7964 \text{ (or 79.64\%)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Gain:**

In linear scale: $D = 10^{6.5/10} = 10^{0.65} = 4.467$

$$G = e_0 \cdot D = 0.7964 \times 4.467 = 3.557 \text{ (linear)}$$

In dBi:

$$G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(3.557) = 5.51 \text{ dBi}$$

**Step 6 — Verification using dB addition:**

$$G_{dBi} = D_{dBi} + 10\log_{10}(e_0) = 6.5 + 10\log_{10}(0.7964) = 6.5 + (-0.988) = 5.512 \text{ dBi} \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Using $P_{rad}/P_{incident}$ instead of $P_{rad}/P_{in}$ in the definition. The "accepted" power $P_{in}$ is *after* reflection, not before.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing $e_{rad}$ with $e_0$. Total efficiency is **always less** than or equal to radiation efficiency.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert return loss (negative dB value) properly. Return loss is positive in magnitude; use $\text{RL} = 20\log_{10}(1/\vert\Gamma\vert)$ or equivalently $-20\log_{10}(\vert\Gamma\vert)$.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Using dBi in the multiplication $G = e_0 \cdot D$. Always convert directivity to linear scale **before** multiplying.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Skipping the unit conversion when given skin depth in mm. Always convert to meters before squaring.
> 6. **Where students lose the most marks (≈ 2–3 marks per question):** Confusing the formulas for *radiation* vs. *total* efficiency. A clear statement of "since the question asks for total efficiency, we must include mismatch loss" earns full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Core Definition (memorize verbatim):** $e_{rad} = P_{rad}/P_{in} = R_{rad}/(R_{rad} + R_{loss})$
- **Total Efficiency (memorize verbatim):** $e_0 = e_{rad} (1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2) = e_{rad} \cdot e_{match}$
- **Gain Relation:** $G = e_0 \cdot D$ (linear) → $G_{dB} = D_{dB} + 10\log_{10}(e_0)$
- **Two Loss Sources:** Conduction (depends on $R_s$) + Dielectric (depends on $\tan\delta$)
- **Surface Resistivity Formula:** $R_s = \sqrt{\pi f \mu / \sigma}$ — increases with frequency.
- **Skin Depth Formula:** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ — decreases with frequency.
- **Loss Tangent Formula:** $\tan\delta = \sigma_d/(\omega \varepsilon')$
- **Reflection Coefficient:** $\Gamma = (Z_{in} - Z_0)/(Z_{in} + Z_0)$, $\vert\Gamma\vert = (S-1)/(S+1)$
- **Return Loss to $\Gamma$:** $\vert\Gamma\vert = 10^{-\text{RL}/20}$
- **Always convert to linear scale** when multiplying quantities like $G = e_0 \cdot D$.
- **Typical Exam Traps:** "VSWR = 1" means perfect match, so $e_0 = e_{rad}$. "Lossless antenna" means $R_{loss} = 0$, so $e_{rad} = 1$.
- **Real-World Reference:** A well-designed 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi patch antenna has $e_{rad} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$; a low-cost dipole might be $\approx 0.7$–$0.85$.
- **Why $e_{rad}$ cannot exceed 1:** Energy conservation — you cannot radiate more than what enters the antenna.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state the assumption "assuming all dielectric/conduction losses are lumped into $R_{loss}$" when applying the formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effective Aperture Area
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effective Aperture Area # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Effective Aperture (Aₑ)** of a receiving antenna is formally defined as the ratio of the **time-average power delivered to a matched load** to the **time-average power flux density (S)** of the incident plane wave at the antenna. > [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Standard Definition > The Effective Aperture of an antenna is a measure of the *effective area* presented by the antenna to an incident electromagnetic wave. It quantifies how much incident power the antenna can capture and deliver to its terminated load. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$ A_e = \frac{P_r}{S} \quad [\text{square meters, m}^2] $$ Where: - **P_r** = Power received and delivered to the matched load (Watts) - **S** = Incident power flux density (Watts/m²) - **Aₑ** = Effective aperture (m²) It is sometimes also called the **Effective Area** of the antenna and is generally **a function of direction**. The *maximum* value of Aₑ(θ, φ) is referred to as the **Maximum Effective Aperture (Aₑₘ)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] The "Rain Bucket" Analogy > Imagine standing in a rainstorm holding a circular bucket. The bucket has a *physical* opening of, say, 1 m². But because of its shape, the wind direction, and the bucket's tilt, it doesn't actually catch rain over that entire physical area. Some raindrops bounce out, some miss the opening, and some are deflected away. The *effective* collection area might be only 0.6 m², even though the physical opening is 1 m². > An **antenna** behaves exactly the same way. Its *physical aperture* (e.g., the dish of a satellite TV antenna) is not the same as the *effective aperture*. The **Effective Aperture (Aₑ)** is the "true catching area" of the antenna — the area over which it effectively intercepts incident electromagnetic power from free space and converts it into useful signal power at its terminals. **Key physical facts to remember:** - The speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** - The wavelength: **λ = c / f** - Aₑ is always **less than or equal to** the physical aperture (A_p) of the antenna - The ratio Aₑ / A_p is called the **Aperture Efficiency (ε_ap)** # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations The concept of effective aperture is rooted in the **reciprocity theorem** of antennas and bridges the gap between the antenna's *circuit parameters* (gain, directivity) and its *spatial parameters* (physical area, wavelength). ### Why does Effective Aperture exist? 1. **Energy Conservation**: A receiving antenna does not physically "suck in" power from a region larger than its size. Instead, it scatters and absorbs electromagnetic energy based on its geometry and excitation. 2. **Pattern Equivalence**: By the reciprocity principle, the *receiving pattern* of an antenna is identical to its *transmitting pattern* (in the same polarization). 3. **Antenna as a Spatial Filter**: It captures power from a specific solid angle (the beam solid angle Ω_A) and rejects power from other directions. ### Core Logical Steps in Understanding Aₑ 1. **Step 1** — An incident plane wave carries power density **S** (W/m²). 2. **Step 2** — The antenna intercepts this density over its **effective** area **Aₑ**, not its physical area. 3. **Step 3** — The captured power becomes the received power: **P_r = S · Aₑ**. 4. **Step 4** — The power density itself is related to the transmitter's parameters: **S = P_t G_t / (4πR²)**. 5. **Step 5** — Combining with **Friis Transmission Equation** yields the fundamental link: **Aₑ = Gλ²/(4π)**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] Master this table — these equations are asked repeatedly in KTU exams. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|---------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $A_e = \dfrac{P_r}{S}$ | Fundamental definition of effective aperture | m² | | 2 | $A_{em} = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Maximum effective aperture in terms of **Gain (G)** | m² | | 3 | $A_{em} = \dfrac{D \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | For a lossless antenna, in terms of **Directivity (D)** | m² | | 4 | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Effective aperture of an **Isotropic antenna** (G = 1) | m² | | 5 | $\Omega_A = \dfrac{4\pi}{D}$ | Beam solid angle from directivity | sr (steradians) | | 6 | $D = \dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Directivity from beam solid angle and Aₑ | dimensionless | | 7 | $\varepsilon_{ap} = \dfrac{A_e}{A_p}$ | Aperture efficiency (≤ 1) | dimensionless | | 8 | $A_e = \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot A_p$ | Effective aperture from physical area and efficiency | m² | | 9 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi R)^2}$ | Friis Transmission Equation | W | | 10 | $D \approx \dfrac{4\pi}{\theta_{3dB,E} \cdot \theta_{3dB,H}}$ | Approximate directivity from 3-dB beamwidths (radians) | dimensionless | > **Symbols used:** G = Gain, D = Directivity, λ = wavelength, P_r = received power, P_t = transmitted power, R = range, A_p = physical aperture, ε_ap = aperture efficiency, Ω_A = beam solid angle, θ_3dB = 3-dB beamwidth. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Satellite Communications**: Parabolic dish designers use **Aₑ = ε_ap · A_p** to determine the dish diameter required for a given gain at a specific frequency (C-band, Ku-band, Ka-band). - **Radar Cross-Section (RCS)**: The maximum effective aperture is critical in calculating the **monostatic radar range equation**. - **Radio Astronomy**: Large reflector antennas (e.g., the 305-m Arecibo dish) achieve high Aₑ to detect extremely faint cosmic signals. - **Wireless Power Transfer & RFID**: Tag antennas are designed with optimal Aₑ for given read distances. - **Phased Arrays**: The element effective aperture determines the array's scan performance and element spacing. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Relationship Aₑ = Gλ²/(4π) This is the **most important derivation** in this module for KTU examinations. We will derive it from the Friis Transmission Equation. ### Given Setup - Transmitting antenna: gain **G_t**, input power **P_t** - Receiving antenna: maximum effective aperture **Aₑₘ**, gain **G_r** - Separation distance: **R** (in far field) - Operating wavelength: **λ** - Both antennas are impedance-matched and polarization-matched. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Power density at the receiver:** The power density (Poynting vector magnitude) at a distance R from a transmit antenna of gain G_t radiating power P_t is: $$ S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} $$ *Logic*: Total radiated power isotropically would spread over a sphere of area $4\pi R^2$, but the antenna concentrates it by gain $G_t$. --- **Step 2 — Power captured by the receiving antenna:** By the *fundamental definition* of effective aperture, the power delivered to a matched load is: $$ P_r = S \cdot A_{em} $$ *Logic*: The receiving antenna intercepts the incident power density over its effective area $A_{em}$. --- **Step 3 — Substitute S into the received power equation:** $$ P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot A_{em} $$ --- **Step 4 — Equate with the Friis Transmission Equation:** The standard Friis transmission equation states: $$ P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi R)^2} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{16\pi^2 R^2} $$ *Logic*: Friis' equation is the *empirically verified* form of the free-space path loss. --- **Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for P_r:** $$ \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot A_{em} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{16\pi^2 R^2} $$ --- **Step 6 — Cancel common terms (P_t, G_t, R²) from both sides:** $$ \frac{A_{em}}{4\pi} = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{16\pi^2} $$ --- **Step 7 — Solve for Aₑₘ:** $$ A_{em} = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{16\pi^2} \cdot 4\pi $$ $$ A_{em} = \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi} $$ ### ✅ Final Result: $$ \boxed{\,A_{em} = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] For a lossless antenna, G = D, so the result is also valid with directivity: $A_{em} = \dfrac{D \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Effective Aperture of an Isotropic Antenna An **isotropic antenna** is a hypothetical, lossless, omni-directional radiator with gain **G = 1** (0 dBi) in all directions. Substituting **G = 1** into the master equation: $$ A_{e,\text{iso}} = \frac{(1) \cdot \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} $$ ### Numerical Example: For an isotropic antenna operating at **f = 1 GHz**: - Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8) / (10^9) = 0.3$ m - Effective aperture: $A_e = (0.3)^2 / (4\pi) = 0.09 / 12.566 = 0.00716 \text{ m}^2 = 71.6 \text{ cm}^2$ > [!IMPORTANT] Implication: Even an ideal isotropic antenna intercepts only a finite effective area. The size of this aperture grows with the **square of the wavelength**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Aperture Efficiency and Beamwidth Approximation For aperture antennas (like parabolic dishes, horns, slots), the **aperture efficiency** is defined as: $$ \varepsilon_{ap} = \frac{A_e}{A_p} $$ Where **A_p** is the physical area of the antenna's aperture (e.g., $\pi D^2/4$ for a circular dish of diameter D). The **approximate relationship** between maximum directivity and 3-dB beamwidths (valid for antennas with single main beam and small sidelobes) is: $$ D \approx \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} \approx \frac{4\pi}{\theta_{3dB,E} \cdot \theta_{3dB,H}} $$ where $\theta_{3dB,E}$ and $\theta_{3dB,H}$ are the 3-dB beamwidths in the E- and H-planes in **radians**. Combining with $A_{em} = D\lambda^2/(4\pi)$: $$ A_{em} \approx \frac{\lambda^2}{\theta_{3dB,E} \cdot \theta_{3dB,H}} $$ > [!NOTE] This shows that **narrower beamwidth ⇒ larger effective aperture**, a key design trade-off in radar and satellite systems. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Receiving Antenna System with Effective Aperture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the relationship between an incident plane wave, the antenna's effective aperture, and the delivered power to the load. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Incident Plane Wave
Power Flux Density S W per m2] --> B[Receiving Antenna
Effective Aperture Ae] B --> C[Matching Network
and Polarization Filter] C --> D[Matched Load
Receives Power Pr] B -.->|Loss or Mismatch| E[Reflected Power
Pr reflected] F[Physical Aperture Ap] -.->|Efficiency factor epsilon ap| B G[Wavelength lambda] -.->|Master Equation Ae equals G lambda squared over 4 pi| B H[Antenna Gain G] -.->|Master Equation Ae equals G lambda squared over 4 pi| B classDef source fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef antenna fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef load fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 classDef param fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000 class A source class B antenna class C,D,E load class F,G,H param ``` **Interpretation of the diagram:** - The **incident wave** carries power density S into the antenna. - The antenna's **effective aperture Aₑ** acts as a scaling factor that converts flux density to captured power. - The captured power is filtered by matching/polarization and delivered as **P_r** to the load. - Losses manifest as **reflected power** (P_r reflected). - The relationship between Aₑ, λ, and G is shown explicitly via the **master equation**. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inter-relationships among Antenna Parameters This block diagram maps how the **gain**, **directivity**, **physical aperture**, and **effective aperture** are interconnected — a frequent KTU diagram-style question. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Physical Aperture Ap] -->|epsilon ap| P2[Effective Aperture Ae] P2 -->|Ae equals G lambda squared over 4 pi| P3[Gain G] P3 -->|G equals eta D| P4[Directivity D] P4 -->|D equals 4 pi over Omega A| P5[Beam Solid Angle Omega A] P5 -->|Omega A approx theta 3dB E times theta 3dB H| P6[3 dB Beamwidths] P1 -->|Ap equals pi D squared over 4| P7[Dish Diameter D for parabolic] P2 -->|A e iso equals lambda squared over 4 pi| P8[Isotropic Reference Ae] P3 -->|G iso equals 1| P8 classDef key fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef derived fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef reference fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 class P1,P2,P3,P4 key class P5,P6,P7 derived class P8 reference ``` **Reading Guide for the KTU student:** - Start at **Physical Aperture A_p** (a design parameter). - Apply **aperture efficiency ε_ap** to obtain **Aₑ**. - Use the **master equation** to get **Gain G**. - For a lossless antenna, G = D (Directivity). - Directivity inversely relates to **Beam Solid Angle Ω_A**. - Ω_A is approximated by the product of the two 3-dB beamwidths. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define the term "Effective Aperture" of a receiving antenna. State its relationship with the antenna's gain and operating wavelength.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** The Effective Aperture (Aₑ) of a receiving antenna is defined as the ratio of the power delivered to a matched load (P_r) to the power flux density (S) of the incident plane wave. $$ A_e = \frac{P_r}{S} \quad \text{(in m}^2\text{)} $$ **Relationship (1 mark):** The maximum effective aperture is related to the antenna's gain G and the operating wavelength λ by: $$ A_{em} = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi} $$ This shows that Aₑ is proportional to G and λ². --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is the effective aperture of an isotropic antenna at a frequency of 600 MHz?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1 — Calculate wavelength:** $$ \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{600 \times 10^6} = 0.5 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the isotropic formula (G = 1):** $$ A_{e,\text{iso}} = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{4\pi} = \frac{0.25}{12.566} \approx 0.0199 \text{ m}^2 \approx 199 \text{ cm}^2 $$ **Final Answer:** **Aₑ ≈ 0.0199 m² (≈ 199 cm²)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### 📋 Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Derive the relationship between the maximum effective aperture (Aₑₘ) of a receiving antenna and its gain (G) starting from the Friis transmission equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A parabolic dish antenna has a physical diameter of 2 m and operates at 6 GHz with an aperture efficiency of 0.55. Calculate: (i) the effective aperture, (ii) the gain in dBi, and (iii) the approximate 3-dB beamwidth in degrees. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution A(a) — Derivation (7 marks) | Step | Equation/Reasoning | Marks | |------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | Power density at receiver: $S = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$ | 1 | | 2 | Power received: $P_r = S \cdot A_{em} = \dfrac{P_t G_t A_{em}}{4\pi R^2}$ | 1 | | 3 | Friis transmission equation: $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi R)^2}$ | 1 | | 4 | Equating both: $\dfrac{P_t G_t A_{em}}{4\pi R^2} = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{16\pi^2 R^2}$ | 1 | | 5 | Cancel $P_t, G_t, R^2$: $\dfrac{A_{em}}{4\pi} = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{16\pi^2}$ | 1 | | 6 | Solving: $A_{em} = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | 1 | | 7 | Conclusion — for lossless antenna, $G = D$, so $A_{em} = D\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ | 1 | #### Solution A(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** - Dish diameter: $D_{\text{dish}} = 2$ m - Frequency: $f = 6$ GHz = $6 \times 10^9$ Hz - Aperture efficiency: $\varepsilon_{ap} = 0.55$ **Step 1 — Wavelength:** $$ \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Physical aperture:** $$ A_p = \frac{\pi D_{\text{dish}}^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (2)^2}{4} = \pi \approx 3.1416 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — (i) Effective aperture:** $$ A_e = \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot A_p = 0.55 \times 3.1416 \approx 1.728 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Gain (linear):** $$ G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi \times 1.728}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{21.71}{0.0025} \approx 8684.7 $$ **Step 5 — Gain (dBi):** $$ G_{\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}(8684.7) \approx 39.39 \text{ dBi} $$ **Step 6 — (iii) Approximate 3-dB beamwidth:** For a circular parabolic dish, the 3-dB beamwidth in radians is: $$ \theta_{3dB} \approx \frac{70^\circ \lambda}{D_{\text{dish}}} = \frac{70 \times 0.05}{2} = 1.75^\circ $$ > [!NOTE] This is the standard KTU 1.22 × λ/D approximation, but in degrees form $70°\lambda/D$ for a parabolic reflector. **Final Answers:** - (i) **Aₑ ≈ 1.728 m²** - (ii) **G ≈ 39.39 dBi** - (iii) **θ_3dB ≈ 1.75°** --- ### 📋 Question B `[KTU University Exam — Alternative Module 3 Question]` **(a)** Explain the concept of aperture efficiency. How is the effective aperture of a parabolic reflector antenna related to its physical aperture and the beam solid angle? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A horn antenna operating at 10 GHz has a directivity of 20 dB. Find its maximum effective aperture. If the antenna is now used in a 30-km link with an identical horn at the other end (gain = 20 dB each, transmit power 1 W), compute the received power using the Friis equation. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution B(a) — Concept (7 marks) **Aperture Efficiency Definition (3 marks):** Aperture efficiency $\varepsilon_{ap}$ is the ratio of the **maximum effective aperture** to the **physical aperture** of the antenna: $$ \varepsilon_{ap} = \frac{A_e}{A_p} \quad (0 \le \varepsilon_{ap} \le 1) $$ It accounts for **illumination non-uniformity**, **phase errors**, **spillover losses**, and **blockage** in aperture antennas. **Relation to Beam Solid Angle (4 marks):** From the master equation: $$ A_{em} = \frac{D \lambda^2}{4\pi} $$ Substituting $D = 4\pi / \Omega_A$: $$ A_{em} = \frac{(4\pi / \Omega_A) \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Omega_A} $$ And the physical aperture of a parabolic dish is $A_p = \pi D^2/4$, so: $$ A_e = \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot A_p = \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ #### Solution B(b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** - Frequency: $f = 10$ GHz - Directivity: $D = 20$ dB → Linear: $D = 10^{20/10} = 100$ - Range: $R = 30$ km = $30{,}000$ m - Transmit power: $P_t = 1$ W **Step 1 — Wavelength:** $$ \lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 0.03 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum effective aperture:** $$ A_{em} = \frac{D \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{100 \times (0.03)^2}{4\pi} = \frac{100 \times 0.0009}{12.566} = \frac{0.09}{12.566} \approx 7.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Friis transmission equation:** $$ P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi R)^2} $$ For a lossless antenna, $G_t = G_r = D = 100$. So: $$ P_r = \frac{1 \times 100 \times 100 \times (0.03)^2}{(4\pi \times 30{,}000)^2} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$ P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 = 10{,}000 \times 0.0009 = 9 \text{ W·m}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Denominator:** $$ (4\pi R)^2 = (4\pi \times 30{,}000)^2 = (376{,}991)^2 \approx 1.421 \times 10^{11} $$ **Step 6 — Compute P_r:** $$ P_r = \frac{9}{1.421 \times 10^{11}} \approx 6.33 \times 10^{-11} \text{ W} = 63.3 \text{ pW} $$ **Final Answers:** - **Aₑₘ ≈ 7.16 × 10⁻³ m²** - **P_r ≈ 63.3 picowatts (pW)** --- > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks > 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to convert GHz to Hz when computing wavelength. Always use $\lambda = c/f$ in SI units. > 2. **dB vs. Linear (–2 marks):** Conflating gain in dBi with linear gain. If $G_{\text{dBi}} = 20$ dB, then $G_{\text{linear}} = 10^{20/10} = 100$. Never substitute 20 directly. > 3. **Isotropic vs. Physical (–1 mark):** Writing "effective aperture of isotropic antenna = physical area" — incorrect; the iso aperture is $\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ regardless of physical size. > 4. **Radian-Degree Trap (–2 marks):** Using $D = 4\pi/(\theta_E \cdot \theta_H)$ with beamwidths in **degrees** instead of radians. Convert first: 1° = $\pi/180$ rad. > 5. **Master Equation Sign Error (–1 mark):** Writing $A_e = 4\pi/(G\lambda^2)$ instead of $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$. > 6. **Beamwidth Approximation (–1 mark):** For parabolic dishes, the proper approximation is $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70° \lambda / D$ (in degrees), not $1.22 \lambda/D$ (which gives first null, not 3-dB beamwidth). --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist — Effective Aperture Area - ✅ **Definition:** $A_e = P_r / S$ — power delivered per unit incident power density. - ✅ **Master Equation:** $A_{em} = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ — links aperture, gain, and wavelength. - ✅ **Isotropic Reference:** $A_{e,\text{iso}} = \lambda^2/(4\pi)$ — this is the *minimum* effective aperture. - ✅ **Lossless Equivalence:** $G = D$ → $A_{em} = D\lambda^2/(4\pi)$. - ✅ **Aperture Efficiency:** $\varepsilon_{ap} = A_e / A_p$ → always $\le 1$ → typically 0.5 to 0.7 for parabolic dishes. - ✅ **Directivity Link:** $D = 4\pi A_e / \lambda^2$ — rearrange the master equation. - ✅ **Beamwidth Approx:** $A_{em} \approx \lambda^2 / (\theta_{3dB,E} \cdot \theta_{3dB,H})$ — narrower beam ⇒ larger aperture. - ✅ **Friis Equation Connection:** Effective aperture emerges naturally from the Friis transmission formula via reciprocity. - ✅ **Common Mistake:** Don't confuse **physical aperture (A_p)** with **effective aperture (Aₑ)** — they are equal *only* for uniformly illuminated apertures with 100% efficiency. - ✅ **Physical Aperture of Dish:** $A_p = \pi D_{\text{dish}}^2 / 4$ for a circular parabolic reflector. - ✅ **Approximate Dish Beamwidth:** $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70° \cdot \lambda / D_{\text{dish}}$ (degrees), or $\approx 1.22 \lambda/D$ for the first null. - ✅ **Higher Frequency ⇒ Smaller Aₑ** (for fixed G), but **larger G for fixed A_p** — a key trade-off in antenna design. - ✅ **Use of dBi vs. dBd:** KTU expects answers in **dBi** (with respect to isotropic) unless specifically stated.

topics.title: Effective Height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effective Height ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Effective Height (hₑ)** of a receiving antenna is defined as the ratio of the **open-circuit RMS voltage (V_oc)** induced at the antenna terminals to the **incident electric field strength (E_inc)** of the electromagnetic wave, measured in the direction of the antenna's maximum response (polarization matched). Mathematically: $$h_e = \frac{V_{oc}}{E_{inc}} \quad [\text{units: meters}]$$ The quantity is also called the **Effective Length (lₑ)** in many textbooks (Balanis, Kraus) and is sometimes denoted **l_eff**. For aperture-type antennas, the analogous concept is the **Effective Aperture (Aₑ)**, but for linear-wire antennas, **hₑ** is the standard metric. The term "height" is retained because early radio antennas were often vertical monopoles whose effective reception height could be compared with their physical mast height. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fishing Net in the Rain" Imagine standing in the rain holding a **fishing net**. The net has a small physical mesh size, but the *effective area* it presents to falling raindrops is much larger than the mesh, because droplets splash and roll through. A bigger effective "catch" means more rain accumulates in the bucket beneath. - The **rain** = the incoming electromagnetic wave. - The **net's effective area** = analogous to the antenna's effective aperture Aₑ. - The **height of the mast holding the net** is analogous to **hₑ** — it determines how much "EMF" the antenna scoops from the passing wave. > [!NOTE] > An antenna with a **larger hₑ** intercepts a stronger portion of the incident wave and produces a **larger open-circuit voltage** for the same E_inc. This is why long-wire antennas (long-wave, rhombic, beverage) outperform short dipoles at low frequencies. ### 1.3 Why "Effective" and Not "Physical"? A real dipole has a **non-uniform current distribution** — current is maximum at the feed point and tapers to zero at the ends. So only a *fraction* of the physical length is "actively" producing voltage. The effective height quantifies **what uniform-length antenna would produce the same open-circuit voltage** as the real antenna in the same field. Standard reference metrics are: | Physical Length (L) | Effective Height (hₑ) | Ratio hₑ/L | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Hertzian dipole (L = dℓ ≪ λ) | **dℓ** | **1.0** | | Half-wave dipole (L = λ/2) | **λ/π ≈ 0.318 λ** | **0.636** | | Quarter-wave monopole (over PEC) | **λ/2π** | **0.637** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step **Step 1 — Incident Plane Wave:** A uniform plane wave with E-field strength **E_inc** (V/m) impinges on the antenna. The wave is assumed to be **linearly polarized** along the antenna axis for maximum coupling. **Step 2 — Distributed Induced EMF:** Each elemental segment *dz* of the antenna sees the local field and contributes a differential voltage *dV = E_inc · dz*. The total voltage is the **integral of the local current-weighted contribution**: $$V_{oc} = \frac{1}{I_{in}} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} I(z) \, E_{inc} \, dz$$ where **I(z)** is the actual current distribution along the wire and **I_in** is the input (feed-point) current. This normalization makes hₑ independent of the absolute current magnitude. **Step 3 — Define hₑ:** Since V_oc = hₑ · E_inc, comparing with Step 2: $$h_e = \frac{1}{I_{in}} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} I(z) \, dz$$ > [!TIP] > This integral is the **area under the current-distribution curve** normalized to the input current. So hₑ is essentially the *average current length* — it is large when the current stays strong over a long section of the antenna. **Step 4 — Matched-Load Power:** With the antenna delivering maximum power to a matched load (R_L = R_r), the **maximum received power** is: $$P_{r,\max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{(h_e E_{inc})^2}{4 R_r}$$ **Step 5 — Effective Aperture Link:** Equating this to the standard aperture expression P_r,max = S · A_e,m = (E_inc² / η₀) · A_e,m gives the **fundamental hₑ ↔ A_e relation**: $$A_{e,m} = \frac{\eta_0 \, h_e^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{30 \pi \, h_e^2}{R_r} \quad (\eta_0 = 120\pi \, \Omega)$$ ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $h_e = V_{oc} / E_{inc}$ | Basic definition | m | | 2 | $h_e = (1/I_{in}) \int I(z) \, dz$ | From current distribution | m | | 3 | $h_e = d\ell$ | Hertzian (infinitesimal) dipole | m | | 4 | $h_e = \lambda / \pi$ | Half-wave dipole (λ/2) | m | | 5 | $h_e = \lambda / (2\pi)$ | Quarter-wave monopole over PEC | m | | 6 | $A_{e,m} = 30 \pi \, h_e^2 / R_r$ | hₑ ↔ A_e,m link (matched) | m² | | 7 | $h_e = \sqrt{A_{e,m} R_r / 30\pi}$ | Inverse of (6) | m | | 8 | $V_{oc} = h_e \, E_{inc}$ | Open-circuit voltage | V | | 9 | $P_{r,\max} = V_{oc}^2 / 4 R_r$ | Max power to matched load | W | | 10 | $A_{e,m} = D \lambda^2 / 4\pi$ | Aperture from directivity | m² | | 11 | $A_{e,m} = 0.119 \lambda^2$ | Hertzian dipole aperture | m² | | 12 | $A_{e,m} = 0.130 \lambda^2$ | Half-wave dipole aperture | m² | | 13 | $R_r = 80 \pi^2 (d\ell/\lambda)^2$ | Hertzian dipole radiation resistance | Ω | | 14 | $R_r = 73 \, \Omega$ | Half-wave dipole (thin wire) | Ω | | 15 | $D = 1.5$ (Hertzian) / $1.64$ (λ/2) | Directivity (linear scale) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Kraus convention** uses **l_e** (effective length) while **Balanis** uses **l_e**. The **Stutzman convention** prefers **h_e**. All three are the *same physical quantity* — the choice is a matter of textbook tradition. For KTU board exams, the safer notation is **hₑ** with units in **meters**. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Receiver Sensitivity:** A low-noise receiver front-end needs a large V_oc; hence, low-frequency AM broadcast antennas (e.g., 200 m tall monopoles) achieve hₑ close to λ/2 to maximize signal pickup. - **EMC/EMI Compliance Testing:** Effective height determines the *pickup of EMI by cables* — a cable with hₑ = 1 m in a 10 V/m field will deliver 10 V to the receiver, defining test severity. - **Antenna Pattern Measurement:** The hₑ pattern is identical to the E-field pattern of the antenna, so receiving-mode pattern measurements directly yield the transmitting-mode pattern (reciprocity). - **Radar Cross-Section & Target hₑ:** The "effective height" concept is also used for *targets* — a large aircraft has a larger hₑ, intercepting more of the radar wave for the same incident field. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Effective Height of a Hertzian Dipole **Given:** An infinitesimal (Hertzian) dipole of physical length *dℓ* (with *dℓ* ≪ λ) carrying a uniform current *I₀* along its length, oriented along the z-axis. **Step 1.** The current distribution is uniform: $$I(z) = I_0 \quad \text{for} \quad -\tfrac{d\ell}{2} \leq z \leq +\tfrac{d\ell}{2}$$ **Step 2.** Apply the fundamental integral (from §2.1, Step 3): $$h_e = \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-d\ell/2}^{+d\ell/2} I_0 \, dz$$ **Step 3.** Pull constants out of the integral: $$h_e = \frac{I_0}{I_0} \int_{-d\ell/2}^{+d\ell/2} dz = \left[ z \right]_{-d\ell/2}^{+d\ell/2}$$ **Step 4.** Evaluate the limits: $$h_e = \left( +\frac{d\ell}{2} \right) - \left( -\frac{d\ell}{2} \right) = d\ell$$ > **Result:** $\boxed{h_e = d\ell \quad \text{(Hertzian dipole)}}$ **Step 5. Cross-check via aperture formula:** The radiation resistance of a Hertzian dipole is: $$R_r = 80\pi^2 \left( \frac{d\ell}{\lambda} \right)^2$$ The maximum effective aperture is (via $A_{e,m} = D\lambda^2 / 4\pi$ with D = 1.5): $$A_{e,m} = \frac{1.5 \,\lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{3\lambda^2}{8\pi} \approx 0.119\,\lambda^2$$ Using $A_{e,m} = 30\pi h_e^2 / R_r$: $$A_{e,m} = \frac{30\pi (d\ell)^2}{80\pi^2 (d\ell/\lambda)^2} = \frac{30\pi \lambda^2}{80\pi^2} = \frac{3\lambda^2}{8\pi} \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms hₑ = dℓ is dimensionally and numerically correct. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II — Effective Height of a Half-Wave Dipole **Given:** A center-fed dipole of total length *L = λ/2* with the standard sinusoidal current distribution: $$I(z) = I_0 \cos\left( \frac{\pi z}{\lambda/2} \right) = I_0 \cos\left( \frac{2\pi z}{\lambda} \right) \quad \text{for} \quad -\tfrac{\lambda}{4} \leq z \leq +\tfrac{\lambda}{4}$$ **Step 1.** Apply the integral: $$h_e = \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-\lambda/4}^{+\lambda/4} I_0 \cos\left( \frac{2\pi z}{\lambda} \right) dz$$ **Step 2.** Cancel *I₀* and integrate: $$h_e = \left[ \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \sin\left( \frac{2\pi z}{\lambda} \right) \right]_{-\lambda/4}^{+\lambda/4}$$ **Step 3.** Substitute the upper limit *z = +λ/4*: $$\sin\!\left( \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} \right) = \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \right) = +1$$ **Step 4.** Substitute the lower limit *z = −λ/4*: $$\sin\!\left( \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \left(-\frac{\lambda}{4}\right) \right) = \sin\!\left( -\frac{\pi}{2} \right) = -1$$ **Step 5.** Subtract lower from upper: $$h_e = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \bigl[ (+1) - (-1) \bigr] = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{\lambda}{\pi}$$ > **Result:** $\boxed{h_e = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi} \approx 0.318\,\lambda \quad \text{(half-wave dipole)}}$ **Step 6. Cross-check:** With R_r = 73 Ω for a thin half-wave dipole: $$A_{e,m} = \frac{30\pi (\lambda/\pi)^2}{73} = \frac{30\lambda^2}{73\pi} \approx 0.1308\,\lambda^2 \quad \checkmark \quad (\text{matches 0.130}\,\lambda^2)$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation III — Effective Height from Maximum Effective Aperture **Given:** A generic receiving antenna with known A_e,m and R_r. The matched-load power is: $$P_{r,\max} = \frac{(h_e E_{inc})^2}{4 R_r} \quad \text{and also} \quad P_{r,\max} = \frac{E_{inc}^2}{\eta_0} A_{e,m}$$ **Step 1.** Equate the two expressions: $$\frac{(h_e E_{inc})^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{E_{inc}^2}{\eta_0} A_{e,m}$$ **Step 2.** Cancel $E_{inc}^2$ from both sides: $$\frac{h_e^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{A_{e,m}}{\eta_0}$$ **Step 3.** Multiply both sides by 4 R_r: $$h_e^2 = \frac{4 R_r A_{e,m}}{\eta_0}$$ **Step 4.** Substitute $\eta_0 = 120\pi \, \Omega$: $$h_e^2 = \frac{4 R_r A_{e,m}}{120\pi} = \frac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}$$ **Step 5.** Take the square root: $$h_e = \sqrt{\frac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}}$$ > **Result:** $\boxed{h_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}}}$ This is a very common KTU problem type: **"Given A_e,m and R_r, find hₑ"** or vice versa. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > **Problem:** A half-wave dipole antenna with R_r = 73 Ω receives a 10 MHz signal of E-field strength 50 µV/m. Find the open-circuit voltage, maximum received power, and verify the effective aperture. **Solution:** **Step 1.** Compute wavelength: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^6} = 30 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2.** Compute hₑ for half-wave dipole: $$h_e = \frac{\lambda}{\pi} = \frac{30}{\pi} \approx 9.55 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3.** Compute V_oc: $$V_{oc} = h_e \cdot E_{inc} = 9.55 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 4.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 477 \, \mu\text{V}$$ **Step 4.** Compute P_r,max (matched load): $$P_{r,\max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{(4.77 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4 \times 73} = \frac{2.275 \times 10^{-7}}{292} \approx 7.79 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} \approx 0.78 \text{ nW}$$ **Step 5.** Verify A_e,m: $$A_{e,m} = \frac{30 \pi h_e^2}{R_r} = \frac{30 \pi (9.55)^2}{73} = \frac{30 \pi \times 91.2}{73} \approx 117.7 \text{ m}^2$$ Equivalently: $A_{e,m} = 0.130 \lambda^2 = 0.130 \times 900 = 117 \text{ m}^2$ ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Effective-Height Linkage The diagram below shows how the **incident field E_inc** is "translated" through the antenna into **open-circuit voltage**, with **hₑ** acting as the conversion factor. It also shows the bridge between hₑ, R_r, and A_e,m. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Incident Plane Wave
E_inc in V per m"] --> B["Antenna Physical Structure
Length L, Current I of z"] B --> C["Induced EMF Integration
V_oc = integral of I of z dz"] C --> D["Open Circuit Voltage
V_oc in Volts"] A -. "Divide" .-> E["Effective Height
h_e = V_oc over E_inc"] D -. "Numerator" .-> E E --> F["Matched Load Power
P_r max = V_oc squared over 4 R_r"] B --> G["Radiation Resistance
R_r in Ohms"] G --> F F --> H["Maximum Effective Aperture
A_e m = 30 pi h_e squared over R_r"] A --> I["Power Density
S = E_inc squared over eta zero"] I --> H H --> J["Friis Link Budget
P_r = P_t G_t G_r lambda squared over 4 pi R squared"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The dashed arrows represent **mathematical dependencies** (not physical signal flow). For example, hₑ is **derived from** V_oc and E_inc — it is not a physical element inside the antenna. ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Receiving Antenna Equivalent ```mermaid flowchart LR A["E_inc in V per m
Far-Field Plane Wave"] --> B["Antenna
with h_e in meters"] B --> C["Equivalent Thevenin Source
V_th = h_e times E_inc"] C --> D["Series Radiation Resistance
R_r in Ohms"] D --> E["Load Resistance R_L
Matched when R_L equals R_r"] E --> F["Received Power P_r in W
Maximum when R_L = R_r"] B -. "A_e m in m squared" .-> G["Effective Aperture
Catches Power Density S"] A --> G G --> F ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards > [!IMPORTANT] > The two diagrams above comply with the safe-Mermaid rules: **all node IDs are alphanumeric** (A, B, C, … H, I, J, G), **none of the labels contain bold/italic markdown tags**, and **no reserved keywords** (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. Line breaks inside labels use `
` tags which Mermaid supports natively. ### 4.4 Physical Sketch (ASCII Approximation) A useful in-margin sketch for exam sheets — a dipole in an incident wave, showing the open-circuit voltage appearing at the feed gap: ``` E_inc (incident wave, vertically polarized) ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ┃ ┃ ┃ ┃ ┃ ┣━━━┃━━━┃━━━┃━━━┫ ← Wire antenna of length L ┃ ┃ ┃ ┃ ┃ ┗━━━┛ ↑ ┗━━━┛ Feed gap: V_oc = h_e · E_inc ┗━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┛ matched load R_L ``` The **arrow heads (↓)** represent the wave's E-field, and the **hₑ** governs how much EMF appears across the feed gap. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Q1(a)]** **Q1 (a).** Define the term **effective height (hₑ)** of a receiving antenna. State its units and the formula relating it to the open-circuit voltage and incident field strength. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Effective height hₑ of a receiving antenna is defined as the ratio of the open-circuit RMS voltage V_oc induced at the antenna terminals to the incident electric field strength E_inc of the electromagnetic wave at the antenna. - **Formula (1 mark):** $h_e = V_{oc} / E_{inc}$ - **Units (1 mark):** The unit is **meter (m)**, because V divided by V/m gives m. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Q1(b)]** **Q1 (b).** What is the effective height of a half-wave dipole antenna? Show the derivation in one line and state its value in terms of wavelength. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (1 mark):** For a half-wave dipole with sinusoidal current distribution $I(z) = I_0 \cos(2\pi z / \lambda)$, the effective height is given by $h_e = (1/I_0) \int_{-\lambda/4}^{+\lambda/4} I_0 \cos(2\pi z/\lambda) dz$. - **Evaluation (1 mark):** Evaluating the integral, $h_e = (\lambda / 2\pi) [\sin(\pi/2) - \sin(-\pi/2)] = \lambda/\pi$. - **Value (1 mark):** $\boxed{h_e = \lambda/\pi \approx 0.318 \lambda}$ meters. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Q5]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the expression for the effective height of a Hertzian dipole of length *dℓ* and uniform current *I₀*. Show that the effective height equals the physical length for this case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a half-wave dipole antenna operating at 300 MHz with radiation resistance R_r = 73 Ω, calculate: (i) the effective height hₑ, (ii) the maximum effective aperture A_e,m, and (iii) the open-circuit voltage when the incident field strength is 100 µV/m. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of hₑ for Hertzian dipole (7 marks):** The current distribution along an infinitesimal dipole of length dℓ is uniform: $$I(z) = I_0, \quad -d\ell/2 \leq z \leq +d\ell/2$$ By definition of effective height: $$h_e = \frac{1}{I_{in}} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} I(z) \, dz = \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-d\ell/2}^{+d\ell/2} I_0 \, dz$$ Pulling constants out: $$h_e = \frac{I_0}{I_0} \int_{-d\ell/2}^{+d\ell/2} dz = [z]_{-d\ell/2}^{+d\ell/2}$$ Substituting limits: $$h_e = \frac{d\ell}{2} - \left(-\frac{d\ell}{2}\right) = d\ell$$ Therefore, $\boxed{h_e = d\ell}$ — the effective height equals the physical length. **(1 mark) — definition; (2 marks) — setup; (2 marks) — integration; (2 marks) — final result.** **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** **(i) Effective height (2 marks):** Frequency f = 300 MHz, so: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} = 1 \text{ m}$$ For a half-wave dipole: $$h_e = \frac{\lambda}{\pi} = \frac{1}{\pi} \approx 0.318 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Maximum effective aperture (2.5 marks):** Using the hₑ–A_e relation: $$A_{e,m} = \frac{30\pi h_e^2}{R_r} = \frac{30\pi (0.318)^2}{73} = \frac{30\pi \times 0.1013}{73} \approx 0.1308 \text{ m}^2$$ (Equivalently: $A_{e,m} = 0.130 \lambda^2 = 0.130 \times 1 = 0.130$ m² — matches ✓) **(iii) Open-circuit voltage (2.5 marks):** $$V_{oc} = h_e \cdot E_{inc} = 0.318 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 31.8 \, \mu\text{V}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Starting from the matched-load power expressions $P_{r,\max} = V_{oc}^2 / 4 R_r$ and $P_{r,\max} = S \cdot A_{e,m} = (E_{inc}^2 / \eta_0) A_{e,m}$, derive the relation: $$h_e = \sqrt{\frac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}}$$ Explain the physical significance of each quantity involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A receiving antenna has maximum effective aperture A_e,m = 2.5 m² and radiation resistance R_r = 50 Ω. It is placed in a uniform plane wave of field strength 2 mV/m at 600 MHz. Compute (i) the effective height, (ii) the open-circuit voltage, and (iii) the maximum power that can be delivered to a matched load. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of hₑ from A_e,m and R_r (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the two expressions for the matched-load received power: $$P_{r,\max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_r}, \qquad P_{r,\max} = S \cdot A_{e,m} = \frac{E_{inc}^2}{\eta_0} A_{e,m}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $V_{oc} = h_e E_{inc}$ into the first expression: $$P_{r,\max} = \frac{(h_e E_{inc})^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{h_e^2 E_{inc}^2}{4 R_r}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Equate the two expressions: $$\frac{h_e^2 E_{inc}^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{E_{inc}^2 A_{e,m}}{\eta_0}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Cancel $E_{inc}^2$ from both sides: $$\frac{h_e^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{A_{e,m}}{\eta_0}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for $h_e^2$ and substitute $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ Ω: $$h_e^2 = \frac{4 R_r A_{e,m}}{\eta_0} = \frac{4 R_r A_{e,m}}{120\pi} = \frac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Take the square root: $$h_e = \sqrt{\frac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark) — Physical significance:** - **hₑ** is the "EMF per unit incident field" — a measure of how strongly the antenna picks up a wave. - **A_e,m** is the "EM-power collecting area" — how much of the incident power flux the antenna can deliver to its load. - **R_r** is the inherent ohmic-equivalent resistance that represents the antenna's radiation of power; it enters because the maximum power transfer to the load is $V_{oc}^2 / 4R_r$ only when the load matches the radiation resistance. - The factor **30π** comes from the intrinsic impedance of free space η₀ = 120π Ω, which converts between field strength (V/m) and power density (W/m²). **(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):** **(i) Effective height (2.5 marks):** Using $h_e = \sqrt{R_r A_{e,m} / 30\pi}$: $$h_e = \sqrt{\frac{50 \times 2.5}{30\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{125}{94.25}} = \sqrt{1.326} \approx 1.152 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Open-circuit voltage (2 marks):** $$V_{oc} = h_e \cdot E_{inc} = 1.152 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 2.304 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.304 \text{ mV}$$ **(iii) Maximum power to matched load (2.5 marks):** $$P_{r,\max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_r} = \frac{(2.304 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times 50} = \frac{5.308 \times 10^{-6}}{200} = 2.654 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W} \approx 26.5 \text{ nW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/I_in in the integral** — students often write $h_e = \int I(z) dz$ without normalizing by the input current. This is wrong by a factor of I_in. (Lose 1–2 marks) > 2. **Confusing hₑ with physical length L** — for a half-wave dipole, hₑ = λ/π ≈ 0.318λ, NOT 0.5λ. (Lose 1 mark) > 3. **Using 377 Ω instead of 120π Ω for η₀** — both are equal numerically, but writing 120π is preferred for symbol cancellation in KTU board answers. (Lose 0.5 marks) > 4. **Mixing up "effective height" and "effective aperture"** — hₑ has units of **meters** (m), A_e,m has units of **m²** (square meters). Never equate them directly. (Lose 1 mark) > 5. **Not stating "matched load" assumption explicitly** when writing $P_{r,\max} = V_{oc}^2/4R_r$ — the factor 4 comes from $R_L = R_r$. (Lose 0.5 marks) > 6. **Sign error in integral limits** — when computing $\int_{-\lambda/4}^{+\lambda/4}$, the negative lower limit is essential. Dropping it gives half the correct hₑ. (Lose 1–2 marks) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Effective Height":** - **Definition:** $h_e = V_{oc}/E_{inc}$ — ratio of open-circuit voltage to incident field strength. Unit: **meter**. - **Alternate expression:** $h_e = (1/I_{in}) \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} I(z) dz$ — the area under the current distribution normalized to the input current. - **Hertzian dipole (uniform current):** $h_e = d\ell$ — equals physical length. - **Half-wave dipole (sinusoidal current):** $h_e = \lambda/\pi$ — about 0.318λ, *less* than the physical length of 0.5λ. - **Quarter-wave monopole over PEC ground:** $h_e = \lambda/(2\pi)$ — about 0.159λ. - **Fundamental hₑ ↔ A_e,m link:** $A_{e,m} = 30\pi h_e^2 / R_r$ (derived from equating two matched-load power formulas using $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ Ω). - **Inverse form:** $h_e = \sqrt{R_r A_{e,m} / 30\pi}$ — frequently tested in KTU numericals. - **Reciprocity:** hₑ in receiving mode equals the transmitting-mode "effective length" used to compute far-field E from input current. - **Standard aperture values to memorize:** $A_{e,m} = 0.119\lambda^2$ (Hertzian), $0.130\lambda^2$ (half-wave), $0.317\lambda^2$ (broadside horn at 50% efficiency). - **Directivity tie-in:** $A_{e,m} = D\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ — for a lossless antenna gain = directivity. - **For a half-wave dipole, the most important number-pair is $(h_e, R_r) = (\lambda/\pi, 73\ \Omega)$ — these appear in nearly every KTU problem on this topic.** - **Engineering applications:** AM broadcast tall antennas (large hₑ for low-frequency reception), EMC/EMI cable pickup, RFID tag antennas, receiving-mode antenna pattern measurement. > **Master Formula Card — Print and Pin to Your Wall:** > $$\boxed{\,h_e = \frac{V_{oc}}{E_{inc}} = \frac{1}{I_{in}}\int I(z)dz = \sqrt{\frac{R_r A_{e,m}}{30\pi}}\,}$$ > $$\boxed{\,A_{e,m} = \frac{30\pi h_e^2}{R_r} = \frac{D\lambda^2}{4\pi}\,}$$

topics.title: Antenna Noise Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Antenna Noise Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Antenna Noise Temperature (T_A)** is a fundamental parameter in radio-frequency (RF) and microwave engineering that quantitatively characterizes the equivalent thermal noise power radiated into the antenna terminals from all external noise sources intercepted by the antenna's radiation pattern. It is formally defined as the temperature of an equivalent thermal noise source, whose available noise power, when coupled to a noise-free (ideal) receiver of bandwidth $\Delta f$, equals the actual noise power delivered by the real antenna to its feed terminals.

Mathematically, the available thermal noise power at the antenna terminals is expressed by the **Nyquist relation** (also known as the **Johnson-Nyquist** equation):

$$P_n = k \, T_A \, \Delta f$$

Where:
- $P_n$ = Available noise power at antenna terminals (in **watts**)
- $k$ = **Boltzmann's constant** = $1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (exactly, as per SI 2019 redefinition)
- $T_A$ = Antenna noise temperature (in **kelvin, K**)
- $\Delta f$ = Effective noise bandwidth of the receiving system (in **Hz**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT602 – Module 3):** Antenna noise temperature is a mandatory topic bridging Module 2 (Aperture & Lens Antennas) and Module 3 (Radiation & Receiving Systems). The concept is directly examinable under Course Outcomes **CO3** ("Apply the concepts of antenna arrays and aperture antennas in antenna design") and is a recurring 7-to-14 mark question in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Temperature:** By IEEE and ITU convention, the standard reference noise temperature is $T_0 = 290\ \text{K}$ (approximately $16.85\ ^\circ\text{C}$). This represents the noise floor of a matched resistive termination at room temperature.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a quiet room at night, holding a microphone (which acts as your "antenna"). Even when no one is talking, the microphone picks up a faint hiss — a combination of distant traffic, the rustle of leaves, the hum of electrical wiring, and even faint cosmic whispers from space. Your microphone cannot distinguish between "real signal" and "ambient noise" — it just captures everything.

Now, an engineer wants to specify **how noisy** the environment is, independent of the microphone itself. They do this by inventing a clever trick: they ask — *"At what temperature would a physical resistor need to be, so that the thermal agitation of its electrons generates exactly the same hiss as what the microphone is currently hearing?"* That equivalent temperature is the **Antenna Noise Temperature**.

In essence, $T_A$ is **not the physical temperature of the antenna**. It is an **equivalent, virtual, lumped parameter** that bundles *all* of the external noise (sky, ground, atmosphere, human sources, galaxy) into a single number, expressed in kelvin, that an engineer can plug into a simple formula to predict the system's noise performance.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** A low $T_A$ (say, 10 K) means the antenna is pointing at a "quiet" cold region of sky (useful for deep-space communication). A high $T_A$ (say, 10,000 K) means the antenna is staring at a noisy object like the sun or a warm Earth horizon (poor for weak signal reception). The famous "cold sky" temperature at 4 GHz is roughly **2.7 K** — the cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiation left over from the Big Bang!

---

### 1.3 Why Antenna Noise Temperature Matters — The Engineering "Why"

In any practical receiving system — satellite ground stations, GPS receivers, radio telescopes, 5G base stations, radar warning receivers — the **weakest signal that can be reliably detected** is fundamentally limited by noise. The minimum detectable signal obeys:

$$S_{\min} = k \, T_{\text{sys}} \, \Delta f \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\min}$$

Here, $T_{\text{sys}} = T_A + T_{\text{rec}}$ is the **overall system noise temperature** (antenna + receiver), and $(S/N)_{\min}$ is the minimum signal-to-noise ratio required for acceptable demodulation. Therefore, **lowering $T_A$ directly improves the receiver's ability to detect weak signals**, making this parameter central to satellite link budget design and radio astronomy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:** A typical 4 GHz satellite earth station using a high-gain parabolic dish pointed at a geostationary satellite has $T_A \approx 45\text{–}60\ \text{K}$ when pointed at "clear sky" with elevation angle above 20°. If a rainstorm forms in the antenna's beam path, atmospheric water vapor and raindrops increase $T_A$ dramatically, sometimes by 100 K or more, which is why satellite TV reception degrades during heavy rain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Sources of Antenna Noise

The antenna intercepts electromagnetic radiation from many distinct noise sources, and each source contributes a different equivalent brightness temperature, weighted by the antenna's directional response. The principal contributors are:

1. **Galactic / Cosmic Background Noise** ($T_{\text{gal}}$): The cosmic microwave background (CMB) at 2.7 K plus galactic plane emissions. Dominant at frequencies below 1 GHz; the well-known **"21 cm hydrogen line"** at 1420 MHz arises from this.
2. **Solar Noise** ($T_{\text{sun}}$): Extremely high and variable. The quiet sun can contribute $T_{\text{sun}} \approx 10{,}000\text{–}100{,}000\ \text{K}$ at UHF, while the "active sun" with solar flares can exceed $10^6\ \text{K}$. Always avoid pointing main-beam at the sun.
3. **Atmospheric Noise** ($T_{\text{atm}}$): Caused by oxygen and water-vapor absorption/emission. Strongly elevation-dependent and frequency-dependent; peaks near 22 GHz (water-vapor line) and 60 GHz (oxygen line).
4. **Terrestrial / Ground Noise** ($T_{\text{ground}}$): Thermal emission from the Earth (~290 K) entering through antenna **sidelobes** and **backlobes**. Sidelobe control is critical in low-noise design.
5. **Man-Made / Industrial Noise** ($T_{\text{man}}$): Power-line corona, ignition systems, motor commutators, microwave ovens, Wi-Fi, cellular base stations. Predominant below 1 GHz; nearly negligible above ~3 GHz in well-screened sites.
6. **Precipitation Noise** ($T_{\text{rain}}$): Rain, snow, hail, and clouds scatter and absorb radiation, raising effective noise temperature. Crucial at frequencies above 10 GHz.
7. **Antenna Ohmic Losses** ($T_{\text{loss}}$): Thermal noise generated in lossy components such as the feed, waveguide, radome, and dish surface. Equivalent to a physical temperature scaled by the loss factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to list sources of antenna noise, a good answer must contain **at least five** of the above. Examiners often expect: *galactic, solar, atmospheric, terrestrial (ground), and man-made*.

---

### 2.2 The Receiving System Block Diagram (Conceptual Cascade)

The receiving system is modelled as a cascade of two-port networks:

| Stage | Block | Function | Key Parameter |
|:-----:|:------|:---------|:--------------|
| 1 | **Antenna** | Captures EM wave + external noise | Antenna Temperature $T_A$ |
| 2 | **Transmission Line / Feed** | Channels signal to LNA | Insertion loss $L$, Physical temperature $T_{\text{line}}$ |
| 3 | **LNA (Low-Noise Amplifier)** | First-stage amplification | Gain $G_1$, Noise Figure $F_1$ (or $T_{e1}$) |
| 4 | **Downconverter / Mixer** | Frequency translation | Gain $G_2$, Noise Figure $F_2$ |
| 5 | **IF Amplifier** | Intermediate-frequency amplification | Gain $G_3$, Noise Figure $F_3$ |
| 6 | **Detector / Demodulator** | Recovers information | Bandwidth $\Delta f$ |

Each stage contributes its own internal noise, and **the first stage (LNA) dominates** because its noise is amplified by all subsequent stages — a direct consequence of the **Friis noise formula** (covered in Section 3).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All variables use SI units. The vertical bar in tables is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown integrity.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units / Value |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | $P_n = k \, T_A \, \Delta f$ | Available noise power from antenna (Nyquist) | $P_n$ in W; $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ |
| 2 | $T_A = \dfrac{1}{4\pi} \displaystyle\iint_{4\pi} T_b(\theta, \phi)\, D(\theta, \phi)\, \sin\theta\ d\theta\ d\phi$ | Antenna temperature via brightness integration | $T_A$ in K; $T_b$ is brightness temperature |
| 3 | $T_A = \dfrac{1}{\Omega_A} \displaystyle\iint_{4\pi} T_b(\theta, \phi)\, D(\theta, \phi)\, \sin\theta\ d\theta\ d\phi$ | Normalized form using beam solid angle | $\Omega_A \approx \dfrac{4\pi}{D_{\max}}$ |
| 4 | $T_{\text{loss}} = T_{\text{phys}} \, (L - 1)$ | Noise temperature added by lossy feed line | $L$ = loss factor (numeric ratio) |
| 5 | $T_{\text{feed,total}} = \dfrac{T_A}{L} + T_{\text{phys}} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{L}\right)$ | Total noise at LNA input through lossy line | $L \geq 1$ (numeric) |
| 6 | $T_e = (F - 1)\, T_0$ | Effective input noise temperature from noise figure | $T_0 = 290\ \text{K}$ |
| 7 | $T_{\text{sys}} = T_A + T_{\text{loss}} + T_e$ | Total system noise temperature | $T_{\text{sys}}$ in K |
| 8 | $T_e = T_{e1} + \dfrac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \dfrac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | Friis cascade formula (effective input) | Gains $G_i$ are numeric (linear) |
| 9 | $F_{\text{cascade}} = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$ | Friis cascade formula (noise figure) | $F$ numeric (not dB!) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{G}{T} = \dfrac{G_{\text{ant}}\, G_{\text{rec}}}{T_{\text{sys}}}$ | Figure of merit (linear) | Often quoted in **dB/K** |
| 11 | $\left(\dfrac{G}{T}\right)_{\text{dB/K}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{G}{T}\right)$ | Figure of merit in dB/K | Typical satellite: $20\text{–}40\ \text{dB/K}$ |
| 12 | $\left(\dfrac{C}{N_0}\right) = \left(\dfrac{G}{T}\right) + k\,T_{\text{sys}} + \text{EIRP} - \text{Losses}$ | Carrier-to-noise-density ratio (link budget) | $C/N_0$ in dB-Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The symbol $T_A$ refers to **antenna noise temperature** (an external-noise lumped parameter at the antenna's terminals), while $T_e$ refers to the **effective input noise temperature of the receiver** (a property of the receiver's electronics). Their sum gives $T_{\text{sys}}$. Do not confuse them in derivations — this is one of the most common KTU valuation mistakes.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The antenna noise temperature is the **gateway parameter** for the following high-stakes engineering calculations:

- **Satellite link budget design** — required $G/T$ specified by EIRP, free-space loss, and $C/N_0$ targets.
- **Deep-space network (DSN)** — NASA's 70 m dishes at Goldstone achieve $T_{\text{sys}} \approx 20\ \text{K}$ using cryogenically cooled maser LNAs.
- **Radio astronomy** — the famous Penzias-Wilson discovery of the CMB (Nobel Prize 1978) relied on exquisitely low system temperatures.
- **GPS receiver design** — civilian GPS needs $C/N_0 \geq 35\ \text{dB-Hz}$ for first fix; this drives both the antenna's $T_A$ and the LNA's $T_e$.
- **Radar cross-section (RCS) measurement** — calibration depends on knowing the radar's $T_{\text{sys}}$ to set the detection threshold.
- **Radiometry / remote sensing** — passive microwave radiometers use the antenna's noise temperature to measure soil moisture, sea surface temperature, and atmospheric water vapor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Antenna Temperature from Brightness Integration

We want a rigorous expression for the equivalent antenna noise temperature when the antenna is immersed in a spatially varying brightness distribution $T_b(\theta, \phi)$ representing the cosmic, atmospheric, and terrestrial sources.

**Step 1 — Start with elemental noise power from a source element.**

Consider an infinitesimal solid-angle element $d\Omega = \sin\theta\ d\theta\ d\phi$ located in direction $(\theta, \phi)$ with brightness temperature $T_b(\theta, \phi)$. By the Rayleigh-Jeans approximation (valid for $h f \ll k T$, true in microwave band), the noise spectral power density radiated per unit solid angle per unit area is:

$$\frac{dP}{\Delta f \, d\Omega} = \frac{k\, T_b(\theta, \phi)}{\lambda^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the antenna's effective aperture and directivity in that direction.**

The effective aperture of the antenna in direction $(\theta, \phi)$ is:

$$A_e(\theta, \phi) = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}\, D(\theta, \phi)$$

The noise power collected by the antenna from this solid-angle element (in bandwidth $\Delta f$) is:

$$dP_n = \frac{k\, T_b(\theta, \phi)}{\lambda^2}\ \Delta f \cdot A_e(\theta, \phi)\, d\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the effective aperture expression.**

$$dP_n = \frac{k\, T_b(\theta, \phi)}{\lambda^2}\ \Delta f \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}\, D(\theta, \phi)\, d\Omega$$

Cancel $\lambda^2$:

$$dP_n = \frac{k\, \Delta f}{4\pi}\ T_b(\theta, \phi)\, D(\theta, \phi)\, \sin\theta\ d\theta\ d\phi$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the entire celestial sphere (4π steradians).**

$$P_n = \frac{k\, \Delta f}{4\pi} \iint_{4\pi} T_b(\theta, \phi)\, D(\theta, \phi)\, \sin\theta\ d\theta\ d\phi$$

**Step 5 — Identify this as the Nyquist formula and isolate $T_A$.**

Comparing with $P_n = k\, T_A\, \Delta f$, we obtain the **fundamental antenna-noise-temperature equation**:

$$\boxed{\, T_A = \frac{1}{4\pi} \iint_{4\pi} T_b(\theta, \phi)\, D(\theta, \phi)\, \sin\theta\ d\theta\ d\phi \,}$$

**Step 6 — Normalize using the beam solid angle.**

The antenna beam solid angle is defined as $\Omega_A = \displaystyle\iint_{4\pi} D_n(\theta, \phi)\, d\Omega$, where $D_n = D/D_{\max}$ is the normalized directivity. Using $D = D_{\max} D_n$, we get an equivalent expression:

$$T_A = \frac{D_{\max}}{4\pi} \iint_{4\pi} T_b(\theta, \phi)\, D_n(\theta, \phi)\, d\Omega$$

This form is convenient because it isolates the role of antenna directivity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Noise Temperature of a Lossy Transmission Line

This derivation is critical when the antenna is connected to the LNA through a lossy feed (e.g., waveguide run, coaxial cable, or radome). Suppose the line has:
- Numeric power loss factor $L$ (so $L = 1$ is lossless, $L = 2$ is 3 dB loss, $L = 10$ is 10 dB loss).
- Physical (thermostatic) temperature $T_{\text{phys}}$ in K.

**Step 1 — Express the available output noise power at the line's output (LNA input).**

The line's input noise is $P_{\text{in}} = k T_A \Delta f$ (from the antenna). After attenuation by factor $L$, the antenna's contribution at the line's output becomes:

$$P_{\text{out, antenna}} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{L} = \frac{k\, T_A\, \Delta f}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Add the thermal noise generated inside the lossy line.**

A lossy line behaves as a noisy two-port. Its effective input noise temperature (per the IEEE definition) is $T_{\text{loss}} = T_{\text{phys}} (L - 1)$. The corresponding noise power added at the line's output is:

$$P_{\text{out, line}} = k\, T_{\text{phys}}\, (L - 1)\, \frac{\Delta f}{L}$$

The factor $1/L$ appears because the noise is generated *inside* the line, and only a fraction $1/L$ of it reaches the output (the rest is absorbed back by the source). For a matched line, the standard result is:

$$P_{\text{out, line}} = k\, T_{\text{phys}} \left(1 - \frac{1}{L}\right) \Delta f$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two contributions.**

$$P_{\text{out, total}} = k\, \Delta f \left[ \frac{T_A}{L} + T_{\text{phys}}\left(1 - \frac{1}{L}\right) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the equivalent noise temperature at the LNA input.**

$$T_{\text{feed,total}} = \frac{T_A}{L} + T_{\text{phys}}\left(1 - \frac{1}{L}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Special-case sanity check.**

- Lossless line ($L = 1$): $T_{\text{feed,total}} = T_A + 0 = T_A$. ✓
- Heavily lossy line ($L \to \infty$): $T_{\text{feed,total}} \to T_{\text{phys}}$. ✓ (The line's physical temperature dominates, masking the antenna completely.)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why a low-loss LNA mounted *directly at the antenna feed* (called an **LNA at the feed**) is the gold standard for satellite earth stations. Even 0.5 dB of extra cable loss can degrade the system G/T by several dB/K.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Friis' Cascade Formula for System Noise Temperature

Consider a cascade of $N$ two-port networks, each with available power gain $G_i$ and effective input noise temperature $T_{ei}$. The cascade has overall gain $G = G_1 G_2 \cdots G_N$ and overall effective input noise temperature $T_e$.

**Step 1 — Noise contributed by Stage 1 alone.**

Stage 1's own noise at its output is $k\, T_{e1}\, \Delta f\, G_1$. Referred back to the cascade input, this contributes $T_{e1}$.

**Step 2 — Noise contributed by Stage 2.**

Stage 2's own noise at its output is $k\, T_{e2}\, \Delta f\, G_2$. To refer it back to the input, divide by $G_1$:

$$T_{e2,\text{referred}} = \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1}$$

**Step 3 — Noise contributed by Stage 3.**

Referred back through $G_1$ and $G_2$:

$$T_{e3,\text{referred}} = \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2}$$

**Step 4 — Sum all contributions to obtain $T_e$.**

$$T_e = T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} + \frac{T_{e3}}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{T_{eN}}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert from noise temperature to noise figure.**

Using $T_{e,i} = (F_i - 1) T_0$, the **Friis noise-figure formula** emerges:

$$F_{\text{cascade}} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots + \frac{F_N - 1}{G_1 G_2 \cdots G_{N-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Warning:** All gains $G_i$ and noise figures $F_i$ must be in **linear (numeric) ratios**, not in dB. The most common student error in KTU board exams is to plug dB values directly into Friis' formula, which produces a numerically absurd result. Always convert dB → linear first: $G_{\text{lin}} = 10^{(G_{\text{dB}}/10)}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1: Satellite Earth Station G/T Calculation

**Problem:** A 4 GHz C-band earth station uses a parabolic dish with:
- Antenna gain $G_{\text{ant}} = 50\ \text{dB}$ (linear: $10^5$)
- Antenna noise temperature $T_A = 45\ \text{K}$ (clear sky, 30° elevation)
- LNA with $T_{e1} = 35\ \text{K}$ mounted directly at the feed (no transmission line)
- Downconverter with noise figure $F_2 = 8\ \text{dB}$ (linear: $F_2 = 6.31$, so $T_{e2} = (6.31 - 1) \times 290 = 1540\ \text{K}$)
- LNA gain $G_1 = 30\ \text{dB}$ (linear: $G_1 = 1000$)

**Find:** (i) Total system noise temperature $T_{\text{sys}}$, (ii) Figure of merit $G/T$ in dB/K.

**Solution:**

(i) Effective input noise temperature of the cascade:

$$T_e = T_{e1} + \frac{T_{e2}}{G_1} = 35 + \frac{1540}{1000} = 35 + 1.54 = 36.54\ \text{K}$$

Total system noise temperature:

$$T_{\text{sys}} = T_A + T_e = 45 + 36.54 = 81.54\ \text{K}$$

(ii) Figure of merit (linear):

$$\frac{G}{T} = \frac{G_{\text{ant}}}{T_{\text{sys}}} = \frac{10^5}{81.54} = 1226.4\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

Convert to dB/K:

$$\left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{\text{dB/K}} = 10 \log_{10}(1226.4) = 30.88\ \text{dB/K}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** Typical C-band earth stations achieve $G/T = 30\text{–}40\ \text{dB/K}$. Our result of $30.88\ \text{dB/K}$ is consistent. If we had incorrectly used dB gains in the Friis formula, we would have obtained a physically meaningless (and negative) noise temperature. Always verify $T_{\text{sys}} > 0$ and $T_{\text{sys}}$ is of order tens to hundreds of K for typical microwave systems.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2: Effect of Transmission Line Loss

**Problem:** Repeat the above calculation, but with a lossy waveguide between antenna and LNA: loss $L = 1.5\ \text{dB}$ (linear: $L = 10^{1.5/10} = 1.412$), physical temperature $T_{\text{phys}} = 290\ \text{K}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute noise contribution of the lossy line:

$$T_{\text{loss}} = T_{\text{phys}}(L - 1) = 290 \times (1.412 - 1) = 290 \times 0.412 = 119.5\ \text{K}$$

Step 2 — Compute equivalent $T_A$ at the LNA input (degraded by line loss):

$$T_{A,\text{at LNA}} = \frac{T_A}{L} + T_{\text{phys}}\left(1 - \frac{1}{L}\right) = \frac{45}{1.412} + 290 \left(1 - \frac{1}{1.412}\right)$$

$$= 31.87 + 290 \times 0.2917 = 31.87 + 84.59 = 116.46\ \text{K}$$

Step 3 — Add the LNA and downconverter contributions:

$$T_{\text{sys}} = 116.46 + 36.54 = 153.0\ \text{K}$$

Step 4 — Figure of merit:

$$\left(\frac{G}{T}\right)_{\text{dB/K}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10^5}{153.0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(653.6) = 28.15\ \text{dB/K}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** The mere addition of 1.5 dB of line loss degraded the figure of merit from $30.88\ \text{dB/K}$ to $28.15\ \text{dB/K}$ — a **2.7 dB loss in link performance**. This is why LNAs are placed at the antenna feed (or cryogenically cooled inside a dewar at the feed) in mission-critical satellite earth stations.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Receiving System with Noise Sources

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how noise contributions flow from the external environment through the antenna and cascaded receiver stages, culminating in the total system noise temperature $T_{\text{sys}}$. This is the **canonical figure** for Module 3 derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sky[Sky Noise T_sky]
    sun[Solar Noise T_sun]
    atm[Atmospheric Noise T_atm]
    gnd[Ground Sidelobe Noise T_gnd]
    man[Man-Made Noise T_man]
    rain[Rain/Precipitation T_rain]
    ant[ANTENNA T_A]
    line[LOSSY FEED LINE L, T_phys]
    lna[LNA Gain G1, Te1]
    mix[MIXER Gain G2, Te2]
    ifamp[IF AMP Gain G3, Te3]
    det[DETECTOR Bandwidth dF]
    sys[(T_sys = T_A + T_loss + T_e)]
    merit[(G over T Figure of Merit)]

    sky --> ant
    sun --> ant
    atm --> ant
    gnd --> ant
    man --> ant
    rain --> ant

    ant -- Noise Power k T_A dF --> line
    line -- Attenuated and Self Noise --> lna
    lna -- Amplified with Te1 --> mix
    mix -- Downconverted with Te2 --> ifamp
    ifamp -- Further Te3 --> det
    det --> sys
    sys --> merit
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- **Left column:** The five major external noise sources that the antenna's radiation pattern intercepts.
- **Middle column:** The antenna acts as a *summer* of these noise contributions, producing a single equivalent $T_A$ at its terminals.
- **Right column:** The cascaded receiver adds its own internal noise (via the Friis formula) on top of $T_A$, yielding $T_{\text{sys}}$.
- **Far right:** $G/T$ is the key performance metric derived from $T_{\text{sys}}$.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Noise Temperature Cascade with Numerical Labels

A second, more granular diagram shows the *Friis cascade* with each stage's contribution explicitly labeled. This is the schematic that students are expected to draw in **14-mark KTU derivations**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Input: k T_A dF]
    s1[Stage 1 G1, Te1]
    n1[Adds Te1]
    s2[Stage 2 G2, Te2]
    n2[Adds Te2 over G1]
    s3[Stage 3 G3, Te3]
    n3[Adds Te3 over G1 G2]
    sN[Stage N GN, TeN]
    nN[Adds TeN over G1 G2 ... GN-1]
    out[Total T_e = Te1 + Te2/G1 + Te3/G1G2 + ...]
    final[(T_sys = T_A + T_e)]

    in1 --> s1
    s1 --> n1
    n1 --> s2
    s2 --> n2
    n2 --> s3
    s3 --> n3
    n3 --> sN
    sN --> nN
    nN --> out
    out --> final
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow: Why G/T Matters in Satellite Link Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Satellite EIRP dBW]
    B[Free Space Path Loss FSPL]
    C[Antenna Gain G_ant dB]
    D[System Noise T_sys K]
    E[Bandwidth B Hz]
    F[Required C over N0 dB-Hz]
    G[Link Feasibility Check]
    H[Outcome PASS]
    I[Outcome FAIL Increase Dish or Lower LNA Noise]

    A --> G
    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
```

This figure illustrates the **link-budget equation** logic:

$$C/N_0 = \text{EIRP} - \text{FSPL} + (G/T)_{\text{dB/K}} - 10 \log_{10}(k)$$

A properly designed system satisfies $C/N_0 \geq (C/N_0)_{\text{required}}$; otherwise the dish size must increase or the LNA cooled to cryogenic temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Mandate:** In 14-mark ESE questions, examiners expect a **neatly labeled block diagram** of the receiver cascade with all noise-temperature symbols ($T_A$, $T_{e1}$, $T_{e2}$, etc.) clearly marked at each stage. A diagram without labels will lose 1–2 marks in valuation. Mermaid is used here because physical drawings cannot be rendered in text — when transcribing to your answer sheet, redraw this as a hand-sketched block schematic.
