```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Known Electric Source Problem] --> B[Identify Source J, Geometry, Medium eps, mu]
    B --> C[Extract Known Field Solution E_orig, H_orig]
    C --> D{Apply Duality Transformations}
    D --> E1[E becomes H]
    D --> E2[H becomes minus E]
    D --> E3[J becomes M]
    D --> E4[eps becomes mu]
    D --> E5[mu becomes eps]
    D --> E6[eta becomes 1 by eta]
    E1 --> F[Assemble Dual Field Solution E_mag, H_mag]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    F --> G[Verify Maxwell Equations with M source]
    G --> H[Interpret Physical Dual Geometry]
    H --> I[Slot Antenna from Dipole]
    H --> J[Magnetic Dipole from Electric Dipole]
    I --> K[Final Dual Solution Ready]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping — Electric–Magnetic Dual Pairs

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Electric Dipole] -.Duality.-> B[Magnetic Dipole]
    C[Wire Antenna] -.Duality.-> D[Slot Antenna]
    E[Current J] -.Duality.-> F[Magnetic Current M]
    G[Epsilon] -.Duality.-> H[Mu]
    I[Eta] -.Duality.-> J[1 by Eta]
    K[E field] -.Duality.-> L[H field]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows in the diagram above represent the **dual mapping**. Each pair (left ↔ right) is connected via the Duality Theorem. The two columns are mathematically equivalent but physically distinct realizations of the same radiation pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the Duality Theorem of electromagnetics. List any four duality transformations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Duality Theorem states that the radiation pattern of a magnetic current element is identical in form to that of an electric current element of the same dimensions, with **E ↔ H** and source/material parameters interchanged. **(1 mark)**

The four duality transformations are: **(2 marks — ½ mark each)**
1. **E → H** and **H → −E**
2. **J → M** and **M → −J**
3. **ε → μ** and **μ → ε**
4. **η → 1/η**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**A slot antenna is the dual of a dipole antenna. Justify this statement using the Duality Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A thin slot cut in an infinite ground plane can be modeled as a magnetic current source **M** flowing along the slot, which is the **dual** of an electric current **J** flowing along a thin wire dipole. **(1 mark)** Applying the Duality Theorem (**E ↔ H, J ↔ M, ε ↔ μ, η ↔ 1/η**), the slot antenna's H-field pattern equals the dipole's E-field pattern. **(1 mark)** Hence, the radiation pattern shape, directivity, and beamwidth of a slot are identical to a dipole of the same physical length — confirming slot is the dual of dipole. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and explain the **Duality Theorem** in electromagnetic theory. Enumerate the complete duality transformation table (all substitutions). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric Hertzian dipole of length **dl = λ/50** carries current **I₀ = 2 A** at **f = 1 GHz** in free space. Using duality, determine the **E-field magnitude** of the dual magnetic current element at a far-field point **r = 100 m**, **θ = 60°**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Statement (2 marks):**
The Duality Theorem states that if a valid solution to Maxwell's equations exists for an electric current source **J**, then a dual valid solution exists for a magnetic current source **M** by interchanging the field vectors and source parameters as follows:

$$\bar{E} \leftrightarrow \bar{H}, \quad \bar{J} \leftrightarrow \bar{M}, \quad \varepsilon \leftrightarrow \mu, \quad \eta \leftrightarrow 1/\eta$$

**Explanation (2 marks):** The theorem exploits the symmetry of Maxwell's curl equations. When electric current **J** is replaced by magnetic current **M**, the roles of Faraday's law and Ampère's law are interchanged, leading to a mathematically equivalent (but physically distinct) field solution.

**Complete Duality Table (3 marks — ½ mark per row, 6 rows):**

| Electric-source Quantity | Magnetic-source Dual |
|---|---|
| E | H |
| H | −E |
| J | M |
| ε | μ |
| μ | ε |
| η | 1/η |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute wavelength and wavenumber.

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^9} = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi}{0.3} = 20.94 \text{ rad/m}$$

$$dl = \frac{\lambda}{50} = \frac{0.3}{50} = 0.006 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The known E-field of the electric dipole is:

$$E_{\theta}^{\text{elec}} = j\,\eta_0\,\frac{k\,I_0\,dl}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply duality: For the magnetic source, the E-field is obtained by replacing η₀ → −1/η₀ and I₀ → M₀, and the field becomes the dual field.

By duality transformation:

$$E_{\phi}^{\text{mag}} = -j\,\eta_0\,\frac{k\,M_0\,dl}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting M₀ = I₀ = 2 A, θ = 60° (sin 60° = √3/2), r = 100 m, η₀ = 120π ≈ 377 Ω:

$$\left\vert E_{\phi}^{\text{mag}}\right\vert = \eta_0 \cdot \frac{k\,I_0\,dl}{4\pi r} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute numerical values.

$$= (120\pi) \cdot \frac{(20.94)(2)(0.006)}{4\pi(100)} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$= (120\pi) \cdot \frac{0.2513}{1256.6} \cdot 0.866$$

$$= (120\pi)(2.0 \times 10^{-4})(0.866)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final numerical evaluation.

$$= 377 \times 1.732 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0653 \text{ V/m}$$

$$\boxed{\left\vert E_{\phi}^{\text{mag}}\right\vert \approx 65.3 \text{ mV/m}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Magnitude only:

$$\left\vert E_{\phi}^{\text{mag}}\right\vert = 0.0653 \text{ V/m} = 65.3 \text{ mV/m}$$

---

### **Question B — Option B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **far-field Eθ expression** of an electric Hertzian dipole from Maxwell's equations, and using duality, write the dual magnetic current element's H-field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **radiation pattern, directivity, and input impedance** of an electric dipole and its magnetic dual (slot antenna of equal length). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Electric vector potential for an infinitesimal electric dipole along z-axis:

$$\bar{A} = \hat{z}\,\frac{\mu\,I_0\,dl}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Magnetic field from **H = (1/μ)∇ × A**:

In spherical coordinates, after algebraic manipulation:

$$\bar{H} = \hat{\phi}\,j\,\frac{k\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** E-field from **E = (1/jωε)∇ × H** (or using **E = η H** in far field):

$$E_{\theta} = \eta\,H_{\phi} = j\,\eta\,\frac{k\,I_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\,e^{-jkr}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Apply duality — replace **I₀ → M₀, η → 1/η, E → H**:

$$\boxed{H_{\theta}^{\text{mag}} = j\,\frac{k\,M_0\,dl\,\sin\theta}{4\pi\,\eta\,r}\,e^{-jkr}}$$

The dual H-field has the **same sin θ angular dependence** as the original E-field, with the **impedance factor 1/η** replacing η.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

| Parameter | Electric Dipole | Magnetic Dual (Slot) | Comparison |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radiation Pattern U(θ)** (2 marks) | sin²θ (donut shape) | sin²θ (donut shape) | **Identical** |
| **Directivity D** (2 marks) | **1.5 (1.76 dBi)** | **1.5 (1.76 dBi)** | **Identical** |
| **HPBW** | 78° (≈ 90° for small) | 78° | **Identical** |
| **Input Impedance** (1.5 marks) | ~73 Ω (resonant) | ~485 Ω (resonant, depends on slot width) | **Complementary** via η ↔ 1/η |
| **Bandwidth** | Narrow | Wider in some configurations | **Different** in practice |

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):** The radiation pattern and directivity are **preserved** under duality, while the input impedance is transformed via **η ↔ 1/η**, giving the slot a much higher input impedance than the dipole.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Many students write **E → H** without the minus sign on **H → −E**. This breaks Maxwell's equation consistency and **loses 1 mark** in Part A.
> 2. **Mixing up E ↔ H with H ↔ E:** In some textbooks, the direction is reversed. Stick to the **KTU 2024 convention**: E → H, H → −E, J → M, M → −J.
> 3. **Forgetting the impedance swap (η → 1/η):** This is a *must*. Without it, dimensional consistency fails. **Loses 1–2 marks** in derivations.
> 4. **Assuming radiation pattern changes:** A common misconception. The radiation intensity **U(θ, φ) is invariant** under duality. Directivity is preserved. **−1 mark** if contradicted.
> 5. **Confusing Duality with Reciprocity:** Reciprocity relates **transmit ↔ receive** modes; Duality relates **electric ↔ magnetic sources**. They are *not* the same theorem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Duality Theorem** maps electric-source solutions to magnetic-source solutions via field/source/material swaps.
- ✅ **Core substitutions:** **E ↔ H**, **H ↔ −E**, **J ↔ M**, **ε ↔ μ**, **η ↔ 1/η**, **k unchanged**.
- ✅ **Radiation pattern (U) and directivity (D) are INVARIANT** under duality.
- ✅ **Wavenumber k** is unchanged; only the impedance η changes.
- ✅ **Engineering application:** Slot antenna = dual of dipole; uses equivalent magnetic current **M** = 2**E** × n̂ on the aperture.
- ✅ **No proof required** in KTU 2024 — focus on **transformation table, applications, and numerical problem-solving**.
- ✅ **Input impedance** of a slot antenna is **complementary** to that of a dipole (typically very high, ~500 Ω).
- ✅ **Sign convention:** KTU uses **H → −E** (not +E) to maintain Faraday's law consistency.
- ✅ **Difference from Reciprocity:** Duality = source-type swap; Reciprocity = transmit–receive role swap.
- ✅ **Most-likely exam questions:** (i) State duality & list transformations, (ii) Apply duality to a given Hertzian dipole to find magnetic-dual fields, (iii) Compare dipole vs. slot antenna parameters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field, Directivity and Radiation Resistance of a Short Dipole and Half Wave Dipole (Far Field Derivation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Field, Directivity and Radiation Resistance of a Short Dipole and Half Wave Dipole (Far Field Derivation) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Hertzian / Short Dipole Antenna > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A **short dipole** (also called the *Hertzian dipole* or *elementary dipole*) is a thin, straight, center-fed wire antenna whose total physical length **L** is very small compared to the operating wavelength (typically $L \le \lambda/10$ or $L \le \lambda/50$). The current is assumed to be **uniform** in magnitude along the entire length, with a **triangular distribution** at the feed gap. The **Hertzian dipole** is the most fundamental building block of all antenna theory. Every practical antenna's behaviour can be understood by treating it as a continuous assembly of these tiny elementary dipoles (Huygens' principle applied to current distributions). **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of a short dipole like a **golf ball hit by a thin pole** that vibrates back and forth. The pole is so short that the entire "wave-pushing" happens almost simultaneously along its length. The EM wave it launches is weak (low radiation resistance), and the radiation pattern is a smooth **donut shape** with no nulls in the horizontal plane. Now imagine stretching that pole until it is exactly half a wavelength long — suddenly the current varies significantly along its length (peaks at the center, zeros at the ends), and the donut shape becomes a slightly fatter, more directional **peanut shape**. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** The far-field region begins at $r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ (Fraunhofer distance), where **D** is the largest dimension of the antenna. For a short dipole, $D = L$, so the far field starts at $r \ge 2L^2/\lambda$. ### 1.2 The Half-Wave Dipole Antenna > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A **half-wave dipole** is a center-fed wire antenna whose total length is exactly $L = \lambda/2$. Because its length matches the half-wavelength of the operating frequency, the current distribution is **sinusoidal** (standing wave) with a maximum at the feed point and **zero** at the two open ends. **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** A half-wave dipole is the **"Goldilocks" antenna** of wireless communications — not too short, not too long, just right. Its balanced radiation (2.15 dBi directivity), practical size, and ~73 Ω input impedance make it the most widely used antenna in real-world systems (FM radio, TV, Wi-Fi, cellular base stations). Imagine a vibrating guitar string: the ends are pinned (current = 0), the center bulges maximally (current = max), and the energy radiates outward as a doughnut-shaped beam. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ → Phase constant (rad/m) — always in **bold** for emphasis > - $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ → **Intrinsic impedance of free space** > - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m → Permeability of free space > - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s → Speed of light in vacuum > - $f$ in Hz, $\lambda$ in meters, antenna length $L$ in meters ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — The Magnetic Vector Potential Both antennas are analyzed using the **magnetic vector potential** $\vec{A}$ defined such that $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ and $\vec{E} = -j\omega\vec{A}$ in the far field (Coulomb gauge, time-harmonic excitation $e^{j\omega t}$). For a thin z-directed linear antenna carrying current $I(z)$: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \frac{I(z)\, e^{-j\beta R}}{R}\, dz$$ where the distance from a source point at coordinate $z$ to an observation point $(r, \theta, \phi)$ in spherical coordinates is: $$R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2 - 2rz\cos\theta}$$ **Far-Field Approximations** (valid when $r \gg L$ and $r \gg 2L^2/\lambda$): - In the **phase** term: $R \approx r - z\cos\theta$ (binomial expansion, 1st order) - In the **amplitude** term: $R \approx r$ (the $z$-term is negligible) - Result: The integral simplifies because the $1/r$ factor is constant and pulled out. ### 2.2 Current Distribution Logic **Why the current distribution differs for the two antennas:** | Antenna Type | Length | Distribution | Mathematical Form | |---|---|---|---| | Hertzian / Short Dipole | $L \ll \lambda$ | Triangular (linear taper from feed to ends) | $I(z) = I_0\left(1 - \dfrac{2\vert z\vert}{L}\right)$ | | Half-Wave Dipole | $L = \lambda/2$ | Sinusoidal standing wave | $I(z) = I_0 \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi z}{\lambda}\right)$ | The triangular distribution exists because at the open ends of a short antenna, the current must vanish — but because the antenna is so short, the current cannot swing through much of a sine cycle, so it appears roughly linear. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these equations appear in 80% of KTU dipole problems.** | # | Parameter | Short Dipole ($L \ll \lambda$) | Half-Wave Dipole ($L = \lambda/2$) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Vector potential $A_z$ | $\dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}$ | $\dfrac{\mu_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r}\cdot\dfrac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin^2\theta}$ | | 2 | $E_\theta$ (V/m) | $j\dfrac{\eta_0 \beta I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\sin\theta$ | $j\dfrac{\eta_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r}\cdot\dfrac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin\theta}$ | | 3 | $H_\phi$ (A/m) | $j\dfrac{\beta I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\sin\theta$ | $j\dfrac{I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r}\cdot\dfrac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin\theta}$ | | 4 | $E_r, E_\phi$ | 0 (far field) | 0 (far field) | | 5 | **Directivity $D_0$** | **1.5 (linear) = 1.76 dBi** | **1.64 (linear) ≈ 2.15 dBi** | | 6 | **Maximum Directivity** | $D_{\max} = 1.5$ at $\theta = 90°$ | $D_{\max} = 1.64$ at $\theta = 90°$ | | 7 | **Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$** | $4\pi/1.5 = 8.378$ sr | $4\pi/1.64 = 7.663$ sr | | 8 | **Radiation Resistance $R_r$** | $80\pi^2 \left(\dfrac{L}{\lambda}\right)^2\ \Omega$ | $\approx 73\ \Omega$ (exact $= 73.08\ \Omega$) | | 9 | **HPBW** | $90°$ | $78°$ | | 10 | **Average Radiated Power** | $I_0^2 \cdot 40\pi^2 (L/\lambda)^2$ | $I_0^2 \cdot 36.54$ | | 11 | **Polarization** | Linear (along $z$) | Linear (along $z$) | | 12 | **Pattern Type** | Omnidirectional in H-plane | Omnidirectional in H-plane, slightly sharper in E-plane | > [!NOTE] > In all the formulas above, $\eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$ is the free-space intrinsic impedance and $I_0$ is the **feed-point current** (input current at the center terminals). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Short dipoles** are used as reference elements in phased arrays, near-field probes, and EMC compliance testing (compact, broadband-friendly when resistively loaded). - **Half-wave dipoles** are the backbone of commercial broadcasting, amateur radio (the classic "doublet"), radar warning receivers, RFID tags, and reference antennas in anechoic chambers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE FAR-FIELD DERIVATION — SHORT (HERTZIAN) DIPOLE **Step 1: Set up the geometry.** A short dipole of length $L$ is centered at the origin, oriented along the z-axis, carrying a triangular current distribution. The observation point is in the far field at spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$. **Step 2: Apply the far-field approximation to the distance $R$.** $$R = \sqrt{r^2 + z^2 - 2rz\cos\theta}$$ Using the binomial expansion valid for $z \ll r$: $$R = r\sqrt{1 - \frac{2z\cos\theta}{r} + \frac{z^2}{r^2}} \approx r\left(1 - \frac{z\cos\theta}{r}\right) = r - z\cos\theta$$ **Step 3: Substitute into the vector potential integral.** $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \frac{I_0\left(1 - \frac{2\vert z\vert}{L}\right) e^{-j\beta(r - z\cos\theta)}}{r - z\cos\theta}\, dz$$ **Step 4: Apply the far-field amplitude approximation.** Since $r \gg L$, the denominator $R \approx r$ (constant), and the phase term $e^{-j\beta(r - z\cos\theta)}$ has $r$ as a common factor (pulled out as a global phase). The integral becomes: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} I_0\left(1 - \frac{2\vert z\vert}{L}\right) e^{j\beta z\cos\theta}\, dz$$ **Step 5: Evaluate the integral using the short-dipole approximation $\beta z \ll 1$.** For a short dipole, $z \le L/2 \ll \lambda$, so $\beta z \le \pi L/\lambda \ll 1$. Therefore, the exponential $e^{j\beta z\cos\theta} \approx 1$ (zero-order Taylor expansion). The integral collapses to: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \cdot I_0 \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2}\left(1 - \frac{2\vert z\vert}{L}\right) dz$$ **Step 6: Compute the integral explicitly.** By symmetry, evaluate from $0$ to $L/2$ and double the result: $$\int_{-L/2}^{+L/2}\left(1 - \frac{2\vert z\vert}{L}\right) dz = 2\int_{0}^{L/2}\left(1 - \frac{2z}{L}\right) dz = 2\left[z - \frac{z^2}{L}\right]_0^{L/2} = 2\left[\frac{L}{2} - \frac{L}{4}\right] = \frac{L}{2}$$ **Step 7: Write the simplified vector potential.** $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{8\pi r}$$ > [!NOTE] > Some textbooks (Balanis) write this as $\mu_0 I_0 L e^{-j\beta r}/(4\pi r)$ by defining $I_0$ as the *peak current at the center*. KTU follows the Balanis convention. The factor is purely notational — verify with your course notes. **Step 8: Convert to the electric field.** In the far field, $E_\theta = -j\omega A_\theta = -j\omega A_z \sin\theta$ (after spherical transformation of the z-component): $$E_\theta = -j\omega \cdot \frac{\mu_0 I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \cdot \sin\theta$$ Using $\omega\mu_0 = \omega \mu_0 = \beta \eta_0$ (since $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ and $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}$): $$E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0\, \beta\, I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta$$ **Step 9: Find $H_\phi$ using the free-space plane-wave relationship.** $$H_\phi = \frac{E_\theta}{\eta_0} = j\,\frac{\beta\, I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta$$ ✅ **Derivation complete for the short dipole.** --- ### 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE FAR-FIELD DERIVATION — HALF-WAVE DIPOLE **Step 1: State the current distribution.** For a half-wave dipole ($L = \lambda/2$), the standing-wave current is: $$I(z) = I_0 \cos\left(\frac{\pi z}{\lambda}\right) = I_0 \cos\left(\frac{\pi z}{L}\right),\quad -L/2 \le z \le L/2$$ **Step 2: Write the vector potential.** $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \frac{I_0 \cos(\pi z/L)\, e^{-j\beta(r - z\cos\theta)}}{r}\, dz$$ Pulling out the constants: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi z}{L}\right) e^{j\beta z\cos\theta}\, dz$$ **Step 3: Use the exponential form of cosine.** Recall $\cos(\pi z/L) = \dfrac{e^{j\pi z/L} + e^{-j\pi z/L}}{2}$: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{8\pi r} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \left[e^{j\pi z/L} + e^{-j\pi z/L}\right] e^{j\beta z\cos\theta}\, dz$$ **Step 4: Combine the exponents.** $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{8\pi r} \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} \left[e^{jz(\beta\cos\theta + \pi/L)} + e^{jz(\beta\cos\theta - \pi/L)}\right] dz$$ **Step 5: Perform the integration.** Using $\int e^{j\alpha z} dz = \dfrac{e^{j\alpha z}}{j\alpha}$ and evaluating at the limits $\pm L/2$: $$\int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} e^{j\alpha z}\, dz = \frac{2\sin(\alpha L/2)}{\alpha}$$ Let $\alpha_1 = \beta\cos\theta + \pi/L$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta\cos\theta - \pi/L$. Then: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \left[\frac{\sin(\alpha_1 L/2)}{\alpha_1} + \frac{\sin(\alpha_2 L/2)}{\alpha_2}\right]$$ **Step 6: Substitute $L = \lambda/2$, so $\beta L/2 = \pi/2$ and $\pi L/(2L) = \pi/2$.** $$\alpha_1 L/2 = \frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta + \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2}(1 + \cos\theta)$$ $$\alpha_2 L/2 = \frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta - \frac{\pi}{2} = -\frac{\pi}{2}(1 - \cos\theta)$$ Note that $\sin\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2}(1 - \cos\theta)\right] = \sin\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2}(1 + \cos\theta)\right]$ (by symmetry of $\sin$). Using the half-angle identity $\cos^2(\theta/2) = (1 + \cos\theta)/2$: $$\sin\left[\frac{\pi}{2}(1 + \cos\theta)\right] = \sin\left[\pi\cos^2(\theta/2)\right]$$ **Step 7: Simplify using $\sin(2x) = 2\sin x \cos x$ and $\cos\theta = 1 - 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$.** After considerable algebra (a standard result, given in Balanis Ch. 4): $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin^2\theta}$$ **Step 8: Convert to the electric field using $E_\theta = -j\omega A_z \sin\theta$.** $$E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin\theta}$$ **Step 9: Magnetic field via plane-wave relation.** $$H_\phi = \frac{E_\theta}{\eta_0} = j\,\frac{I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin\theta}$$ ✅ **Derivation complete for the half-wave dipole.** --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Directivity — Half-Wave Dipole **Step 1: Compute the radiation intensity $U(\theta) = \dfrac{r^2}{2\eta_0}\vert E_\theta\vert^2$.** $$U(\theta) = \frac{r^2}{2\eta_0} \cdot \frac{\eta_0^2 I_0^2}{4\pi^2 r^2} \cdot \frac{\cos^2\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin^2\theta} = \frac{\eta_0 I_0^2}{8\pi^2} \cdot \frac{\cos^2\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin^2\theta}$$ **Step 2: Compute the maximum radiation intensity (at $\theta = 90°$):** At $\theta = 90°$, $\cos(0) = 1$ and $\sin(90°) = 1$, so: $$U_{\max} = \frac{\eta_0 I_0^2}{8\pi^2} = \frac{120\pi\, I_0^2}{8\pi^2} = \frac{15 I_0^2}{\pi}$$ **Step 3: Compute the total radiated power $P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} U(\theta) \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$.** $$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 I_0^2}{8\pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \int_0^{\pi} \frac{\cos^2\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin\theta}\, d\theta$$ This integral evaluates numerically to $\approx 1.2188$ (a standard tabulated value). Therefore: $$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 I_0^2}{4\pi} \cdot 1.2188 = 30 \cdot I_0^2 \cdot 1.2188 = 36.564\, I_0^2$$ **Step 4: Compute the beam solid angle.** $$\Omega_A = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi \cdot 15 I_0^2 / \pi}{36.564\, I_0^2} = \frac{60}{36.564} \approx 1.641$$ **Step 5: Directivity.** $$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{1.641} \approx 1.64 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_0\,(\text{dBi}) = 10\log_{10}(1.64) \approx 2.15\ \text{dBi}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Radiation Resistance **For the Short Dipole:** Starting from the average power: $$P_{rad} = \oint \frac{\vert E_\theta\vert^2}{2\eta_0} r^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$$ $$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2\eta_0} \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \left(\frac{\eta_0 \beta I_0 L}{4\pi r}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta \cdot r^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$$ $$= \frac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 L^2}{32\pi^2} \int_0^{2\pi} d\phi \int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta\, d\theta$$ Using $\int_0^{\pi} \sin^3\theta\, d\theta = 4/3$ and $\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi$: $$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 L^2}{32\pi^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta_0 \beta^2 I_0^2 L^2}{12\pi}$$ Substitute $\eta_0 = 120\pi$ and $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$: $$P_{rad} = \frac{120\pi \cdot (2\pi/\lambda)^2 \cdot I_0^2 L^2}{12\pi} = 40\pi^2\, I_0^2 \left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^2$$ By definition $P_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_r$, so: $$R_r = \frac{2 P_{rad}}{I_0^2} = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 789.6 \left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^2\ \Omega$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Numerical Tip:** For $L = \lambda/50$, $R_r \approx 0.316\ \Omega$ — this is why short dipoles are so inefficient and need impedance-matching networks. **For the Half-Wave Dipole:** Using the result $P_{rad} \approx 36.56\, I_0^2$: $$R_r = \frac{2 P_{rad}}{I_0^2} = 73.08\ \Omega \approx 73\ \Omega$$ > [!NOTE] > The value **73 Ω** is iconic in antenna engineering — it is the standard reference impedance to which RF test equipment (network analyzers) is often calibrated. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Radiation Pattern Comparison (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hertzian Short Dipole
L less than lambda/10] --> B[Current Distribution
Triangular Linear Taper] C[Half Wave Dipole
L equals lambda/2] --> D[Current Distribution
Sinusoidal Cosine Wave] B --> E[Vector Potential Az] D --> E E --> F[Far Field Approximation
R approx r minus z cos theta] F --> G[Electric Field E_theta] F --> H[Magnetic Field H_phi] G --> I[Radiation Intensity U theta] I --> J[Total Radiated Power P_rad] I --> K[Maximum Radiation U_max] J --> L[Directivity D0] K --> L J --> M[Radiation Resistance R_r] K --> M L --> N[Short Dipole
1.5 equals 1.76 dBi] L --> O[Half Wave Dipole
1.64 equals 2.15 dBi] M --> P[Short Dipole
80 pi squared L over lambda squared] M --> Q[Half Wave Dipole
73.08 ohms] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#80C8FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px style N fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style O fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#0066CC style P fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style Q fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#0066CC ``` ### 4.2 Geometry and Coordinate System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Origin 0 0 0
Feed Point] --> B[Short Dipole
Vertical Wire] A --> C[Half Wave Dipole
Vertical Wire] B --> D[Observation Point P
Spherical r theta phi] C --> D D --> E[Far Field Region
r greater than 2D squared over lambda] E --> F[Field Components
E_theta and H_phi only] style A fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#FFB6C1 style E fill:#DDA0DD style F fill:#F0E68C ``` ### 4.3 Power-Flow Architecture for Radiation Resistance Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Current I0] --> B[Current Distribution I of z] B --> C[Vector Potential Az] C --> D[Far Field E theta H phi] D --> E[Poynting Vector
S equals half Re of E cross H star] E --> F[Radiation Intensity U theta
equals r squared over 2 eta 0 times E theta squared] F --> G[Integrate U sin theta d theta d phi
over full sphere] G --> H[Total Radiated Power P_rad] H --> I[R_r equals 2 P_rad divided by I0 squared] I --> J[Match against KTU Result
73 ohms or 80 pi squared L over lambda squared] style A fill:#FFE4B5 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke-width:3px style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Radiation Pattern Shapes (Donut vs Peanut) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Short Dipole
Pattern] --> B[Smooth Donut
Omnidirectional in H plane] C[Half Wave Dipole
Pattern] --> D[Slightly Compressed Donut
Peanut shape in E plane] B --> E[HPBW 90 degrees] D --> F[HPBW 78 degrees] style B fill:#FFE4B5 style D fill:#B0E0FF style E fill:#FFA07A style F fill:#87CEFA ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define radiation resistance. State its value for a half-wave dipole.** **Model Answer:** Radiation resistance $R_r$ is the fictitious resistance that would dissipate the same amount of power as the antenna radiates into free space when the feed current $I_0$ flows through it. Mathematically, $P_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_r$. For a **half-wave dipole**: $R_r \approx 73\ \Omega$ (exact value: 73.08 Ω). The radiation resistance is evaluated at the **current maximum** (i.e., at the feed point for a half-wave dipole). It is a key figure of merit — a higher $R_r$ indicates more efficient radiation for a given feed current. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the 73 Ω value.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Why is the directivity of a half-wave dipole (1.64) greater than that of a short dipole (1.5)?** **Model Answer:** The directivity is determined by the **shape of the radiation pattern**. Both dipoles produce an omnidirectional doughnut-shaped pattern with a maximum at $\theta = 90°$ (broadside). However: - The **short dipole** has a smooth, broad $\sin^2\theta$ pattern with no zeros except at $\theta = 0°$ and $180°$. - The **half-wave dipole** has a slightly **narrower** pattern in the E-plane because the pattern function $\dfrac{\cos^2[(\pi/2)\cos\theta]}{\sin^2\theta}$ has a sharper fall-off near $\theta = 0°$ and $180°$ (numerator vanishes faster). A narrower beam concentrates the radiated power into a smaller solid angle $\Omega_A$, which by definition $D_0 = 4\pi/\Omega_A$ gives a **higher** directivity. The HPBW decreases from $90°$ (short dipole) to $78°$ (half-wave dipole), confirming the higher directivity. *(3 marks: 1 mark for pattern comparison, 1 mark for solid angle argument, 1 mark for numerical conclusion.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Expect sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks. Show full derivations — partial steps still earn partial credit. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Derivation-Focused* **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]** **(a) Starting from the magnetic vector potential, derive the far-field expressions for $E_\theta$ and $H_\phi$ of a short (Hertzian) dipole. Clearly state all the far-field approximations. (7 marks)** **(b) A short dipole of length $L = 0.05\lambda$ carries a uniform current of 2 A (peak). Calculate: (i) the maximum radiated power, (ii) the radiation resistance, and (iii) the maximum directivity in dBi. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1** above. The examiner expects the following sequence of steps for full marks: 1. State the vector potential integral (1 mark) 2. Apply the far-field distance approximation $R \approx r - z\cos\theta$ (1 mark) 3. Substitute the triangular current distribution and apply the short-dipole phase approximation $e^{j\beta z\cos\theta} \approx 1$ (1 mark) 4. Evaluate the integral to obtain $A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}$ (1 mark) 5. Convert to $E_\theta$ using $E_\theta = -j\omega A_z \sin\theta$ and $\omega\mu_0 = \beta\eta_0$ (1.5 marks) 6. State $H_\phi = E_\theta / \eta_0$ with a clear free-space plane-wave argument (1 mark) 7. List all **far-field conditions** explicitly: $r \gg L$, $r \gg 2L^2/\lambda$, and state that only $E_\theta$ and $H_\phi$ survive (0.5 marks) **Final equations to present:** $$E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta_0 \beta I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta,\qquad H_\phi = j\,\frac{\beta I_0 L\, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta$$ **Model Solution (b):** Given: $L = 0.05\lambda = \lambda/20$, $I_0 = 2$ A (peak). **(i) Maximum radiated power:** $$P_{rad} = 40\pi^2\, I_0^2 \left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 40\pi^2 \cdot (2)^2 \cdot (0.05)^2$$ $$P_{rad} = 40 \times 9.8696 \times 4 \times 0.0025 = 3.948\ \text{W} \approx 3.95\ \text{W}$$ **(ii) Radiation resistance:** $$R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{L}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 80 \times 9.8696 \times (0.05)^2$$ $$R_r = 789.57 \times 0.0025 = 1.974\ \Omega \approx 1.97\ \Omega$$ **(iii) Maximum directivity in dBi:** For a short dipole, $D_{\max} = 1.5$ (this is independent of length). $$D_{\max}\,(\text{dBi}) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76\ \text{dBi}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:** > - **Forgetting to square $I_0$:** Power goes as $I_0^2$ — using just $I_0$ loses 1–2 marks. > - **Mixing up peak vs RMS current:** If the question gives RMS, divide the answer by 2. The dipole formulas above assume **peak** $I_0$. > - **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $R_r = 2P_{rad}/I_0^2$:** Many students write $P_{rad}/I_0^2$ — this is wrong. The 2 comes from time-averaging the $e^{j\omega t}$ sinusoidal phasor. > - **Directivity is constant for short dipole:** Do NOT compute a "gain" here — only directivity is asked. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Mixed Conceptual + Numerical* **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **(a) With necessary diagrams, derive the far-zone electric field of a half-wave dipole. (7 marks)** **(b) For a half-wave dipole, compute the total radiated power and radiation resistance when fed by a peak current of 1.5 A. Also, determine the maximum directivity in linear and dB units. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** Refer to **Section 3.2** above. Full marks require: 1. State the sinusoidal current distribution $I(z) = I_0 \cos(\pi z/L)$ (1 mark) 2. Set up the vector potential integral with the far-field approximations (1.5 marks) 3. Use exponential form of cosine, combine exponents, and evaluate the integral (2.5 marks) 4. Simplify using $\sin$ identities and the half-angle formula $\cos^2(\theta/2) = (1+\cos\theta)/2$ to obtain the closed form (1 mark) 5. Convert to $E_\theta$ and $H_\phi$ (1 mark) **Final equations:** $$E_\theta = j\,\frac{60 I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{r} \cdot \frac{\cos\left[(\pi/2)\cos\theta\right]}{\sin\theta},\quad H_\phi = \frac{E_\theta}{120\pi}$$ (where the $2\pi$ denominator has been absorbed using $120\pi$). **Model Solution (b):** Given: $I_0 = 1.5$ A (peak). **(i) Total radiated power:** $$P_{rad} = 36.56\, I_0^2 = 36.56 \times (1.5)^2 = 36.56 \times 2.25 = 82.26\ \text{W}$$ **(ii) Radiation resistance:** $$R_r = \frac{2P_{rad}}{I_0^2} = 73.08\ \Omega \approx 73\ \Omega$$ (Verification: $\dfrac{2 \times 82.26}{2.25} = 73.12\ \Omega$ ✓ — small numerical error due to rounding 36.56) **(iii) Maximum directivity:** $$D_{\max} = 1.64\ \text{(linear)},\qquad D_{\max}\,(\text{dBi}) = 10\log_{10}(1.64) = 2.15\ \text{dBi}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Half-Wave Dipole:** > - **Directivity is 1.64, NOT 1.5.** Many students write 1.5 for every dipole. The 1.5 figure is **only** for the short dipole. > - **The 73 Ω value is for a free-space half-wave dipole.** Real dipoles near ground or with finite conductor thickness have slightly different values. > - **Use peak current for $P_{rad}$** unless the problem explicitly states RMS. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam ⚡** - ✅ **Short Dipole ($L \ll \lambda$):** Triangular current distribution, $E_\theta \propto \sin\theta$, $D_0 = 1.5 = 1.76$ dBi, $R_r = 80\pi^2(L/\lambda)^2$. - ✅ **Half-Wave Dipole ($L = \lambda/2$):** Sinusoidal current, $E_\theta \propto \dfrac{\cos[(\pi/2)\cos\theta]}{\sin\theta}$, $D_0 = 1.64 = 2.15$ dBi, $R_r = 73\ \Omega$. - ✅ **Far-field conditions** (write these in every answer): $r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$, $r \gg D$, and only $E_\theta$, $H_\phi$ components survive. - ✅ **Plane-wave relation** in the far field: $H_\phi = E_\theta / \eta_0$ with $\eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$. - ✅ **Directivity formula** (always remember the $4\pi$): $D_0 = \dfrac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{rad}}$. - ✅ **Radiation resistance definition** (board-favorite): $P_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_r$ — the **factor of 1/2** is non-negotiable. - ✅ **Beam solid angle**: $\Omega_A = 4\pi/D_0$. For short dipole, $\Omega_A = 8.38$ sr; for half-wave, $\Omega_A = 7.66$ sr. - ✅ **Polarization** of both dipoles: **Linear**, oriented along the dipole axis (z-axis). At broadside ($\theta = 90°$), the E-field is in the $\hat{\theta}$ direction. - ✅ **HPBW:** Short dipole = 90°, Half-wave dipole = 78°. - ✅ **Universal trick:** Whenever you see a final answer involving "73 Ω", double-check the problem is about a **half-wave dipole** — mixing it with the short-dipole formula is a classic KTU board-exam blunder. - ✅ **Units reminder:** All powers in watts, currents in amps (peak), lengths in meters, $\lambda$ in meters. If $L$ is in cm, convert first. - ✅ **Free-space boundary:** Both dipoles are assumed in **vacuum/air** with $\eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$. For other media, replace $\eta_0$ with the medium's intrinsic impedance. - ✅ **Pattern nulls:** Both dipoles have **perfect nulls** along the dipole axis ($\theta = 0°$ and $180°$). Maximum radiation is **broadside** ($\theta = 90°$). - ✅ **Why the half-wave has higher directivity:** Its pattern function is **narrower** in the E-plane (numerator $\cos[(\pi/2)\cos\theta]$ vanishes faster near the axis), compressing the beam and raising $D_0$ from 1.5 to 1.64.

topics.title: Field of Two Isotropic Point Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Field of Two Isotropic Point Sources** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **isotropic point source** is a hypothetical, lossless antenna that radiates electromagnetic energy **equally in all directions** with a **spherical wavefront**. It serves as the fundamental building block (reference radiator) for analyzing more complex antenna arrays in **Antenna Array Theory**. The **Field of Two Isotropic Point Sources** refers to the resultant electromagnetic field distribution in space produced by **two identical isotropic radiators** separated by a uniform distance $d$, each excited by an RF current of the same frequency. The two sources may be fed with **equal amplitudes and various phase relationships** (in-phase, out-of-phase, or with a progressive phase shift $\alpha$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The field of two isotropic point sources is a foundational topic that introduces the student to the principles of **array theory**, **pattern multiplication**, and the generation of **directional radiation patterns** from non-directional elementary radiators. The governing principle is: *A directional antenna array can be synthesized from multiple isotropic (or simple) radiators by properly controlling their relative amplitudes and phases.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in the middle of a perfectly still pond. If you drop **one pebble**, ripples spread out in **perfect circles** (this is the isotropic source — uniform in all directions). Now drop **two pebbles** simultaneously, spaced a small distance apart. The ripples from each pebble collide and **interfere** with each other: - In some directions, the wave crests **add up** (constructive interference → loud regions, called **lobes**). - In other directions, a crest from one source meets a trough from the other, and they **cancel out** (destructive interference → quiet regions, called **nulls**). By carefully choosing **how far apart** the pebbles are ($d$), and **whether you drop them together or with a small time delay** (phase difference $\alpha$), you can make the ripples **strongest in a chosen direction** — for example, directly away from the line joining the pebbles (**broadside**) or along the line itself (**end-fire**). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The radiation pattern of an antenna is essentially a *spatial interference pattern*. Two isotropic sources form the simplest "musical duet" of antenna theory — the listener (far-field observer) hears a complex interference pattern whose shape depends entirely on the *spacing* $d$ and the *relative phase* $\alpha$ between the two players. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Speed of light** in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - **Wavelength**: $\lambda = c / f$ (in meters, when $f$ is in Hz) - **Wave number**: $\beta = 2\pi / \lambda$ (radians per meter) - **Intrinsic impedance of free space**: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega$ - **Directivity of a single isotropic source**: $D_0 = 1$ (0 dB, by definition) - **Directivity of two-source broadside array (d = λ/2, in-phase)**: $D = 1.5$ (1.76 dB) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Setup (Geometric Configuration) Consider two isotropic point sources, **Source 1** and **Source 2**, placed along the **y-axis** in free space, separated by a uniform distance $d$. The origin is taken at the midpoint between the two sources. - Source 1 is located at $(0, +d/2, 0)$ - Source 2 is located at $(0, -d/2, 0)$ - Both sources are excited by identical amplitude $I_0$ but with a possible **progressive phase difference** $\alpha$ (Source 2 lags Source 1 by $\alpha$). - A far-field observation point $P$ lies at distance $r$ from the origin, making an angle $\phi$ with the positive x-axis (in the H-plane / xy-plane). > [!IMPORTANT] > The two-source array is a **linear array** with $N = 2$ elements. The analysis assumes a **one-dimensional far-field** in the H-plane, which is sufficient for KTU board-level derivations. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Far-Field Derivation **Step 1 — Write the field from each source separately.** Each isotropic source radiates a spherical wave. At the far-field observation point $P$: $$ E_1 = \frac{E_0}{r_1} e^{-j\beta r_1} $$ $$ E_2 = \frac{E_0}{r_2} e^{-j\beta r_2 + j\alpha} $$ where: - $E_0$ is the reference field amplitude (proportional to the feed current $I_0$) - $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the distances from Source 1 and Source 2 to point $P$ - $\alpha$ is the **progressive phase shift** (phase of Source 2 relative to Source 1) - The factor $e^{+j\alpha}$ accounts for Source 2 leading or lagging Source 1 **Step 2 — Apply the far-field approximation.** For observation points at a large distance ($r \gg d$), the path difference simplifies dramatically. The distance from each source to the far-field point can be approximated as: $$ r_1 \approx r - \frac{d}{2} \cos\phi $$ $$ r_2 \approx r + \frac{d}{2} \cos\phi $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **standard Fresnel / Fraunhofer approximation**: the small phase term $(d^2 / 8r)$ in the amplitude is neglected (since $r \gg d$), but the linear phase term $(d \cos\phi / 2)$ is retained because, when multiplied by $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$, it produces a non-negligible phase shift. **Step 3 — Apply the amplitude approximation.** Since both $r_1$ and $r_2$ are very close to $r$ in magnitude, the amplitude factors can be replaced by a common denominator: $$ \frac{1}{r_1} \approx \frac{1}{r_2} \approx \frac{1}{r} $$ This step is crucial because it allows us to factor out the common amplitude and isolate the angular (directional) information in a clean form. **Step 4 — Total field by superposition (vector sum).** By the principle of superposition, the total far-field is the sum of the individual fields. The common factor $\frac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r}$ can be factored out, yielding: $$ E = \frac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r} \left[ 1 + e^{j\psi} \right] $$ where the **total phase difference** $\psi$ between the two waves arriving at $P$ is: $$ \boxed{\psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This single equation $\psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha$ is the **master equation** for two-element array analysis. Every specific case (broadside, end-fire, etc.) emerges from choosing appropriate values of $d$ and $\alpha$. **Step 5 — Simplify using the complex exponential identity.** Using the identity $1 + e^{j\psi} = 2 \cos(\psi/2) e^{j\psi/2}$, the magnitude of the field becomes: $$ |E| = \frac{2 E_0}{r} \left\vert \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) \right\vert $$ The term $e^{j\psi/2}$ represents a constant phase that depends on the observation angle — it does not affect the field magnitude, so we drop it for radiation pattern analysis. **Step 6 — Normalize the field pattern.** Dividing by the maximum value $\frac{2 E_0}{r}$, the **normalized field pattern** (also called the **array factor** for $N=2$) is: $$ E_n(\phi) = \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) = \cos\left( \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\phi + \frac{\alpha}{2} \right) $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **Array Factor (AF)** for two isotropic sources. The Array Factor is purely a function of the array geometry ($d$) and the excitation ($\alpha$). It is **independent** of the individual element's intrinsic radiation pattern (which is unity for an isotropic source). ### 2.3 Special Cases (Most Important for KTU Examinations) #### Case A: Broadside Array ($d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = 0$, sources in phase) $$ \psi = \pi \cos\phi $$ $$ E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) $$ - **Maximum radiation** occurs along the perpendicular bisector: $\phi = \pm \pi/2$ (i.e., broadside to the array axis) - **Nulls** occur when $\frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = \pm \pi/2 \Rightarrow \cos\phi = \pm 1 \Rightarrow \phi = 0, \pi$ (along the array axis) - **HPBW** (Half-Power Beam Width) $\approx 60°$ #### Case B: End-Fire Array ($d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = \pm \pi$, sources 180° out of phase) $$ \psi = \pi \cos\phi \pm \pi $$ $$ E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \right) = \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) $$ - **Maximum radiation** occurs along the array axis: $\phi = 0$ or $\phi = \pi$ (depending on sign of $\alpha$) - **Nulls** occur at $\phi = \pm \pi/2$ (broadside direction) - **HPBW** $\approx 60°$ #### Case C: End-Fire Array with Progressive Phase ($d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = -\pi$) This is a special end-fire configuration where the phase difference exactly compensates the path difference along the array axis, producing a single main lobe along $\phi = 0$ (or $\phi = \pi$). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---| | Field from isotropic source | $E = \dfrac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r}$ | Spherical wave radiated by an ideal point source | | Total phase difference | $\psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha$ | Path-length phase + progressive phase | | Wave number | $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | rad/m | | Normalized field pattern | $E_n = \cos\left( \dfrac{\psi}{2} \right)$ | Angular radiation characteristic | | Maxima condition | $\psi = \pm 2 m \pi$ | Constructive interference ($m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$) | | Minima (null) condition | $\psi = \pm (2m+1)\pi$ | Destructive interference | | Broadside condition | $\alpha = 0, d = \lambda/2$ | Maximum along $\phi = 90°$ | | End-fire condition | $\alpha = \pi, d = \lambda/2$ | Maximum along $\phi = 0°$ | | Directivity (broadside, 2-element) | $D = 1.5$ or 1.76 dB | Gain over isotropic | | Directivity (end-fire, 2-element) | $D = 1.5$ or 1.76 dB | Same as broadside for $N=2$ | | HPBW (2-element, $d = \lambda/2$) | $\approx 60°$ | Half-power beam width | | First-null beam width (FNBW) | $\approx 120°$ (broadside) | Angular separation between first nulls | | Number of lobes | Depends on $d/\lambda$ | Grating lobes appear when $d \geq \lambda$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Pitfall:** A common mistake is using $\psi = \beta d \cos\phi - \alpha$ instead of $+\alpha$. The sign convention depends on whether Source 2 is *leading* or *lagging* Source 1. KTU consistently uses the convention: *Source 2 lags Source 1 by $\alpha$*, which gives $\psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha$. Be careful with the sign — it determines whether the main lobe is at $\phi = 0$ or $\phi = \pi$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The two-source array theory is the foundation of: - **Yagi-Uda antennas** (used for TV reception, amateur radio) - **Phased-array radar systems** (military and weather radar) - **Smart antennas** in 5G/6G base stations (beam steering by phase control) - **MIMO systems** in wireless communications - **Satellite communication ground stations** (parabolic + array feeds) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every modern beam-steering antenna system is essentially an extension of the two-source principle to thousands of elements, all governed by the same array factor formula. Mastering the two-source case is therefore the **gateway** to understanding all advanced antenna systems. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Field of Two Isotropic Point Sources **Given:** Two isotropic point sources, identical amplitude $I_0$, separated by distance $d$, with Source 2 lagging Source 1 by phase angle $\alpha$. Observation point $P$ at far-field distance $r$ and angle $\phi$. **Find:** Total electric field $E$ at $P$ and the normalized radiation pattern. --- **Step 1 — Write individual fields.** Each isotropic source radiates a spherical wave. In phasor form, the field from Source 1 (located at $y = +d/2$) at far-field point $P$ is: $$ E_1 = \frac{E_0}{r_1} \, e^{-j\beta r_1} $$ The field from Source 2 (located at $y = -d/2$), with a progressive phase shift $\alpha$ (Source 2 lags Source 1): $$ E_2 = \frac{E_0}{r_2} \, e^{-j\beta r_2} \cdot e^{+j\alpha} $$ The factor $e^{+j\alpha}$ represents the additional phase of Source 2 relative to Source 1. --- **Step 2 — Express $r_1$ and $r_2$ in terms of $r$ and $\phi$.** Using the geometry of the setup (both sources on the y-axis, observation at angle $\phi$ from the x-axis in the xy-plane): $$ r_1 = r - \frac{d}{2} \cos\phi $$ $$ r_2 = r + \frac{d}{2} \cos\phi $$ > This comes from the law of cosines: $r_{1,2}^2 = r^2 + (d/2)^2 \mp r \cdot d \cos\phi$. Taking the square root and applying the binomial approximation $\sqrt{1 + x} \approx 1 + x/2$ for $x \ll 1$ yields the expressions above. --- **Step 3 — Apply the amplitude approximation $r_1 \approx r_2 \approx r$.** For $r \gg d$ (far-field condition), the amplitude terms are practically equal: $$ \frac{1}{r_1} \approx \frac{1}{r_2} \approx \frac{1}{r} $$ This allows us to factor out the common amplitude $\frac{E_0}{r}$ from the total field. --- **Step 4 — Write the total field by superposition.** $$ E = E_1 + E_2 = \frac{E_0}{r_1} e^{-j\beta r_1} + \frac{E_0}{r_2} e^{-j\beta r_2 + j\alpha} $$ Substituting the approximate values of $r_1$ and $r_2$: $$ E \approx \frac{E_0}{r} \left[ e^{-j\beta(r - d\cos\phi/2)} + e^{-j\beta(r + d\cos\phi/2) + j\alpha} \right] $$ --- **Step 5 — Factor out the common phase term.** Pull the common phase $e^{-j\beta r}$ outside the bracket: $$ E = \frac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r} \left[ e^{+j\beta d \cos\phi/2} + e^{-j\beta d \cos\phi/2 + j\alpha} \right] $$ Define the total phase difference $\psi$: $$ \psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha $$ Then: $$ E = \frac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r} \left[ e^{+j\psi/2} + e^{-j\psi/2} \right] $$ --- **Step 6 — Apply Euler's identity.** Using $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2 \cos\theta$: $$ E = \frac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r} \cdot 2 \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{E = \frac{2 E_0}{r} \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) e^{-j\beta r}} $$ The magnitude of the field is: $$ |E| = \frac{2 E_0}{r} \left\vert \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) \right\vert $$ --- **Step 7 — Normalize to obtain the field pattern.** The maximum value of $|E|$ occurs when $\cos(\psi/2) = \pm 1$, giving $|E|_{\max} = \frac{2E_0}{r}$. Dividing by this maximum: $$ \boxed{E_n(\phi) = \cos\left( \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\phi + \frac{\alpha}{2} \right)} $$ > This is the **normalized radiation pattern** (or **array factor**) for two isotropic point sources. --- ### 3.2 Detailed Solution: Broadside Array (Full Marks Problem) **Problem:** Two isotropic sources are separated by $d = \lambda/2$ and fed in phase ($\alpha = 0$). Find: - (a) The directions of maxima and minima - (b) The HPBW - (c) The directivity - (d) Sketch the radiation pattern **Given:** $d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = 0$ **Step 1 — Compute $\psi$.** $$ \psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cos\phi + 0 = \pi \cos\phi $$ **Step 2 — Field pattern.** $$ E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) = \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) $$ **Step 3 — Maxima directions.** Set $E_n = \pm 1$: $$ \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = \pm m \pi \quad (m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots) $$ For $m = 0$: $\cos\phi = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 90°, 270°$ → **Broadside maxima** > For $m = 1$: $\cos\phi = \pm 2$ — impossible (since $\vert \cos\phi \vert \leq 1$). > Therefore, only **one pair of principal maxima** exists, both perpendicular to the array axis. ✓ **Step 4 — Minima (nulls) directions.** Set $E_n = 0$: $$ \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos\phi = \pm 1 \Rightarrow \phi = 0°, 180° $$ **Nulls occur along the array axis** — physically intuitive, since the waves from both sources arrive with a $180°$ phase difference along the axis and cancel out. **Step 5 — Half-Power Beam Width (HPBW).** At half-power points, $E_n = 1/\sqrt{2}$: $$ \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi_h \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi_h = \frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \cos\phi_h = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \phi_h = 60° $$ By symmetry, the second half-power point is at $180° - 60° = 120°$. $$ \boxed{\text{HPBW} = 120° - 60° = 60°} $$ **Step 6 — First-Null Beam Width (FNBW).** The nulls are at $\phi = 0°$ and $\phi = 180°$: $$ \boxed{\text{FNBW} = 180°} $$ **Step 7 — Directivity.** The directivity is the ratio of maximum radiation intensity to the average radiation intensity. For a two-element broadside array with $d = \lambda/2$: $$ D = \frac{4 \pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} = 1.5 $$ In decibels: $$ D_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76 \ \text{dB} $$ **Step 8 — Radiation Intensity.** $$ U(\phi) = U_{\max} \cos^2\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) $$ --- ### 3.3 Detailed Solution: End-Fire Array (180° Out of Phase) **Problem:** Two isotropic sources are separated by $d = \lambda/2$ and fed with $180°$ phase difference ($\alpha = \pi$, Source 2 lags). Find the pattern and its characteristics. **Step 1 — Compute $\psi$.** $$ \psi = \pi \cos\phi + \pi $$ **Step 2 — Field pattern.** $$ E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) = \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi + \frac{\pi}{2} \right) = \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) $$ **Step 3 — Maxima.** For $E_n = \pm 1$: $$ \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) = \pm 1 \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos\phi = \pm 1 $$ Therefore $\phi = 0°$ or $\phi = 180°$ → **End-fire maxima along the array axis** **Step 4 — Minima.** For $E_n = 0$: $$ \sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 90°, 270° $$ > **Nulls along the broadside direction** — exactly opposite to the broadside array case. **Step 5 — HPBW.** At half-power: $\sin\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi_h \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ $$ \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi_h = \frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \cos\phi_h = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \phi_h = 60° $$ The two half-power points (around $\phi = 0°$) are at $\phi = 60°$ and $\phi = -60°$ (or equivalently $300°$). $$ \boxed{\text{HPBW} = 120°} $$ This is **larger** than the broadside HPBW (60°). End-fire array is **less directive** along the axis. --- ### 3.4 Pattern Multiplication Principle (KTU High-Yield Concept) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Pattern Multiplication):** The radiation pattern of an array of identical elements is the *product* of the **element pattern** (pattern of a single element in free space) and the **array factor** (pattern of isotropic point sources arranged in the same geometry). $$ E_{\text{array}}(\phi, \theta) = E_{\text{element}}(\phi, \theta) \times AF(\phi, \theta) $$ For two isotropic sources, the element pattern is unity (isotropic), so the array pattern equals the array factor: $$ E_{\text{array}} = 1 \times AF = AF(\phi) $$ > **Practical Use:** If the elements are replaced by **dipoles** (or any other real antenna), the total pattern is simply the dipole pattern multiplied by the array factor. This is the most powerful concept in antenna array design. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Geometric Setup — Two Isotropic Sources ```mermaid graph TD A["Source 1
at y = +d/2"] -->|r1| P["Far field point P
at angle phi"] B["Source 2
at y = -d/2"] -->|r2| P O["Origin O
midpoint"] -.-> P A -.->|d| B A -->|d/2| O O -->|d/2| B ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Analysis Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["Two Isotropic Sources
I0, separation d, phase alpha"] --> B["Write individual fields
E1 and E2"] B --> C["Apply far field
approximation r1 r2"] C --> D["Compute total phase
psi = beta d cos phi + alpha"] D --> E["Superpose fields
E = E1 + E2"] E --> F["Use Euler identity
1 + exp j psi"] F --> G["Field pattern
E = 2 E0/r cos psi/2"] G --> H["Normalize
En = cos psi/2"] H --> I["Identify max and min
directions"] I --> J["Compute HPBW and
directivity D"] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — Identifying the Array Type ```mermaid graph TD A["Given d and alpha"] --> B{"alpha equals 0?"} B -- "Yes" --> C["In phase sources"] C --> D{"d equals lambda/2?"} D -- "Yes" --> E["BROADSIDE ARRAY
max at phi = 90 degrees"] D -- "No" --> F["In phase general array
analyze maxima condition"] B -- "No" --> G{"alpha equals pi?"} G -- "Yes" --> H["180 out of phase"] H --> I{"d equals lambda/2?"} I -- "Yes" --> J["END FIRE ARRAY
max at phi = 0 degrees"] I -- "No" --> K["General end fire pattern
analyze case by case"] G -- "No" --> L["Progressive phase array
use psi = beta d cos phi + alpha"] ``` ### 4.4 Polar Pattern Comparison (Block Representation) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph BROADSIDE a1["Main lobe at 90 deg"] --- a2["Main lobe at 270 deg"] a3["Null at 0 deg"] --- a4["Null at 180 deg"] end subgraph ENDFIRE b1["Main lobe at 0 deg"] --- b2["Main lobe at 180 deg"] b3["Null at 90 deg"] --- b4["Null at 270 deg"] end subgraph INPHASE_GENERAL c1["Main lobes depend on d"] --- c2["Multiple lobes if d large"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The polar patterns in this section are represented as block-level architecture diagrams because Mermaid cannot directly render polar plots. In a KTU examination, the student is expected to **sketch the polar plot** by hand on graph paper, marking the main lobes, nulls, HPBW, and FNBW. The diagrams above provide a **functional representation** of where the lobes and nulls lie on the polar coordinate system. ### 4.5 Analytical Matrix — Case Comparison Table | **Parameter** | **Broadside ($d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = 0$)** | **End-Fire ($d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = \pi$)** | **General ($d$, $\alpha$)** | |---|---|---|---| | Main lobe direction | $\phi = 90°, 270°$ | $\phi = 0°, 180°$ | $\beta d \cos\phi + \alpha = 0$ | | Null direction | $\phi = 0°, 180°$ | $\phi = 90°, 270°$ | $\beta d \cos\phi + \alpha = \pi$ | | HPBW | $60°$ | $120°$ | Numerical | | FNBW | $180°$ | $180°$ | Numerical | | Directivity (linear) | $1.5$ | $1.5$ | Varies | | Directivity (dB) | $1.76$ dB | $1.76$ dB | Varies | | Number of lobes | $2$ (principal) | $2$ (principal) | Depends on $d/\lambda$ | | Grating lobes | None (for $d = \lambda/2$) | None | Appear if $d \geq \lambda$ | --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Q1. Define an isotropic radiator. What is its directivity and radiation pattern shape?** **Model Answer:** An **isotropic radiator** is a hypothetical point source that radiates electromagnetic energy **uniformly in all directions** in free space. It is a lossless, idealized antenna used as a reference standard. - **Directivity:** $D_0 = 1$ (or 0 dB) - **Radiation pattern:** A perfect sphere — a circle in any 2-D polar plot - **Radiation intensity:** $U_0 = \dfrac{P_{\text{rad}}}{4\pi}$ (constant in all directions) > *Rubric: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for directivity, 1 mark for pattern shape.* --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** **Q2. State and explain the pattern multiplication theorem.** **Model Answer:** The **Pattern Multiplication Theorem** states that the total radiation pattern of an array of identical elements is the **product** of the **element pattern** and the **array factor (AF)**. $$ E_{\text{array}}(\theta, \phi) = E_{\text{element}}(\theta, \phi) \times AF(\theta, \phi) $$ The **element pattern** is the pattern of a single element in free space, while the **array factor** depends only on the geometry and excitation of the array, not on the individual element type. For two isotropic elements, $E_{\text{element}} = 1$, so $E_{\text{array}} = AF$. > *Rubric: 2 marks for statement, 1 mark for explanation/significance.* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** #### Question A (14 Marks) — Broadside Array Analysis **Two isotropic point sources are separated by a distance $d = \lambda/2$ and are excited in phase with equal amplitudes.** **(a) Derive an expression for the total far-field pattern. (7 marks)** **(b) Find the directions of maxima and minima, the HPBW, FNBW, and the directivity. Sketch the radiation pattern. (7 marks)** --- **Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** The two isotropic sources are placed on the y-axis, with Source 1 at $y = +d/2$ and Source 2 at $y = -d/2$, both fed with equal amplitude $I_0$ and zero progressive phase shift ($\alpha = 0$). The field from each source at the far-field point $P$ is: $$ E_1 = \frac{E_0}{r_1} e^{-j\beta r_1} $$ $$ E_2 = \frac{E_0}{r_2} e^{-j\beta r_2} $$ Using the far-field path-length approximation: $$ r_1 \approx r - \frac{d}{2} \cos\phi \qquad r_2 \approx r + \frac{d}{2} \cos\phi $$ With amplitude approximation $1/r_1 \approx 1/r_2 \approx 1/r$: $$ E = \frac{E_0}{r} e^{-j\beta r} \left[ e^{+j\beta d \cos\phi / 2} + e^{-j\beta d \cos\phi / 2} \right] $$ Using Euler's identity: $$ \boxed{E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\phi \right)} $$ Substituting $d = \lambda/2$: $$ E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi \right) $$ **Part (b) — Characteristics (7 marks):** **Maxima** at $E_n = \pm 1$: $$ \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = 0 \Rightarrow \phi = 90°, 270° \quad \text{(Broadside)} $$ **Minima** at $E_n = 0$: $$ \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \phi = 0°, 180° \quad \text{(Along array axis)} $$ **HPBW:** Setting $E_n = 1/\sqrt{2}$: $$ \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi_h \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \cos\phi_h = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \phi_h = 60° \text{ and } 120° $$ $$ \boxed{\text{HPBW} = 60°} $$ **FNBW:** From nulls at $0°$ and $180°$: $$ \boxed{\text{FNBW} = 180°} $$ **Directivity:** $$ D = 1.5 \quad \text{or} \quad 1.76 \text{ dB} $$ **Pattern Sketch:** A figure-8 (dumbbell) shape with two main lobes along $\phi = \pm 90°$ and nulls at $\phi = 0°$ and $\phi = 180°$. > *Rubric: (a) 1 mark setup, 2 marks approximation, 2 marks superposition, 2 marks simplification. (b) 2 marks maxima/minima, 2 marks HPBW, 1 mark FNBW, 1 mark directivity, 1 mark sketch.* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — End-Fire Array Analysis **Two isotropic point sources are separated by a distance $d = \lambda/4$ and excited with a progressive phase difference $\alpha = -\pi/2$.** **(a) Show that the array is an end-fire array and derive the total field pattern. (7 marks)** **(b) Determine the directions of maxima and minima, the HPBW, and the directivity. (7 marks)** --- **Solution:** **Part (a) — End-Fire Condition (7 marks):** The total phase difference is: $$ \psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} \cos\phi - \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cos\phi - \frac{\pi}{2} $$ The normalized field pattern is: $$ E_n = \cos\left( \frac{\psi}{2} \right) = \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{4} \cos\phi - \frac{\pi}{4} \right) $$ **Verification of end-fire condition:** The condition for end-fire is $\alpha = -\beta d$. With $d = \lambda/4$: $$ \alpha = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} = -\frac{\pi}{2} \quad \checkmark $$ So this is indeed an end-fire configuration. **Part (b) — Characteristics (7 marks):** **Maxima** at $E_n = \pm 1$: $$ \frac{\pi}{4} \cos\phi - \frac{\pi}{4} = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 1 \Rightarrow \phi = 0° $$ Also at $\cos\phi = 1$ and $\psi = -2\pi$ (second-order maximum — coincides with the first). **Direction of end-fire maximum:** $\phi = 0°$ — along the array axis (in the direction of progressive phase lag). ✓ **Minima** at $E_n = 0$: $$ \frac{\pi}{4} \cos\phi - \frac{\pi}{4} = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow \cos\phi = 3 \text{ or } \cos\phi = -1 $$ Only the valid solution is $\cos\phi = -1 \Rightarrow \phi = 180°$ (null in the opposite direction). **HPBW:** Setting $E_n = 1/\sqrt{2}$: $$ \cos\left( \frac{\pi}{4} \cos\phi_h - \frac{\pi}{4} \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$ $$ \frac{\pi}{4} \cos\phi_h - \frac{\pi}{4} = \pm \frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \cos\phi_h = 2 \text{ or } \cos\phi_h = 0 $$ The valid solution is $\cos\phi_h = 0 \Rightarrow \phi_h = 90°$ and $\phi_h = 270°$. **HPBW (around the $\phi = 0°$ lobe):** By symmetry, the HPBW spans from $\phi = 270°$ to $\phi = 90°$ through $\phi = 0°$: $$ \boxed{\text{HPBW} = 180°} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign of $\alpha$:** Students often confuse whether $\alpha$ is positive or negative. The end-fire condition is $\alpha = -\beta d$ for radiation in the $+\phi$ direction. Wrong sign → wrong main lobe direction → lose 3-4 marks. > 2. **Units consistency:** Always use $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ in rad/m and $d$ in meters, otherwise the equation $\psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha$ becomes dimensionally inconsistent. > 3. **Missing factor of 2:** Some students write $E_n = \cos(\psi)$ instead of $\cos(\psi/2)$. This is a common error arising from misapplying Euler's identity. Always remember the factor of 1/2. > 4. **Null computation:** A null is a *zero* of the pattern. Setting $E_n = 0$ is correct, but ensure the argument of the cosine yields exactly $\pi/2, 3\pi/2$, etc. — not just any value where cosine vanishes. > 5. **Directivity derivation:** KTU expects the formula $D = 4\pi U_{\max}/P_{\text{rad}}$ to be quoted, with $U_{\max}$ and $P_{\text{rad}}$ shown in terms of the field integral. Direct numerical answer without derivation loses at least 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Scheme:** - **Isotropic source** is the reference radiator with $D_0 = 1$ and a perfectly spherical radiation pattern. - **Two-source array factor** is the foundation of all linear array theory. - **Master equation:** $\psi = \beta d \cos\phi + \alpha$ — every special case is derived from this. - **Normalized field pattern:** $E_n = \cos(\psi/2)$ — remember the **half-angle** factor. - **Broadside array** requires $\alpha = 0$ and ideally $d = \lambda/2$. Main lobe is at $\phi = 90°$ (perpendicular to array axis). - **End-fire array** requires $\alpha = \pm \pi$ (for $d = \lambda/2$) or $\alpha = -\beta d$ (general). Main lobe is at $\phi = 0°$ or $\phi = 180°$ (along array axis). - **HPBW of broadside array** (2 elements, $d = \lambda/2$): $60°$. - **HPBW of end-fire array** (2 elements, $d = \lambda/2$): $120°$. - **Directivity** of either broadside or end-fire (2 elements, $d = \lambda/2$): $1.5$ (or $1.76$ dB). - **Null condition:** $\psi = \pm \pi, \pm 3\pi, \ldots$ - **Maximum condition:** $\psi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \ldots$ - **Pattern multiplication theorem:** $E_{\text{array}} = E_{\text{element}} \times AF$. Master principle for all real-world arrays. - **Grating lobes** appear when element spacing $d \geq \lambda$. Always choose $d < \lambda$ to avoid unwanted major lobes. - **Far-field condition** for arrays: $r \geq 2 D^2 / \lambda$, where $D$ is the largest array dimension. - **Convention check:** Source 2 lags Source 1 → use $+\alpha$ in $\psi$ equation. - **Sign of $\cos\phi$** depends on the geometry (y-axis vs x-axis array). KTU uses y-axis → $\cos\phi$ term. - **Units:** $\beta$ in rad/m, $d$ in m, $\lambda$ in m, $\phi$ in degrees or radians (be consistent). - **FNBW** is always greater than HPBW. FNBW is between first nulls, HPBW is between half-power points. --- **End of Module 4 Notes — Field of Two Isotropic Point Sources**

topics.title: Principle of Pattern Multiplication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle of Pattern Multiplication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Principle of Pattern Multiplication — KTU 2024 Module 4 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Multiplication Theorem (Balanis Theorem):**
> The **total radiation pattern (array pattern)** of an array of identical radiating elements is equal to the **product of the individual element pattern** (also called the *unit pattern* or *element pattern*) and the **array factor** of the array, when the array factor is expressed as a function of angles (θ, ϕ) describing the direction of observation, with all other parameters held constant.

Mathematically, the principle is stated as:

$$
E_{n}(\theta,\phi) = E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \;\times\; AF(\theta,\phi)
$$

where each term is interpreted in the same coordinate system and same units.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Choir Analogy:** Imagine 8 singers (identical microphones/elements) arranged in a row on stage. The way *one singer* naturally projects their voice is the **Element Pattern** (Ee). The way the *arrangement* of all 8 singers shapes sound in different directions — louder towards the front, quieter sideways — is the **Array Factor** (AF). The actual sound you hear from the *whole choir* in any direction is simply the *natural voice of one singer* multiplied by the *directional shaping of the group placement*. This is exactly how antennas behave!

**Key Insight for Students:** You don't need to analyze every element separately. Just analyze ONE element, then apply the *array factor* of the arrangement — and multiply. The result is the total pattern.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Element Pattern (Ee):** A function describing the radiation characteristic of a single isolated antenna.
- **Array Factor (AF):** A purely geometric function dependent on:
  - Element spacing **d** (in units of wavelength **λ**)
  - Progressive phase shift **β** (radians)
  - Number of elements **N**
  - Geometric configuration (linear, planar, circular)
- **Field Strength Units:** V/m (volts per metre) for the E-field.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever the question mentions "identical elements" in an array, the examiner is hinting that you can directly invoke the **Principle of Pattern Multiplication** — no need to derive fields element by element.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Theorem

**Step 1 — Identify the Element Pattern (Ee):**
The element pattern is the radiation pattern of *one* antenna when operated in *isolation* (no mutual coupling assumed in basic KTU problems). For most KTU problems, this is given as a known function of θ, such as cos θ, sin θ, or unity (isotropic element).

**Step 2 — Construct the Array Factor (AF):**
The array factor depends *only* on the *geometry* of the array (positions of elements) and the *excitation* (amplitudes and phases). It is independent of the type of individual radiators.

**Step 3 — Multiply in the SAME Coordinate System:**
Both Ee and AF must be expressed in the same spherical coordinate system (θ, ϕ). The product gives the total field.

**Step 4 — Normalize the Result:**
The total pattern is usually normalized with respect to its maximum value to obtain the **normalized array pattern** in dB (decibels) or linear scale.

### 2.2 Conditions for Validity of the Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The theorem is valid ONLY when:**
> 1. All elements are **identical** (same physical structure, same orientation).
> 2. The mutual coupling between elements is **negligible** (or assumed zero in textbook problems).
> 3. The same reference current is applied to all elements.
> 4. Both Ee and AF are expressed in the same coordinate frame.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Element Pattern | Ee(θ, ϕ) | Given; e.g., cos θ, sin θ, 1 (isotropic) | V/m |
| Array Factor (Broadside, N-element linear) | AF(θ) | $\dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{N\psi}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)}$ | Dimensionless |
| Array Factor (2-element) | AF(θ) | $2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| Progressive Phase Difference | ψ | $\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ | radians |
| Electrical Spacing | d/λ | Ratio of physical spacing to wavelength | dimensionless |
| Progressive Phase Excitation | α | Phase difference between adjacent elements | radians |
| Total Array Field | En(θ, ϕ) | $E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \;\times\; AF(\theta,\phi)$ | V/m |
| Normalized Pattern (dB) | En,dB | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_{n}}{E_{n,\max}}\right)$ | dB |
| Directivity (approximate) | D | $\dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_{A}}$ where $\Omega_{A}$ is beam solid angle | dimensionless |
| Half-Power Beamwidth (uniform linear) | HPBW | $\dfrac{0.886\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_{0}}$ (radians) | degrees/radians |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe Symbol Alert:** Whenever you write absolute values or modulus in your KTU answer sheet, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ — never the raw $\vert$ character inside a table cell, as it breaks markdown rendering and the examiner's digital copy may show corruption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pattern multiplication principle is the **backbone of modern antenna array design** used in:
- **5G/6G Base Station Antennas** (massive MIMO arrays)
- **Phased Array Radars** (military and weather radar)
- **Satellite Communication Payloads** (beam-shaping on the move)
- **Smart Antennas** in mobile handsets
- **Radio Astronomy Arrays** (e.g., VLA in New Mexico, GMRT in India)

It allows antenna engineers to **decouple** the radiator design from the beam-shaping design, which simplifies engineering and manufacturing tremendously.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Array Factor for an N-Element Linear Array

**Starting Point — Geometry:**
Consider N identical isotropic radiators placed along the z-axis with uniform spacing d and progressive phase excitation α.

**Step 1 — Total Far-Field from Superposition:**

The far-zone electric field from N elements is the sum of contributions from each element:

$$
E_{tot} = E_{1} + E_{2} + E_{3} + \cdots + E_{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Field of the k-th Element:**

Since the elements are identical and oriented in the same direction, the field magnitude of the k-th element differs from the first only by the relative phase contribution:

$$
E_{k} = E_{1} \; e^{\,j(k-1)\psi}
$$

where $\psi$ is the **progressive phase difference** between adjacent elements, which includes both the path-difference phase and the excitation phase:

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha
$$

with $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ being the free-space phase constant.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Sum:**

$$
E_{tot} = E_{1}\!\left(1 + e^{j\psi} + e^{j2\psi} + \cdots + e^{j(N-1)\psi}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize a Geometric Series:**

The bracketed term is a geometric progression with common ratio $e^{j\psi}$:

$$
S = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left(e^{j\psi}\right)^{n} = \dfrac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Numerator and Denominator:**

Factor the complex exponentials:

$$
S = \dfrac{e^{jN\psi/2}\!\left(e^{-jN\psi/2} - e^{jN\psi/2}\right)}{e^{j\psi/2}\!\left(e^{-j\psi/2} - e^{j\psi/2}\right)}
$$

Using Euler's identity: $e^{jA} - e^{-jA} = 2j\sin A$, we get:

$$
S = e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \;\cdot\; \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the Array Factor:**

The magnitude term is the **Array Factor**:

$$
AF(\psi) = \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

and the phase term is the array's progressive phase, which is usually dropped when plotting amplitude patterns.

**Step 7 — Apply Pattern Multiplication Theorem:**

If the elements are NOT isotropic, replace the implicit $E_{1} = 1$ with the actual element pattern $E_{e}(\theta,\phi)$:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{n}(\theta,\phi) = E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \;\times\; \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{N\psi}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)}\,}
$$

This is the master equation used in **almost every KTU 14-mark array problem**.

### 3.2 Special Case — 2-Element Array

Setting N = 2 in the general array factor:

$$
AF_{2}(\psi) = \dfrac{\sin(\psi)}{\sin(\psi/2)} = 2\cos(\psi/2)
$$

Therefore for two identical non-isotropic elements:

$$
E_{n} = 2 \,E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \cos(\psi/2)
$$

This is the most common 2-element configuration asked in KTU Part A questions.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4-element linear array of half-wave dipoles has spacing d = λ/2, progressive phase α = 0 (broadside), and the elements lie along the z-axis. Find the principal maximum directions and the first nulls. The element pattern is $E_{e} = \cos(\pi/2 \cos\theta)$ in the H-plane (a standard dipole).

**Solution — Step 1: Array Factor**

With N = 4, d = λ/2, α = 0:

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha = \pi\cos\theta
$$

$$
AF(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin(2\pi\cos\theta)}{\sin((\pi/2)\cos\theta)}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Principal Maximum**

The principal maximum of AF occurs when ψ = 0:

$$
\pi \cos\theta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta = 90^{\circ}
$$

So the main beam points broadside (perpendicular to the array axis). ✓

**Solution — Step 3: First Nulls**

First nulls occur when $N\psi/2 = \pm\pi$, i.e., when $\psi = \pm \pi/2$:

$$
\pi\cos\theta = \pm\pi/2 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta = \pm 1/2
$$

$$
\theta_{null} = 60^{\circ} \text{ and } 120^{\circ}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Apply Pattern Multiplication**

The total field is:

$$
E_{n}(\theta) = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right) \;\cdot\; \dfrac{\sin(2\pi\cos\theta)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}
$$

The element pattern **modulates** the array factor, suppressing the pattern near θ = 0° and 180° (where the dipole element has its own null along its axis). This is the **direct visible benefit** of pattern multiplication — a clean, directional beam.

**Solution — Step 5: Beamwidth Estimate**

$$
