HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_0} = \dfrac{0.886\lambda}{4 \cdot (\lambda/2) \cdot 1} = \dfrac{0.886}{2} \approx 0.443 \text{ rad} \approx 25.4^{\circ}
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pattern Multiplication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identical Antenna Elements N] --> B[Element Pattern Ee of theta phi]
    A --> C[Array Factor AF of psi]
    D[Geometric Parameters: d, alpha, N] --> C
    E[Element Type: Dipole, Patch, Horn] --> B
    B --> F[Pattern Multiplier Operator X]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Total Array Pattern En of theta phi]
    G --> H[Normalization to Max Value]
    H --> I[Polar or Cartesian Plot in dB]
    I --> J[Beamwidth, SLL, Directivity Extraction]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Flow of Pattern Multiplication on a 2D Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Plot Element Pattern Ee theta in polar form]
    P2[Step 2: Plot Array Factor AF psi in same coordinate]
    P3[Step 3: At every angle theta, multiply magnitudes]
    P4[Step 4: Plot resulting En theta]
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P5[Nulls appear where Ee OR AF is zero] -.-> P4
    P6[Main beam shaped by BOTH] -.-> P4
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Applying the Theorem in KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Question: Array of identical elements?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Apply Pattern Multiplication]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use full superposition integral]
    Q2 --> R1{Element pattern given?}
    R1 -- Yes --> R2[Multiply Ee with AF directly]
    R1 -- No --> R3[Assume isotropic Ee equals 1]
    R3 --> R2
    R2 --> R4[Find psi, compute AF, multiply]
    R4 --> R5[Plot or extract HPBW, SLL, directivity]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU diagrams in answer sheets:** Draw the polar plot of $E_{e}$, the polar plot of AF, and the polar plot of $E_{n}$ on the same angular axis. Visually show how the nulls of the product are the **union** of nulls of Ee and AF.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **State the principle of pattern multiplication. Mention any two conditions for its validity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The principle of pattern multiplication (also called Balanis theorem) states that the total radiation pattern of an array of identical elements is the **product of the element pattern and the array factor** of the array, both expressed in the same coordinate system.
>
> Two essential conditions:
> 1. All elements in the array must be **identical** in type, orientation, and excitation reference.
> 2. **Mutual coupling** between elements must be **negligible** (assumed zero in ideal analysis).
>
> *(Third point for completeness: the element pattern and array factor must be expressed in the same spherical coordinates θ, ϕ.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **For a 2-element array with identical dipoles, write the expression for the total field using the pattern multiplication principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a 2-element array with identical dipoles:

$$
E_{n}(\theta,\phi) = 2 \,E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \cdot \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)
$$

where $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$.

Here $E_{e}(\theta,\phi)$ is the element pattern (for a half-wave dipole: $E_{e} \propto \cos(\pi/2 \cos\theta)$ in the principal H-plane), d is the inter-element spacing, and α is the progressive phase excitation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the array factor expression for an N-element uniform linear array with progressive phase excitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-element broadside uniform linear array of half-wave dipoles with inter-element spacing d = λ/2 is oriented along the z-axis. Using the principle of pattern multiplication, find: (i) the directions of principal maxima, (ii) the first null directions, (iii) the half-power beamwidth, and (iv) plot qualitatively the total array pattern. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Place N identical isotropic elements along the z-axis with uniform spacing d and progressive phase α. The far-field of the k-th element has the same magnitude as the first but is phase-shifted by $(k-1)\psi$ where $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$.

**Step 2:** Sum all N contributions:

$$
E_{tot} = E_{1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{jn\psi} = E_{1} \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}
$$

**Step 3:** After simplification using Euler's identity (as shown in Section 3.1), the magnitude is the **array factor**:

$$
AF(\psi) = \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

The maximum value of AF is N, occurring when $\psi = 0$.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the normalized array factor is:

$$
AF_{n}(\psi) = \dfrac{1}{N} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Principal Maxima**

For broadside, α = 0. The principal maximum occurs at $\psi = 0$:

$$
\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta = 90^{\circ}
$$

So the main beam is **broadside** (perpendicular to the array axis). ✓

**Step 2: First Nulls**

First nulls at $N\psi/2 = \pm\pi$:

$$
N \cdot \dfrac{\beta d \cos\theta}{2} = \pm\pi \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta = \pm\dfrac{1}{N} = \pm\dfrac{1}{4} = \pm 0.25
$$

$$
\theta_{null} = \cos^{-1}(0.25) \approx 75.5^{\circ} \text{ and } 104.5^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3: Half-Power Beamwidth**

Using the standard uniform linear array formula:

$$
HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_0} = \dfrac{0.886}{4 \cdot 0.5} = 0.443 \text{ rad} \approx 25.4^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4: Total Array Pattern (Pattern Multiplication)**

For a half-wave dipole, $E_{e}(\theta) \approx \cos(\pi/2 \cos\theta)$ in the H-plane:

$$
E_{n}(\theta) = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right) \cdot \dfrac{\sin(2\pi\cos\theta)}{\sin(\pi\cos\theta/2)}
$$

The product gives a sharp broadside beam with suppressed end-fire lobes (because the dipole null aligns with end-fire).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget the factor of 2** when computing the 2-element array factor — many students write $AF = \cos(\psi/2)$ instead of $2\cos(\psi/2)$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Do not mix up the principal maximum and grating lobe condition:** Grating lobes occur when $d/\lambda > 0.5$ with α = 0. In this problem d = λ/2 — borderline; just above this, grating lobes will appear.
> 3. **Always express both Ee and AF in θ**, not in ψ. Examiners will deduct marks for "AF in terms of ψ" without substituting ψ = βd cosθ + α.
> 4. **Beamwidth formula selection:** Use $0.886\lambda / (Nd\cos\theta_0)$ only for uniform linear arrays — wrong formula loses 2–3 marks.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of pattern multiplication with a neat mathematical statement and list the conditions under which it is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-element uniform linear array of isotropic radiators has spacing d = λ/2 and progressive phase α = −90° (end-fire configuration). Using pattern multiplication: (i) write the array factor expression, (ii) find the direction of the main beam, (iii) find the 3 dB beamwidth, and (iv) determine the first side-lobe level relative to the main beam maximum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Mathematical Statement:**

The principle of pattern multiplication is mathematically expressed as:

$$
E_{n}(\theta,\phi) = E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \cdot AF(\theta,\phi)
$$

where $E_{n}$ is the **total field pattern**, $E_{e}$ is the **element pattern**, and AF is the **array factor** evaluated in the same spherical coordinate system.

**Conditions of Validity:**

1. The radiating elements must be **identical** in geometry, orientation, and feed.
2. Mutual coupling effects must be **negligible**.
3. Element and array patterns must be expressed in the **same coordinate frame**.
4. Reference excitation amplitude and phase must be the **same across all elements** (only the progressive phase α and amplitude weighting distinguish them).

**Engineering Significance:**

It allows independent design of the radiator (Ee) and the beam-shaping geometry (AF), simplifying the design of complex arrays.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Array Factor for N = 6, d = λ/2, α = −π/2**

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha = \pi\cos\theta - \dfrac{\pi}{2}
$$

$$
AF(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin(3\psi)}{\sin(\psi/2)} = \dfrac{\sin(3(\pi\cos\theta - \pi/2))}{\sin((\pi\cos\theta - \pi/2)/2)}
$$

**Step 2: Direction of Main Beam**

Principal maximum when $\psi = 0$:

$$
\pi\cos\theta - \pi/2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta = 1/2 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta = 60^{\circ}
$$

So the main beam is tilted towards end-fire, at 60° from broadside.

**Step 3: Half-Power Beamwidth**

For uniform linear array with N = 6, d = λ/2, and main beam at θ₀ = 60°:

$$
HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_0} = \dfrac{0.886}{6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5} = \dfrac{0.886}{1.5} \approx 0.591 \text{ rad} \approx 33.8^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4: First Side-Lobe Level**

For a uniform amplitude N-element array, the first side-lobe level (SLL) is approximately:

$$
SLL \approx -13.2 \text{ dB}
$$

relative to the main beam maximum. This is a **standard textbook value** for uniform arrays and does not depend on N for large N.

Since the elements are isotropic, $E_{e} = 1$, and pattern multiplication gives $E_{n} = AF$ directly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for the KTU board:**

- **Master Equation (must memorize):**
  $$E_{n}(\theta,\phi) = E_{e}(\theta,\phi) \times AF(\psi)$$
- **Array Factor — Uniform Linear Array:**
  $$AF(\psi) = \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}, \quad \text{where } \psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$$
- **Two-element simplification:**
  $$AF_{2} = 2\cos(\psi/2)$$
- **Principal maximum condition:** $\psi = 0$ (modulo $2\pi$).
- **Null condition:** $N\psi/2 = \pm m\pi$ where $m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (excluding $m$ such that $\psi = 0$).
- **Grating lobe appearance threshold:** $d/\lambda \geq 1$ for broadside, or $d/\lambda \geq 0.5$ when scanning off-broadside.
- **Beamwidth for uniform array:**
  $$HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_0}$$
- **First side-lobe level (uniform excitation):** ≈ **−13.2 dB** (memorize this constant).
- **Conditions of validity:** identical elements, negligible mutual coupling, common reference frame.
- **Pattern multiplication is a multiplication of magnitudes**, not powers. To get dB plots, convert AFTER multiplication.
- **Hansen-Woodyard end-fire condition** (special case to remember): requires $\alpha \approx -\beta d - \pi/N$ for enhanced directivity.
- **Typical KTU marks split (14 marks):** 7 for derivation + 7 for numerical application.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing "pattern multiplication" with "array factor" — they are NOT the same. The product includes the ELEMENT pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-liner for viva:** *"Pattern multiplication decouples element design from beam-shape design — you can change the radiator type (dipole, patch, horn) without recomputing the array factor geometry, and vice versa."*

---

*End of KTU 2024 Module 4 Notes — Principle of Pattern Multiplication*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Array Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Array Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In antenna array theory, the **Array Factor (AF)** is defined as the normalized (or un-normalized) far-field radiation pattern of an array of identical radiating elements, evaluated as if the individual elements were replaced by **isotropic point sources** located at the same spatial positions, carrying the same complex excitation (amplitude and phase) as the actual elements.

Mathematically, the Array Factor isolates the *spatial and excitation* contribution of the array from the *element* contribution. According to the **Principle of Pattern Multiplication**, the total array field is the product:

$$E_{total}(\theta,\phi) = E_{element}(\theta,\phi) \times AF(\theta,\phi)$$

For a general array of N elements located at positions $\vec{r}_n$ with complex excitations $A_n e^{j\alpha_n}$, the Array Factor is:

$$AF(\theta,\phi) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_n \, e^{j\left[k\hat{r}\cdot\vec{r}_n + \alpha_n\right]}$$

where:
- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ is the **wavenumber** (in rad/m)
- $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength
- $\hat{r}$ is the unit vector in the observation direction
- $A_n$ and $\alpha_n$ are the amplitude and phase of the $n^{th}$ element excitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, the focus is restricted to **uniform linear arrays (ULA)** along the z-axis (or x-axis), where amplitudes are equal ($A_n = A$) and phases follow a *linear progression* ($\alpha_n = n\beta$). This simplification is what the question papers test.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **choir of 8 singers standing in a line**, each holding a microphone. They all sing the same note, but the conductor tells each singer to start *just a fraction of a second later* than the previous one.

- When you stand **directly in front** of the choir (perpendicular to the line), all sound waves arrive **in phase** → loud, clear sound. This is the **principal maximum**.
- When you walk to the **side**, the delays accumulate, and at some angles the sound waves **cancel out** (destructive interference) → silence. These are the **nulls**.
- The **Array Factor** is essentially a *mathematical score sheet* that tells you exactly **how loud the choir will sound at every angle** around them, depending on:
  1. The **spacing** between singers (element spacing, $d$)
  2. The **progressive delay** each singer is given (progressive phase shift, $\beta$)
  3. The **number** of singers (N)

So the AF is the *spatial fingerprint* of an array — it is the "shape" of the beam that the array projects into space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Wavelength: $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$
> - Wavenumber: $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{c}$
> - Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = 377 \, \Omega$

---

## 1.3 Why Array Factor Matters (Engineering Motivation)

A single antenna element (like a half-wave dipole) radiates **omnidirectionally** (or with a very broad pattern). To obtain a **high-gain, narrow, steerable beam** — required in radar, satellite communication, 5G base stations, and radio astronomy — engineers combine multiple elements into an **array**. The Array Factor is the **design tool** that allows us to:
- **Steer the beam electronically** (by varying $\beta$)
- **Shape the beamwidth** (by choosing N and $d$)
- **Suppress side lobes** (by tapering $A_n$ — though Module 4 mainly uses uniform $A_n$)
- **Place nulls toward jammers** (adaptive arrays)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Problem: N-Element Uniform Linear Array (ULA)

Consider **N identical isotropic radiators** placed along the **z-axis** at positions:
$$z_n = n\,d, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

Each element is fed with:
- **Equal amplitude:** $A_n = A_0$ (uniform excitation)
- **Linear progressive phase:** $\alpha_n = n\beta$

where $\beta$ is the progressive phase shift between adjacent elements (in radians). The observation point is in the far-field, at angles $(\theta, \phi)$ measured from the array axis.

The phase contribution of the $n^{th}$ element, relative to the origin, is:
$$\psi_n = k\,z_n\cos\theta + \alpha_n = n(kd\cos\theta + \beta) = n\psi$$

where $\psi$ is the **total inter-element phase difference** (also called the *array phase variable*).

$$\boxed{\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single variable $\psi$ encodes **everything** — the geometry ($d$), the observation direction ($\theta$), and the excitation ($\beta$). All AF analysis reduces to studying $\psi$ as a function of $\theta$.

---

## 2.2 The Two Foundational Equations of the Array Factor

### 2.2.1 Exponential (Phasor) Form

$$AF = A_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{jn\psi}$$

This is a **finite geometric series** of N terms with common ratio $e^{j\psi}$.

### 2.2.2 Closed-Form Trigonometric Form

Using the geometric sum formula $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \dfrac{1-r^N}{1-r}$:

$$AF = A_0 \left[\frac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}\right]$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $e^{-jN\psi/2}$ and $e^{-j\psi/2}$ respectively:

$$AF = A_0 \, e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$$

The **magnitude** (which is what is plotted) is:

$$\boxed{\,|AF| = A_0 \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of the magnitude:** It behaves like a *sinc-like function* — it is maximum (= 1) at $\psi = 0$ (and multiples of $2\pi$), oscillates with period $2\pi/N$ in the numerator, and has poles at $\psi = 2m\pi$ (which are *removable* — the function is still finite there by L'Hôpital's rule, equalling $N$).

---

## 2.3 Conditions for Maxima and Minima

| Feature | Condition on $\psi$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Principal Maximum** | $\psi = 2m\pi$ (for m = 0) | Maximum radiation direction |
| **Nulls (Zeros)** | $\psi = \dfrac{2p\pi}{N}$, $p \neq mN$ | Directions of complete cancellation |
| **Secondary Maxima (Side Lobes)** | Between successive nulls | Unwanted radiation directions |
| **First Null** | $\psi = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | Edge of the main beam |

The **principal maximum** corresponds to $m=0$:
$$kd\cos\theta_0 + \beta = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0}$$

This is the *beam-steering equation*. To point the beam at angle $\theta_0$, feed the elements with progressive phase $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$.

---

## 2.4 Broadside and End-Fire Arrays (Two Special Cases)

### Case 1: Broadside Array ($\theta_0 = 90^\circ$)
- $\cos\theta_0 = 0 \Rightarrow \beta = 0$
- All elements fed **in phase**.
- The beam points **perpendicular** to the array axis.
- Principal max condition: $\psi = kd\cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ$.

### Case 2: End-Fire Array ($\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$)
- For $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$: $\beta = -kd$
- For $\theta_0 = 180^\circ$: $\beta = +kd$
- The beam points **along the array axis**.
- Principal max condition: $\psi = kd(\cos\theta + 1) = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 0^\circ$ (or $180^\circ$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hansen-Woodyard Condition** (Enhanced Directivity End-Fire): For a longer end-fire array, the optimum progressive phase is:
> $$\beta_{HW} = -\left(kd + \frac{\pi}{N}\right)$$
> This gives about **3 dB higher directivity** than the ordinary end-fire case. This is a frequently asked KTU question.

---

## 2.5 Grating Lobes (Forbidden Operating Regime)

Grating lobes are **unwanted replicas** of the principal maximum appearing in *visible space* ($0 \le \theta \le 180^\circ$). They occur when $\psi = \pm 2\pi$ has another solution in visible space besides $\theta = \theta_0$.

For a broadside array ($\beta = 0$): the second maximum at $m=1$ gives $kd\cos\theta = 2\pi$, i.e., $\cos\theta = \lambda/d$. This is in visible space only if $|\cos\theta| \le 1$, i.e., $d/\lambda \ge 1$.

**Grating-lobe-free condition:**
$$\boxed{\dfrac{d}{\lambda} \le \dfrac{1}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}}$$

For broadside ($\theta_0 = 90^\circ$): $d/\lambda \le 1$.
For end-fire ($\theta_0 = 0^\circ$): $d/\lambda \le 1/2$.

---

## 2.6 Beamwidth and Directivity Approximations

**Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW)** in radians (broadside, large N):
$$\text{HPBW} \approx \dfrac{0.886 \,\lambda}{N d}\quad\text{(in radians)} \quad\text{or}\quad \dfrac{50.6 \,\lambda}{N d}\quad\text{(in degrees)}$$

**Directivity** (for a uniformly illuminated linear array, broadside):
$$D \approx 2 \,\dfrac{N d}{\lambda}$$

These are "first-pass" engineering estimates and appear commonly in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Print This!)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Array phase variable | $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$ | radians |
| 2 | Wavenumber | $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ | rad/m |
| 3 | AF (exponential form) | $AF = A_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{jn\psi}$ | complex |
| 4 | AF (closed form) | $AF = A_0 e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}\dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$ | complex |
| 5 | $\vert AF \vert$ (magnitude) | $\vert AF \vert = A_0 \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Principal max condition | $\psi = \pm 2m\pi,\ m = 0,\ \pm 1,\dots$ | m = 0 is main beam |
| 7 | Nulls | $\psi = 2p\pi/N,\ p \ne mN$ | p is integer |
| 8 | Beam-steering equation | $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$ | radians |
| 9 | Broadside array | $\beta = 0$ | $\theta_0 = 90^\circ$ |
| 10 | Ordinary end-fire | $\beta = -kd$ | $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ |
| 11 | Hansen-Woodyard end-fire | $\beta = -kd - \pi/N$ | higher directivity |
| 12 | Grating-lobe-free limit | $d/\lambda \le 1/(1+\vert\cos\theta_0\vert)$ | $d$ in metres |
| 13 | HPBW (broadside, large N) | $\approx 0.886\,\lambda/(Nd)$ | radians |
| 14 | Directivity (broadside ULA) | $D \approx 2Nd/\lambda$ | dimensionless (linear) |
| 15 | Pattern multiplication | $E_{total} = E_{element} \times AF$ | total field |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, vertical bars `|x|` are written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table. **In your answer sheet, write them as `|x|`** — KTU examiners expect it.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Array Factor |
|---|---|
| **Phased-Array Radar** (e.g., AN/APG-81 in F-35) | Electronic beam steering by varying $\beta$ — no moving parts. |
| **5G Massive MIMO Base Stations** | 64/128/256 elements; AF designed for narrow, user-specific beams. |
| **Satellite Payloads** | Shaped-beam antennas use non-uniform $A_n$ and tailored $\beta_n$. |
| **Radio Astronomy** (e.g., VLA, SKA) | Large N → very high resolution; HPBW ∝ 1/N. |
| **Smart Antennas in Mobile Handsets** | Adaptive AF nulls out interferers in real time. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Array Factor for N-Element ULA (from Phasor Sum to Closed Form)

**Starting Premise:** Place N isotropic radiators at $z_n = n d$ for $n=0,1,2,\dots,N-1$. Element $n$ is fed with amplitude $A_0$ and phase $\alpha_n = n\beta$.

**Step 1 — Field contribution of element n:**

The far-field at distance $R$ from origin, at angle $\theta$ from the +z axis, has path-length difference $z_n \cos\theta$ between element $n$ and the origin element. The path-length phase delay is $k z_n \cos\theta = k n d \cos\theta$. Adding the feed phase $n\beta$:

$$\text{Field from element } n \;\propto\; A_0 \, e^{j(k n d \cos\theta + n\beta)} = A_0 \, e^{j n (kd\cos\theta + \beta)} = A_0 \, e^{j n \psi}$$

where we define $\psi \equiv kd\cos\theta + \beta$.

**Step 2 — Total Array Factor (superposition):**

$$AF(\psi) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_0 \, e^{j n \psi} = A_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (e^{j\psi})^n$$

**Step 3 — Recognize geometric series:**

This is a finite geometric series with first term 1, common ratio $r = e^{j\psi}$, and N terms. Using the standard identity:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \frac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}, \quad r \ne 1$$

we obtain:

$$AF = A_0 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{jN\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to trigonometric form using Euler identities:**

Use the identity $1 - e^{j\phi} = e^{j\phi/2}(e^{-j\phi/2} - e^{j\phi/2}) = -2j \, e^{j\phi/2} \sin(\phi/2)$:

$$1 - e^{j\psi} = -2j \, e^{j\psi/2} \sin(\psi/2)$$

$$1 - e^{jN\psi} = -2j \, e^{jN\psi/2} \sin(N\psi/2)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF &= A_0 \cdot \frac{-2j \, e^{jN\psi/2} \sin(N\psi/2)}{-2j \, e^{j\psi/2} \sin(\psi/2)} \\[6pt]
&= A_0 \cdot e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude (what is plotted on radiation pattern):**

$$|AF| = A_0 \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$$

This is the **master equation** of Module 4. Every radiation pattern question reduces to evaluating this expression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Principal Maximum Condition and Beam Steering

**Step 1 — Find $\psi$ that maximizes $|AF|$:**

The denominator $\sin(\psi/2)$ has zeros at $\psi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \dots$ The numerator also has zeros at $\psi = 0, \pm 2\pi/N, \pm 4\pi/N, \dots$

At $\psi = 2m\pi$ (for integer m), both numerator and denominator are zero — apply L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\lim_{\psi \to 2m\pi} \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} = \lim_{\psi \to 2m\pi} \frac{N\cos(N\psi/2)}{\frac{1}{2}\cos(\psi/2)} = \frac{N \cdot \cos(mN\pi)}{\frac{1}{2}\cos(m\pi)} = \pm N$$

So the principal maximum is $|AF| = N A_0$ at $\psi = 2m\pi$.

**Step 2 — The m = 0 case (main beam):**

$$\psi = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; kd\cos\theta_0 + \beta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$$

This is the **beam-steering equation**. Solve for $\theta_0$:

$$\cos\theta_0 = -\frac{\beta}{kd}$$

For given $\beta$, this gives the direction of maximum radiation.

**Step 3 — Verify special cases:**

- **Broadside:** $\theta_0 = 90^\circ \Rightarrow \cos\theta_0 = 0 \Rightarrow \beta = 0$ ✓ (in-phase feeding)
- **End-fire (toward +z):** $\theta_0 = 0^\circ \Rightarrow \cos\theta_0 = 1 \Rightarrow \beta = -kd$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Null Directions and Number of Side Lobes

**Step 1 — Nulls of |AF|:**

$|AF| = 0$ when $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$ but $\sin(\psi/2) \ne 0$:

$$N\psi/2 = p\pi, \quad p \text{ integer, } p \ne mN$$

$$\psi_p = \frac{2p\pi}{N}, \quad p = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots, \pm(N-1), \pm(N+1), \dots \text{ (excluding multiples of } N\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to angles:**

$$\cos\theta_p = \frac{1}{kd}\left(\frac{2p\pi}{N} - \beta\right)$$

A null is **physically realizable** only if $\vert \cos\theta_p \vert \le 1$.

**Step 3 — Number of side lobes between two principal maxima:**

Between two successive principal maxima (say $m=0$ and $m=1$), $\psi$ ranges over $2\pi$. There are $N-1$ nulls and $N-2$ secondary maxima (side lobes) in this range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Beamwidth of the Principal Maximum

**Step 1 — Locate the first null adjacent to $\psi = 0$:**

For $m=0$ principal max, the nearest null is at $p = \pm 1$:

$$\psi_{null} = \pm \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Translate to angle (assume broadside, $\beta = 0$):**

$$\psi = kd\cos\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad kd\cos\theta_{null} = \pm \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

$$\cos\theta_{null} = \pm \frac{\lambda}{Nd}$$

For large arrays ($Nd \gg \lambda$), the beam is narrow and $\theta_{null} \approx 90^\circ \pm \Delta\theta$ where $\Delta\theta$ is small. Using $\cos(90^\circ - \Delta\theta) = \sin\Delta\theta \approx \Delta\theta$:

$$\Delta\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{Nd}$$

**Step 3 — Full First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW):**

$$\text{FNBW} = 2\Delta\theta = \frac{2\lambda}{Nd} \text{ radians}$$

**Step 4 — Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW):**

The half-power point corresponds to $|AF|^2 = (N/\sqrt{2})^2$, i.e., $|AF| = N/\sqrt{2}$. Solving the sinc equation numerically gives:

$$\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{0.886\,\lambda}{Nd} \text{ radians} \approx \frac{50.6^\circ \lambda}{Nd}$$

This is the **standard textbook approximation** for the main beam of a uniformly illuminated broadside linear array.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Directivity of a Broadside Uniform Linear Array

**Step 1 — Directivity definition:**

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi \, U_{max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$$

where $\Omega_A$ is the **beam solid angle** (in steradians).

**Step 2 — For a narrow-beam broadside array:**

The beam is approximately an ellipse in $(\theta, \phi)$ space with half-power widths $\theta_{HP}$ and $\phi_{HP}$. For a linear array along z, the pattern is omnidirectional in $\phi$ and narrow in $\theta$:

$$\Omega_A \approx \theta_{HP} \cdot 2\pi \quad (\text{one dimension is narrow, the other is broad})$$

**Step 3 — Substitute HPBW:**

$$\Omega_A \approx 2\pi \cdot \frac{0.886\,\lambda}{Nd} = \frac{1.772\pi\,\lambda}{Nd}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for directivity:**

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi \, Nd}{1.772\pi\,\lambda} \approx 2.26 \cdot \frac{Nd}{\lambda}$$

Using the slightly more accurate factor (depending on element pattern), the **commonly quoted result** in KTU textbooks is:

$$\boxed{D_0 \approx 2 \cdot \frac{Nd}{\lambda}}$$

This shows that **directivity grows linearly with both N and $d/\lambda$** — this is why engineers build large arrays for high gain.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A 4-element uniform linear array with $d = \lambda/2$ has progressive phase $\beta = -90^\circ$. Find:
(a) The direction of the principal maximum.
(b) The directions of the first nulls adjacent to the main beam.
(c) The half-power beamwidth (in degrees).

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Beam-steering equation: $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$.

Given $\beta = -90^\circ = -\pi/2$ rad and $kd = (2\pi/\lambda)(\lambda/2) = \pi$ rad.

$$-\frac{\pi}{2} = -\pi \cos\theta_0 \;\Rightarrow\; \cos\theta_0 = \frac{1}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_0 = 60^\circ$$

**Step (b):** First nulls are at $\psi = \pm 2\pi/N = \pm \pi/2$:

$$kd\cos\theta_{null} + \beta = \pm \frac{\pi}{2}$$

$$\pi\cos\theta_{null} - \frac{\pi}{2} = \pm\frac{\pi}{2}$$

Solving:
- Right null: $\pi\cos\theta = \pi \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 0^\circ$
- Left null: $\pi\cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ$

**Step (c):** HPBW in $\psi$-space (large N): $\Delta\psi_{HP} \approx 0.886 \cdot 2\pi/N = 0.443\pi$ rad.

Convert to angle using $\Delta\psi = kd \, |\sin\theta| \, \Delta\theta$ near $\theta_0 = 60^\circ$:

$$\Delta\theta_{HP} \approx \frac{0.886\,\lambda}{Nd \sin\theta_0} = \frac{0.886 \cdot \lambda}{4 \cdot 0.5\lambda \cdot \sin 60^\circ} = \frac{0.886}{2 \cdot 0.866} \approx 0.512 \text{ rad} \approx 29.3^\circ$$

Total HPBW $\approx 2 \times 29.3^\circ = 58.6^\circ$. (Refinement needed for small N=4, but this gives the engineering estimate.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: N-Element ULA Architecture and AF Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: N, d, lambda, beta, A0"] --> B["Compute k = 2 pi / lambda"]
    B --> C["For each theta in 0 to 180 deg"]
    C --> D["Compute psi = k d cos theta + beta"]
    D --> E["Compute N psi / 2 and psi / 2"]
    E --> F["Evaluate |AF| = |sin N psi/2 / sin psi/2|"]
    F --> G{"Is |AF| at maximum?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Mark Principal Lobe Direction theta_0"]
    G -- "No" --> I{"Is |AF| = 0?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Mark Null Direction"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Mark Side Lobe"]
    H --> L["Output: Radiation Pattern |AF| vs theta"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **logical flow** for plotting a ULA radiation pattern. The same algorithm is used by MATLAB's `phased.LinearArray` system object and by HFSS/CST post-processors. Every block is a single line of code or a single calculation step in a KTU numerical answer.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pattern Multiplication Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["Excitation and Geometry"]
        A1["Element Pattern E_elem theta phi"]
        A2["Array Factor AF theta phi"]
    end
    A1 --> M["Multiplication: E_total = E_elem x AF"]
    A2 --> M
    M --> O["Total Array Pattern E_total theta phi"]
    O --> P1["Beamwidth HPBW"]
    O --> P2["Side Lobe Level SLL"]
    O --> P3["Directivity D0"]
    O --> P4["Null Directions"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Pattern Multiplication** theorem is the cornerstone of array analysis. It states that the total field of an array of identical elements is the **product** of the single-element pattern and the array factor. This is *not* a sum — it is a product, evaluated point-by-point at every angle.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Linear Array Physical Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["Element 0<br/>A0 phase 0"] -->|"d"| N1["Element 1<br/>A0 phase beta"]
    N1 -->|"d"| N2["Element 2<br/>A0 phase 2 beta"]
    N2 -->|"d"| N3["Element 3<br/>A0 phase 3 beta"]
    N3 -->|"d"| N4N["Element N-1<br/>A0 phase N-1 beta"]
    N0 -.->|"Far-field<br/>observation<br/>point P at angle theta"| OBS["Observer at angle theta from array axis"]
    N1 -.-> OBS
    N2 -.-> OBS
    N3 -.-> OBS
    N4N -.-> OBS
```

> [!NOTE]
> The progressive phase shift $\beta$ between elements is the *control knob* for beam steering. By changing $\beta$ electronically (using phase shifters), the beam can be swept through space without physically rotating the array — this is the defining feature of **phased-array antennas** used in modern radar (AESA — Active Electronically Scanned Array).

---

## 4.4 Concept Map: Array Factor Knowledge Graph

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Array Factor AF))
    Variables
      N Number of elements
      d Inter element spacing
      lambda Wavelength
      beta Progressive phase
      A0 Element amplitude
    Derived Quantities
      psi Array phase variable
      theta_0 Beam direction
      HPBW Half power beamwidth
      D0 Directivity
      SLL Side lobe level
    Array Types
      Broadside beta 0
      End fire beta kd
      Hansen Woodyard
      Phased Array
    Constraints
      Grating lobe free
      Visible space condition
      Uniform amplitude
    Key Equations
      Closed form sin N psi 2 over sin psi 2
      Beam steering beta kd cos theta 0
      Null condition
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Define Array Factor. For a two-element array with equal amplitudes and a progressive phase shift of $\beta$, derive the expression for the Array Factor.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Array Factor (AF)** is the far-field radiation pattern of an array of isotropic sources, evaluated at the spatial positions and complex excitations of the actual array elements. It represents the *spatial and excitation* contribution of the array, isolated from the individual element's own pattern.

For a 2-element array with elements at $z_0 = 0$ and $z_1 = d$, amplitudes $A_0$, $A_0$ and phases $0$, $\beta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AF &= A_0 e^{j(0)} + A_0 e^{j(kd\cos\theta + \beta)} \\
&= A_0 \left[1 + e^{j\psi}\right], \quad \psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta \\
&= A_0 \cdot e^{j\psi/2}\left[e^{-j\psi/2} + e^{j\psi/2}\right] \\
&= A_0 \cdot e^{j\psi/2} \cdot 2\cos(\psi/2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitude: $\vert AF \vert = 2A_0 \left\vert \cos(\psi/2) \right\vert$.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for derivation)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between broadside and end-fire arrays. What is the condition for the progressive phase shift $\beta$ in each case?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Broadside Array | End-Fire Array |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of max radiation | Perpendicular to array axis ($\theta_0 = 90^\circ$) | Along the array axis ($\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$) |
| Progressive phase $\beta$ | $\beta = 0$ (all elements in phase) | $\beta = -kd$ (for $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$) or $\beta = +kd$ (for $\theta_0 = 180^\circ$) |
| Physical realization | Feed network with equal-length lines | Feed network with progressive line lengths of $\lambda/(2\pi) \cdot \beta$ |
| Beam steering | Steer by changing $\beta$ from 0 | Steer by changing $\beta$ away from $\pm kd$ |

**(3 marks: 2 for distinction table, 1 for conditions)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Modified from Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the array factor expression for an N-element uniform linear array with equal amplitude $A_0$ and progressive phase shift $\beta$. Show that:

$$|AF| = A_0 \left|\frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}\right|$$

where $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-element uniform linear array operates at 3 GHz with inter-element spacing $d = \lambda/2$. The progressive phase shift is $\beta = -kd\cos(60^\circ)$. Determine:
   (i) The direction of the principal maximum.
   (ii) The directions of the first two nulls on either side of the main beam.
   (iii) The approximate HPBW in degrees.
   (iv) Whether grating lobes are present. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

[See **Section 3.1** for the complete derivation. The student should reproduce the following logical chain:]

1. Place N elements at $z_n = nd$, $n=0,1,...,N-1$, fed with $A_0 e^{jn\beta}$.
2. Element $n$ contributes $A_0 e^{jn(kd\cos\theta + \beta)} = A_0 e^{jn\psi}$.
3. Sum the N phasors: $AF = A_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{jn\psi}$.
4. Apply geometric series formula.
5. Convert to trigonometric form using $1 - e^{j\phi} = -2j e^{j\phi/2}\sin(\phi/2)$.
6. Arrive at $|AF| = A_0 \left|\sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)\right|$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 3$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 0.1$ m, $d = \lambda/2 = 0.05$ m, $N = 10$, $\beta = -kd\cos 60^\circ$.

$$kd = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = \pi \text{ rad}$$

$$\beta = -\pi \cdot 0.5 = -\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} = -90^\circ$$

**(i) Principal max direction:** Beam-steering equation:
$$\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0 \Rightarrow -\frac{\pi}{2} = -\pi\cos\theta_0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta_0 = 0.5 \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 60^\circ$$

**(ii) First nulls:** $\psi_{null} = 2\pi/N = 2\pi/10 = \pi/5 = 36^\circ$.

$$kd\cos\theta_{null} + \beta = \pm \frac{\pi}{5}$$

$$\pi\cos\theta_{null} - \frac{\pi}{2} = \pm \frac{\pi}{5}$$

For $+ \pi/5$: $\pi\cos\theta = \pi/2 + \pi/5 = 7\pi/10 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0.7 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 45.57^\circ$.

For $- \pi/5$: $\pi\cos\theta = \pi/2 - \pi/5 = 3\pi/10 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0.3 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 72.54^\circ$.

So first nulls are at **$\theta \approx 45.57^\circ$** and **$\theta \approx 72.54^\circ$**.

**(iii) HPBW:** Using the broadside formula and scaling by $\sin\theta_0$:

$$\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{0.886\lambda}{Nd\sin\theta_0} = \frac{0.886 \times 0.1}{10 \times 0.05 \times \sin 60^\circ} = \frac{0.0886}{0.5 \times 0.866} \approx 0.2047 \text{ rad} \approx 11.73^\circ$$

**(iv) Grating lobes?** Grating-lobe-free condition: $d/\lambda \le 1/(1 + |\cos\theta_0|)$:

$$\frac{1}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.667$$

Given $d/\lambda = 0.5 < 0.667$. **No grating lobes are present.** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Modified from May 2024]**

**(a)** For a uniform linear array, derive the conditions for:
   (i) Principal maximum
   (ii) Nulls
   (iii) Secondary maxima
   (iv) The first-null beamwidth (FNBW). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a 6-element broadside uniform linear array operating at 10 GHz. The array must have:
   (i) Maximum possible spacing $d$ without producing grating lobes.
   (ii) Calculate the progressive phase $\beta$.
   (iii) HPBW in degrees.
   (iv) Approximate directivity (in dB).
   Take $A_0 = 1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**(i) Principal Maximum:**
$|AF|$ is maximum when $\psi = \pm 2m\pi$ (m = 0, 1, 2, ...). For m = 0, the main beam condition is:
$$\psi = 0 \Rightarrow kd\cos\theta_0 + \beta = 0 \Rightarrow \beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$$
Maximum value of $|AF| = N \cdot A_0$ (by L'Hôpital's rule).

**(ii) Nulls:**
$|AF| = 0$ when $\sin(N\psi/2) = 0$ but $\sin(\psi/2) \ne 0$:
$$\psi = \frac{2p\pi}{N}, \quad p = \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots, \pm(N-1), \pm(N+1), \dots$$
(Excluding multiples of N, which give the principal maxima.)

**(iii) Secondary Maxima:**
Between two consecutive nulls there lies one secondary maximum. The approximate location of the first side lobe is at $\psi \approx \pm 3\pi/N$ (for large N). The first side-lobe level (for uniform amplitude) is:
$$\text{SLL} = -13.2 \text{ dB} \text{ (i.e., } \approx 1/3 \text{ of main lobe)}$$

**(iv) First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW):**
The first null on either side of the main beam is at $\psi = \pm 2\pi/N$. For a broadside array ($\beta = 0$), $\psi = kd\cos\theta$:
$$kd\cos\theta_{null} = \pm \frac{2\pi}{N} \Rightarrow \cos\theta_{null} = \pm \frac{\lambda}{Nd}$$

For $Nd \gg \lambda$, $\theta_{null} \approx 90^\circ \pm \lambda/(Nd)$:
$$\boxed{\text{FNBW} = 2\Delta\theta = \frac{2\lambda}{Nd} \text{ radians}}$$

**(b) Design Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $N = 6$, $f = 10$ GHz, broadside ($\beta = 0$).

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 3\times 10^8/10^{10} = 0.03$ m = 3 cm.

**(i) Maximum spacing for broadside (no grating lobes):**
For broadside, $d/\lambda \le 1 \Rightarrow d_{max} = \lambda = 3$ cm. (Strictly, $d < \lambda$; we take $d = \lambda$ as the limit.)

Choose $d = 0.9\lambda = 2.7$ cm for a safe margin (or use $d = \lambda = 3$ cm if the problem allows equality).

**(ii) Progressive phase:** $\beta = 0$ (broadside, all elements in phase).

**(iii) HPBW:**
$$\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{0.886\lambda}{Nd} = \frac{0.886 \times 0.03}{6 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.886}{6} = 0.1477 \text{ rad} \approx 8.46^\circ$$

**(iv) Approximate Directivity:**
$$D_0 \approx 2 \cdot \frac{Nd}{\lambda} = 2 \times 6 \times 1 = 12 \text{ (linear)}$$

In dB: $D_0 \text{(dB)} = 10\log_{10}(12) \approx 10.79 \text{ dB}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Array Factor questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the magnitude absolute value** — Students write $\sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)$ without the outer $\vert \cdot \vert$. A radiation pattern is always non-negative; KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Mixing up degrees and radians** — $\beta$ in the feed network and $kd$ in the wavenumber are in *radians*, but the direction $\theta$ is in *degrees* on your final plot. Convert consistently or risk a unit-error deduction of 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Applying the broadside formula ($\beta = 0$) to a general $\beta \ne 0$ problem** — The formula $\cos\theta_0 = -\beta/(kd)$ must be derived fresh, not memorized for broadside only.
> 4. **Confusing FNBW and HPBW** — FNBW is the angle between first nulls (full width); HPBW is between half-power points. They are related by FNBW $\approx 2 \times$ HPBW (approximate).
> 5. **Ignoring the visibility check** — When asked for a null at $\theta_p$, you *must* check $\vert \cos\theta_p \vert \le 1$. A null that falls outside $[0, 180^\circ]$ is **not physical** and is worth 0 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the $e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$ phase term** in the complex AF — Even though it doesn't affect magnitude, the *total field* in the pattern multiplication theorem requires the complex form. KTU part (a) questions on derivation of complex AF carry 2-3 marks for this term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Array Factor — Final Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- [ ] **Definition**: AF = far-field pattern of array with isotropic elements at the actual positions and excitations.
- [ ] **Pattern Multiplication**: $E_{total} = E_{element} \times AF$ (this is THE theorem).
- [ ] **ULA Setup**: N elements along z-axis, $z_n = nd$, amplitude $A_0$, phase $n\beta$.
- [ ] **Master Variable**: $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$ — encodes *all* array behavior.
- [ ] **AF Magnitude**: $\vert AF \vert = A_0 \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$ — memorize this verbatim.
- [ ] **Principal Max**: $\psi = \pm 2m\pi$ → $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$ (beam steering).
- [ ] **Nulls**: $\psi = 2p\pi/N$ (with $p \ne mN$); check visibility $\vert \cos\theta_p \vert \le 1$.
- [ ] **Side Lobes**: Number between two principal maxima = $N-2$; first SLL $\approx -13.2$ dB.
- [ ] **Broadside**: $\beta = 0$, $\theta_0 = 90^\circ$.
- [ ] **Ordinary End-Fire**: $\beta = \mp kd$, $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$.
- [ ] **Hansen-Woodyard**: $\beta = -kd - \pi/N$ (enhanced end-fire, ~3 dB more directivity).
- [ ] **Grating-Lobe-Free**: $d/\lambda \le 1/(1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert)$.
- [ ] **FNBW**: $\approx 2\lambda/(Nd)$ rad.
- [ ] **HPBW**: $\approx 0.886\lambda/(Nd)$ rad (broadside) — divide by $\sin\theta_0$ for steered beams.
- [ ] **Directivity** (broadside ULA): $D_0 \approx 2Nd/\lambda$ (linear).
- [ ] **Wavenumber**: $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ — always check units (rad/m if $\lambda$ in m).
- [ ] **Numerical tip**: For a phased array steered to $\theta_0 \ne 90^\circ$, always include the $\sin\theta_0$ factor in HPBW to account for the projected aperture.
- [ ] **Common KTU trap**: A 2-element AF gives $2A_0 \cos(\psi/2)$ (not a sinc — that is for large N).

> **End of Module 4 — Array Factor Notes.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Arrays of 'n' Isotropic Point Sources with Equal Amplitude
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Arrays of 'n' Isotropic Point Sources with Equal Amplitude

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Array (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **linear antenna array** is a systematic arrangement of **n identical radiating elements** (typically isotropic point sources) positioned along a **straight line** (collinear axis), with **uniform inter-element spacing `d`** between adjacent elements, and excited by currents of **equal amplitude** but with a **uniform progressive phase shift `α`** between successive elements.

**Isotropic Point Source:** A hypothetical, lossless, ideal radiator that radiates electromagnetic energy **uniformly in all directions** with a spherical radiation pattern. It serves as the fundamental building block (reference antenna) for all array theory in KTU examinations.

**Field from a single isotropic source** at distance `r`:
$$E_{single} = \frac{I_0 \, e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = current amplitude (constant)
- $\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ = phase constant
- $r$ = radial distance from source

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wave Train" Intuition

Imagine you are at a **stadium wave demonstration** (like the "Mexican wave"). A single person clapping once produces a small sound. But when **100 people** clapping with the **same strength** but with a **tiny, carefully timed delay** (progressive phase) between each person, you get a powerful, focused sound wave that travels in a single direction.

**Key Mapping:**
| Stadium Wave | Antenna Array |
|---|---|
| Each person clapping | Each isotropic radiator |
| Equal clapping loudness | Equal amplitude excitation ($A_0$) |
| Delay between claps | Progressive phase shift ($\alpha$) |
| Distance between people | Spacing ($d$) |
| Direction the wave travels | Beam steering direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The genius of arrays is that the **individual elements are weak (isotropic)**, but by clever **phasing**, the combined radiation focuses into a narrow, high-gain beam — this is called **constructive interference** in a chosen direction and **destructive interference** in all other directions.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Free-space wavelength:** $\lambda$ (meters) — constant for the operating frequency
- **Phase constant:** $\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ (radians/meter)
- **Inter-element spacing:** $d$ (commonly expressed in fractions of $\lambda$, e.g., $d = 0.5\lambda$)
- **Progressive phase shift between adjacent elements:** $\alpha$ (radians)
- **Angular position of observation point:** $\theta$ (measured from broadside direction)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Phasor Logic of Array Combination

Let us consider **n isotropic point sources** placed along the **z-axis**, with **element 0** at the origin and the **k-th element** at position $z_k = k \cdot d$ (where $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1$).

**Why does positioning matter?**
- For an observer at far-field angle $\theta$ (from broadside, i.e., from the x-axis in standard convention), the **path difference** between element $k$ and element $0$ is $\Delta r_k = k d \cos\theta$.
- The corresponding **path phase difference** is $\beta \cdot k d \cos\theta = k \cdot \psi_r$ where $\psi_r = \beta d \cos\theta$ is the *spatial phase difference*.
- The **progressive phase excitation** adds an extra $k\alpha$ to the element $k$.
- So the **total phase** of element $k$ is $k(\psi_r + \alpha) = k\psi$.

**Therefore, the total electric field of the n-element array is:**

$$E_{total}(\theta) = E_0 \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{j k \psi} = E_0 \left(1 + e^{j\psi} + e^{j2\psi} + \ldots + e^{j(n-1)\psi}\right)$$

where the **generalized phase term** is:
$$\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$$

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the 10 most important KTU-board-tested formulas for this topic. Memorize and practice each one.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notation & Units** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generalized phase variable | $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ | $\beta$ rad/m, $d$ m, $\theta$ rad, $\alpha$ rad |
| 2 | Array Factor (Geometric Series Sum) | $AF(\psi) = \dfrac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Total Array Field | $E(\theta) = E_0 \cdot AF(\psi)$ | V/m |
| 4 | Magnitude of Array Factor | $\vert AF \vert = \left\vert \dfrac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Principal Maximum Condition | $\psi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \ldots$ | Radians |
| 6 | Null (Zero) Condition | $\psi = \dfrac{\pm 2m\pi}{n},\ m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (excluding integer multiples of $2\pi$) | Radians |
| 7 | Broadside Array Condition | $\alpha = 0$ | Max at $\theta = 90°$ |
| 8 | End-Fire Array Condition | $\alpha = -\beta d$ | Max at $\theta = 0°$ or $\theta = 180°$ |
| 9 | Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW) | $HPBW \approx \dfrac{0.886 \lambda}{n d \sin\theta_0}$ | Radians |
| 10 | Directivity of Broadside Array | $D \approx \dfrac{2n d}{\lambda}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on Formula #2 (CRITICAL):** The array factor formula $\frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$ is derived from a **finite geometric series** $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{jk\psi} = \frac{1 - e^{jn\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}$. The numerator is simplified using Euler's identity: $1 - e^{jn\psi} = 2j e^{jn\psi/2} \sin(n\psi/2)$ and the denominator is $1 - e^{j\psi} = 2j e^{j\psi/2} \sin(\psi/2)$.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear arrays with equal amplitude excitation form the backbone of:
- **Radar systems** (phased array radars — e.g., Akash missile radar, AN/TPY-2)
- **5G/6G base station antennas** (massive MIMO arrays)
- **Satellite communication** (uplink/downlink beam steering)
- **Radio astronomy** (VLA — Very Large Array at Socorro, New Mexico)
- **Smart antennas** in mobile handsets (adaptive beamforming)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Equal Amplitude" assumption** is what makes the array factor become a simple closed-form Dirichlet kernel ($\frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$). For tapered (non-uniform) amplitude arrays, Bessel function or numerical integration is required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Array Factor of an n-Element Linear Array (The Heart of This Topic)

**Problem Setup:** Consider `n` isotropic point sources located along the z-axis with:
- Uniform spacing $d$ between consecutive elements
- Equal amplitude $A_0$ (no tapering)
- Progressive phase shift $\alpha$ between consecutive elements
- Element 0 at origin, element $k$ at $z_k = kd$
- Far-field observation point at angle $\theta$ from the broadside (x-axis)

**Reference Diagram Setup:**
```
Element positions:  0   d   2d   3d  ...  (n-1)d     [along z-axis]
Observation point P at (r, θ, 0)   [in far field]
Broadside direction: +x axis
End-fire directions: +z and -z
```

---

**STEP 1: Field from a single isotropic element at origin**

For an isotropic radiator of amplitude $A_0$, the far-field electric field at distance $r$ is:
$$E_0(r) = A_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r}$$

where $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the free-space wave number.

---

**STEP 2: Field from the k-th element**

The k-th element is located at $z_k = kd$. The distance from this element to the far-field point P is approximately (using the **far-field parallel-ray approximation**):
$$r_k \approx r - kd \cos\theta$$

**Reasoning:** In the far field, the rays from all elements to P are nearly parallel. The extra path traveled by the ray from element 0 to reach element k's position is the projection $kd\cos\theta$.

The amplitude $1/r_k \approx 1/r$ (negligible amplitude variation in the far field), but the **phase** changes significantly.

So the field from the k-th element is:
$$E_k(r,\theta) = A_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-j\beta (r - kd\cos\theta)}}{r} = A_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r} \cdot e^{+j\beta kd\cos\theta}$$

Additionally, the **progressive phase excitation** adds $k\alpha$ to the k-th element's current phase. So the k-th element's amplitude becomes:
$$A_k = A_0 \cdot e^{jk\alpha}$$

Combining both effects (spatial path phase + excitation phase):
$$E_k(r,\theta) = A_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r} \cdot e^{j k (\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)}$$

---

**STEP 3: Total field by superposition principle**

By the principle of **linear superposition** of fields (valid for isotropic radiators in linear media):
$$E_{total}(r,\theta) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} E_k(r,\theta)$$

$$E_{total}(r,\theta) = A_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{jk(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)}$$

**Factor out the common exponential term** (representing a single element's pattern):
$$E_{total}(r,\theta) = E_0 \cdot \underbrace{\left[\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{jk\psi}\right]}_{Array\ Factor}$$

where we define:
- $E_0 = A_0 \cdot \frac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r}$ (single element field)
- $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ (generalized phase difference between adjacent elements)

---

**STEP 4: Evaluate the geometric series (the array factor)**

The sum $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{jk\psi}$ is a **finite geometric series** with first term $1$, common ratio $e^{j\psi}$, and $n$ terms.

Using the geometric series formula:
$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} x^k = \frac{1 - x^n}{1 - x}$$

with $x = e^{j\psi}$:
$$AF(\psi) = \frac{1 - e^{jn\psi}}{1 - e^{j\psi}}$$

---

**STEP 5: Simplify numerator and denominator using Euler's formula**

**Numerator simplification:**
$$1 - e^{jn\psi} = 1 - \cos(n\psi) - j\sin(n\psi)$$

Using the half-angle identity $1 - \cos(n\psi) = 2\sin^2(n\psi/2)$ and $\sin(n\psi) = 2\sin(n\psi/2)\cos(n\psi/2)$:
$$1 - e^{jn\psi} = 2\sin^2(n\psi/2) - j \cdot 2\sin(n\psi/2)\cos(n\psi/2)$$

Factor out $2j\sin(n\psi/2)$:
$$1 - e^{jn\psi} = 2j\sin(n\psi/2)\left[\frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{j} - \cos(n\psi/2)\right]$$

A cleaner form using the exponential identity:
$$1 - e^{jn\psi} = e^{jn\psi/2}(e^{-jn\psi/2} - e^{jn\psi/2}) = -2j \, e^{jn\psi/2} \sin(n\psi/2)$$

So:
$$1 - e^{jn\psi} = -2j \, e^{jn\psi/2} \sin(n\psi/2)$$

**Denominator simplification** (same logic):
$$1 - e^{j\psi} = -2j \, e^{j\psi/2} \sin(\psi/2)$$

---

**STEP 6: Final expression for the array factor**

Dividing numerator by denominator:
$$AF(\psi) = \frac{-2j \, e^{jn\psi/2} \sin(n\psi/2)}{-2j \, e^{j\psi/2} \sin(\psi/2)}$$

The $-2j$ factors cancel:
$$AF(\psi) = e^{j(n-1)\psi/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$$

**Taking the magnitude** (since $e^{j(n-1)\psi/2}$ has unit magnitude):
$$\boxed{\;\vert AF(\psi) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert\;}$$

This is the famous **Dirichlet kernel** form. The total field is:
$$\boxed{\;E_{total}(\theta) = E_0 \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{n}{2}(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)\right)}{\sin\!\left(\frac{1}{2}(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)\right)}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Conditions for Principal Maximum

The **principal maximum** is the direction where the array factor reaches its absolute maximum value of $n$.

**Mathematical condition:** The denominator $\sin(\psi/2) \to 0$ AND the numerator $\sin(n\psi/2) \to 0$ simultaneously, and the ratio $\to n$ (by L'Hôpital's rule).

This requires:
$$\frac{\psi}{2} = m\pi, \quad \text{where } m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

Equivalently:
$$\psi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \ldots$$

Since $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$, for $m = 0$:
$$\beta d \cos\theta_0 + \alpha = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\cos\theta_0 = -\frac{\alpha}{\beta d}\;}$$

**Special cases:**

**Case A: Broadside Array ($\alpha = 0$)**
$$\cos\theta_0 = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 90° \text{ (broadside direction)}$$

**Case B: End-Fire Array ($\alpha = -\beta d$)**
$$\cos\theta_0 = \frac{\beta d}{\beta d} = 1 \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 0° \text{ (end-fire direction)}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Condition for Nulls (Minima)

The array factor equals zero when the numerator is zero but the denominator is not. This gives:
$$\sin(n\psi/2) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sin(\psi/2) \neq 0$$

The numerator vanishes when:
$$\frac{n\psi}{2} = \pm p\pi \quad (p = 1, 2, 3, \ldots)$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\;\psi = \frac{\pm 2p\pi}{n}\;}$$

**Exclusion:** We must exclude those values of $p$ that would also make the denominator zero (i.e., exclude $p = n, 2n, 3n, \ldots$ because those correspond to principal maxima).

**First nulls** (most important for HPBW calculation): $\psi = \pm 2\pi/n$

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Half-Power Beam Width (HPBW)

The HPBW is defined as the angular separation between the two half-power points, where the array factor magnitude is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its maximum value $n$:

$$\left|\frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}\right| = \frac{n}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Approximation method** (assuming $\psi$ is small near the main beam, so $\sin(\psi/2) \approx \psi/2$):

$$\left|\frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\psi/2}\right| = \frac{n}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This is the standard sinc function $\frac{\sin(u)}{u} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ where $u = n\psi/2$.

Solving numerically: $u_{3dB} = 1.3916$ radians (this is a well-known constant).

So:
$$\frac{n\psi_{3dB}}{2} = 1.3916 \Rightarrow \psi_{3dB} = \frac{2.7832}{n}$$

The total HPBW is **twice** this (between +3dB and -3dB points):
$$\Delta\psi_{3dB} = \frac{5.567}{n} \approx \frac{0.886 \cdot 2\pi}{n}$$

Converting to angular beamwidth in the $\theta$ domain for a broadside array ($\alpha = 0$, so $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta$):

$$\boxed{\;HPBW \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{n d \sin\theta_0}\;}\quad \text{(radians)}$$

where $\theta_0$ is the direction of the principal maximum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example for KTU:** For a broadside array with $n=4$ and $d = 0.5\lambda$, the HPBW $\approx \frac{0.886}{4 \times 0.5 \times 1} = 0.443$ radians $\approx 25.4°$.

---

### 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Directivity of Broadside Array

Directivity is the ratio of maximum radiation intensity to average radiation intensity.

For a long broadside array (large $n$), the **solid beamwidth** in both $\theta$ and $\phi$ directions is approximately:
$$\Omega_A \approx HPBW_\theta \times HPBW_\phi$$

For a linear array (no $\phi$ variation), the beam is omnidirectional in the $\phi$ plane (rotational symmetry around the array axis). So:
$$\Omega_A \approx 2\pi \times HPBW$$

Directivity:
$$D = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{2\pi \times HPBW} = \frac{2}{HPBW}$$

Substituting:
$$\boxed{\;D \approx \frac{2 \cdot n d}{0.886 \lambda} \approx \frac{2.26 \, n d}{\lambda}\;}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of n-Element Linear Array Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    source[Current Source I0] --> splitter[1 to n Power Splitter with Phase Shift Network]
    splitter --> e0[Element 0: Phase 0 deg, Position 0]
    splitter --> e1[Element 1: Phase alpha, Position d]
    splitter --> e2[Element 2: Phase 2 alpha, Position 2d]
    splitter --> e3[Element 3: Phase 3 alpha, Position 3d]
    splitter --> eN[Element n-1: Phase n-1 times alpha, Position n-1 times d]
    
    e0 --> path0[Path Phase Shift: beta times 0 times d cos theta]
    e1 --> path1[Path Phase Shift: beta times d cos theta]
    e2 --> path2[Path Phase Shift: 2 beta d cos theta]
    e3 --> path3[Path Phase Shift: 3 beta d cos theta]
    eN --> pathN[Path Phase Shift: n-1 times beta d cos theta]
    
    path0 --> sum[Vector Sum at Far Field Point P]
    path1 --> sum
    path2 --> sum
    path3 --> sum
    pathN --> sum
    
    sum --> af[Array Factor AF of psi]
    af --> pattern[Normalized Radiation Pattern E of theta]
    
    pattern --> broadside{Broadside if alpha equals 0}
    pattern --> endfire{End Fire if alpha equals minus beta d}
    
    broadside --> maxbroad[Principal Max at theta equals 90 deg]
    endfire --> maxend[Principal Max at theta equals 0 or 180 deg]
    
    style source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style splitter fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    style sum fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px
    style pattern fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px
    style af fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Array Factor vs. ψ Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    psiNeg[psi negative region] --> main[Main Lobe Peak at psi equals 0]
    main --> sidelobe1[First Sidelobe at psi equals 3 pi over n]
    sidelobe1 --> firstNull[First Null at psi equals 2 pi over n]
    firstNull --> sidelobe2[Second Sidelobe]
    sidelobe2 --> secondNull[Second Null at psi equals 4 pi over n]
    secondNull --> sidelobe3[Third Sidelobe]
    sidelobe3 --> psiPos[psi positive region]
    
    main -. Max Value equals n .-> AFmax[AF equals n at Principal Max]
    firstNull -. AF equals 0 .-> AFnull[AF equals 0 at Nulls]
    sidelobe1 -. AF equals 22.5 dB down .-> AFsl[AF equals approx 0.212 n]
    
    style main fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:3px
    style firstNull fill:#000000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    style AFmax fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Text-Based Radiation Pattern Visualization (for n=4, d=0.5λ, broadside)

```
         E(theta)  Plot for 4-Element Broadside Array
         |
    1.0  |              MAIN LOBE
         |             /    \
    0.8  |            /      \
         |           /        \
    0.6  |          /          \
         |         /            \
    0.4  |        /    SIDE      \
         |       /    LOBES       \
    0.2  |      /                  \
         |     /                    \
    0.0  +----+----+----+----+----+----+----+----+---> theta (deg)
         0   30   60   90  120  150  180

         theta = 90 deg  = Principal Max (Broadside)
         HPBW approx 25 deg
         Sidelobe level approx -13.2 dB (for uniform array)
```
