<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define an isotropic radiator. Why is it used as a reference element in antenna array analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- An **isotropic radiator** is an ideal, hypothetical point source that radiates electromagnetic energy **equally in all directions** in 3D space, producing a perfect spherical wavefront. (1 mark)
- It has a **gain of unity (0 dB)** and **directivity of 1**. (1 mark)
- It is used as a reference in array theory because **the radiation pattern of any practical array can be obtained by multiplying the element pattern with the array factor (Pattern Multiplication Theorem)**, and the array factor is computed assuming isotropic elements. This greatly simplifies the analysis. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the "Pattern Multiplication" theorem. Write the array factor expression for a 4-element linear array with uniform spacing `d` and progressive phase shift `α`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pattern Multiplication Theorem:** The total radiation pattern of an array of identical elements is the **product** of the **element pattern** (pattern of a single element) and the **array factor** (pattern of the array of isotropic point sources). (1.5 marks)
- Mathematically: $E_{total}(\theta, \phi) = E_{element}(\theta, \phi) \times AF(\theta, \phi)$ (0.5 marks)
- For a 4-element linear array:
$$AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin\!\left[2(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)\right]}{\sin\!\left[\frac{1}{2}(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)\right]}$$
(1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **array factor** of an *n*-element linear array with **equal amplitude and uniform spacing**. Define all variables used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-element broadside linear array with inter-element spacing $d = 0.5\lambda$:
   - (i) Find the directions of principal maxima, nulls, and first sidelobes. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Calculate the **HPBW** in degrees. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) What is the maximum value of the array factor? **(2 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question A):**

**(a) Derivation of Array Factor (7 marks):**

The detailed step-by-step derivation from **Section 3.1 above** should be reproduced in the answer booklet. Key checkpoints for full 7 marks:

| **Step** | **Concept** | **Marks** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Setup: n elements at positions $z_k = kd$ along z-axis | 1 |
| 2 | Field from k-th element with spatial + excitation phase: $E_k = A_0 \frac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r} e^{jk(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)}$ | 1.5 |
| 3 | Superposition to obtain total field with AF inside bracket | 1 |
| 4 | Recognize AF as finite geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{jk\psi}$ | 1 |
| 5 | Apply geometric series formula $\frac{1-e^{jn\psi}}{1-e^{j\psi}}$ | 1 |
| 6 | Euler simplification: numerator $= -2j e^{jn\psi/2}\sin(n\psi/2)$ and denominator $= -2j e^{j\psi/2}\sin(\psi/2)$ | 1 |
| 7 | Final expression $\vert AF \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$ with definition $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ | 0.5 |

**(b) Solution for 4-element broadside array (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 4$, $d = 0.5\lambda$, $\alpha = 0$ (broadside)

Therefore: $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot 0.5\lambda \cdot \cos\theta = \pi \cos\theta$

**(i) Principal Maxima:** Set $\psi = 0 \Rightarrow \pi \cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0$
$$\theta_{max} = 90° \text{ (broadside direction)}$$
Maximum value: $AF_{max} = n = 4$ (1 mark)

**Nulls:** Set $n\psi/2 = \pm p\pi \Rightarrow 2\pi \cos\theta = \pm p\pi \Rightarrow \cos\theta = \pm p/2$

For $p = 1$: $\cos\theta = \pm 0.5 \Rightarrow \theta = 60°, 120°$ (1 mark)
For $p = 2$: $\cos\theta = \pm 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 0°, 180°$ (these correspond to grating lobes, since $d = 0.5\lambda$ allows grating lobes at end-fire)

**First Sidelobes:** Located approximately midway between nulls, occurring where $\sin(n\psi/2) = 1$ (local max). For $n = 4$, first sidelobe is at $\psi = 3\pi/2$ giving sidelobe level $= 0.212$ of main lobe $= -13.2$ dB. (1 mark)

**(ii) HPBW calculation:**
$$HPBW = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{n d \sin\theta_0} = \frac{0.886}{4 \times 0.5 \times 1} = 0.443 \text{ rad}$$
$$HPBW = 0.443 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = 25.4°$$
(2 marks)

**(iii) Maximum AF value:**
$$\boxed{AF_{max} = n = 4}$$
(2 marks)

---

#### **QUESTION B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear **block diagram**, explain the formation of the radiation pattern of an n-element linear array. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-element linear array has inter-element spacing $d = 0.5\lambda$ and progressive phase shift $\alpha = -90°$. Determine:
   - (i) Whether the array is broadside or end-fire. **(2 marks)**
   - (ii) The directions of principal maxima. **(3 marks)**
   - (iii) The nulls adjacent to the principal maximum. **(2 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (Question B):**

**(a) Block diagram of n-element linear array formation (7 marks):**

The student should draw and label:
- The n isotropic radiators along a straight line (1 mark)
- The feed network with progressive phase shifters (1.5 marks)
- The far-field observation point and the parallel-ray approximation (1.5 marks)
- Vector addition phasor diagram at the observation point (1.5 marks)
- Conclusion: Constructive interference in desired direction, destructive in others (1.5 marks)

**Phasor Explanation:** The phasor of element $k$ has phase $k\psi$. For the principal maximum, all phasors align in the same direction. For nulls, they form a closed polygon (sum = 0). This is the geometric interpretation of the AF formula.

**(b) Solution for 6-element array (7 marks):**

Given: $n = 6$, $d = 0.5\lambda$, $\alpha = -90° = -\pi/2$ rad

$\beta d = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \times 0.5\lambda = \pi$ rad

The generalized phase: $\psi = \pi \cos\theta - \pi/2$

**(i) Type of array (2 marks):**
For an end-fire array, condition is $\alpha = -\beta d$
Here, $\alpha = -\pi/2$ and $-\beta d = -\pi$
Since $\alpha \neq -\beta d$, this is **neither pure broadside nor pure end-fire**, but an **intermediate** or **scanned** array.
Principal max direction: $\psi = 0 \Rightarrow \pi \cos\theta - \pi/2 = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1/2 \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 60°$
(2 marks)

**(ii) Directions of principal maxima (3 marks):**
The principal maxima occur for $\psi = \pm 2m\pi$, $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

For $m = 0$: $\pi \cos\theta - \pi/2 = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1/2 \Rightarrow \theta = 60°$ and $\theta = 300°$ (1.5 marks)
For $m = 1$: $\pi \cos\theta - \pi/2 = 2\pi \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 5/2$ → **no real solution** (no grating lobe in visible space) (1 mark)
For $m = -1$: $\pi \cos\theta - \pi/2 = -2\pi \Rightarrow \cos\theta = -3/2$ → no real solution (0.5 marks)

So only **one** principal maximum at $\theta = 60°$.

**(iii) First nulls (2 marks):**
First null condition: $\psi = \pm 2\pi/n = \pm 2\pi/6 = \pm \pi/3$

For $\psi = +\pi/3$: $\pi \cos\theta - \pi/2 = \pi/3 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 5/6 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 33.6°$ (1 mark)
For $\psi = -\pi/3$: $\pi \cos\theta - \pi/2 = -\pi/3 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 1/6 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 80.4°$ (1 mark)

So the **first nulls** are at $\theta \approx 33.6°$ and $\theta \approx 80.4°$.

**HPBW** $= 80.4° - 33.6° = 46.8°$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deduction in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the magnitude bars** on the array factor: The expression $\frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$ can be **negative**; the radiation pattern is the **magnitude** of this. Many students write AF without the absolute value, losing 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\psi$ with $\beta d$:** $\psi$ is the *total* phase difference (path + excitation), while $\beta d$ is only the spatial component. Many students set $\psi = \beta d$, missing the $\alpha$ term, which is a fatal error for end-fire arrays.
>
> 3. **Not specifying the m-value in null conditions:** Always state the allowed values of $m$ (or $p$) explicitly. Forgetting "where m = 1, 2, ... excluding integer multiples of n" loses 0.5 mark.
>
> 4. **Angle unit confusion:** The condition $\cos\theta_0 = -\alpha/\beta d$ gives an angle in **radians**, but the final answer in KTU is often expected in **degrees**. Convert: $\theta(°) = \theta(rad) \times 180/\pi$.
>
> 5. **Sidelobe level miscalculation:** First sidelobe level for uniform array is fixed at **-13.2 dB** (= 0.212 of main lobe). Students who write "-13 dB" without justification lose marks; show the calculation: $20\log_{10}(1/4.7) = -13.2$ dB.
>
> 6. **Pattern Multiplication confusion:** Element pattern $\times$ Array Factor = Total Pattern — this is valid only if all elements are **identical**. If they differ, the theorem does not apply.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Array Factor formula:** $\vert AF(\psi) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(n\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert$ where $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$
- [ ] **Maximum value of AF:** $AF_{max} = n$ (occurs at principal maximum direction)
- [ ] **Principal Maximum Condition:** $\psi = \pm 2m\pi$ for $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- [ ] **Null Condition:** $\psi = \pm 2p\pi/n$ for $p = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (excluding $p = n, 2n, \ldots$)
- [ ] **Broadside Array:** $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow$ maximum at $\theta = 90°$ (perpendicular to array axis)
- [ ] **End-Fire Array:** $\alpha = -\beta d \Rightarrow$ maximum at $\theta = 0°$ (along array axis)
- [ ] **Steering Formula:** $\cos\theta_0 = -\alpha / (\beta d)$ (master equation for beam steering)
- [ ] **HPBW Formula:** $HPBW \approx 0.886\lambda / (nd\sin\theta_0)$ radians
- [ ] **Sidelobe Level (uniform):** -13.2 dB below main lobe peak
- [ ] **Directivity of broadside array:** $D \approx 2.26 \, nd / \lambda$
- [ ] **Grating Lobe Condition:** $d/\lambda \geq 1/(1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert)$ — avoid by keeping $d \leq 0.5\lambda$ for visible-space-only scanning
- [ ] **Pattern Multiplication Theorem:** Total Pattern = Element Pattern $\times$ Array Factor
- [ ] **Isotropic radiator assumption** = used to compute AF; replace with practical element pattern using pattern multiplication
- [ ] **Equal amplitude** = uniform feeding = Dirichlet kernel form (closed form AF)
- [ ] **Number of elements 'n'** is always assumed to be a **positive integer** ($\geq 2$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark problems, **always draw the geometry**, **label all variables clearly**, **state assumptions upfront** (equal amplitude, uniform spacing, isotropic elements), and **box your final answer**. KTU examiners reward methodical presentation with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grating Lobes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Grating Lobes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grating Lobes** are **unwanted, major secondary maxima** in the radiation pattern of an antenna array, having radiation intensity **comparable to the main beam (principal maximum)**. They occur when the **inter-element spacing `d` becomes equal to or greater than one wavelength `λ`** (i.e., `d ≥ λ`), causing the array to radiate constructively in **multiple directions simultaneously** in addition to the desired scan angle.

In formal array theory terms, grating lobes are those maxima of the **Array Factor (AF)** that satisfy the phase condition `βd cos θ + α = 2πm` for any **integer `m ≠ 0`**, while lying within the **visible region** defined by `−1 ≤ cos θ ≤ +1` (equivalently `−1 ≤ u ≤ +1` in `u`-space, where `u = sin θ` or `cos θ` depending on convention).

### Key Terminology Alignment with KTU 2024 Outcomes
| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Principal Maximum** | The desired main beam steered to angle `θ₀` (where `m = 0`) |
| **Grating Lobe** | Any maximum at angle `θ_g ≠ θ₀` where `m ≠ 0` AND the angle lies in the visible region |
| **Visible Region** | The angular range `−1 ≤ u ≤ +1` corresponding to physically realizable directions `−90° ≤ θ ≤ +90°` |
| **Invisible Region** | The region `|u| > 1` where maxima are **mathematically present but physically cannot propagate** (evanescent) |
| **Element Spacing** | The uniform distance `d` between adjacent isotropic radiators in a linear array |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stadium Wave Phenomenon 🎟️

Imagine you are in a packed cricket stadium, and a small section of fans in row 1 stands up and raises their arms at exactly the same moment to start a "Mexican wave." The wave travels around the stadium smoothly. Now, suppose the rows are **very close together** — the wave appears as one clean, single, beautifully-coordinated front moving around the arena. 👏

But if you **double or triple the distance between rows** (keeping the same wave-starting delay), what happens? Suddenly, **multiple wave-fronts** appear to be propagating in different directions — the timing between rows is now so large that the same up-and-arm motion registers as a "peak" at several azimuths simultaneously. The crowd is no longer waving in **one** direction — they are creating **phantom waves** in unintended directions.

**This is exactly what happens in an antenna array when `d ≥ λ`:**
- Each antenna element is like a row of fans.
- The progressive phase delay `α` between elements is like the timing offset of the wave.
- When `d` is small (`d < λ`), the signals add constructively in **only one direction** → clean beam.
- When `d` is large (`d ≥ λ`), the same phase condition is satisfied in **multiple directions** → **grating lobes appear**, stealing energy from the main beam and creating **interference, false targets in radar, and ghost signals in communication systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering impact:** Grating lobes in a phased-array radar can cause it to detect a *false target* at an angle where no actual object exists — a catastrophic failure in air-traffic control or military surveillance systems. This is why **antenna array designers religiously enforce `d ≤ λ/2` (or tighter)** in critical applications.

---

## 1.3 The Critical Spacing Threshold

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Array Design (KTU High-Yield):**
> 
> $$\boxed{\; d_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert} \;}$$
> 
> For a **broadside array** (`θ₀ = 90°`): `d_max = λ`
> For a **scanned array** (e.g., `θ₀ = 60°`): `d_max = λ/1.5 ≈ 0.667λ`
> For **endfire** (`θ₀ = 0°`): `d_max = λ/2`

Spacing **larger** than this bound **guarantees** the entry of at least one grating lobe into the visible region.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Grating Lobes — The Phase Matching Condition

Consider a **uniform linear array (ULA)** of `N` isotropic elements with:
- Uniform spacing `d` (in metres)
- Uniform progressive phase shift `α` (radians) between adjacent elements
- Uniform amplitude excitation `I₀`

The total Array Factor (AF) is given by Balanis's classical formulation:

$$
AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} I_0 \, e^{\,j\,n\,(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)}
$$

where the free-space phase constant is `β = 2π/λ`.

The AF achieves its **principal maximum** at the scan angle `θ₀` when the exponent vanishes for `m = 0`:

$$
\beta d \cos\theta_0 + \alpha = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \alpha = -\beta d \cos\theta_0
$$

Substituting back, the **general condition for ANY maximum** of the array factor (in any direction `θ` in `u`-space, where `u = cos θ`) is:

$$
\boxed{\, d\,(u - u_0) = m\lambda, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \,}
$$

where `u₀ = cos θ₀` and `u = cos θ`.

> **Interpretation:**
> - `m = 0` → Principal maximum at the desired `θ₀`. ✅
> - `m ≠ 0` → Grating lobes at `cos θ_m = cos θ₀ + mλ/d`.
> - For a grating lobe to be **physically visible**, we need `|cos θ_m| ≤ 1`.

## 2.2 Condition for Grating Lobe Entry into the Visible Region

A grating lobe enters the visible region when its corresponding `u_m` value satisfies `|u_m| ≤ 1`. Starting from the maximum condition:

$$
u_m = u_0 + \dfrac{m\lambda}{d}
$$

The **first grating lobes** occur at `m = ±1`. For the lobe at `m = +1`:

$$
u_{+1} = u_0 + \dfrac{\lambda}{d}
$$

For this to lie within the visible region, we need `u_{+1} ≤ 1`:

$$
u_0 + \dfrac{\lambda}{d} \leq 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d \geq \dfrac{\lambda}{1 - u_0}
$$

Similarly for the lobe at `m = -1` to be visible: `u_{-1} ≥ -1`, giving:

$$
u_0 - \dfrac{\lambda}{d} \geq -1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; d \geq \dfrac{\lambda}{1 + u_0}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grating Lobe Visibility Condition (Consolidated):**
> 
> $$d \geq \dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \vert u_0 \vert} = \dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}$$
> 
> Equivalently, to **completely avoid** all grating lobes in the visible region:
> 
> $$\boxed{\, d < \dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert} \,}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `u = sin θ` (or `cos θ`) | Direction cosine | u-space plotting |
| 2 | `u_m = u₀ + mλ/d` | Location of m-th grating lobe | Find angle of grating lobe |
| 3 | `α = -βd cos θ₀` | Progressive phase for scan to `θ₀` | Beam-steering design |
| 4 | `d_max = λ / (1 + \|u₀\|)` | Maximum spacing to avoid grating lobes | Array design constraint |
| 5 | `Δu = λ/d` | Grating lobe spacing in u-space | Pattern sketching |
| 6 | `BW_u = 2/(Nd)` | First-null beamwidth in u-space | For ULA of N elements |
| 7 | `HPBW ≈ 0.886 λ/(Nd)` in u | Half-power beamwidth in u-space | Approximate calculations |
| 8 | `N_nulls = N − 1` | Number of nulls between principal maxima | Counting features of AF |
| 9 | `N_side + grating = N − 1` | Total secondary maxima (incl. grating) | Verify array factor count |
| 10 | `d < λ/2` (broadside) | Standard safe spacing | Most common KTU assumption |

> **Note on notation:** All `|x|` quantities in formulas use `\vert x \vert` notation to preserve table integrity in the rendered markdown.

## 2.4 Why Grating Lobes are Catastrophic — Engineering Implications

> [!WARNING]
> **Grating lobes are NOT like ordinary side lobes.** Side lobes are typically 10–30 dB below the main beam and are tolerable. Grating lobes have **amplitude equal to the main beam** (≈ 0 dB relative) and cause:
> 
> 1. **Radar systems:** Ambiguous range / false targets (the famous "ghosts" in early phased-array radars).
> 2. **Communication systems:** Co-channel interference from unintended directions, breaking link isolation.
> 3. **Direction-finding systems:** Gross angular estimation errors (an interferer at a grating lobe angle is indistinguishable from the true signal).
> 4. **Power inefficiency:** Radiated power is divided between the main beam and grating lobes, reducing the effective gain of the desired beam.
> 5. **Imaging arrays (MRI, radio astronomy):** Spatial aliasing — high-frequency spatial content "folds back" into the low-frequency band, corrupting the image.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility and Design Trade-offs

- **Wide-angle scanning** (e.g., `±60°`) **forces** the designer to use `d < 0.536λ`, increasing the number of elements `N` for a given aperture → higher cost and complexity.
- **Electronic Warfare (EW) and modern AESA radars** (e.g., AN/APG-81 of F-35) use sub-array architectures and **irregular / randomized spacing** (thinned arrays) to push grating lobes below the noise floor while maintaining wide scan capability.
- **Sub-λ spacing** is also essential for **mutual coupling** to be manageable and predictable.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Phase Condition for Array Factor Maxima

We start from the AF of a uniform linear array of `N` isotropic elements:

$$
AF(\theta) = I_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{\,j\,n\psi}
$$

where the **array phase term** is defined as:

$$
\psi \;\triangleq\; \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha
$$

**Step 1: Factor out the geometric series.**

$$
AF(\theta) = I_0 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{\,j\,N\psi}}{1 - e^{\,j\,\psi}}
$$

**Step 2: Express in the standard magnitude-phase form** (using the identity `1 - e^{jφ} = 2j sin(φ/2) · e^{jφ/2}`):

$$
AF(\theta) = I_0 \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{N\psi}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\psi}{2}\right)} \cdot e^{\,j\,(N-1)\psi/2}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the magnitude envelope.**

$$
|AF(\theta)| = I_0 \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert
$$

**Step 4: Locate the maxima.** The maximum of `|AF|` occurs when **both** numerator and denominator vanish simultaneously — i.e., when `sin(ψ/2) = 0` and `sin(Nψ/2) = 0`. This requires:

$$
\psi = 2\pi m, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back the definition of ψ.**

$$
\beta d \cos\theta_m + \alpha = 2\pi m
$$

$$
\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, d\, \cos\theta_m + \alpha = 2\pi m
$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to get the locus of m-th maximum.**

$$
\cos\theta_m = \frac{-\alpha}{kd} + \frac{m\lambda}{d}
$$

**Step 7: For the principal maximum (`m = 0`)** to occur at scan angle `θ₀`, we set:

$$
\cos\theta_0 = \frac{-\alpha}{kd} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \alpha = -kd\cos\theta_0
$$

**Step 8: Substitute this back to obtain the elegant universal result.**

$$
\boxed{\,\cos\theta_m - \cos\theta_0 = \frac{m\lambda}{d}\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental grating-lobe equation** that every KTU student must commit to memory. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Critical Spacing for Grating Lobe Onset

We need to find the largest `d` such that **no `m ≠ 0` solution** gives `|cos θ_m| ≤ 1`.

**Step 1: Express the grating-lobe condition for `m = 1`.**

$$
\cos\theta_1 = \cos\theta_0 + \frac{\lambda}{d}
$$

**Step 2: For the grating lobe to JUST appear at the edge of the visible region**, set `cos θ_1 = 1`:

$$
1 = \cos\theta_0 + \frac{\lambda}{d_c}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for the critical spacing `d_c`.**

$$
d_c = \frac{\lambda}{1 - \cos\theta_0}
$$

**Step 4: Check the `m = -1` case similarly.**

$$
\cos\theta_{-1} = \cos\theta_0 - \frac{\lambda}{d_c} = -1
$$

Solving gives the symmetric critical value:

$$
d_c = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \cos\theta_0}
$$

**Step 5: The smaller of these two values is the binding constraint.** Since `|cos θ₀| ≤ 1`, the **more restrictive** condition (smaller `d_c`) comes from the `+cos θ₀` term when `cos θ₀ > 0` and from the `-cos θ₀` term when `cos θ₀ < 0`. Combining both:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem (KTU 2019 Pattern, Dec 2018 Adapted):** A 10-element uniform linear array operates at `f = 3 \text{ GHz}` and is to be scanned to `θ₀ = 30°` from broadside. Find: (a) the maximum allowable inter-element spacing `d_max` to avoid grating lobes, (b) the locations of the first two grating lobes (in degrees) if `d = 0.7λ`, and (c) whether these grating lobes are visible.

**Given:**
- `N = 10`
- `f = 3 GHz` → `λ = c/f = (3 × 10⁸)/(3 × 10⁹) = 0.1 m`
- `θ₀ = 30°` → `cos θ₀ = √3/2 ≈ 0.866`

**Part (a):**

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert\cos 30°\vert} = \frac{0.1}{1 + 0.866} = \frac{0.1}{1.866}
$$

$$
d_{\max} \approx 0.0536 \text{ m} = 0.536\,\lambda
$$

**Part (b):** With `d = 0.7λ`, the grating lobe locations are:

$$
\cos\theta_m = \cos 30° + \frac{m}{0.7}
$$

For `m = +1`:
$$
\cos\theta_{+1} = 0.866 + \frac{1}{0.7} = 0.866 + 1.4286 = 2.295
$$

For `m = -1`:
$$
\cos\theta_{-1} = 0.866 - 1.4286 = -0.563
$$

So `θ_{-1} = arccos(-0.563) ≈ 124.2°`.

**Part (c):**
- `m = +1` lobe: `cos θ = 2.295 > 1` → **invisible** (lies in the invisible region, no real-angle solution). ✅
- `m = -1` lobe: `cos θ = -0.563`, with `|−0.563| < 1` → **visible!** This is a real grating lobe at `θ ≈ 124.2°`. ⚠️

> **Conclusion:** The choice `d = 0.7λ` is **invalid** for scanning to `30°` because a grating lobe has entered the visible region. The designer must reduce `d` to at most `0.536λ`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Each Grating Lobe Become Visible?

| Scan angle `θ₀` | `cos θ₀` | `d_max` to avoid ALL grating lobes | First grating lobe to enter as `d` increases | Entry direction (θ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 90° (broadside) | 0 | λ | `m = ±1` (symmetric) | 0° and 180° |
| 60° | 0.5 | 0.667λ | `m = -1` first | ≈ 131.8° |
| 30° | 0.866 | 0.536λ | `m = -1` first | ≈ 180° (endfire) |
| 0° (endfire) | 1.0 | 0.5λ | `m = -1` (becomes 180° endfire) | 180° |
| 120° (mirror) | -0.5 | 0.667λ | `m = +1` first | ≈ 48.2° |
| 150° (mirror) | -0.866 | 0.536λ | `m = +1` first | ≈ 0° (endfire) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Grating Lobe Spacing in u-Space (Rapid Sketching Method)

The locus of array factor maxima in `u`-space is **equally spaced** with period:

$$
\Delta u = \frac{\lambda}{d}
$$

The visible region spans `u ∈ [-1, +1]`, so the **maximum number of grating lobes (including the principal)** that can possibly fit inside the visible region is:

$$
N_{\text{total maxima}} = \left\lfloor \frac{2}{\lambda/d} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{2d}{\lambda} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Sketching Tip (KTU Examiner's Secret):**
> 1. Mark `u = -1` and `u = +1` as the visible-region edges.
> 2. Place the principal maximum at `u₀ = cos θ₀`.
> 3. Mark off `Δu = λ/d` on **both sides** of `u₀` to locate every grating lobe.
> 4. Any marker falling within `[-1, +1]` is a **visible** grating lobe → bad!
> 5. The full `u`-space plot looks like a regular comb of teeth, with the visible region acting as a sliding "window" of width 2.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 u-Space Geometry of Grating Lobes (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of how element spacing `d` controls the appearance of grating lobes in the visible region of `u`-space.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputA["Element Spacing d"] --> CalcA["Compute Period = lambda over d"]
    CalcA --> CombGen["Generate Comb of Maxima at u = u0 + m times lambda over d"]
    InputB["Scan Angle theta0"] --> CalcB["Compute Principal u0 = cos theta0"]
    CalcB --> CombGen
    CombGen --> Window["Apply Visible Region Filter: minus 1 to plus 1"]
    Window --> OutputA["Visible Principal Maximum at u0"]
    Window --> OutputB["Visible Grating Lobes at u sub m where m is not 0 and absolute u sub m is less than 1"]
    Window --> OutputC["Invisible Maxima at absolute u greater than 1"]
    InputC["Check: d less than lambda over 1 plus absolute cos theta0"] --> Decision{"Grating Lobe Free?"}
    OutputA --> Decision
    OutputB --> Decision
    OutputC --> Decision
    Decision -- YES --> Pass["Design Valid: Use d"]
    Decision -- NO --> Fail["Reduce d or change scan range"]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **Comb Generator** is the symbolic representation of the equally-spaced maxima of the array factor in `u`-space.
- The **Visible Region Filter** acts as a sliding window of width 2, only "admitting" maxima that fall within `[-1, +1]`.
- The **Decision Block** enforces the design rule: if any `m ≠ 0` maximum is admitted, the design is flagged for revision.

---

## 4.2 Comparative u-Space Plot — Sub-Wavelength vs. Super-Wavelength Spacing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CaseA["Case A: d equals 0.5 lambda (SAFE)"]
        UA1["u equals minus 1"] --> UA2["u equals 0"]
        UA2 --> UA3["u equals plus 1"]
        UA1 -. "PRINCIPAL" .- UA2
        UA2 -. "grating at u equals plus 2" .- UA3
    end
    subgraph CaseB["Case B: d equals 1.0 lambda (BORDERLINE)"]
        UB1["u equals minus 1"] --> UB2["u equals 0"]
        UB2 --> UB3["u equals plus 1"]
        UB1 -. "grating at u equals minus 1" .- UB2
        UB2 -. "PRINCIPAL at u equals 0" .- UB3
        UB3 -. "grating at u equals plus 1" .- UB4["u equals plus 1 boundary"]
    end
    subgraph CaseC["Case C: d equals 1.5 lambda (GRATING LOBE INSIDE)"]
        UC1["u equals minus 1"] --> UC2["u equals minus 0.5"]
        UC2 --> UC3["u equals 0"]
        UC3 --> UC4["u equals 0.5"]
        UC4 --> UC5["u equals plus 1"]
        UC1 -. "grating at minus 1" .- UC2
        UC2 -. "grating at minus 0.5" .- UC3
        UC3 -. "PRINCIPAL at 0" .- UC4
        UC4 -. "grating at plus 0.5" .- UC5
        UC5 -. "grating at plus 1" .- UC6["u equals plus 1 boundary"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each labeled tick mark represents an **array-factor maximum**. The principal maximum is in solid label-style notation. In Case A, all grating lobes lie **outside** the visible window — safe design. In Case C, **three** grating lobes are inside the window — total design failure.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: How Grating Lobes Form in Hardware

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RF["RF Signal Source at frequency f0"] --> PhaseCtrl["Phase Shifter Network"]
    PhaseCtrl --> Elem1["Array Element 1 with progressive phase alpha equals 0"]
    PhaseCtrl --> Elem2["Array Element 2 with phase alpha"]
    PhaseCtrl --> ElemN["Array Element N with phase N minus 1 times alpha"]
    Elem1 --> Space["Free Space Propagation with spacing d"]
    Elem2 --> Space
    ElemN --> Space
    Space --> Observer["Observer at angle theta"]
    Observer --> Decision{"Does phase alignment occur at m is not 0?"}
    Decision -- YES --> GL["Grating Lobe at theta sub m"]
    Decision -- NO --> ML["Main Beam Only at theta 0"]
    Space --> SpacingCheck{"Check d versus lambda"}
    SpacingCheck -- "d equals 0.5 lambda" --> Safe["SAFE: No Grating Lobes"]
    SpacingCheck -- "d equals lambda" --> Border["BORDERLINE: Grating at horizon"]
    SpacingCheck -- "d greater than lambda" --> Danger["DANGER: Grating Lobes in visible region"]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree for an Antenna Array Designer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["New Array Design: Choose d and theta0"] --> Q1{"Is d less than lambda over 2?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q1Yes["Universally safe for ALL scan angles from 0 to 180 degrees"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Is theta0 fixed?"}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3["Apply d less than lambda over 1 plus absolute cos theta0"]
    Q3 --> Q3a{"Inequality satisfied?"}
    Q3a -- YES --> Q3Yes["Safe for this scan angle"]
    Q3a -- NO --> Q3No["GRATING LOBE PRESENT: Reduce d or use thinned array"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4["Need full-scan safety: enforce d less than lambda over 2"]
    Q4 --> Q4End["Safe from 0 to 180 degree scan"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 (Conceptual) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Define grating lobes. Why do they occur in antenna arrays, and what is the design rule to avoid them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme):**

Grating lobes are **undesired major maxima of the array radiation pattern** that have the **same magnitude as the main beam (principal maximum)** but appear at angles other than the desired scan angle θ₀.

**They occur (1 mark):** When the inter-element spacing `d` becomes large enough that the array factor achieves a secondary maximum within the visible region `|u| ≤ 1`. This happens mathematically when the phase condition `kd cos θ + α = 2πm` is satisfied for some integer `m ≠ 0` at a real angle θ.

**Design rule to avoid them (1 mark):** For a scan angle `θ₀`, the maximum allowable spacing is:

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}
$$

For a broadside array, this reduces to `d < λ`. For wide-scan or endfire arrays, the rule becomes more restrictive (`d < λ/2`).

**Consequence (1 mark):** Grating lobes cause ambiguity, false targets, and power wastage; they must be eliminated in critical systems like radar and direction finders.

---

### Question A.2 (Numerical) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: A uniform linear array has `d = λ`. Will a grating lobe appear in the visible region for broadside radiation? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For broadside radiation, `θ₀ = 90°` → `cos θ₀ = 0`.

**Apply the condition (1 mark):**

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert 0 \vert} = \lambda
$$

Since `d = λ = d_max`, the array is **at the critical boundary** (1 mark).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A grating lobe is **just on the verge** of appearing at `θ = 0°` and `θ = 180°` (the array factor has maxima at `cos θ = 0 ± 1`, i.e., at `cos θ = ±1`). At exactly `d = λ`, the grating lobes coincide with the **endfire directions** and are typically considered "at the horizon" — for strict safety, designers use `d < λ` (e.g., `d = 0.9λ` or smaller).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Starting from the array factor of a uniform linear array, derive the condition for the occurrence of grating lobes. Clearly state the expression for the angle of the m-th grating lobe. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 20-element uniform linear array operates at 10 GHz and is required to scan to `θ₀ = 45°`. Determine: (i) the maximum inter-element spacing to avoid grating lobes, (ii) the angular positions of the first two grating lobes if the actual spacing is `d = 0.8λ`, and (iii) the maximum scan angle achievable with `d = 0.6λ` without admitting a grating lobe. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the array factor for a ULA of `N` elements with uniform amplitude `I₀`, spacing `d`, and progressive phase `α`:

$$
AF(\theta) = I_0 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{\,j\,n\,(\beta d \cos\theta + \alpha)}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Introduce the array phase `ψ = βd cos θ + α` and express AF in closed form:

$$
AF(\theta) = I_0 \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \cdot e^{\,j\,(N-1)\psi/2}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** State that maxima occur when `sin(ψ/2) = 0`, i.e., `ψ = 2πm` for integer `m`:

$$
\beta d \cos\theta_m + \alpha = 2\pi m
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For the principal maximum at `θ₀` (with `m = 0`):

$$
\alpha = -\beta d \cos\theta_0 = -k d \cos\theta_0
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute back to get the general result:

$$
\cos\theta_m = \cos\theta_0 + \frac{m\lambda}{d}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the visibility condition `|cos θ_m| ≤ 1` and derive the no-grating-lobe design rule:

$$
d \leq \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note that `m = 0` gives the main beam; `m ≠ 0` gives the grating lobes; only those with `|cos θ_m| ≤ 1` are visible.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- `N = 20`, `f = 10 GHz` → `λ = c/f = 0.03 m`
- Scan angle `θ₀ = 45°` → `cos θ₀ = √2/2 ≈ 0.707`

**(i) Maximum spacing to avoid grating lobes (2 marks):**

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos 45°\vert} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + 0.707} = \frac{\lambda}{1.707}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\max} \approx 0.586\,\lambda \approx 0.01758 \text{ m} \approx 1.758 \text{ cm}\,}
$$

**(ii) Grating lobe positions for `d = 0.8λ` (3 marks):**

$$
\cos\theta_m = \cos 45° + \frac{m}{0.8} = 0.707 + 1.25m
$$

For `m = +1`: `cos θ₊₁ = 0.707 + 1.25 = 1.957 > 1` → **invisible** (no real solution).

For `m = -1`: `cos θ₋₁ = 0.707 - 1.25 = -0.543`, so `θ₋₁ = arccos(-0.543) ≈ 122.9°` → **visible!**

For `m = +2`: `cos θ₊₂ = 0.707 + 2.5 = 3.207 > 1` → invisible.

For `m = -2`: `cos θ₋₂ = 0.707 - 2.5 = -1.793 < -1` → invisible.

**Result:** The first visible grating lobe is at `θ ≈ 122.9°` (in the backward quadrant). This confirms `d = 0.8λ` is invalid.

**(iii) Maximum scan angle for `d = 0.6λ` without grating lobes (2 marks):**

The no-grating-lobe condition is `d < λ/(1 + |cos θ₀|)`. For `d = 0.6λ`:

$$
0.6 < \frac{1}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert}
$$

$$
1 + \vert \cos\theta_0 \vert < \frac{1}{0.6} = 1.667
$$

$$
\vert \cos\theta_0 \vert < 0.667
$$

$$
\cos\theta_0 < 0.667 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \theta_0 > arccos(0.667) \approx 48.2°
$$

So the scan angle must satisfy **`θ₀ > 48.2°`** (i.e., farther from endfire, closer to broadside). Equivalently, the **maximum safe scan** is `θ₀_max ≈ 48.2°` from endfire (or `131.8°` in the backward direction). The grating lobe would just appear at the endfire boundary.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain the concept of grating lobes with the help of a suitable u-space diagram. Discuss the role of element spacing and scan angle in determining whether a grating lobe will appear in the visible region. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An 8-element uniform linear array operates at 2 GHz. The inter-element spacing is `d = 0.4λ` and the array is to be scanned to `θ₀ = 60°`. (i) Calculate the progressive phase shift `α` between elements. (ii) Determine whether any grating lobes will appear in the visible region. (iii) What is the maximum scan angle achievable if the spacing is increased to `d = 0.6λ`? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual + Diagram (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Grating lobes are extra major maxima of the array factor that appear at angles other than the desired scan direction `θ₀`, with amplitudes comparable to the main beam. They occur when the element spacing `d` is too large relative to the wavelength.

**u-space explanation (3 marks):** In `u`-space, the array factor maxima form an **equally-spaced comb** with period `Δu = λ/d`, centered around the principal maximum at `u₀ = cos θ₀`. The **visible region** is the window `u ∈ [-1, +1]`. Grating lobes appear whenever one of the comb teeth (other than the principal) falls inside this window. The further apart the comb teeth (i.e., the larger `d`), the more grating lobes enter the visible region.

**Role of element spacing (1.5 marks):** Smaller `d` (e.g., `d < λ/2`) → comb period `Δu > 2` → **no grating lobe can fit in the visible region**, no matter the scan angle. Larger `d` → smaller `Δu` → multiple grating lobes appear in the visible region, severely degrading performance.

**Role of scan angle (1.5 marks):** As the array scans away from broadside toward endfire, the principal maximum `u₀` moves toward `±1`, and the **safe window for the nearest grating lobe shrinks**. Therefore, the maximum allowable `d` decreases as the scan approaches endfire (`θ₀ → 0°`), reaching the strictest bound `d < λ/2` at endfire.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** `N = 8`, `f = 2 GHz` → `λ = 0.15 m`, `d = 0.4λ = 0.06 m`, `θ₀ = 60°`.

**(i) Progressive phase shift `α` (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha = -k d \cos\theta_0 = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot d \cdot \cos 60° = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot 0.4\lambda \cdot 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha = -0.4\pi \text{ rad} = -72°\,}
$$

**(ii) Grating lobe check for `d = 0.4λ` (2 marks):**

Maximum safe spacing for `θ₀ = 60°`:

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \cos 60°\vert} = \frac{\lambda}{1.5} = 0.667\,\lambda
$$

Since `d = 0.4λ < 0.667λ`, the array is **safe** — no grating lobes in the visible region. ✅

Equivalently: `cos θ₊₁ = 0.5 + 1/0.4 = 3.0 > 1` (invisible), `cos θ₋₁ = 0.5 - 1/0.4 = -2.0 < -1` (invisible). ✅

**(iii) Maximum scan angle for `d = 0.6λ` (3 marks):**

Use the grating-lobe condition: `d_max(θ₀) = λ/(1 + |cos θ₀|)`. Set `d = d_max`:

$$
0.6 = \frac{1}{1 + \vert \cos\theta_{0,\max} \vert}
$$

$$
1 + \vert \cos\theta_{0,\max} \vert = \frac{1}{0.6} = 1.667
$$

$$
\vert \cos\theta_{0,\max} \vert = 0.667
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\theta_{0,\max} = 48.19° \text{ (from endfire)}\,}
$$

So the **maximum safe scan angle is 48.19°** (in the forward direction) or 131.81° (in the backward direction). Beyond this, a grating lobe enters the visible region.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value:** Writing `d_max = λ/(1 + cos θ₀)` without the `|·|` sign loses 1 mark when `θ₀ > 90°` (e.g., for arrays radiating in the backward hemisphere).
>
> 2. **Confusing visible and invisible regions:** Students often report a grating lobe at `θ = 0°` (endfire) when `d = λ` and call it "visible" — strictly, the **array factor has a maximum** there, but the **pattern also vanishes due to element factor** in many cases. State clearly: *"the grating lobe lies at the edge of the visible region"*.
>
> 3. **Wrong formula for scan-phase:** Using `α = -kd sin θ₀` instead of `α = -kd cos θ₀` (or vice-versa) — this depends on whether `θ` is measured from endfire or broadside. KTU convention: `θ` is measured from **positive z-axis (endfire)**.
>
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** Always verify `|cos θ_m| ≤ 1` after computing grating lobe angles. Reporting a non-real angle is a mark-deduction trigger.
>
> 5. **Ignoring N when answering:** A 2-element and a 100-element array have the **same** grating-lobe positions; `N` only controls the beamwidth. Do not include `N` in grating-lobe formulas unless asked.
>
> 6. **Units and prefixes:** `d_max` must be reported in metres (or `λ` units). Mixing cm and mm within one step loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for the night before the exam):**
>
> - **Definition (1-liner):** Grating lobes are unwanted principal-maxima-equivalent beams in the array pattern that appear at angles other than the scan angle.
>
> - **Master equation:** `cos θ_m = cos θ₀ + mλ/d` for `m = 0, ±1, ±2, ...`
>
> - **Design rule (no grating lobes):** `d < λ/(1 + |cos θ₀|)`
>
> - **Broadside special case:** `d < λ`
>
> - **Endfire special case:** `d < λ/2` (most restrictive)
>
> - **u-space period:** `Δu = λ/d` between adjacent maxima
>
> - **Visible region:** `u ∈ [-1, +1]` always
>
> - **Grating lobe is visible when:** `|cos θ_m| ≤ 1` and `m ≠ 0`
>
> - **Number of secondary maxima between principal maxima:** Exactly `N − 2` in a ULA of `N` elements (this includes all side lobes AND any grating lobes, if present).
>
> - **Why they form:** Constructive interference happens in unintended directions when phase repetition occurs across the array before the field traverses one full wavelength.
>
> - **How to eliminate them:** Reduce `d` below `λ/2`; use **non-uniform (thinned/logarithmic) spacing**; use **sub-array weighting**; use **overlapping sub-arrays with random phase centres**.
>
> - **Real-world killers:** Radar false targets, communication co-channel interference, direction-finding ambiguity, MRI image aliasing.
>
> - **3 marks →** Define + state condition + 1-line consequence.
> - **7 marks →** Derive from array factor OR plot u-space diagram with calculation of visible grating lobes.
> - **14 marks →** Full derivation + numerical example with at least 2 sub-parts.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Grating Lobes (PCECT602 / KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Broadside and End Fire Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design of Broadside and End Fire Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Antenna Array** is a system of two or more radiating elements (usually identical) arranged in a specific geometric configuration (linear, planar, or circular) and excited with appropriate relative amplitudes and phases to obtain a desired radiation pattern that is not achievable with a single isolated element.

A **Broadside Array** is a linear uniform array in which the **maximum radiation** is directed **perpendicular (broadside, θ = 90°)** to the axis of the array. The progressive phase shift **α = 0**, while elements are fed in phase.

An **End-Fire Array** is a linear uniform array in which the **maximum radiation** is directed **along the axis of the array** (θ = 0° or θ = 180°). The progressive phase shift **α = ±βd**, such that the waves from successive elements add constructively in the end-fire direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Design of Broadside and End-Fire Arrays is a *core analytical topic* under Module 4. The student must master array factor, phasing, HPBW, directivity, and the Hansen-Woodyard condition. Expect derivations and numerical design problems in University exams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a row of people standing in a straight line (the **array axis**):

- **Broadside Array Analogy:** Picture 8 students in a line, all clapping *in perfect synchrony* (zero phase difference). The loudest sound (maximum radiation) is heard by a listener standing perpendicular to the line — i.e., looking at the broad side of the line of students. If you walk along the line direction, you hear almost nothing because the contributions from different students are out of phase. This is exactly how a broadside array radiates — strongest perpendicular to the array axis.

- **End-Fire Array Analogy:** Now imagine the same 8 students clapping with carefully *delayed* synchronization. The student at the far end claps first, the next one a tiny moment later, and so on. The sound waves travel along the line and add constructively at one end (the end-fire direction), like a "wave of claps" rolling down the line. A listener standing at that end hears a strong combined signal — this is the end-fire radiation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Wavelength (λ):** $c/f$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Phase constant (β):** $2\pi/\lambda$ (rad/m)
- **Number of elements (N):** Integer ≥ 2
- **Element spacing (d):** Typically $\lambda/2 \leq d \leq \lambda$ to avoid grating lobes
- **Progressive phase shift (α):** Radians
- **HPBW (Half-Power Beamwidth):** Radians or degrees
- **Directivity (D):** Dimensionless (or in dB)
- **First Minor Lobe Level (FMLL):** -13.46 dB for uniform broadside array (large N)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Rule:** For uniform arrays, the first minor lobe level is **-13.46 dB** below the main beam maximum. This is a fundamental constant of Fourier series / Chebyshev-like behavior. The Hansen-Woodyard end-fire array sacrifices this slightly (-12.0 dB) for ~78.9% increase in directivity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Array Factor — The Heart of the Array

Consider **N** isotropic radiators arranged uniformly along the z-axis with uniform spacing **d** and progressive phase **α**. The **Array Factor (AF)** represents the far-field pattern due to array geometry alone (assuming identical element patterns).

The total field observed at a distant point is the sum of contributions from each element. The phase of the n-th element's contribution at observation angle θ is:

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos \theta + \alpha
$$

where:
- **β d cos θ** = spatial phase difference due to path length
- **α** = progressive phase shift (feed phasing)

The Array Factor is therefore:

$$
AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n e^{j n \psi}
$$

For **uniform amplitude** ($a_n = 1$ for all n), this becomes the geometric series:

$$
AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

## 2.2 Phasing Conditions — The "Why" Behind Array Steering

The principal maximum of AF occurs when $\psi = 0$ (denominator and numerator both zero, with ratio → N).

**Setting ψ = 0:**

$$
\beta d \cos \theta_0 + \alpha = 0
$$

**Case 1 — Broadside Array ($\theta_0 = 90°$):**

$$
\alpha = 0
$$

All elements fed in phase; radiation maximum perpendicular to array axis.

**Case 2 — Ordinary End-Fire Array ($\theta_0 = 0°$):**

$$
\alpha = -\beta d
$$

Phase progressively delayed by βd; radiation maximum along +z direction.

**Case 3 — Hansen-Woodyard End-Fire Array:**

$$
\alpha = -\left(\beta d + \frac{\pi}{N}\right)
$$

Enhanced directivity end-fire condition; moves first maximum slightly off-axis to optimize end-fire gain.

## 2.3 Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW) — The Resolution Metric

The HPBW is found by locating the angle $\theta_h$ where AF = N/√2.

**Broadside Array HPBW (approximate, large N):**

$$
HPBW_{\text{broadside}} \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{L} = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd \sin \theta_0} \text{ (radians)}
$$

For $\theta_0 = 90°$:

$$
HPBW_{\text{broadside}} \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd} \text{ (radians)}
$$

**End-Fire Array HPBW:**

$$
HPBW_{\text{end-fire}} \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd}} \text{ (radians)} \quad \text{(Ordinary)}
$$

$$
HPBW_{\text{end-fire, HW}} \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd + 0.5\lambda}} \text{ (radians)} \quad \text{(Hansen-Woodyard)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The end-fire HPBW depends on **√(λ/L)** whereas broadside HPBW depends on **λ/L**. This makes end-fire beams inherently wider in the H-plane and narrower in the E-plane for the same array length L = Nd.

## 2.4 Directivity — The Gain Metric

**Broadside Array Directivity (Balanis approximation, large N):**

$$
D_{\text{broadside}} \approx \frac{2Nd}{\lambda}
$$

**End-Fire Array Directivity (Ordinary):**

$$
D_{\text{end-fire}} \approx \frac{4Nd}{\lambda}
$$

**End-Fire Array Directivity (Hansen-Woodyard):**

$$
D_{\text{HW}} \approx 1.789 \times \frac{4Nd}{\lambda} = 1.789 \times D_{\text{ordinary}}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Broadside Formula | End-Fire (Ordinary) Formula | End-Fire (H-W) Formula |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Progressive phase α | $\alpha = 0$ | $\alpha = -\beta d$ | $\alpha = -(\beta d + \pi/N)$ |
| Max radiation direction | $\theta_0 = 90°$ | $\theta_0 = 0°$ | $\theta_0 = 0°$ |
| Array Factor | $AF = \sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)$ | Same | Same |
| HPBW (rad) | $0.886\lambda/(Nd)$ | $2\sqrt{0.886\lambda/(Nd)}$ | $2\sqrt{0.886\lambda/(Nd + 0.5\lambda)}$ |
| Directivity D | $2Nd/\lambda$ | $4Nd/\lambda$ | $1.789 \times 4Nd/\lambda$ |
| First Minor Lobe Level | $-13.46$ dB | $-13.46$ dB | $\approx -12.0$ dB |
| Spacing condition | $d \geq \lambda/2$ for single lobe | $d \leq \lambda/2$ for single lobe | $d \leq \lambda/2$ for single lobe |
| Beam Solid Ω_A (sr) | $\approx \lambda^2/(Nd\lambda)$ | $\approx 1.27\lambda^2/(Nd\lambda)$ | $\approx 0.71\lambda^2/(Nd\lambda)$ |

| Variable | Meaning | Typical Range |
|:---|:---|:---|
| N | Number of isotropic elements | 2 to 50+ |
| d | Inter-element spacing | $0.1\lambda$ to $1.0\lambda$ |
| λ | Operating wavelength | cm/mm range (microwave) |
| α | Progressive phase shift | $-\pi$ to $+\pi$ |
| θ | Observation angle from array axis | $0°$ to $180°$ |
| L = Nd | Total array length | $0.5\lambda$ to $10\lambda+$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "BESe" for design choices:**
> - **B**roadside → α = 0
> - **E**nd-fire → α = ±βd
> - **S**pacing: d ≤ λ/2 for end-fire, d = λ/2 to λ for broadside
> - **e**nhanced: H-W for extra directivity

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadside Arrays:** Used in **radar surveillance systems**, **TV broadcasting antennas**, **cellular base station sectoral antennas**, and **satellite communication earth stations** where a fan-beam perpendicular to the mast is required.
- **End-Fire Arrays:** Used in **phased-array radar**, **aircraft wing-tip antennas for direction finding**, **Yagi-Uda parasitic arrays (a form of end-fire)**, **radio telescopes**, and **missile-seeker homing antennas** where the beam must align with the platform axis.
- **Hansen-Woodyard Arrays:** Used in **long-range communication links** and **radio direction finding** where maximum directivity in a fixed end-fire direction is essential.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Array Factor for Uniform Linear Array (ULA)

**Starting Point:** Consider N isotropic point sources placed along the z-axis at positions $z_n = nd$, with uniform amplitude $A_n = A$ and progressive phase $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Field from a Single Element:**
The far-field of an isotropic radiator at the origin is:

$$
E_n(\theta) = A \frac{e^{-j\beta R_n}}{R_n}
$$

**Step 2 — Path Difference for n-th Element:**
For the n-th element located at $z_n = nd$, the distance to the far-field observation point P at angle θ is:

$$
R_n \approx R_0 - n d \cos \theta
$$

where $R_0$ is the distance from origin to P. This uses the standard far-field approximation: $R_n \approx R_0 - z_n \cos \theta$.

**Step 3 — Phase Contribution from n-th Element:**
The total phase (including progressive phase α) of the n-th element's contribution is:

$$
\Phi_n = -\beta R_n + n \alpha = -\beta (R_0 - n d \cos \theta) + n \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Sum All Element Contributions:**

$$
E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \frac{A e^{-j\beta R_0}}{R_0} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n (\beta d \cos \theta + \alpha)}
$$

**Step 5 — Define ψ and Recognize the Series:**
Let $\psi = \beta d \cos \theta + \alpha$. The summation is a finite geometric series:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi} = \frac{1 - e^{j N \psi}}{1 - e^{j \psi}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Euler's Identity and Simplify:**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-j N \psi/2}$ and $e^{j \psi/2}$ respectively:

$$
AF(\theta) = e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

**Step 7 — Drop the Linear Phase Term (|AF| is the observable pattern):**

$$
|AF(\theta)| = \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert
$$

This is the **fundamental Array Factor** used in ALL subsequent KTU calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Broadside Array HPBW

**Goal:** Find the angle $\theta_h$ where $|AF(\theta_h)| = N/\sqrt{2}$ (the half-power point).

**Step 1 — Principal Maximum Condition:**
Maximum of AF occurs at $\psi = 0$, giving $|AF|_{max} = N$.

**Step 2 — Half-Power Condition:**
The -3 dB point occurs when $\sin(N\psi_h/2)/\sin(\psi_h/2) = N/\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 3 — Approximation for Large N:**
For large N, the main beam is narrow and $\psi_h$ is small. The denominator $\sin(\psi_h/2) \approx \psi_h/2$. The numerator reaches its first zero when $N\psi/2 = \pi$, i.e., $\psi = 2\pi/N$. The half-power point lies near this zero.

**Step 4 — Linear Approximation in the Linear Region:**
The main lobe is approximately triangular near its peak. The -3 dB point occurs at:

$$
\sin(N\psi_h/2) \approx N\psi_h/2 \cdot \frac{\sin(\psi_h/2)}{\psi_h/2}
$$

For small $\psi_h$:

$$
\frac{N\psi_h/2}{\psi_h/2} = N
$$

But to get -3 dB, we need $N/\sqrt{2}$. Equating:

$$
\sin(N\psi_h/2) = \frac{N \sin(\psi_h/2)}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solving (Numerical/Approximate):**
For large N, the solution is:

$$
N \psi_h / 2 \approx 0.4429 \text{ rad}
$$

giving $\psi_h \approx 0.8858/N$.

**Step 6 — Convert to Angular Space (Broadside, α=0):**
For broadside, $\psi = \beta d \cos \theta$. At θ = 90° (max), $\psi = 0$. Differentiating:

$$
d\psi = -\beta d \sin \theta \, d\theta
$$

Near $\theta = 90°$, $\sin \theta \approx 1$, so $d\psi \approx -\beta d \, d\theta$.

**Step 7 — HPBW Result:**

$$
HPBW = 2 \left\vert d\theta \right\vert = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8858/N}{\beta d} = \frac{0.8858}{N \beta d / 2}
$$

Since $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$
HPBW_{\text{broadside}} = \frac{0.8858 \lambda}{N \pi d} = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d} \text{ (radians)}
$$

**In degrees:**

$$
HPBW_{\text{broadside}} = \frac{50.76°}{N \cdot (d/\lambda)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ordinary End-Fire Array HPBW

**Step 1 — Phasing Condition:**
For ordinary end-fire, $\alpha = -\beta d$, so:

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos \theta - \beta d = \beta d (\cos \theta - 1)
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Location:**
Maximum at $\theta = 0°$, where $\cos \theta = 1$, giving $\psi = 0$. ✓

**Step 3 — Taylor Expansion Near θ = 0°:**
Let $\theta$ be small, so $\cos \theta \approx 1 - \theta^2/2$. Then:

$$
\psi \approx \beta d \left( 1 - \frac{\theta^2}{2} - 1 \right) = -\frac{\beta d \theta^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Half-Power Condition:**
We need $|AF(\theta_h)| = N/\sqrt{2}$. The half-power point occurs approximately at:

$$
\sin(N\psi_h/2) \approx N\psi_h/2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

This gives $N \left\vert \psi_h \right\vert /2 \approx 0.4429$ rad, so $\left\vert \psi_h \right\vert \approx 0.8858/N$.

**Step 5 — Solve for θ_h:**

$$
\frac{\beta d \theta_h^2}{2} = \frac{0.8858}{N}
$$

$$
\theta_h^2 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8858}{N \beta d} = \frac{1.7716}{N \beta d}
$$

**Step 6 — HPBW = 2θ_h:**

$$
HPBW_{\text{end-fire}} = 2 \theta_h = 2 \sqrt{\frac{1.7716}{N \beta d}}
$$

Substituting $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$
HPBW_{\text{end-fire}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{1.7716 \lambda}{2 \pi N d}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.282 \lambda}{N d}}
$$

A common alternate form (using the broadside coefficient 0.886):

$$
HPBW_{\text{end-fire}} \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d}} \text{ (radians)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Directivity of Broadside Array

**Step 1 — Definition of Directivity:**

$$
D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}
$$

where $\Omega_A$ is the beam solid angle.

**Step 2 — Approximate Beam Solid Angle for Narrow Beam:**

$$
\Omega_A \approx \theta_{HP} \cdot \theta_{EP} \text{ (in radians, principal plane approximation)}
$$

For a linear array, the principal E-plane and H-plane beamwidths are:

$$
\Omega_A \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d \sin \theta_0} \cdot \frac{0.886 \lambda}{L_y} = \frac{(0.886)^2 \lambda^2}{N d \sin \theta_0 \cdot L_y}
$$

For a 2-D array with length $L_x = N d$ and width $L_y$ (assuming element pattern is isotropic), the directivity simplifies to:

$$
D_0 \approx \frac{4 \pi N d L_y \sin \theta_0}{0.886^2 \lambda^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Broadside Specialization ($\theta_0 = 90°$, $\sin \theta_0 = 1$):**
Assuming a thin line source ($L_y$ corresponds to single element):

$$
D_{\text{broadside}} \approx \frac{4 \pi N d}{0.886^2 \lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{(\text{element factor normalization})}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Form (Balanis):**
For a uniform broadside array of N isotropic elements with spacing d:

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{broadside}} \approx \frac{2 N d}{\lambda}}
$$

This is the **most-cited result** in KTU exams.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** Design a 10-element uniform broadside array for operation at 1.5 GHz. The elements are isotropic and spaced at $d = \lambda/2$. Calculate (a) HPBW in degrees, (b) Directivity in dB, and (c) First minor lobe level.

**Given:**
- N = 10
- d = λ/2
- f = 1.5 GHz
- λ = c/f = (3 × 10⁸) / (1.5 × 10⁹) = 0.2 m

**Step 1 — Broadside Array Factor:**
For broadside, α = 0, so:

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos \theta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cdot \cos \theta = \pi \cos \theta
$$

**Step 2 — HPBW Calculation:**

$$
HPBW = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d} = \frac{0.886 \times 0.2}{10 \times 0.1} = \frac{0.1772}{1.0} = 0.1772 \text{ rad}
$$

In degrees:

$$
HPBW = 0.1772 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = 10.16°
$$

**Step 3 — Directivity Calculation:**

$$
D = \frac{2 N d}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 10 \times 0.1}{0.2} = 10
$$

In dB:

$$
D_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(10) = 10 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — First Minor Lobe Level:**
For uniform broadside array:

$$
FMLL = -13.46 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify No Grating Lobes:**
Since d = λ/2, the condition $d \leq \lambda/(1 + \vert \sin \theta_0 \vert)$ is satisfied (no grating lobes in visible space).

**Results Summary:**
- HPBW = 10.16°
- Directivity = 10 (10 dB)
- First Minor Lobe = -13.46 dB

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

| Design Goal | Choose | Phasing α | Spacing d | Directivity D |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Wide-angle fan beam | Broadside | 0 | λ/2 ≤ d ≤ λ | $2Nd/\lambda$ |
| Sharp pencil beam | Broadside (2-D) | 0 | λ/2 | $\pi (Nd/\lambda)(M d_y/\lambda)$ |
| Axial beam, simple | End-fire (Ordinary) | $-\beta d$ | $\leq \lambda/2$ | $4Nd/\lambda$ |
| Maximum axial directivity | End-fire (H-W) | $-(\beta d + \pi/N)$ | $\leq \lambda/2$ | $1.789 \times 4Nd/\lambda$ |
| Lowest side lobes | Binomial taper | 0 (B) or $-\beta d$ (EF) | λ/2 | Lower than uniform |
| Equal side lobes | Dolph-Tschebyscheff | Computed | λ/2 to λ | Specified F/B |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Design Flow for Broadside and End-Fire Arrays

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Array Requirements] --> B[Specify N elements]
    B --> C[Choose element spacing d]
    C --> D{Choose Array Type}
    D -- Broadside --> E[Set alpha = 0]
    D -- End-Fire Ordinary --> F[Set alpha = -beta times d]
    D -- End-Fire Hansen-Woodyard --> G[Set alpha = -beta d minus pi by N]
    E --> H[Compute Array Factor AF theta]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Verify single main lobe condition]
    I --> J{Grating lobe check}
    J -- YES --> K[Reduce d to lambda by 2]
    J -- NO --> L[Compute HPBW using formula]
    L --> M[Compute Directivity D0]
    M --> N[Plot AF versus theta]
    N --> O{Design meets specs?}
    O -- YES --> P[FINAL DESIGN READY]
    O -- NO --> Q[Adjust N or d and iterate]
    Q --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Array Radiation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Network with Phase Shifter] --> B[Element 1 at z=0]
    A --> C[Element 2 at z=d]
    A --> D[Element 3 at z=2d]
    A --> E[Element N at z=N-1 times d]
    B --> F[Far Field Region]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{Path difference}
    G -- Broadside path --> H[Constructive interference at theta=90]
    G -- End-fire path --> I[Constructive interference at theta=0 or 180]
    H --> J[Resultant Radiation Pattern]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Beam with HPBW and Sidelobes]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Comparative Schematic

| Block Component | Broadside Configuration | End-Fire Configuration |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Element placement | Linear, along z-axis | Linear, along z-axis |
| Feed phasing network | In-phase corporate feed (0,0,0,...,0) | Progressive phase (-βd, -2βd, ..., -(N-1)βd) |
| Phase shifter requirement | **None** (all in phase) | **Required** (precise progressive delay) |
| Main beam direction | Perpendicular (90° from axis) | Along axis (0° or 180°) |
| Typical spacing | λ/2 to λ | λ/4 to λ/2 (must avoid grating) |
| First null position | at $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\pm\lambda/Nd)$ | Asymmetric — design dependent |
| Application sector | Radar, TV, base stations | Direction finding, homing, Yagi |
| Implementation complexity | **Simpler** | **More complex** (needs phase shifters) |
| Directivity per unit length | Lower (factor 2) | Higher (factor 4 to 7.16) |

## 4.4 Array Geometry Visual Representation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z_AXIS[Array along z axis]
        Z0[Element 1 at z=0]
        Z1[Element 2 at z=d]
        Z2[Element 3 at z=2d]
        Z3[Element N at z=N-1 d]
    end
    Z0 -.phase 0.-> OBS[Observer at angle theta]
    Z1 -.phase beta d cos theta.-> OBS
    Z2 -.phase 2 beta d cos theta.-> OBS
    Z3 -.phase N-1 beta d cos theta.-> OBS
    OBS --> RESULT[Resultant Field E total theta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams represent the **functional flow of signals** through the array system. In a physical lab setting, the elements would be metallic dipoles or patches, and the phase shifters would be realized using transmission line lengths (e.g., for end-fire) or a corporate feed network (e.g., for broadside).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Broadside Array and End-fire Array. Mention the phasing condition for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Broadside Array | End-Fire Array |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Maximum radiation direction | Perpendicular to array axis (θ = 90°) | Along array axis (θ = 0° or 180°) |
| Progressive phase α | α = 0 | α = -βd (Ordinary) or -(βd + π/N) (H-W) |
| Feed arrangement | All elements in phase | Progressive phase delay |
| Typical spacing | λ/2 ≤ d ≤ λ | d ≤ λ/2 |
| Directivity | $2Nd/\lambda$ | $4Nd/\lambda$ (or 1.789 × higher for H-W) |
| Beam shape | Fan beam (wide in one plane) | Pencil/cone along axis |

The **phasing condition** is the key:
- Broadside: $\alpha = 0$ → all elements excited in phase.
- End-fire: $\alpha = -\beta d$ → elements progressively delayed by spatial phase.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is Hansen-Woodyard end-fire array? Why is it preferred over the ordinary end-fire array?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hansen-Woodyard (H-W) end-fire array** is a special end-fire configuration where the progressive phase shift is:

$$
\alpha = -\left(\beta d + \frac{\pi}{N}\right)
$$

This is **larger in magnitude** than the ordinary end-fire phasing ($\alpha = -\beta d$) by an additional $\pi/N$.

**Reasons for preference:**

1. **Higher Directivity:** It provides a directivity enhancement factor of approximately **1.789** (i.e., ~2.53 dB more) compared to the ordinary end-fire array.

2. **Optimized Beam:** The extra phase shift shifts the main beam pattern to concentrate more energy in the end-fire direction, increasing effective aperture.

3. **Trade-off:** Slightly higher first minor lobe level (~-12 dB vs. -13.46 dB).

4. **Application:** Used in long-range communication, direction-finding antennas, and high-gain Yagi-like designs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 4 — Redesigned for 2024 Scheme]**

(a) **Derive the Array Factor for a uniform linear array of N isotropic elements with spacing d and progressive phase α. Show the principal maximum condition and discuss the case of (i) broadside and (ii) end-fire arrays.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **Design a 8-element broadside array operating at 3 GHz with inter-element spacing of λ/2. Calculate the directivity, HPBW, and first minor lobe level. State the spacing condition to avoid grating lobes.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Complete Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**
Consider N isotropic radiators placed along the z-axis at positions $z_n = nd$ (n = 0, 1, ..., N-1). Each element is fed with uniform amplitude A and progressive phase α.

**Step 2 — Field Expression:**
The far field from the n-th element at angle θ is:

$$
E_n(\theta) = \frac{A e^{-j\beta R_n}}{R_n}
$$

where $R_n \approx R_0 - n d \cos \theta$ (far-field approximation).

**Step 3 — Total Field (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \frac{A e^{-j\beta R_0}}{R_0} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n (\beta d \cos \theta + \alpha)}
$$

**Step 4 — Array Factor (1.5 marks):**
Let $\psi = \beta d \cos \theta + \alpha$. The summation is a geometric series:

$$
AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi} = \frac{1 - e^{j N \psi}}{1 - e^{j \psi}} = e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

The magnitude:

$$
|AF(\theta)| = \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert
$$

**Step 5 — Principal Maximum Condition (1 mark):**
Maximum occurs when $\psi = 0$ (denominator → 0, ratio → N). Setting:

$$
\beta d \cos \theta_0 + \alpha = 0
$$

**(i) Broadside Case (1 mark):** $\theta_0 = 90°$, hence $\cos \theta_0 = 0$:

$$
\alpha = 0
$$

All elements in phase.

**(ii) End-Fire Case (1 mark):** $\theta_0 = 0°$, hence $\cos \theta_0 = 1$:

$$
\alpha = -\beta d
$$

Progressive phase delay equal to spatial phase.

---

#### Part (b) — Complete Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- N = 8 elements
- f = 3 GHz → λ = c/f = (3 × 10⁸)/(3 × 10⁹) = 0.1 m = 10 cm
- d = λ/2 = 0.05 m = 5 cm
- Array type: Broadside (α = 0)

**Step 1 — Wavelength (0.5 marks):**
λ = 0.1 m

**Step 2 — Directivity Calculation (2 marks):**
For a broadside array:

$$
D = \frac{2 N d}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 0.05}{0.1} = \frac{0.8}{0.1} = 8
$$

In dB: $D_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(8) = 9.03$ dB

**Step 3 — HPBW Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
HPBW = \frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d} = \frac{0.886 \times 0.1}{8 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.0886}{0.4} = 0.2215 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
HPBW = 0.2215 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = 12.69°
$$

**Step 4 — First Minor Lobe Level (1 mark):**
For a uniform broadside array:

$$
FMLL = -13.46 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Grating Lobe Condition (1.5 marks):**
To avoid grating lobes, the spacing must satisfy:

$$
d \leq \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert \sin \theta_0 \vert}
$$

For broadside, $\theta_0 = 90°$, $\sin \theta_0 = 1$:

$$
d \leq \frac{\lambda}{1 + 1} = \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

Since our d = λ/2, the design is **at the boundary** — just satisfying the no-grating-lobe condition. For safety, d = 0.45λ or d = 0.4λ is often used in practice.

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Directivity: D = 8 (linear) = 9.03 dB
- HPBW: 12.69°
- First Minor Lobe Level: -13.46 dB
- Grating Lobe Condition: d ≤ λ/2 ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 4]**

(a) **Explain the design procedure of an end-fire array with neat diagrams. Derive the HPBW for an ordinary end-fire array.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **A 6-element end-fire array is to be designed for operation at 10 GHz. The array length is $L = 3\lambda$. Calculate: (i) the element spacing, (ii) the progressive phase shift α, (iii) the HPBW in degrees, and (iv) the directivity. Compare the performance with a Hansen-Woodyard end-fire array of the same dimensions.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Complete Model Solution (7 marks)

**End-Fire Array Design Procedure (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the requirement: maximum radiation along array axis (θ = 0° or 180°).

**Step 2:** Choose N (number of elements) based on required gain. Higher N → higher directivity, narrower beam.

**Step 3:** Choose spacing d such that $d \leq \lambda/2$ to suppress grating lobes. (End-fire is more sensitive to grating lobes than broadside because the main beam is at $\cos \theta = \pm 1$.)

**Step 4:** Compute progressive phase shift:
- Ordinary: $\alpha = -\beta d$
- Hansen-Woodyard: $\alpha = -(\beta d + \pi/N)$

**Step 5:** Compute Array Factor:
$$AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$$
with $\psi = \beta d (\cos \theta - 1)$ (for ordinary end-fire).

**Step 6:** Compute HPBW and Directivity. Verify main beam at θ = 0°. Plot pattern.

**HPBW Derivation for Ordinary End-Fire (3 marks):**

For end-fire, $\alpha = -\beta d$:

$$
\psi = \beta d \cos \theta + \alpha = \beta d (\cos \theta - 1)
$$

Near $\theta = 0°$, using $\cos \theta \approx 1 - \theta^2/2$:

$$
\psi \approx -\frac{\beta d \theta^2}{2}
$$

The half-power condition is $\sin(N\psi_h/2)/\sin(\psi_h/2) = N/\sqrt{2}$. For large N, the half-power point is approximately where $N|\psi_h|/2 \approx 0.4429$ rad:

$$
|\psi_h| = \frac{0.8858}{N}
$$

Equating:

$$
\frac{\beta d \theta_h^2}{2} = \frac{0.8858}{N}
$$

$$
\theta_h^2 = \frac{1.7716}{N \beta d} = \frac{1.7716 \lambda}{2 \pi N d}
$$

Therefore:

$$
HPBW = 2 \theta_h = 2 \sqrt{\frac{1.7716 \lambda}{2 \pi N d}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.282 \lambda}{N d}} \text{ (radians)}
$$

**Common alternative form:**

$$
HPBW_{\text{end-fire}} \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d}} \text{ (radians)}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Complete Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- N = 6 elements
- f = 10 GHz → λ = c/f = (3 × 10⁸)/(10 × 10⁹) = 0.03 m = 3 cm
- Total array length L = 3λ = 9 cm

**Step 1 — Element Spacing (1.5 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{3\lambda}{6} = 0.5 \lambda = 1.5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Progressive Phase α (1.5 marks):**
For ordinary end-fire:

$$
\alpha = -\beta d = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = -\pi \text{ rad}
$$

For Hansen-Woodyard:

$$
\alpha_{HW} = -\left(\beta d + \frac{\pi}{N}\right) = -\left(\pi + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) = -\frac{7\pi}{6} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — HPBW in Degrees (2 marks):**
For ordinary end-fire:

$$
HPBW = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.886 \times \lambda}{6 \times 0.5\lambda}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.886}{3}} = 2 \times 0.5433 = 1.0866 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
HPBW = 1.0866 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = 62.27°
$$

For Hansen-Woodyard end-fire:

$$
HPBW_{HW} = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.886 \lambda}{N d + 0.5\lambda}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.886}{3 + 0.5}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.886}{3.5}} = 2 \times 0.5032 = 1.0064 \text{ rad} = 57.66°
$$

**Step 4 — Directivity Comparison (2 marks):**
For ordinary end-fire:

$$
D = \frac{4 N d}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \times 6 \times 0.5\lambda}{\lambda} = 12
$$

In dB: $D_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(12) = 10.79$ dB

For Hansen-Woodyard end-fire:

$$
D_{HW} = 1.789 \times 12 = 21.47
$$

In dB: $D_{HW,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(21.47) = 13.32$ dB

**Performance Comparison:**

| Parameter | Ordinary End-Fire | Hansen-Woodyard |
|:---|:---|:---|
| α | $-\pi$ rad | $-7\pi/6$ rad |
| HPBW | 62.27° | 57.66° (narrower) |
| Directivity | 12 (10.79 dB) | 21.47 (13.32 dB) |
| FMLL | -13.46 dB | ~-12.0 dB |

**Conclusion:** Hansen-Woodyard end-fire provides **13.32 - 10.79 = 2.53 dB** higher directivity (factor 1.789) and a **narrower beam** at the cost of slightly higher minor lobes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing array axis reference angle:** In some textbooks θ is measured from broadside, in others from the array axis. **ALWAYS** clarify in the first line of the answer: "θ is measured from the array axis (z-axis)."
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the end-fire directivity formula:** Students often write $D = 2Nd/\lambda$ for end-fire (which is the broadside formula). **Correct end-fire formula is $D = 4Nd/\lambda$.**
>
> 3. **Not specifying which end-fire variant:** When a problem says "end-fire array," state whether it's ordinary or Hansen-Woodyard. The KTU examiner deducts 0.5-1 mark for ambiguity.
>
> 4. **Grating lobe condition:** For broadside, $d \leq \lambda$ is acceptable. For end-fire, $d \leq \lambda/2$ is mandatory. Writing $d \leq \lambda$ for end-fire is a **common error** and loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **HPBW unit conversion:** Provide HPBW in both **radians and degrees** in the final answer. Examiners often deduct marks for missing unit.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the (N-1)/2 phase factor:** The full AF expression is $e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \cdot \sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)$. The phase factor is often dropped silently — this is correct for the **magnitude pattern** but should be acknowledged in a complete derivation.
>
> 7. **Sign of α for end-fire:** $\alpha = -\beta d$ is correct for maximum at $\theta = 0°$. For maximum at $\theta = 180°$, $\alpha = +\beta d$. Always state the direction.
>
> 8. **Forgetting the $10 \log_{10}$ conversion:** Directivity is often required in dB. $D_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(D)$, NOT $20 \log_{10}$. This is a 0.5-mark error that recurs every year.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Broadside & End-Fire Array Design**

**Core Concepts to Memorize:**

- [x] **Array Factor (AF) is the master equation:** $|AF| = \vert \sin(N\psi/2) / \sin(\psi/2) \vert$, where $\psi = \beta d \cos \theta + \alpha$
- [x] **Phasing Cheat Sheet:** Broadside: α = 0 | Ordinary End-Fire: α = -βd | Hansen-Woodyard: α = -(βd + π/N)
- [x] **HPBW:** Broadside = $0.886\lambda/(Nd)$ rad | Ordinary End-Fire = $2\sqrt{0.886\lambda/(Nd)}$ rad
- [x] **Directivity:** Broadside = $2Nd/\lambda$ | End-Fire = $4Nd/\lambda$ | H-W End-Fire = $1.789 \times 4Nd/\lambda$
- [x] **First Minor Lobe Level:** -13.46 dB (uniform) | -12.0 dB (H-W)
- [x] **Grating Lobe Condition:** Broadside: $d \leq \lambda/(1 + \vert \sin \theta_0 \vert)$ | End-Fire: $d \leq \lambda/2$
- [x] **λ formula:** $\lambda = c/f$ where c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s

**Engineering Intuition Shortcuts:**

- **Higher N** → narrower beam, higher gain, more side lobes (with uniform excitation)
- **Higher d** → narrower main beam BUT grating lobes appear if d > λ/2 (end-fire) or d > λ (broadside)
- **Longer L = Nd** → higher directivity (both cases)
- **H-W phasing** trades side-lobe level (worse) for directivity (better)

**Numerical Problem Workflow:**

1. Read N, d, f → compute λ = c/f, β = 2π/λ
2. Identify array type → set α accordingly
3. Compute HPBW using the appropriate formula
4. Compute D using the appropriate formula → convert to dB
5. Verify grating lobe condition
6. State First Minor Lobe Level

**Exam Day Tips:**

- Always start with a labeled diagram (even a rough sketch)
- Write the general Array Factor before specializing
- Show every step of substitution
- Box the final answers
- Mention units explicitly (radians vs. degrees for HPBW)

**Maximum Marks Strategy (KTU 2024):**
For a 14-mark design problem, the typical breakup is:
- Diagram and array factor derivation: 4 marks
- Phasing condition identification: 2 marks
- HPBW calculation: 3 marks
- Directivity calculation: 3 marks
- Discussion of side lobes / grating lobes: 2 marks

**Single Most Important Formula to Memorize:**

$$
\boxed{AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin\left(\frac{N}{2}(\beta d \cos \theta + \alpha)\right)}{\sin\left(\frac{1}{2}(\beta d \cos \theta + \alpha)\right)}}
$$

This single equation, combined with the right phasing condition, solves 90% of KTU Module 4 problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phased Array Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Phased Array Principle** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phased Array Antenna:** A phased array is a multiple-antenna system in which the relative phase of the **RF signal** fed to each radiating element is varied electronically such that the effective **radiation pattern** of the entire array is reinforced in a desired direction (beam steering) and suppressed in undesired directions — all without any mechanical movement of the antenna structure. The *beam steering* (or *beam squinting*) is achieved by introducing a controlled **progressive phase shift** $\alpha$ between successive elements of the array. The principle relies on **constructive interference** of far-field contributions in the look direction and **destructive interference** elsewhere. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stadium Wave" Imagine a stadium crowd performing the famous *"Mexican wave."* Each person stands up and sits down in sequence, with a small **time delay** between adjacent people. Even though no individual leaves their seat, a wave *appears* to travel around the entire stadium. The direction of the wave is controlled purely by **sequencing the timing** of each person — not by anyone physically moving. A **phased array works exactly the same way:** - Each **antenna element** is a "person in the crowd." - The **phase shifter** at each element controls the *timing* (phase) of its transmission. - By progressively adjusting phase from element to element, the radiated wavefront tilts — pointing the beam in a new direction. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this useful?** > Phased arrays enable **electronic beam scanning** at microsecond speeds — orders of magnitude faster than mechanical gimbals.
