**1. Apex half-angle (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \alpha &= \frac{1 - \tau}{4\sigma} = \frac{1 - 0.88}{4 \times 0.15} = \frac{0.12}{0.60} = 0.20 \\[6pt]
\boxed{\alpha &= \tan^{-1}(0.20) = 11.31^\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Number of elements (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_s &= \frac{f_{\max}}{f_{\min}} = \frac{200}{30} = 6.667 \\[6pt]
N &= 1 + \frac{\log B_s}{\log(1/\tau)} = 1 + \frac{\log(6.667)}{\log(1/0.88)} = 1 + \frac{0.8239}{0.0555} \\[6pt]
N &= 1 + 14.84 = 15.84 \rightarrow \boxed{N = 16 \text{ elements (rounded up)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Longest and shortest elements (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\max} &= \frac{c}{2 f_{\min}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 30 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^7} = 5 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
L_{\min} &= \frac{c}{2 f_{\max}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 200 \times 10^6} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^8} = 0.75 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
\boxed{L_{\max} &= 5.0 \text{ m}, \quad L_{\min} = 0.75 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Length of 4th element (1 mark):**

Using $L_n = L_{\max} \cdot \tau^{(n-1)}$ with longest as element 1:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_4 &= L_{\max} \cdot \tau^{(4-1)} = 5.0 \times (0.88)^3 \\[6pt]
L_4 &= 5.0 \times 0.6815 = 3.407 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
\boxed{L_4 &= 3.41 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**5. Position $R_4$ of 4th element (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_n &= \frac{2\sigma L_n}{1 - \tau} = \frac{2 \times 0.15 \times L_n}{1 - 0.88} = \frac{0.30 \times L_n}{0.12} = 2.5 L_n \\[6pt]
R_4 &= 2.5 \times 3.407 = 8.518 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
\boxed{R_4 &= 8.52 \text{ m from apex}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Design + Concept**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Question]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat schematic, explain the **principle of operation** of a Log-Periodic Dipole Array. Discuss the role of the "active region" and explain how the antenna achieves frequency independence.

**(b) [7 marks]** A Log-Periodic Dipole Array has **12 elements** with $\tau = 0.92$ and operates from **100 MHz to 1 GHz**. Calculate:
1. The structure bandwidth $B_s$ and verify $N$ is sufficient
2. The longest and shortest element lengths
3. The boom length (assume $\sigma = 0.10$)
4. The element bandwidth $B_n$
5. The number of elements in the active region (assuming $B_{ar} = 1.6$)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Principle of Operation (7 marks):**

**Schematic Description (1 mark):**
> Draw a top-view LPDA: a boom (feeder line) with N parallel dipoles of progressively increasing length from the apex to the back. Label the apex (feed point), the longest element $L_{\max}$, the shortest $L_{\min}$, and show the angles $\alpha$ and the geometric ratio $\tau$.

**Principle (4 marks):**

The LPDA operates on the principle of **frequency-dependent current localization**. Although the antenna is geometrically static, the *electrical* behavior is non-stationary:

- At any operating frequency $f$, only those dipoles whose lengths are **near $\lambda/2$** at that frequency carry significant currents. This cluster of 3–5 elements forms the **active region**.
- Elements much shorter than $\lambda/2$ (say, $L < 0.4\lambda$) present high capacitive reactance, so very little current flows — they are essentially invisible to the feed.
- Elements much longer than $\lambda/2$ (say, $L > 1.0\lambda$) operate below resonance. Currents in them are 180° out of phase and are largely cancelled by the radiation from the active region; they also act as **directors** of sorts, narrowing the beam.

As the operating frequency **increases**, the active region **slides toward the shorter elements**; as it **decreases**, the active region slides **toward the longer elements**. This electronic "movement" of the active region is what makes the antenna broadband.

**Frequency Independence (2 marks):**

The geometry is defined by **angles only** (no preferred linear dimension). The scale factor $\tau$ ensures that if you scale the entire antenna by $1/\tau$, it looks exactly the same — only the frequency at which it operates changes by a factor of $\tau$. Therefore, all electrical properties (impedance, gain, pattern) are **periodic functions of $\log f$** with period $\log(1/\tau)$. While the response is not exactly constant within one period, the periodic repetition over many decades of frequency makes the antenna effectively frequency-independent.

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

Given: $N = 12$, $\tau = 0.92$, $f_{\min} = 100$ MHz, $f_{\max} = 1$ GHz, $\sigma = 0.10$.

**1. Structure bandwidth and verification (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_s &= \frac{f_{\max}}{f_{\min}} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10 \\[6pt]
\text{Coverage of N = 12 elements: } \tau^{-(N-1)} = 0.92^{-11} = 2.327 \quad \text{(NOT enough!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Wait — this is a common KTU trap!** With $N = 12, \tau = 0.92$, the array covers only a frequency ratio of $2.33$, not $10$. To cover $B_s = 10$, we need at least $N = 1 + \log(10)/\log(1/0.92) = 1 + 1/0.0362 = 28.6 \rightarrow N = 29$ elements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often compute $B_s$ and stop, missing that the **given $N$ is inconsistent with $B_s$**. In the actual KTU exam, if the question says "$N = 12$ operates from 100 MHz to 1 GHz", the answer should **flag this inconsistency** and either (a) re-compute the achievable bandwidth, or (b) re-compute the required $N$. Examiners reward students who spot design inconsistencies (1–2 marks).

**For continuing the design, let us use the **achievable** bandwidth: $B_s = 0.92^{-11} = 2.33$, i.e., the actual operating range is $f_{\min} = 100$ MHz to $f_{\max} = 233$ MHz.** (Or, treat the design as $f_{\min} = 100$ MHz, and find the actual $f_{\max}$.)

For consistency with the design equations, let us **re-interpret** the problem: assume $N = 12, \tau = 0.92$, $f_{\min} = 100$ MHz, and compute the achievable $f_{\max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\max} &= f_{\min} \cdot \tau^{-(N-1)} = 100 \times 0.92^{-11} = 100 \times 2.327 = 232.7 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. Longest and shortest element lengths (1 mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\max} &= \frac{c}{2 f_{\min}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 100 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
L_{\min} &= \frac{c}{2 f_{\max}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 232.7 \times 10^6} = 0.645 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
\boxed{L_{\max} &= 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad L_{\min} = 0.645 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Boom length (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{boom}} &= \frac{2\sigma L_{\max}}{1-\tau} (1 - \tau^N) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 0.10 \times 1.5}{1 - 0.92} \times (1 - 0.92^{12}) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.30}{0.08} \times (1 - 0.3677) \\[6pt]
&= 3.75 \times 0.6323 \\[6pt]
\boxed{L_{\text{boom}} &= 2.371 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. Element bandwidth $B_n$ (1 mark):**
$$
\boxed{B_n = \tau = 0.92}
$$
(Each element's bandwidth ratio is 0.92, i.e., each element covers from its resonant frequency down to 92% of that frequency.)

**5. Number of elements in the active region (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{ar} &= \tau^{-(k-1)} \quad \text{where } k \text{ is the number of active elements} \\[6pt]
1.6 &= 0.92^{-(k-1)} \\[6pt]
\ln(1.6) &= (k-1) \times (-\ln 0.92) = (k-1) \times 0.0834 \\[6pt]
0.470 &= (k-1) \times 0.0834 \\[6pt]
k - 1 &= 5.63 \\[6pt]
k &= 6.63 \\[6pt]
\boxed{k &\approx 7 \text{ elements (rounded)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Some KTU answer keys round to $k = 6$ or simply state "$k \approx 5$ to $7$ elements" for the active region. Both are accepted.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on LPDA Questions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP $N$:** If the calculation gives $N = 15.84$, the answer is **$N = 16$**, not $15$. Off-by-one element errors cost **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $L_{\max}$ and $L_{\min}$ conventions:** Some textbooks use $L_1 = L_{\max}$, others use $L_1 = L_{\min}$. State your convention explicitly at the start of the answer. Examiners **deduct marks** for ambiguous conventions.
>
> 3. **Using $\sigma = (1-\tau)/(4 \tan \alpha)$ vs $\sigma = (\tau - 0.5)/\tan \alpha$:** These two forms differ by a factor and use different definitions of $\alpha$. Use the form your textbook uses; KTU 2024 Scheme generally uses **Carrel's form** with $\sigma = (\tau - 0.5)/\tan \alpha$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Element lengths in **meters**, frequencies in **Hz**, angles in **degrees** (or radians, but be consistent). The KTU valuation script explicitly checks for unit consistency.
>
> 5. **Skipping the active region discussion:** In **conceptual questions (Part A 3-mark)**, the active region is often worth 1.5–2 marks. Don't just say "wideband antenna" — explicitly mention the **3–5 element active region** and its **sliding behavior**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: LPDA**

- ✅ **LPDA = Log-Periodic Dipole Array** — broadband, frequency-independent antenna
- ✅ **Three design parameters:** $\tau$ (scale factor, 0.7–0.95), $\sigma$ (spacing factor, 0.05–0.20), $\alpha$ (apex half-angle)
- ✅ **Master relation:** $\sigma = (\tau - 0.5)/\tan \alpha$ — given any two, find the third
- ✅ **Number of elements:** $N = 1 + \log(B_s)/\log(1/\tau)$, **always round up**
- ✅ **Structure bandwidth:** $B_s = f_{\max}/f_{\min}$
- ✅ **Longest element:** $L_{\max} = c/(2 f_{\min})$
- ✅ **Shortest element:** $L_{\min} = c/(2 f_{\max})$
- ✅ **n-th element length:** $L_n = L_{\max} \cdot \tau^{(n-1)}$ (or $L_{\min} \cdot \tau^{(N-n)}$)
- ✅ **n-th position from apex:** $R_n = 2\sigma L_n/(1-\tau)$
- ✅ **Boom length:** $L_{\text{boom}} = 2\sigma L_{\max}(1-\tau^N)/(1-\tau)$
- ✅ **Element bandwidth:** $B_n = \tau$
- ✅ **Active region:** **3 to 5 elements** near $\lambda/2$ resonance, $B_{ar} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$
- ✅ **Sliding behavior:** Active region moves to longer elements as $f$ decreases
- ✅ **Beam direction:** Toward the longer elements (back of the array)
- ✅ **Gain:** Typically **7 to 10 dBi** for standard designs
- ✅ **Applications:** TV reception, EMC testing, electronic warfare, spectrum monitoring
- ✅ **Conventions:** Always state whether $L_1$ is the longest or shortest element
- ✅ **Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s** unless otherwise specified
- ✅ **Logarithm base:** KTU uses base-10 (common logarithm) for $\log$
- ✅ **Units check:** $f$ in Hz, $L$ in m, $\alpha$ in degrees

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Helical Antenna: Design Equations, Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Helical Antenna: Design Equations, Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Helical Antenna** (also called the *helix antenna*) is a broadband VHF/UHF antenna consisting of a conducting wire wound in the form of a **helical (spiral) coil** of **N turns**, typically mounted above a ground plane and excited by a coaxial transmission line. It was systematically analyzed by **John D. Kraus (1947)**, and it radiates a circularly polarized wave along the axis of the helix (broadside or end-fire) depending on its geometric proportions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The helical antenna is a *Travelling Wave Antenna* (TWA). It belongs to the family of frequency-independent, broadband radiators used widely in satellite telemetry, GPS, and deep-space communication links (e.g., 1970s lunar missions and modern Inmarsat systems).

### 1.2 Geometric Intuition & Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **slinky spring** stretched out along a vertical pole. Now imagine a small electric current (like a tiny lightning bolt) flowing up and down the coils in a circular zigzag pattern. The spiral geometry causes the current to travel forward (in the axial direction) at a controlled rate — much like a corkscrew pushing a particle forward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Analogy:**
> - The **helix** acts like a *delayed transmission line*: current takes a finite time to climb from the bottom to the top of the spiral.
> - This intentional **delay** shapes the radiated wavefront into a **circularly polarized**, narrow beam along the axis — just like a phased array whose elements are arranged in a circle and progressively phase-shifted.

### 1.3 Standard Helical Antenna Geometry

A standard helix is characterized by the following KTU-defined parameters:

| Symbol | Parameter | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D** | Helix diameter | meters (m) |
| **C** | Circumference of helix: $C = \pi D$ | meters (m) |
| **S** | Spacing between turns (pitch) | meters (m) |
| **N** | Total number of turns | dimensionless |
| **L** | Axial length: $L = N \cdot S$ | meters (m) |
| **α** | Pitch angle | radians (rad) |

The pitch angle $\alpha$ relates the geometry as:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{S}{C} = \frac{S}{\pi D}$$

$$L = N \cdot S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Units:** All lengths are in **meters**, frequencies in **Hz** or **GHz**, and angles in **degrees** or **radians** as explicitly noted in the formula sheet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Radiation of a Helical Antenna

A helix can radiate in three distinct modes depending on the ratio of the helix circumference to the operating wavelength. This ratio, **C/λ**, is the most critical design parameter.

#### Mode 1 — Normal Mode (Broadside Radiation)

- Occurs when: **$C \ll \lambda$** (helix is electrically small; $D \ll \lambda / \pi$).
- The radiation is maximum in the **plane perpendicular to the helix axis** (broadside).
- The pattern is **linearly polarized** in the broadside direction.
- Antenna behaves as a combination of a small loop and a short dipole.
- Very low directivity (≈ 1.5, similar to a short dipole).
- Rarely used in practice because of poor radiation efficiency.

#### Mode 2 — Axial Mode (End-Fire, Circularly Polarized) — MOST IMPORTANT

- Occurs when: **$\frac{3}{4} < \frac{C}{\lambda} < \frac{4}{3}$** (Kraus condition).
- Approximately, when $C \approx \lambda$, i.e., $D \approx \lambda/\pi$.
- The radiation is concentrated in a **narrow beam along the axis of the helix**.
- Produces **circular polarization** (CP) over a wide bandwidth.
- This is the **practical, high-gain mode** used in 90% of real-world helical antenna applications (satellite TV, GPS, telemetry).
- The wave travels along the helix like a travelling-wave current with phase velocity close to the speed of light.

#### Mode 3 — Higher-Order (Conical) Mode

- Occurs when: **$C/\lambda > 1.5$ (approx)** — i.e., helix becomes electrically large.
- The main lobe splits into a **conical pattern** with a null along the axis.
- Higher-order modes ($C/\lambda \approx 2, 3, 4...$) produce doughnut-shaped or multi-lobed patterns tilted away from the axis.
- Useful in specialized multi-beam applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Axial Mode Special?**
> In axial mode, the currents on adjacent turns have a near-equal phase progression. The progressive phase delay $S/\lambda$ in the axial direction, combined with the natural circumferential delay, builds up a **travelling wave** that radiates constructively only along the axis. This is the **fundamental design principle** behind the helical antenna's circular polarization.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Kraus Design Equations for Axial Mode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kraus Design Equations (Axial Mode, CP):** Valid for $\frac{3}{4} < C/\lambda < 4/3$.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW) | $\text{HPBW} = \dfrac{52^\circ}{\frac{C}{\lambda} \sqrt{\dfrac{N \cdot S}{\lambda}}}$ | degrees |
| 2 | Beamwidth Between First Nulls (BWFN) | $\text{BWFN} = \dfrac{115^\circ}{\frac{C}{\lambda} \sqrt{\dfrac{N \cdot S}{\lambda}}}$ | degrees |
| 3 | Directivity (Power Gain approx) | $D_0 = 15 \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \cdot N \cdot \left(\dfrac{S}{\lambda}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Directivity (dB form) | $D_0 \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(15 \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \cdot N \cdot \left(\dfrac{S}{\lambda}\right)\right)$ | dB |
| 5 | Axial Ratio (within HPBW) | $\text{AR} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2N}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Input Impedance (real) | $R_{in} \approx 140 \cdot \dfrac{C}{\lambda} \;\; \Omega$ | ohms |
| 7 | Axial Length | $L = N \cdot S$ | meters |
| 8 | Pitch Angle | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{\pi D}\right)$ | degrees |
| 9 | Phase Velocity (travelling wave) | $v_p \approx \dfrac{c}{\sin\alpha + \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{C/\lambda}}$ | m/s |
| 10 | Helix Circumference | $C = \pi D$ | meters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note (no pipe in tables):** Where absolute values or ratios are required, use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX — never the literal pipe `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of the Helical Antenna

- **Satellite communication** (e.g., TVRO receivers, Inmarsat).
- **Global Positioning System (GPS)** patch/helix receivers.
- **Radio astronomy and deep-space probes** (the Voyager, Pioneer, and Apollo missions used helical arrays).
- **WLAN and IoT devices** operating in the 2.4 GHz and 5.8 GHz ISM bands.
- **Electronic warfare (EW)** for broadband ECM/ECCM systems because of its wide axial-ratio bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Axial Mode HPBW (Balanis Approach)

The half-power beamwidth of a uniform helical array of N isotropic elements spaced by *S* along the z-axis can be derived by approximating the helix as an **N-element linear array** with progressive phase $\beta_0$ between adjacent elements, where:

$$\beta_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot S - \frac{\pi}{N_{\text{turns per cycle}}}$$

In the Hansen-Woodyard end-fire condition, the progressive phase shift is set slightly larger than $kS$ to maximize end-fire directivity, giving:

$$\beta_0 = -\left(kS + \frac{\pi}{N}\right)$$

The normalized array factor for a uniform N-element array is:

$$AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{N\psi}{2}\right)}{N \sin\!\left(\frac{\psi}{2}\right)}$$

where

$$\psi = kS\cos\theta + \beta_0$$

At the half-power point, $|AF|^2 = 1/2$, leading after simplification to:

$$\text{HPBW} = \frac{52^\circ}{\frac{C}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{NS}{\lambda}}}$$

**Step-by-step algebraic expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Set } |AF(\theta)|^2 &= \frac{1}{2} \\
\frac{\sin^2(N\psi/2)}{N^2 \sin^2(\psi/2)} &= \frac{1}{2} \\
\text{Using small-angle expansion near } \theta = 0: \quad \psi &\approx kS(\cos\theta - 1) - \frac{\pi}{N} \\
\text{Let } \theta_0 &= \text{HPBW}/2 \\
\text{Substituting and solving for } \theta_0 &\text{ yields the Kraus constant } 52^\circ.
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Directivity $D_0$ (Kraus 1947)

Kraus obtained the directivity by integrating the radiated far-field power pattern of the helical antenna and comparing it with the total radiated power. The final empirical result, valid for **axial mode**, is:

$$D_0 = 15 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{S}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Why the factor 15?** It comes from numerical evaluation of the Bessel-function-based far-field expressions for a helix and integrating the conical beam over solid angle. Kraus's original work (1947) tabulated it as 12 to 15 depending on the assumed beam shape; **15 is the canonical KTU value**.

**Conversion to dB form:**

$$D_0 \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left[15 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{S}{\lambda}\right)\right]$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Input Impedance

For a helix operating in axial mode, the input impedance is **essentially real (resistive)** and is given empirically by Kraus as:

$$R_{in} \approx 140 \cdot \frac{C}{\lambda} \;\; \Omega$$

**Physical origin:** Each turn of the helix is approximately a one-wavelength loop antenna whose self-impedance is ~140 Ω, and these turns are arranged in parallel to ground. Coupling effects raise the practical value to 140 · C/λ, which for C/λ ≈ 1 gives **R_in ≈ 140 Ω**. Hence a 50 Ω coax feed is matched using a quarter-wave transformer of impedance:

$$Z_{QT} = \sqrt{50 \times 140} \approx 83.7 \; \Omega$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Selection Guide for Helical Mode

| If C/λ is … | Mode | Pattern Shape | Polarization | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| < 0.5 | Normal | Broadside (donut) | Linear | Rare; low-gain |
| 0.75 to 1.33 | **Axial (Kraus)** | **End-fire pencil beam** | **Circular (CP)** | **Practical antennas** |
| 1.5 to 2.0 | Conical 1 | Tilted doughnut | Circular | Specialized |
| > 2.5 | Higher conical | Multi-lobed | Elliptical | Research only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Helical Antenna Feed System

The diagram below maps the **functional interaction** between the helix, its ground plane, the matching network, and the feed line — as required for a frequency-independent broadband radiator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Coaxial Feed Line 50 ohm] --> nodeB[Quarter Wave Impedance Transformer 83.7 ohm]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Helix Base Feed Point]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Helical Spiral N turns]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Traveling Wave Current along Helix]
    nodeE --> nodeF[End Fire Circularly Polarized Radiation Pattern]
    nodeG[Ground Plane Reflector Disc] --- nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeH[Far Field Region Fraunhofer]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Receive Mode Direction Axis Z]
    nodeF --> nodeH
```

### 4.2 Geometry of the Helical Antenna (3D Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Ground Plane Disc GP] --> nodeB[Feed Point at Base]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Turn 1 spacing S diameter D]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Turn 2 spacing S diameter D]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Turn 3 spacing S diameter D]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Turn N spacing S diameter D]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Free End Top of Helix]
    nodeA --- nodeA2[Reflected Image Helix below ground plane]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Pitch Angle alpha from helix axis]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Axial Direction Z is main beam direction]
```

### 4.3 Mode Selection Flowchart (KTU Decision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Choose Operating Wavelength lambda] --> nodeQ1{Compute Circumference C of helix}
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2{Compare C over lambda}
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR1[C over lambda less than 0.5]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR2[C over lambda between 0.75 and 1.33]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeR3[C over lambda greater than 1.5]
    nodeR1 --> nodeM1[Normal Mode broadside linear polarization]
    nodeR2 --> nodeM2[Axial Mode end fire circular polarization]
    nodeR3 --> nodeM3[Conical Mode higher order multi-lobed pattern]
    nodeM2 --> nodeApply[Suitable for satellite and GPS applications]
```

### 4.4 Design Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Step 1 Specify operating frequency f and wavelength lambda] --> nodeS2[Step 2 Select design C over lambda within 0.75 to 1.33]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Step 3 Compute helix diameter D equals lambda over pi]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Step 4 Choose number of turns N for desired gain]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Step 5 Compute pitch angle alpha typically 12 to 14 degrees]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Step 6 Compute spacing S equals pi D tan alpha]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Step 7 Compute HPBW BWFN and Directivity using Kraus formulas]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Step 8 Design matching network 50 to 140 ohm]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[Step 9 Verify Axial Ratio equals 1 plus 1 over 2N]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[Step 10 Final helical antenna ready]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each block represents a functional or mathematical stage in the design of an axial-mode helical antenna following the Kraus methodology. The diagram abstracts the 3D geometry of the helix into a process flow for clarity, since Mermaid cannot render 3D solids directly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*List the different modes of radiation of a helical antenna. State the condition for axial mode of radiation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A helical antenna radiates in three principal modes:
1. **Normal mode** — when $C \ll \lambda$ (broadside radiation, linear polarization).
2. **Axial mode** — when $\frac{3}{4} < \frac{C}{\lambda} < \frac{4}{3}$ (end-fire radiation, circular polarization).
3. **Conical (higher-order) mode** — when $C/\lambda > 1.5$ (tilted doughnut-shaped pattern).

The axial mode is the most practical and provides circular polarization.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define pitch angle of a helical antenna. What is its typical range for axial mode operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The pitch angle $\alpha$ is the angle made by one turn of the helix with the plane perpendicular to its axis. It is given by $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{S}{\pi D}\right)$.

**Typical range for axial mode:** $12^\circ < \alpha < 14^\circ$ (approximately $13^\circ$ is the KTU-accepted optimum).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Helical Antenna Design

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the design equations for **HPBW**, **BWFN**, **Directivity**, **Axial Ratio**, and **Input Impedance** of a helical antenna operating in axial mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a helical antenna operating in axial mode for a frequency of **1.5 GHz** to give a directivity of **14 dB** approximately. Assume the pitch angle to be $13^\circ$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The axial mode helical antenna is analyzed as an *N-element uniform linear array* of isotropic elements with progressive phase $\beta_0 = -kS - \pi/N$ (Hansen–Woodyard condition) so as to enhance the end-fire radiation.

The array factor is:

$$AF(\theta) = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{N \sin(\psi/2)}$$

with $\psi = kS\cos\theta + \beta_0$.

Setting $|AF(\theta)|^2 = 1/2$ at the half-power angle $\theta_0 = \text{HPBW}/2$ and solving yields the **Kraus HPBW formula**:

$$\boxed{\text{HPBW} = \frac{52^\circ}{\frac{C}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{NS}{\lambda}}}}$$

Similarly, setting $|AF(\theta)|^2 = 0$ at the first null gives:

$$\boxed{\text{BWFN} = \frac{115^\circ}{\frac{C}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{NS}{\lambda}}}}$$

The directivity is obtained by integrating the total radiated power over the far-field solid angle. Kraus's empirical result for axial mode is:

$$\boxed{D_0 = 15 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^2 \cdot N \cdot \left(\frac{S}{\lambda}\right)}$$

The **axial ratio** within the HPBW is governed by the geometric current distribution along the helix and is given by:

$$\boxed{\text{AR} = 1 + \frac{1}{2N}}$$

The **input impedance** of a single turn near C/λ ≈ 1 is ~140 Ω, and since the helix is fed at the base, the input impedance is:

$$\boxed{R_{in} \approx 140 \cdot \frac{C}{\lambda} \;\; \Omega}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 1.5$ GHz, $D_0 = 14$ dB, $\alpha = 13^\circ$.

**Step 1: Operating wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^9} = 0.2 \; \text{m} = 20 \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 2: Convert directivity from dB to linear scale**

$$D_0 = 10^{14/10} = 10^{1.4} = 25.12$$

**Step 3: Choose C/λ within axial mode range. Take $C/\lambda = 1$ (optimum).**

So $C = 0.2$ m, and

$$D = \frac{C}{\pi} = \frac{0.2}{\pi} = 0.0637 \; \text{m} = 6.37 \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 4: Compute spacing S using pitch angle $\alpha = 13^\circ$**

$$S = \pi D \tan(\alpha) = \pi \times 0.0637 \times \tan(13^\circ)$$

$$S = 0.2001 \times 0.2309 = 0.0462 \; \text{m} = 4.62 \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 5: Compute $S/\lambda$**

$$\frac{S}{\lambda} = \frac{0.0462}{0.2} = 0.231$$

**Step 6: Solve for N using the directivity equation**

$$D_0 = 15 \cdot (C/\lambda)^2 \cdot N \cdot (S/\lambda)$$

$$25.12 = 15 \cdot (1)^2 \cdot N \cdot 0.231$$

$$25.12 = 3.465 \cdot N$$

$$N = \frac{25.12}{3.465} \approx 7.25 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \boxed{N = 7 \text{ turns}}$$

**Step 7: Axial length**

$$L = N \cdot S = 7 \times 0.0462 = 0.323 \; \text{m} = 32.3 \; \text{cm}$$

**Step 8: Input impedance**

$$R_{in} = 140 \times (C/\lambda) = 140 \times 1 = 140 \; \Omega$$

**Step 9: Axial Ratio**

$$\text{AR} = 1 + \frac{1}{2N} = 1 + \frac{1}{14} = 1.071$$

**Final Design Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Operating Frequency | 1.5 GHz |
| Wavelength $\lambda$ | 0.2 m |
| Helix Diameter $D$ | 6.37 cm |
| Pitch Angle $\alpha$ | $13^\circ$ |
| Turn Spacing $S$ | 4.62 cm |
| Number of Turns $N$ | 7 |
| Axial Length $L$ | 32.3 cm |
| Directivity | 14 dB |
| Input Impedance | 140 Ω |
| Axial Ratio | 1.071 |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Modes of a Helical Antenna

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the different **modes of radiation** of a helical antenna with neat diagrams. What are the conditions under which each mode occurs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A helical antenna has $N = 12$ turns, turn spacing $S = 0.12\lambda$, and circumference $C = \lambda$. Compute **(i)** HPBW, **(ii)** BWFN, **(iii)** Directivity (linear and dB), **(iv)** Axial Ratio, and **(v)** Input Impedance. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

(Draw and label the helix with diameter D, spacing S, axial length L, and pitch angle α. Discuss normal, axial, and conical modes as in Section 2.1 of these notes. State the conditions for each mode clearly.)

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 12$, $S = 0.12\lambda$, $C = \lambda$.

**(i) HPBW:**

$$\text{HPBW} = \frac{52^\circ}{(C/\lambda)\sqrt{NS/\lambda}} = \frac{52^\circ}{1 \cdot \sqrt{12 \times 0.12}}$$

$$\sqrt{12 \times 0.12} = \sqrt{1.44} = 1.2$$

$$\text{HPBW} = \frac{52^\circ}{1.2} = 43.33^\circ$$

**(ii) BWFN:**

$$\text{BWFN} = \frac{115^\circ}{1.2} = 95.83^\circ$$

**(iii) Directivity:**

$$D_0 = 15 \cdot (1)^2 \cdot 12 \cdot 0.12 = 15 \cdot 1.44 = 21.6$$

$$D_0 \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(21.6) = 13.34 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Axial Ratio:**

$$\text{AR} = 1 + \frac{1}{2N} = 1 + \frac{1}{24} = 1.0417$$

**(v) Input Impedance:**

$$R_{in} = 140 \cdot (C/\lambda) = 140 \times 1 = 140 \; \Omega$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| HPBW | $43.33^\circ$ |
| BWFN | $95.83^\circ$ |
| Directivity (linear) | 21.6 |
| Directivity (dB) | 13.34 dB |
| Axial Ratio | 1.0417 |
| Input Impedance | 140 Ω |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing Normal and Axial Modes:** Many students write "axial mode occurs when C/λ < 1." **Wrong.** The correct Kraus condition is $\frac{3}{4} < \frac{C}{\lambda} < \frac{4}{3}$.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in HPBW Formula:** The constant 52° assumes C, S, λ all in **same units**. Mixing cm and m without conversion = full mark deduction.
> 3. **Pitch Angle Range:** A common answer writes $\alpha = 45^\circ$. The KTU-accepted optimum is **12° to 14°**; values outside this destroy the axial-mode condition.
> 4. **dB vs Linear Directivity:** Forgetting to convert $D_0$ from linear to dB form in numerical problems is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Neglecting the 50 Ω to 140 Ω Matching Network:** Real helical antennas need a **quarter-wave transformer** of $\sqrt{50 \times 140} \approx 83.7 \; \Omega$. Skipping this in design = 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Polarization Mistake:** Students often write "axial mode gives linear polarization." It is **circular polarization (CP)** in axial mode — this is a 2-mark blunder.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 — Helical Antenna):**

- **Helical antenna = Travelling Wave Antenna (TWA)** with a **circular polarization** property in axial mode.
- **Three modes:** Normal (broadside, $C \ll \lambda$), **Axial (end-fire, $0.75 < C/\lambda < 1.33$)**, Conical ($C/\lambda > 1.5$).
- **Axial mode condition:** $0.75 < C/\lambda < 1.33$, ideally $C/\lambda \approx 1$.
- **Pitch angle range:** $12^\circ < \alpha < 14^\circ$.
- **HPBW:** $\text{HPBW} = \frac{52^\circ}{(C/\lambda)\sqrt{NS/\lambda}}$.
- **BWFN:** $\text{BWFN} = \frac{115^\circ}{(C/\lambda)\sqrt{NS/\lambda}}$.
- **Directivity:** $D_0 = 15 \cdot (C/\lambda)^2 \cdot N \cdot (S/\lambda)$.
- **Axial Ratio:** $\text{AR} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2N}$ — approaches 1 (perfect CP) as $N \to \infty$.
- **Input Impedance:** $R_{in} \approx 140 \cdot (C/\lambda) \; \Omega$ — match to 50 Ω via $\lambda/4$ transformer of ~83.7 Ω.
- **Axial Length:** $L = N \cdot S$.
- **Pitch Angle:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{\pi D}\right)$.
- **Circumference:** $C = \pi D$.
- **Kraus constant 15** is empirical; do not derive it in exams — just state the formula.
- **Bandwidth:** Wide (typically 2:1) — frequency-independent behavior.
- **Applications:** Satellite TV, GPS, deep-space missions, telemetry, WLAN.
- **Feed:** Coaxial cable with inner conductor connected to helix base and outer to the ground plane.
- **Polarization sense:** Right-hand CP if helix wound clockwise (viewed from feed); left-hand CP if wound counter-clockwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microstrip Rectangular Patch Antennas - Design Equations, Important Feeding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microstrip Rectangular Patch Antennas - Design Equations, Important Feeding Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Microstrip Rectangular Patch Antenna (MRPA)** is a low-profile, planar resonant antenna fabricated using printed circuit technology. It consists of a flat rectangular metallic *patch* (typically copper or gold) of dimensions $W \times L$ etched on one side of a thin, grounded dielectric substrate of thickness $h$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. The opposite side of the substrate is fully metallized to form the **ground plane**. The patch operates as a **half-wavelength ($\lambda/2$) microstrip resonator**, radiating primarily from the fringing fields along its two radiating edges (the width $W$ edges). It is formally classified under the *Printed Antennas* family in the IEEE antenna taxonomy and is the most widely studied configuration in the KTU Module-4 syllabus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to master the standard analytical design equations (Bahl & Bhartia / Balanis formulation), the effective length concept, and the four canonical feeding techniques: **Microstrip Line Feed, Coaxial (Probe) Feed, Aperture-Coupled Feed, and Proximity-Coupled (Electromagnetically Coupled) Feed.** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tin Roof on a Foam Bed" Imagine placing a small, flat copper plate (the **patch**) on top of a thin slab of foam (the **dielectric substrate**). Beneath the foam is another large copper sheet (the **ground plane**). Now, suppose you pump a high-frequency alternating current into the top plate. The electromagnetic field has nowhere to go sideways — the foam confines it — but at the **two open ends** of the plate, the field "leaks" or "fringes" out into free space, radiating radio waves. This leakage is not a defect; it *is* the antenna's mechanism! The patch is essentially a controlled "leaky resonator." The two radiating edges behave like two **in-phase slot antennas** spaced $\lambda/2$ apart, producing a broadside radiation pattern (maximum radiation perpendicular to the patch surface). > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** The dielectric substrate acts like the *voice box* of the antenna — it controls how much energy escapes (radiates) versus how much is trapped. A **thicker, lower-$\varepsilon_r$** substrate = more radiation (higher gain, wider bandwidth). A **thinner, higher-$\varepsilon_r$** substrate = tighter confinement (more compact, but narrowband). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall) - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega$ - **Standard substrate thickness range:** $h = 0.003\lambda_0$ to $0.05\lambda_0$ (commonly $h \approx 1.6$ mm on FR4 for 2–3 GHz designs) - **Common dielectric materials:** RT-Duroid ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2.2$), FR4 epoxy ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4.4$), Rogers RO4003 ($\varepsilon_r \approx 3.38$), Alumina ($\varepsilon_r \approx 9.8$) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Transmission Line Model (TLM) The most intuitive analytical model treats the rectangular patch as a **microstrip transmission line of length $L$ and width $W$** that resonates in the dominant $TM_{010}$ mode. The model assumes: 1. The patch-to-ground separation $h \ll \lambda_0$ (so only vertical fields exist between patch and ground). 2. The field is uniform along the width $W$ (no variation in the $y$-direction). 3. Radiation occurs only from the two **radiating slots** at the ends of length $L$ (the edges $x=0$ and $x=L$). The two slots are separated by approximately $\lambda_g/2$ (where $\lambda_g = \lambda_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}$ is the guided wavelength), so they radiate **in-phase** along the broadside direction ($+z$), reinforcing each other. Along the end-fire direction ($\pm x$), the fields cancel (out of phase). This produces the canonical **broadside pattern** with a deep null at grazing angles. ### 2.2 Why the "Effective" Length Matters — The Fringing Effect A naive engineer might think the patch length is simply $L = \lambda_g/2 = c/(2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$. **This is wrong.** The fringing fields at the radiating edges make the patch *electrically longer* than its physical length. To compensate, we must use: - An **effective dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_{reff}$ (lower than $\varepsilon_r$, because some field exists in the air above the substrate). - A **length extension** $\Delta L$ on each side, capturing the fringing. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every equation** expected in KTU 2024 ESE questions on rectangular patch design. Memorize the design sequence: **$W \to \varepsilon_{reff} \to L_{eff} \to \Delta L \to L \to$ feed position**. | # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units | |---|-----------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | **Patch Width $W$** | $W = \dfrac{c}{2f_r}\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_r+1}}$ | Sets impedance level and radiation efficiency. $f_r$ = resonant frequency (Hz). | | 2 | **Effective Dielectric Constant $\varepsilon_{reff}$** | $\varepsilon_{reff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r+1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r-1}{2}\left[1 + 12\dfrac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2}$ | Dimensionless, $1 < \varepsilon_{reff} < \varepsilon_r$. | | 3 | **Effective Length $L_{eff}$** | $L_{eff} = \dfrac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}}$ | Length if the patch were in a uniform medium of $\varepsilon_{reff}$. (meters) | | 4 | **Normalized Length Extension $\Delta L$** | $\Delta L = 0.412h \cdot \dfrac{(\varepsilon_{reff}+0.3)(W/h+0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{reff}-0.258)(W/h+0.8)}$ | Extra electrical length per radiating edge due to fringing. (meters) | | 5 | **Actual Patch Length $L$** | $L = L_{eff} - 2\Delta L$ | The physical dimension to be fabricated. (meters) | | 6 | **Resonant Frequency (validation)** | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2L\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}}$ | Used to confirm the design. (Hz) | | 7 | **Resonant Input Resistance $R_{in}$** | $R_{in}(y_0) = R_{in}(0)\cos^2\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L}y_0\right)$, where $R_{in}(0) \approx 90\ \Omega$ for $W \ll \lambda_0$ | Input impedance seen at inset feed position $y_0$. (Ohms) | | 8 | **Ground Plane Dimensions (rule of thumb)** | $L_g \geq L + 6h$, $W_g \geq W + 6h$ | To approximate infinite ground plane and avoid pattern distortion. (meters) | | 9 | **Conductivity Loss Tangent (substrate)** | $\tan\delta$ | Appears in efficiency formulas; FR4 $\tan\delta \approx 0.02$. | | 10 | **Free-space wavelength** | $\lambda_0 = c/f_r$ | For quick sanity checks. (meters) | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GPS antennas (1.575 GHz):** Use high-$\varepsilon_r$ ceramic substrates (e.g., $\varepsilon_r \approx 20$) to shrink the patch to < 25 mm. - **5G mmWave arrays (28/38 GHz):** Rectangular patches form the unit cell of phased arrays on low-loss Rogers laminates. - **Satellite TV reception (10–12 GHz):** Aperture-coupled patches dominate due to their clean polarization purity and low feed radiation. - **Wi-Fi routers (2.4 / 5.8 GHz):** Cost-driven FR4 substrate with probe-fed patches. - **RFID tags:** Compact, near-resonant patches on flexible substrates. The design equations above are the **starting point of every PCB antenna layout** — a CAD tool (ANSYS HFSS, CST Studio) then refines them via full-wave FEM/FDTD simulation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Patch Width $W$ **Starting Premise:** A practical antenna designer wants the highest possible radiation efficiency and a reasonable bandwidth. It can be shown (Balanis, *Antenna Theory*, Ch. 14) that the **radiation conductance per unit width** is maximized when the patch is a *wide* resonator. Hence, a design rule is selected such that $W = \lambda_0/2$ at the *effective* velocity, but rescaled by the permittivity of the surrounding medium. **Step 1 — Free-space half-wavelength:** $$ \frac{\lambda_0}{2} = \frac{c}{2f_r} $$ **Step 2 — Weighted average permittivity of the fields:** The fields in the patch reside partly in the substrate ($\varepsilon_r$) and partly in the air above it ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). For $W \gg h$, the average is dominated by the substrate, but for the initial $W$ estimate, the **arithmetic mean** of the two regimes is used: $$ \varepsilon_{r,\text{avg}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Combine to get the design $W$:** $$ W = \frac{c}{2f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r+1}} $$ > **Textual Logic:** The factor $\sqrt{2/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$ arises because the *effective* electrical length of a resonator depends on $\sqrt{\varepsilon}$ — so taking the inverse-square root of the mean permittivity yields the correct geometric length for the desired $f_r$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Dielectric Constant $\varepsilon_{reff}$ **Physical Picture:** Microstrip lines support a quasi-TEM mode where the electric field lines are partly in the dielectric (below the strip) and partly in the air (above). The *filling factor* of the field in the substrate increases as $W/h$ increases. **Step 1 — Quasi-static approximation (Wheeler / Schneider):** $$ \varepsilon_{reff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\cdot F(W/h) $$ **Step 2 — Form factor $F(W/h)$ for $W/h > 1$ (which is always true for practical patches):** $$ F\left(\frac{W}{h}\right) = \left[1 + 12\frac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2} $$ **Step 3 — Substituting:** $$ \varepsilon_{reff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left[1 + 12\frac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** When $W/h \to \infty$, $F \to 1$ and $\varepsilon_{reff} \to \varepsilon_r$ (field fully in substrate). When $W/h \to 0$, $F \to 0$ and $\varepsilon_{reff} \to (\varepsilon_r+1)/2$ (half-air, half-substrate). ### 3.3 Derivation of the Length Extension $\Delta L$ **Step 1 — The fringing capacitance model:** The fringing fields at the radiating edges can be modeled as a **capacitive load** $\Delta L$ that extends the electrical length of the patch. Hammerstad's empirical formula captures this: $$ \Delta L = 0.412h \cdot \frac{(\varepsilon_{reff}+0.3)(W/h+0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{reff}-0.258)(W/h+0.8)} $$ **Step 2 — Effective electrical length:** The patch now behaves *as if* its length were: $$ L_{eff} = L + 2\Delta L $$ **Step 3 — Resonance condition:** For the $TM_{010}$ mode, $L_{eff} = \lambda_g/2 = c/(2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}})$. **Step 4 — Solve for the physical length:** $$ L = L_{eff} - 2\Delta L = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}} - 2\Delta L $$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem) > **Problem:** Design a rectangular patch antenna for $f_r = 2.4$ GHz on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $h = 1.6$ mm, $\tan\delta = 0.0009$. Compute $W$, $\varepsilon_{reff}$, $\Delta L$, and $L$. **Step 1 — Constants:** $$ c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}, \quad \frac{c}{2f_r} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.0625 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $W$:** $$ W = 0.0625 \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{2.2+1}} = 0.0625 \times \sqrt{0.625} = 0.0625 \times 0.7906 $$ $$ W = 0.04941 \text{ m} = 49.41 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\varepsilon_{reff}$:** $$ \frac{h}{W} = \frac{1.6}{49.41} = 0.0324 $$ $$ F = (1 + 12 \times 0.0324)^{-1/2} = (1.3888)^{-1/2} = 0.8486 $$ $$ \varepsilon_{reff} = \frac{2.2+1}{2} + \frac{2.2-1}{2}\times 0.8486 = 1.6 + 0.5092 = 2.1092 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $\Delta L$:** $$ \frac{W}{h} = \frac{49.41}{1.6} = 30.88 $$ $$ \Delta L = 0.412 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{(2.1092+0.3)(30.88+0.264)}{(2.1092-0.258)(30.88+0.8)} $$ $$ = 0.6592 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{(2.4092)(31.144)}{(1.8512)(31.68)} $$ $$ = 0.6592 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{75.022}{58.646} = 0.6592 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2792 $$ $$ \Delta L = 0.8432 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.8432 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $L_{eff}$:** $$ L_{eff} = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}} = \frac{0.0625}{\sqrt{2.1092}} = \frac{0.0625}{1.4523} = 0.04304 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6 — Compute physical $L$:** $$ L = L_{eff} - 2\Delta L = 43.04 - 2(0.8432) = 43.04 - 1.686 = 41.35 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 7 — Sanity check (resonant frequency):** $$ f_r = \frac{c}{2L\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.04135 \times 1.4523} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{0.1201} = 2.498 \text{ GHz} $$ The slight offset (2.498 vs 2.4 GHz) is normal — iteration between $W$ and $\varepsilon_{reff}$ (since $W$ depends on $\varepsilon_r$ and $\varepsilon_{reff}$ depends on $W$) converges in 2–3 cycles. The first-pass answer is **acceptable for a 14-mark KTU problem**. ### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Feeding Method to Choose? | Criterion | Microstrip Line | Coaxial Probe | Aperture-Coupled | Proximity-Coupled | |-----------|:--------------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|:-----------------:| | **Fabrication complexity** | ★ (Easy) | ★ (Easy) | ★★★ (Hard) | ★★★ (Hard) | | **Soldering required?** | No (etched) | Yes (pin) | No | No | | **Bandwidth** | 2–5% | 1–2% | 5–10% (best) | 5–13% | | **Spurious feed radiation** | High | Moderate | **Very Low** | Low | | **Polarization purity** | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** | Good | | **Suitable for arrays** | Yes | Difficult | **Yes (best)** | Yes | | **Fabrication cost** | Lowest | Low | High | High | | **Substrate requirement** | Single | Single | **Two (dual-layer)** | Two (dual-layer) | | **KTU exam popularity** | High | High | Medium | Medium | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Physical Geometry of a Rectangular Patch Antenna > **Note to Student:** A photorealistic drawing of the radiating patch is impossible in Mermaid. Instead, I provide a **block-level functional architecture flow** that captures the geometry, the field distribution, and the four feeding methods in one integrated view. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph G1["GEOMETRY LAYER (Top View and Cross-Section)"] A1["Patch conductor: W x L rectangle
on top of dielectric substrate"] A2["Dielectric substrate
epsilon_r, thickness h"] A3["Ground plane
full metallization on bottom face"] A1 --- A2 --- A3 end subgraph G2["FIELD DISTRIBUTION (TM010 Mode)"] B1["E-field is maximum at edges x=0 and x=L
zero at center x=L/2"] B2["H-field is maximum at center x=L/2
zero at radiating edges"] B3["Current flows along the length L
forms a half-wave standing wave"] B1 --- B2 --- B3 end subgraph G3["FOUR FEEDING METHODS"] C1["METHOD 1: Microstrip Line Feed
Edge-fed, etched strip of width w_f
notch or inset for matching"] C2["METHOD 2: Coaxial Probe Feed
Inner pin from below through ground
connects to patch at feed point y0"] C3["METHOD 3: Aperture-Coupled Feed
Two substrates separated by ground
slot in ground couples microstrip below to patch above"] C4["METHOD 4: Proximity-Coupled Feed
Microstrip line ends under the patch
no slot, capacitive coupling only"] end subgraph G4["RADIATION MECHANISM"] D1["Fringing fields at the two W-edges
behave as two in-phase slot antennas"] D2["Slots spaced lambda_g/2 apart
produce broadside pattern"] D3["Maximum radiation along +z axis
(perpendicular to patch surface)"] D1 --- D2 --- D3 end G1 --> G2 G2 --> G3 G3 --> G4 C1 --> C5["Easy fab, narrow BW, spurious radiation"] C2 --> C6["Easy fab, narrow BW, easy matching"] C3 --> C7["Hard fab, wide BW, clean polarization"] C4 --> C8["Hard fab, wide BW, capacitive coupling"] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the Four Feeding Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph M1["METHOD 1: Microstrip Line"] M1A["Source"] --> M1B["50 ohm microstrip line of width w_f"] M1B --> M1C["Inset cut on patch edge for matching"] M1C --> M1D["Radiating patch"] end subgraph M2["METHOD 2: Coaxial Probe"] M2A["Source"] --> M2B["Coaxial connector beneath ground"] M2B --> M2C["Inner pin penetrates substrate"] M2C --> M2D["Soldered to patch at y0 position"] M2D --> M2E["Radiating patch"] end subgraph M3["METHOD 3: Aperture-Coupled"] M3A["Source"] --> M3B["Microstrip feedline on lower substrate"] M3B --> M3C["Slot etched in shared ground plane"] M3C --> M3D["Radiating patch on upper substrate"] end subgraph M4["METHOD 4: Proximity-Coupled"] M4A["Source"] --> M4B["Open-ended microstrip line on lower substrate"] M4B --> M4C["Capacitive coupling through dielectric"] M4C --> M4D["Radiating patch on upper substrate (no slot in ground)"] end ``` ### 4.3 Resonant Resistance Variation Along the Patch (Feed Positioning) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Radiating edge x=0
R_in = 90 to 200 ohm"] --> B["Moving inward along L
R_in decreases as cos-squared of pi y0 / L"] B --> C["Center x=L/2
R_in = 0 ohm
voltage null"] C --> D["Other radiating edge x=L
R_in returns to 90 to 200 ohm"] D --> E["Feed position y0 selected such that
R_in y0 equals 50 ohm
for standard connector match"] ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram of the Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["STEP 1: Inputs
f_r, epsilon_r, h, desired Z_in"] --> S2["STEP 2: Calculate W
W = c over 2 f_r times sqrt of 2 over epsilon_r + 1"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Calculate epsilon_reff
Hammerstad formula"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Calculate L_eff
L_eff = c over 2 f_r times sqrt of epsilon_reff"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Calculate delta L
Hammerstad extension formula"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Calculate L
L = L_eff minus 2 delta L"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Choose feed method
and compute feed position y0"] S7 --> S8["STEP 8: Validate via simulation
HFSS or CST full-wave solver"] S8 --> S9["STEP 9: Iterate
if f_r shifted, adjust L"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a microstrip patch antenna. List any **three** advantages and **two** limitations.* **Model Answer:** A microstrip patch antenna is a low-profile printed antenna consisting of a flat metallic patch of dimensions $W \times L$ on a grounded dielectric substrate of thickness $h$ and permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. It radiates primarily from fringing fields at its edges. **Three Advantages:** 1. Low profile and light weight — conformal to curved surfaces (aircraft, missiles). 2. Easy and inexpensive to fabricate using standard photolithographic PCB processes. 3. Compatible with MMIC and active-circuit integration (amplifiers, phase shifters on the same substrate). **Two Limitations:** 1. **Narrow bandwidth** (typically 2–5%) — a major drawback for broadband applications. 2. **Low power handling capacity** and **low gain** (typically 6–8 dBi) — the small separation $h$ limits the aperture. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for advantages, 1 for limitations)* --- **Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is **effective dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_{reff}$? Why is it **less than** $\varepsilon_r$?* **Model Answer:** The effective dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{reff}$ is the equivalent uniform permittivity of a hypothetical homogeneous medium that produces the same phase velocity as the quasi-TEM mode in the actual microstrip structure (where fields exist partly in air and partly in the substrate). $$ \varepsilon_{reff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r+1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r-1}{2}\left[1+12\frac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2} $$ **Why $\varepsilon_{reff} < \varepsilon_r$:** Because the electric field lines of the patch's resonant mode do not lie entirely within the substrate — a significant portion of the field extends into the **air region** above the patch (where $\varepsilon_r = 1$). Since $\varepsilon_{reff}$ is a *weighted average* of the two permittivities, it must lie strictly between 1 and $\varepsilon_r$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for explanation with formula)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Derive the **complete set of design equations** for a rectangular microstrip patch antenna operating in the $TM_{010}$ mode. Explain the role of $\Delta L$ and $\varepsilon_{reff}$ in the design. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A rectangular patch antenna is to be designed for a resonant frequency of **2.5 GHz** using a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, $h = 1.6$ mm. Compute $W$, $\varepsilon_{reff}$, $\Delta L$, and the physical length $L$. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation of Design Equations:** **Step 1 — Choose patch width $W$:** For maximum radiation and good efficiency, the width is selected as the half-wavelength of a medium with permittivity $(\varepsilon_r+1)/2$: $$ W = \frac{c}{2f_r}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\varepsilon_r+1}} \quad \text{...(1)} $$ **Step 2 — Effective dielectric constant:** Because the field is partly in the substrate and partly in air, the *quasi-static* filling factor yields: $$ \varepsilon_{reff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r+1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r-1}{2}\left[1+12\frac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2} \quad \text{...(2)} $$ **Step 3 — Effective length:** A patch in a uniform medium of $\varepsilon_{reff}$ resonates at $L_{eff} = \lambda_g/2$: $$ L_{eff} = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}} \quad \text{...(3)} $$ **Step 4 — Length extension $\Delta L$:** Fringing at the two radiating edges adds an *electrical extension* of $\Delta L$ at each end, modeled by Hammerstad's empirical relation: $$ \Delta L = 0.412h \cdot \frac{(\varepsilon_{reff}+0.3)(W/h+0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{reff}-0.258)(W/h+0.8)} \quad \text{...(4)} $$ **Step 5 — Actual length:** Subtracting the two extensions from $L_{eff}$: $$ L = L_{eff} - 2\Delta L \quad \text{...(5)} $$ **Role of $\varepsilon_{reff}$:** It captures the *inhomogeneous* (air + dielectric) field environment of the patch in a single parameter, enabling a uniform-medium transmission-line model. **Role of $\Delta L$:** It compensates for the fact that fringing fields make the patch *electrically longer* than its physical length. Without subtracting $2\Delta L$, the design would resonate at a *lower* frequency than desired. *(7 marks: 2 for $W$ derivation, 2 for $\varepsilon_{reff}$, 2 for $L$ and $\Delta L$, 1 for explanation of roles)* --- **(b) Numerical Computation:** Given: $f_r = 2.5$ GHz, $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, $h = 1.6$ mm = $1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m. **Step 1 — Compute $W$:** $$ \frac{c}{2f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.06 \text{ m} $$ $$ W = 0.06 \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{4.4+1}} = 0.06 \times \sqrt{0.3704} = 0.06 \times 0.6086 $$ $$ \boxed{W = 0.03652 \text{ m} = 36.52 \text{ mm}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\varepsilon_{reff}$:** $$ \frac{h}{W} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03652} = 0.04381 $$ $$ 1 + 12 \times 0.04381 = 1.526 \implies (1.526)^{-1/2} = 0.8095 $$ $$ \varepsilon_{reff} = \frac{4.4+1}{2} + \frac{4.4-1}{2} \times 0.8095 = 2.7 + 1.3762 $$ $$ \boxed{\varepsilon_{reff} = 4.076} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\Delta L$:** $$ \frac{W}{h} = \frac{0.03652}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 22.825 $$ $$ \Delta L = 0.412 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{(4.076+0.3)(22.825+0.264)}{(4.076-0.258)(22.825+0.8)} $$ $$ = 6.592 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{(4.376)(23.089)}{(3.818)(23.625)} = 6.592 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{101.04}{90.20} $$ $$ = 6.592 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.1202 = 7.385 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta L = 0.7385 \text{ mm}} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $L_{eff}$:** $$ L_{eff} = \frac{0.06}{\sqrt{4.076}} = \frac{0.06}{2.019} = 0.02972 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $L$:** $$ L = 0.02972 - 2 \times 7.385 \times 10^{-4} = 0.02972 - 0.001477 = 0.02824 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{L = 28.24 \text{ mm}} $$ *(7 marks: 1 for $W$, 2 for $\varepsilon_{reff}$, 2 for $\Delta L$, 2 for $L$ — full marks only if all numerical steps are shown)* --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **four major feeding techniques** used for microstrip patch antennas. Compare their **bandwidth**, **fabrication complexity**, and **suitability for arrays**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **concept of inset feed** in a microstrip line-fed patch antenna. How is the feed inset depth $y_0$ chosen to achieve a 50 $\Omega$ input impedance? *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a) Four Feeding Techniques:** **1. Microstrip Line Feed (Edge Feed):** The patch is fed directly by a microstrip line of width $w_f$ etched on the same substrate. The feed point can be at the edge (giving 100–400 $\Omega$ input resistance) or **inset** (see part b). Easy to fabricate, but narrow bandwidth and spurious feed-line radiation distort the pattern. **2. Coaxial (Probe) Feed:** A coaxial connector is mounted beneath the ground plane. The **inner conductor** protrudes through a hole in the substrate and is soldered to the patch at a chosen position $y_0$. The outer conductor bonds to the ground plane. Allows easy impedance matching by varying $y_0$, but drilling and soldering are required; the probe introduces **inductance** that limits bandwidth (typically 1–2%). **3. Aperture-Coupled Feed (David Pozar / Bialkowski design):** A **two-substrate structure** is used. The lower substrate carries the open-ended microstrip feedline; the upper substrate carries the patch. A small **slot** (typically rectangular, of dimensions $L_s \times W_s$) etched in the shared ground plane electromagnetically couples energy from the feedline to the patch. The slot length is roughly $\lambda_s/2$ (slot resonant). This method **isolates the feed network from the radiator**, yielding: - **Bandwidth 5–10%** (the widest of all four). - **Excellent polarization purity** (low cross-polarization). - **No spurious feed radiation** in the back hemisphere. **4. Proximity-Coupled (Electromagnetically Coupled) Feed:** Also a two-substrate structure, but **no slot** in the ground. The microstrip feedline on the lower substrate is **open-circuited** and terminated beneath the patch; energy is transferred via **capacitive (fringing) coupling** through the thin upper substrate. Achieves **5–13% bandwidth** (often the widest of the four), but the two-substrate alignment is critical during fabrication. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Microstrip Line | Coaxial Probe | Aperture-Coupled | Proximity-Coupled | |--------|----------------|---------------|------------------|-------------------| | Bandwidth | 2–5% | 1–2% | 5–10% | 5–13% | | Fabrication | Easiest | Easy (drilling) | Hard (alignment, two layers) | Hard (two layers) | | Array suitability | Good | Poor (probe height issues) | **Excellent** | Good | | Spurious radiation | High | Moderate | **Negligible** | Low | *(7 marks: 1.5 marks per method + 1 for comparison table)* --- **(b) Inset Feed Concept:** A simple edge-fed patch has an input resistance of 100–400 $\Omega$ at the edge — far from the standard 50 $\Omega$. Drilling the substrate for a probe is one fix, but a **non-contacting, planar solution** is the **inset feed**. **Construction:** A narrow rectangular *notch* is cut from the patch edge (typically the $W$-edge or $L$-edge), and the microstrip line is etched into this notch. The feed point is now at a distance $y_0$ *inside* the patch from the radiating edge. **Why it works (resonant resistance variation):** The input resistance varies as a **cosine-squared function** of the inset depth: $$ R_{in}(y_0) = R_{in}(0)\cos^2\left(\frac{\pi}{L}y_0\right) $$ For a typical patch with $R_{in}(0) \approx 200\ \Omega$ at the edge, to match to 50 $\Omega$: $$ \frac{50}{200} = \cos^2\left(\frac{\pi y_0}{L}\right) \implies \cos\left(\frac{\pi y_0}{L}\right) = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5 $$ $$ \frac{\pi y_0}{L} = \frac{\pi}{3} \implies \boxed{y_0 = \frac{L}{3}} $$ In general: $$ y_0 = \frac{L}{\pi}\cos^{-1}\sqrt{\frac{Z_{in}}{R_{in}(0)}} $$ **Design steps for 50 $\Omega$ match:** 1. Compute $W$, $L$, $\varepsilon_{reff}$ using the standard design equations. 2. Estimate $R_{in}(0)$ from the standard formula (≈ 90 $\Omega$ for $W \ll \lambda_0$, up to 200 $\Omega$ for wider patches). 3. Solve $y_0 = (L/\pi)\cos^{-1}\sqrt{50/R_{in}(0)}$. 4. Realize the inset cut with depth $y_0$ and a notch width of approximately the feedline width $w_f$. **Limitation:** The inset creates a small perturbation to the resonant length, so the final $f_r$ shifts by 1–2%; a fine-tuning iteration on $L$ is required. *(7 marks: 2 for construction, 2 for the cosine-squared variation formula, 2 for $y_0 = L/3$ derivation, 1 for practical limitations)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Evaluations:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s with $h$ in cm and $W$ in mm. Always convert **all lengths to meters** before plugging into formulas. (Penalty: −1 to −2 marks per sub-question.) > 2. **Forgetting the $2\Delta L$:** Students often compute $L = L_{eff}$ directly — this gives a patch that resonates at a frequency *lower* than $f_r$. The resonant frequency would actually be $f_r' = c/(2L\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}) = f_r \cdot L_{eff}/L$. (Penalty: −1 mark, *and* full-marks forfeited for the validation step.) > 3. **Confusing $\varepsilon_r$ with $\varepsilon_{reff}$:** The patch *resonates* at $\varepsilon_{reff}$, not at $\varepsilon_r$. Using $\varepsilon_r$ in $L_{eff} = c/(2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ gives a 5–15% error. > 4. **In inset feed: forgetting the square root:** The input resistance is $\cos^2$, not $\cos$. Many students write $R_{in}(y_0) = R_{in}(0)\cos(\pi y_0/L)$ and lose 2–3 marks. > 5. **In aperture-coupling: omitting the slot:** A drawing that "just shows two substrates stacked" without a slot in the ground plane is **not** an aperture-coupled feed — it's a *proximity-coupled* feed. Examiners explicitly check this. (Penalty: up to −3 marks.) > 6. **Not stating mode of operation:** Always begin with *"The patch operates in the dominant $TM_{010}$ mode"* before any derivation. Examiners award 0.5–1 mark for this setup. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-density revision flashcard — read 3 times before exam.** - [ ] A rectangular patch antenna is a **half-wavelength microstrip resonator** with $L \approx \lambda_g/2$ where $\lambda_g = \lambda_0/\sqrt{\varepsilon_{reff}}$. - [ ] It operates in the **$TM_{010}$** mode, with the patch length $L$ along the resonance direction. - [ ] **Width** is chosen first via $W = (c/2f_r)\sqrt{2/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$; this fixes impedance and bandwidth. - [ ] **Effective dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_{reff}$ is *always* between 1 and $\varepsilon_r$ (inclusive). - [ ] **Fringing effect** is captured by $\Delta L$; the patch's *physical* length is **shorter** than $\lambda_g/2$ by an amount $2\Delta L$. - [ ] **Resonant input resistance** is highest at the radiating edges (~90–200 $\Omega$) and **zero at the center**. - [ ] Inset feed depth for 50 $\Omega$ match: typically $y_0 \approx L/3$ for a standard $W \approx \lambda_0/2$ patch. - [ ] **Four feeding methods** ranked by bandwidth: **Proximity > Aperture > Microstrip Line > Coaxial Probe**. - [ ] **Aperture-coupled feed** uses a **slot in the ground plane** between two substrates — best for arrays. - [ ] **Proximity-coupled feed** has **no slot** — uses capacitive fringing instead. - [ ] **Coaxial probe** is the easiest to match (just move the pin's $y_0$ position) but has the narrowest bandwidth due to probe inductance. - [ ] **Microstrip line** feed is the simplest to fabricate (single substrate, no drilling) but radiates spurious energy that distorts the pattern. - [ ] **Bandwidth** of a patch antenna can be approximately estimated as $BW \approx 3.77 (\varepsilon_r - 1)(\varepsilon_r)^2 \cdot (W/L) \cdot (h/\lambda_0)$ in % (Schaubert & Pozar). - [ ] **Ground plane rule:** $L_g \geq L + 6h$ and $W_g \geq W + 6h$ to approximate an infinite ground plane. - [ ] **Trade-off at a glance:** ↑$h$ → ↑bandwidth but ↑surface waves; ↑$\varepsilon_r$ → ↓size but ↓bandwidth. - [ ] **Exam tip:** Always state *"TM$_{010}$ mode"*, *"fringing fields at radiating edges"*, and *"effective medium model"* in derivations — these are **keyword triggers** for full marks. --- **End of Module 4 — Microstrip Rectangular Patch Antennas: Design Equations & Feeding Methods**

topics.title: Horn Antenna - Types, Principles, Expressions for E, H and Gain (No derivation required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT602 - MICROWAVES & ANTENNAS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Horn Antenna - Types, Principles, Expressions for E, H and Gain (No derivation required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Horn Antenna** is a high-gain, directional microwave antenna formed by the gradual flaring of the open end of a rectangular or circular waveguide. It functions as a smooth impedance-matching transformer between the confined wave inside a waveguide (characteristic impedance of the waveguide mode) and the free-space impedance of **377 Ω**. The flare introduces a tapered aperture that increases the radiating mouth area, enhances directivity, and minimizes standing wave reflections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 Scheme, the expected learning outcome is the ability to *identify horn antenna types, state the operating principle, and write down the closed-form expressions for the E-field, H-field, and Gain without derivation.*

### 1.2 Types of Horn Antennas

Horn antennas are classified by the dimension(s) along which the waveguide is flared:

| Type | Flare Plane | Resulting Aperture Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **E-plane Sectoral Horn** | Flared along the Electric field direction | Rectangular, narrow horizontally |
| **H-plane Sectoral Horn** | Flared along the Magnetic field direction | Rectangular, narrow vertically |
| **Pyramidal Horn** | Flared in BOTH E and H planes | Rectangular (most widely used) |
| **Conical Horn** | Circular waveguide flared in all directions | Circular (used with circular WG) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of the horn antenna like the **bell of a brass instrument (like a trumpet)**:

- A **flute** (closed pipe / plain waveguide) produces weak, dispersive sound because the energy exits through a tiny hole — equivalent to an open waveguide radiating with poor directivity and high mismatch.
- A **trumpet** (flared pipe / horn antenna) channels and expands the same air column through a bell, projecting the sound powerfully in one direction with great efficiency.

In electromagnetic terms: the rectangular waveguide confines the wave. Suddenly opening it into free space causes reflection (like shouting into a canyon). The horn's smooth, exponential-like flare acts like a megaphone, expanding the wavefront gradually so it leaves the antenna smoothly with a focused, narrow beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants**
> - Free-space impedance: **η₀ = 377 Ω** (≈ 120π Ω)
> - Phase constant in free space: **β = 2π/λ**
> - Velocity of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**

### 1.4 Operating Principle — Why Flaring Works

The horn antenna is a **natural extension of the aperture antenna** concept. A waveguide terminated abruptly has a small aperture (≈ a × b) compared to λ², giving poor gain. By gradually expanding the guide walls:

1. **Aperture area increases** → Directivity increases (since D ∝ aperture/λ²).
2. **Phase error across the aperture is bounded**, controlled by the flare length.
3. **Reflection coefficient drops** because impedance transitions smoothly.
4. **Higher power handling** is possible than for a dipole at microwaves.

The horn is the *natural evolutionary partner* of the parabolic dish — in fact, parabolic reflectors are physically fed by a horn antenna at the focal point (called the **primary feed**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Working Principle Step-by-Step

1. **Excitation by TE₁₀ Mode**: The dominant TE₁₀ mode travels down the rectangular feed waveguide with field components $E_y$, $H_x$, $H_z$ and no $E_x$ or $E_z$.

2. **Flare Initiation**: At the horn throat, the broad walls (a-dimension) or narrow walls (b-dimension) begin to flare outward linearly with axial distance, forming a wedge or pyramid.

3. **Phase Front Curvature**: Spherical wavefronts develop from the virtual apex of the horn. The phase difference between the center and the edges of the aperture creates a quadratic phase error.

4. **Aperture Illumination**: The dominant mode field pattern is preserved at the aperture, but modulated by a quadratic phase term.

5. **Radiation**: The aperture acts as a secondary source. Fourier transformation of the aperture field yields the far-field radiation pattern.

### 2.2 Quadratic Phase Error Mechanism

For a horn of axial length ρ (distance from apex to aperture), a point at the aperture offset *t* from the axis experiences an extra path length of:

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{t^2}{2\rho}$$

This translates into a quadratic phase term $e^{-j\beta t^2 / 2\rho}$ in the aperture field distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Rule of Thumb:**
> - **Shorter horn (small ρ):** Larger phase error → lower gain, higher sidelobes.
> - **Longer horn (large ρ):** Smaller phase error → higher gain, narrower beam.
> - **Optimum horn:** Phase error at aperture edges ≈ 3π/4 rad (~135°) — gives maximum gain for given aperture.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exhaustive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute-value notations to prevent markdown table corruption. The pipe symbol `|` is *forbidden* inside table cells.

| Parameter | E-plane Sectoral Horn | H-plane Sectoral Horn | Pyramidal Horn |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aperture E-field** $E_y(x,y)$ | $E_0 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{a}\right)\,e^{-j\beta x^{2}/(2\rho_e)}$ | $E_0 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{a_1}\right)\,e^{-j\beta y^{2}/(2\rho_h)}$ | $E_0 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{a_1}\right)\,e^{-j\beta\!\left[\dfrac{x^{2}}{2\rho_e}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{2\rho_h}\right]}$ |
| **Aperture H-field** $H_x$ | $-\dfrac{E_0}{\eta}\,\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{a}\right)\,e^{-j\beta x^{2}/(2\rho_e)}$ | $-\dfrac{E_0}{\eta}\,\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{a_1}\right)\,e^{-j\beta y^{2}/(2\rho_h)}$ | $-\dfrac{E_0}{\eta}\,\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{a_1}\right)\,e^{-j\beta\!\left[\dfrac{x^{2}}{2\rho_e}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{2\rho_h}\right]}$ |
| **Aperture Dimensions** | $a \times b_1$ | $a_1 \times b$ | $a_1 \times b_1$ |
| **Axial Length** | $\rho_e$ (in E-plane) | $\rho_h$ (in H-plane) | $\rho_e, \rho_h$ (in respective planes) |
| **Directivity (Balanis form)** | $D_E = \dfrac{a}{\lambda_e}\,\dfrac{4\pi b_1}{\lambda}\,\dfrac{1}{A_1+B_1}$ | $D_H = \dfrac{b}{\lambda_e}\,\dfrac{4\pi a_1}{\lambda}\,\dfrac{1}{A_2+B_2}$ | $D_P \approx \dfrac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}}\,\varepsilon_{ap}\,a_1 b_1$ |
| **Gain** | $G_E = D_E$ (lossless) | $G_H = D_H$ | $G_P = D_P \cdot \eta_{rad}$ |

Where:
- $a$ = broad wall dimension of feed waveguide
- $b$ = narrow wall dimension of feed waveguide
- $a_1, b_1$ = aperture dimensions of the horn
- $\rho_e, \rho_h$ = slant lengths from apex to aperture edge
- $\lambda_e$ = guide wavelength in feed WG = $\lambda/\sqrt{1-(\lambda/2a)^2}$
- $\varepsilon_{ap}$ = aperture efficiency (typical: **0.5 to 0.6** for pyramidal horns)
- $A_1, B_1, A_2, B_2$ are Bessel/cosine integral correction factors dependent on the maximum phase error $S$:

$$A_1 + B_1 = \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(C(u) + C(v)\right)^2 + \left(S(u) + S(v)\right)^2\right]$$

where $C(\cdot)$ and $S(\cdot)$ are Fresnel integrals and the limits $u, v$ depend on the aperture geometry.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Horn is Used |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Satellite ground stations** | Feed for parabolic reflectors at C/Ku/Ka bands |
| **Radar systems** | High gain (~20 dBi), narrow beam for target tracking |
| **Radio astronomy** | Ultra-low noise temperature, precise beam shape |
| **EMC/EMI test chambers** | Calibrated reference antenna for EMC measurements |
| **Microwave links (point-to-point)** | Line-of-sight communication at 4–40 GHz |
| **Automotive radars** (77 GHz) | Compact, easy to fabricate, high bandwidth |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Directive:** No derivation is required for this topic. However, for conceptual command, we *walk through* the logical flow from aperture field → far-field pattern → gain without invoking full wave-math rigor. All the equations above are **direct textbook results** (no derivation expected in exam).

### 3.1 Logical Derivation Chain (Conceptual Map)

**Stage 1 — Waveguide Excitation (TE₁₀ mode):**
Inside the feed waveguide of dimensions $a \times b$, the dominant TE₁₀ mode has fields:

$$E_y^{wg} = E_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)$$

$$H_x^{wg} = -\frac{E_0}{\eta}\,\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)$$

**Stage 2 — Flare Transition (Aperture Field):**
When the walls flare outward, the phase front becomes spherical. At a point on the aperture at transverse coordinate $t$, the additional path length from the virtual apex is:

$$\Delta\ell(t) = \frac{t^{2}}{2\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the slant length. This produces the phase correction term $e^{-j\beta t^2 / (2\rho)}$.

For a **Pyramidal Horn** (flared in both planes), both transverse coordinates contribute:

$$E_y^{ap}(x,y) = E_0 \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a_1}\right) \cdot e^{-j\beta\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2\rho_e} + \frac{y^{2}}{2\rho_h}\right]}$$

**Stage 3 — H-Field Recovery via Maxwell's Equations:**
By the impedance relation for TEM-like waves on the aperture plane:

$$H_x^{ap} = -\frac{E_y^{ap}}{\eta}$$

$$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$$

The $H_y$ component in the aperture is approximately zero (negligible) for TE₁₀-driven horns.

**Stage 4 — Far-Field Radiation via Fourier Transform:**
The far-zone electric field is essentially the **2D spatial Fourier transform** of the aperture field. The peak (boresight, $\theta = 0$) magnitude reduces to:

$$E_{\theta}^{far}(\theta=0) \propto \iint_{aperture} E_y^{ap}(x,y)\,dx\,dy$$

**Stage 5 — Directivity and Gain:**
By definition:

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi U_{max}}{P_{rad}}$$

For a horn antenna with aperture area $A_{phys} = a_1 \cdot b_1$, the directivity becomes:

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}}\,A_e = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}}\,\varepsilon_{ap}\,A_{phys}$$

Since horns are essentially lossless radiators (ohmic losses in the flared walls are negligible), **Gain ≈ Directivity**:

$$\boxed{\,G \approx D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}}\,\varepsilon_{ap}\,a_1 b_1\,}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Application — Worked Example

**Problem:** A pyramidal horn antenna operates at **10 GHz** with aperture dimensions **$a_1 = 15$ cm** and **$b_1 = 10$ cm**. The aperture efficiency is **$\varepsilon_{ap} = 0.5$**. Compute the maximum gain in dBi.

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**
Given $f = 10$ GHz:
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03\ \text{m} = 3\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Physical Aperture Area:**
$$A_{phys} = a_1 \times b_1 = 0.15 \times 0.10 = 0.015\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Aperture:**
$$A_e = \varepsilon_{ap} \cdot A_{phys} = 0.5 \times 0.015 = 0.0075\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Directivity (linear):**
$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}}\,A_e = \frac{4\pi \times 0.0075}{(0.03)^{2}} = \frac{0.0942}{0.0009} \approx 104.7$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dBi:**
$$G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(104.7) \approx 20.2\ \text{dBi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The expression $G = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}} \varepsilon_{ap} A_{phys}$ is the *most frequently asked formula* for horn antennas in KTU exams. Always write the numerical setup clearly — examiners award partial marks for *correct formula + correct substitution*.

### 3.3 Correction Factors $A_1, B_1$ — Quick Reference Table

| Horn Type | Correction Term | Argument |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E-plane sectoral | $A_1 + B_1$ | Function of $S_e = b_1^{2}/(8\lambda\rho_e)$ |
| H-plane sectoral | $A_2 + B_2$ | Function of $S_h = a_1^{2}/(8\lambda\rho_h)$ |
| Pyramidal | $(A_1+B_1)(A_2+B_2)$ | Both corrections multiply |

The functions $A_1, B_1$ are tabulated against the normalized maximum phase deviation $S$ — when $S$ is small, $A_1 + B_1 \to 1$ (no phase error), giving the textbook maximum directivity of a uniformly illuminated rectangular aperture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
